docs.title: Autonomous Research–Training–Execution System for Quantitative Trading
docs.text: An AI-powered trading system using LangGraph for orchestration, Optuna for optimization, and multi-agent LLMs for decision-making.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
# Clone with submodules
git clone --recurse-submodules https://github.com/manupadillaph/rainbow-trader.git
cd rainbow-trader

# Setup environment
cp .env.example .env
# Edit .env with your API keys

# Start with Docker
docker compose build
docker compose up -d
docker compose exec rainbow-dev bash

# (Recommended) install modules in editable mode once per venv for imports/tests
pip install \
  -e modules/common \
  -e modules/database  \
  -e modules/core  \
  -e modules/data-fetcher \
  -e modules/data-processing \
  -e modules/labeling-service \
  -e modules/window-preparer \
  -e modules/model-trainer \
  -e modules/backtesting-engine \
  -e modules/bot-logic \
  -e modules/housekeeping \
  -e modules/control-panel \
  -e modules/live-trading-runner \
  -e modules/graph-orchestrator\
  -e modules/graph-utils

# Create database file and schema (if it doesn't exist)
python -m rainbow.database create

# Apply migrations (database MUST exist)
python -m rainbow.database migrate generate "migration message"
python -m rainbow.database migrate apply

# Emit events (for orchestration/testing)
python -m rainbow.database emit-event --event DATA_FETCH_DONE

# Browse database with DB Browser for SQLite
python -m rainbow.database browse

# VPN (if needed)
python -m rainbow.data_fetcher start-vpn


# Fetch specific symbol/timeframe manually
python -m rainbow.data_fetcher fetch-symbol --symbol BTCUSDT --timeframe 1d --start 2024-01-01 --end 2024-04-01 --save-parquet --emit

# Delete Database (requires --execute)
python -m rainbow.housekeeping delete-database --preview  # safe preview
python -m rainbow.housekeeping delete-database --execute

# Delete artifacts (requires --execute)
python -m rainbow.housekeeping delete-parquet-files --preview  # safe preview
python -m rainbow.housekeeping delete-parquet-files --execute  # add --no-recreate to skip recreating folder

# Remove orphan parquet rows (requires --execute or --preview)
python -m rainbow.housekeeping cleanup-orphan-parquets --preview
python -m rainbow.housekeeping cleanup-orphan-parquets --execute

# Delete parquet files and db entries (requires --execute)
# add --preserve_raw_ohlcv to skip deleting raw entries
python -m rainbow.housekeeping delete-parquets --preserve_raw_ohlcv --preview  # safe preview
python -m rainbow.housekeeping delete-parquets --preserve_raw_ohlcv --execute

# Archive experiment files (requires --execute)
python -m rainbow.housekeeping archive-experiment-files --preview  # safe preview
python -m rainbow.housekeeping archive-experiment-files --execute

# Delete checkpoint files (requires --execute)
python -m rainbow.housekeeping delete-checkpoints --preview  # safe preview
python -m rainbow.housekeeping delete-checkpoints --execute

# List parquets with optional filters
python -m rainbow.core parquet list
python -m rainbow.core parquet list --category OHLCV --limit 10
python -m rainbow.core parquet list --symbol BTCUSDT
python -m rainbow.core parquet list --symbol BTCUSDT --timeframe 1d
python -m rainbow.core parquet list --experiment-id 1 --check-disk

# Show specific parquet by ID or config hash
python -m rainbow.core parquet get --id 1 --check-disk
python -m rainbow.core parquet get --config-hash 03cbc931499cd126946903bed86253a1f50077150beaa546a6de7f33feb471b3

# Load and display parquet file content with summary
python -m rainbow.core parquet read --id 1

# Experiments: create from config or fetch by id/uuid/name
python -m rainbow.core experiment create -d --config data/plans/experiments/templates/base/example-experiment.base.json
python -m rainbow.core experiment create --config '{"name": "exp1", "experiment_type": "trading", ...}'
python -m rainbow.core experiment get --id 1
python -m rainbow.core experiment get --uuid f7773217-56f9-4ecb-93fe-9dee6ab42b85
python -m rainbow.core experiment get --name example-experiment

# Analyze and fetch experiment requirements
python -m rainbow.core -d experiment view-requirements --id 1
python -m rainbow.core -d experiment fetch-requirements --id 1

# List experiments
python -m rainbow.core experiment list

# Generate labels
python -m rainbow.core -d experiment generate-labels --id 1
python -m rainbow.labeling_service -d generate-labels --experiment-id 1

# Generate sampled labels
python -m rainbow.core -d experiment generate-sampling --id 1
python -m rainbow.labeling_service -d generate-sampling --experiment-id 1

# Generate FAT windows for experiment
python -m rainbow.core -d experiment generate-fat-windows --id 1
python -m rainbow.window_preparer generate-fat-windows --experiment-id 1 --save-parquet

python -m rainbow.core -d experiment run-training --id 1
python -m rainbow.core -d experiment run-training --id 1 --suffix NEW_RUN --copy PREV_RUN

python -m rainbow.core -d experiment validate-parquets --id 1

python -m rainbow.core experiment backtest \
  --trial-id 1 \
  --initial-equity 10000

python -m rainbow.control_panel serve

# SERVE WITH HOT RELOAD
python -m rainbow.control_panel serve --no-build
python -m rainbow.control_panel serve --reload --no-build

cd modules/control-panel/frontend 
npm run build
npm run kill
npm run dev




optuna-dashboard sqlite:///data/checkpoints/2026-02-13T19-39-11Z_experiment-6-TEST_2026-02-13T19-38-50Z_TEST_02/optuna_study.sqlite

tensorboard --logdir data/checkpoints/2026-02-13T19-39-11Z_experiment-6-TEST_2026-02-13T19-38-50Z_TEST_02

python -m rainbow.model_trainer lit \
  --trial-id 500 \
  --max-examples 2000 \
  --host 0.0.0.0 \
  --port 7860

docs.title: Repository Structure
docs.text:
This is a monorepo with git submodules:

rainbow-trader/                     # Main repo (this one)
├── modules/                        # All modules (git submodules)
│   ├── graph-orchestrator/         # The Brain — LangGraph orchestration
│   ├── common/                     # Shared utilities (env, logging, config)
│   ├── database/                   # SQLite/Postgres + ORM
│   ├── data-fetcher/               # data download
│   ├── data-processing/            # Shared numeric logic
│   ├── labeling-service/           # Label generation
│   ├── window-preparer/            # FAT/SMALL window construction
│   ├── model-trainer/              # Optuna + model training
│   ├── bot-logic/                  # Order decision engine
│   ├── backtesting-engine/         # Offline simulation
│   ├── live-trading-runner/        # Real-time trading
│   ├── housekeeping/               # Maintenance tasks
│   └── control-panel/              # Web dashboard + CLI
├── docs/                           # All documentation
│   ├── MASTER-PLAN.md
│   ├── [...others].md
│   └── plans/                      # Work plans for large changes
├── data/                           # Heavy artifacts (gitignored)
└── database/                       # SQLite database file

docs.title: Tech Stack
docs.text:
• Orchestration: LangChain + LangGraph
  • Optimization: Optuna
  • Database: SQLite (dev) / PostgreSQL (prod)
  • Data: Polars
  • ML:
  • API: FastAPI + Uvicorn
  • Container: Docker + Docker Compose

docs.title: Rainbow Trader — Code Generation Standards
docs.text:
Single Source of Truth for all code generation, styling, documentation, and quality requirements.

All code in Rainbow Trader — whether generated by AI agents (coder-node) or human developers — MUST follow these standards.

docs.title: 1.1 General Rules
docs.text: Aspect Requirement Language All code, comments, docstrings, and documentation in English Style Guide PEP 8 Line Length Maximum 120 characters Indentation 4 spaces (no tabs) Encoding UTF-8

docs.title: 1.2 Python Style Guide
docs.text:
# Good: Clear, descriptive names
def calculate_moving_average(prices: list[float], window_size: int) -> float:
    """Calculate the simple moving average for a price series."""
    ...

# Bad: Abbreviated, unclear names
def calc_ma(p, w):
    ...

Naming Conventions:

Type Convention Example Functions snake_case calculate_rsi() Variables snake_case window_size Classes PascalCase ExperimentPlan Constants UPPER_SNAKE_CASE MAX_RETRIES Private _leading_underscore _internal_helper() Modules snake_case data_fetcher.py

docs.title: 1.3 Docstrings (Google Style)
docs.text:
All public functions, classes, and modules MUST have docstrings:

def compute_rsi(series: pl.Series, period: int = 14) -> pl.Series:
    """Compute the Relative Strength Index (RSI) for a price series.

    Args:
        series: Price series (typically close prices).
        period: Lookback period for RSI calculation. Defaults to 14.

    Returns:
        Series containing RSI values (0-100 scale).

    Raises:
        ValueError: If period is less than 2.

    Example:
        >>> prices = pl.Series([44, 44.5, 45, 44.8, 45.2])
        >>> rsi = compute_rsi(prices, period=3)
    """
    ...

docs.title: 1.4 Type Hints
docs.text:
Required for ALL function signatures. Enforced by mypy --strict.

from typing import Optional, List, Dict, Any
import polars as pl

def process_data(
    df: pl.DataFrame,
    columns: List[str],
    config: Optional[Dict[str, Any]] = None,
) -> pl.DataFrame:
    ...

docs.title: 2.0 CRITICAL: Always Use .venv
docs.text:
⚠️ ALL commands MUST be run inside the .venv virtual environment!

# Activate .venv FIRST (from project root)
source .venv/bin/activate

# Or prefix commands with .venv/bin/
.venv/bin/python -m pytest
.venv/bin/pip install -e .
.venv/bin/mypy src --strict

NEVER use system Python, conda, or any other environment!

The .venv is located at project root: rainbow-trader/.venv/

docs.title: 2.1 Before ANY Commit
docs.text:
Every code change MUST pass these checks without errors or warnings:

# Activate .venv first!
source .venv/bin/activate

# Run from module root directory

# 1. Unit tests with 100% coverage
pytest --cov=src --cov-fail-under=100

# 2. Type checking (strict mode)
mypy src --strict

# 3. Linting (zero errors, zero warnings)
ruff check src

All three must pass before committing.

docs.title: 2.2 Testing Requirements
docs.text:
Test Type Coverage When Required Unit Tests 100% All modules Property Tests Recommended Numeric functions, data transformations Integration Tests All interfaces Module boundaries, DB interactions E2E Tests Critical paths Full pipeline workflows

Test file naming:

  • test_<module_name>.py for unit tests
  • test_integration_<feature>.py for integration tests
  • test_e2e_<workflow>.py for end-to-end tests

docs.title: 2.3 Logging Standards
docs.text:
Use structlog for structured logging:

from common import get_logger

logger = get_logger(__name__)

# Log levels
logger.debug("Processing batch", batch_size=100, duration_ms=45)
logger.info("Experiment started", experiment_id="exp_123")
logger.warning("Approaching budget limit", current=4.5, limit=5.0)
logger.error("Failed to fetch data", symbol="BTCUSDT", error=str(e))
logger.critical("Database connection lost")

Rules:

  • All module entry/exit points: INFO level
  • All exceptions: ERROR level with stack trace
  • Performance-critical operations: DEBUG level with duration
  • CLI entrypoints MUST route logging config through the shared --config/--set pattern and honor the LOG__LEVEL env var as default.
  • Before configuring logging, always call common.ensure_env_loaded() once per process. This guarantees .env.local / .env are loaded hierarchically (submodule first, then repo root).
  • Wrap error-prone sections in try/except and log with log.error(..., exc_info=True) before re-raising or translating the exception. Add log.debug breadcrumbs so failures can be reconstructed from logs.
  • Do not swallow exceptions silently—every catch block must emit a log entry.
  • Block Comments: Write code in logical task blocks, each starting with a comment explaining what that block does. This makes code readable from start to finish. Don't be afraid to add many comments—the more, the better.
  • Extensive try/except: Wrap risky operations in try/except blocks with log.debug breadcrumbs throughout all code paths.

docs.title: 2.4 Environment Loading & CLI Flags
docs.text: Configuration definitions (variable names, precedence, examples) are centralized in CONFIGURATION.md. Use the shared patterns when wiring any CLI so behavior stays consistent across modules.

docs.title: Configuration Best Practices
docs.text: • Keep module READMEs focused on module-specific-config variables; link to CONFIGURATION.md for globals and precedence.

docs.title: Example Usage
docs.text:
python -m module_name --help
python -m module_name --config config/local.yaml run
python -m module_name --set log.level=DEBUG run
CONFIG_NAME__LOG__LEVEL=INFO python -m module_name run

docs.title: 3. Required Libraries
docs.text:
Purpose Library Notes Testing pytest With pytest-cov for coverage Data Processing Polars Use df.to_pandas() ONLY for library interop CLI Typer All command-line interfaces Logging structlog Structured logging Linting ruff Linting and formatting Type Checking mypy Strict mode required

Important: Use Polars, NOT pandas, for all data processing.

# Good
import polars as pl
df = pl.read_parquet("data.parquet")

# Bad (avoid unless required for library interop)
import pandas as pd
df = pd.read_parquet("data.parquet")

docs.title: 3.1 Common Utilities
docs.text:
Shared helpers live in the modules/common repository—use them instead of re-implementing logging, environment, or path utilities.

  • Logging: from common.logging
  • Env: from common.env
  • Paths: from common.paths
  • Install deps (pulls modules/common): pip install -r requirements.txt

docs.title: 4.1 Standard Folder Layout
docs.text:
Every module MUST follow this structure:

rainbow-{module-name}/
├── .gitignore
├── .env.example          # MANDATORY - env vars template for this module
├── CHANGELOG.md          # MANDATORY - change tracking
├── README.md             # Use README_MODULE_TEMPLATE.md as base
├── pyproject.toml
├── requirements.txt
├── config/
│   └── cli_defaults.yaml # MANDATORY - default CLI config values
├── src/{module_name}/
│   ├── __init__.py
│   ├── cli.py            # Typer CLI entry point
│   ├── defaults.py       # MANDATORY - Python dict fallback for config
│   └── ...
└── tests/
    ├── __init__.py
    └── test_*.py

docs.title: 4.1.1 Required Configuration Files
docs.text:
File Purpose .env.example Template showing all env vars the module supports (prefix MODULE_NAME__). Copy to .env for local overrides. config/cli_defaults.yaml Default values loaded by CLI when no user config is provided. src/{module}/defaults.py Python dict with same keys as YAML; serves as ultimate fallback if YAML is missing. Keep in sync with cli_defaults.yaml.

Configuration Loading Precedence (highest → lowest):

  1. CLI --set flags (--set log.level=DEBUG, --config, etc.)
  2. Environment variables (MODULE__SECTION__KEY=value)
  3. User-provided YAML (--config path/to/config.yaml)
  4. Module default YAML (config/cli_defaults.yaml)
  5. Python defaults (src/{module}/defaults.py)

Use common.load_cli_config() to merge all layers automatically.

docs.title: 4.2 File Naming Conventions
docs.text: Type Convention Example Python modules snake_case.py data_fetcher.py Test files test_<module>.py test_data_fetcher.py Config files snake_case.yaml graph_config.yaml Documentation UPPER_CASE.md README.md, CHANGELOG.md

docs.title: 5.1 Format
docs.text:
Every code modification MUST be documented in CHANGELOG.md at the module root:

# Changelog

All notable changes to this module are documented in this file.

For code generation standards, see [CODE-GENERATION.md](../CODE-GENERATION.md).

## CHANGE [YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM] - Brief Description

### Changes

- **Added/Modified/Removed:** `path/to/file.py`
  - Description of what was changed
  - Function/class affected: `function_name()` or `ClassName`

### Verification

\```bash
# Command(s) to verify the change works
pytest tests/test_module.py -v
\```


### Notes (template)

- Any relevant context, dependencies, or follow-up tasks

> **MD032 reminder:** Markdown lists MUST have a blank line **before and after** the list block, and nested lists inside a bullet require a blank line between levels. When editing `CHANGELOG.md`, make sure there is a blank line between the `### Changes` heading and the first bullet, as well as after the last bullet, otherwise `markdownlint` will raise `MD032/blanks-around-lists`.

Note for coder: dont put results of commands in Verification section, just the commands to run

Note for coder: we are using triple backslashes \`` above to escape the backticks in this document. In actualCHANGELOG.md`, use standard triple backticks.

docs.title: 5.2 Rules
docs.text:
1. One entry per change session — Group related changes in a single entry
  2. Timestamp required — Use ISO format: YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM
  3. Include verification commands — Tests or CLI commands to validate the change
  4. Be specific — List exact files, functions, and classes modified
  5. Link to context — Reference experiment IDs, issue numbers, or research findings if applicable
  6. First line must be a heading — Start file with # Changelog (MD041 compliance)
  7. Newest entries go on top — Append every new ## CHANGE [...] section immediately after the file header so the changelog is reverse chronological.
  8. Bulk changes from git — If many commits were made during a session, use git to reconstruct the changelog:

```bash # Get commits from last session (adjust date/count as needed) git log --oneline -20

# Get detailed changes for a commit range git log --pretty=format:"- %s" HEAD~10..HEAD

# See files changed git diff --stat HEAD~10..HEAD

# See full diff for specific files git diff HEAD~10..HEAD -- src/module/file.py ```

Then consolidate all commits into a single CHANGELOG entry with the most important changes summarized.

docs.title: 5.3 Complete Example
docs.text:
# Changelog

All notable changes to this module are documented in this file.

For code generation standards, see [CODE-GENERATION.md](../CODE-GENERATION.md).

## CHANGE [2024-12-15 14:30] - Added RSI indicator with relative pipeline

### Changes

- **Added:** `src/core_processing/indicators/rsi.py`
  - New function `compute_rsi(series, period)` for raw RSI calculation
  - New function `compute_rsi_relative(series, period)` for price-relative RSI
- **Modified:** `src/core_processing/indicators/__init__.py`
  - Exported new RSI functions

### Verification

\```bash
# Run unit tests
pytest tests/indicators/test_rsi.py -v

# Run with coverage
pytest tests/indicators/test_rsi.py --cov=src/core_processing/indicators/rsi --cov-fail-under=100

# Manual verification
python -c "from core_processing.indicators import compute_rsi; print(compute_rsi.__doc__)"
\```

### Notes (RSI example)

- Related to ExperimentPlan exp_abc123 feature requirements
- Follows existing indicator pattern from EMA implementation

## CHANGE [2024-12-14 10:00] - Initial module scaffold

### Changes

- **Added:** Initial module structure with CLI stub

### Verification

\```bash
python -m module_name --help
\```

### Notes (initial scaffold example)

- Baseline scaffold before implementation

Note for coder: we are using triple backslashes \`` above to escape the backticks in this document. In actualCHANGELOG.md`, use standard triple backticks.

Note for coder: the header is this, and we keep it always firts:

# Changelog

All notable changes to this module are documented in this file.

For code generation standards, see [CODE-GENERATION.md](../CODE-GENERATION.md).

docs.title: 6.1 Required Sections
docs.text:
Every module README MUST include:

  1. Title & Description — Module name and one-line description
  2. References — Links to MASTER-PLAN sections
  3. Current Sprint — Status and next action
  4. Code Generation Requirements — Quick reference to this document
  5. Installation — How to install the module
  6. Usage — CLI and library usage examples
  7. Testing — How to run tests
  8. Related Modules — Dependencies and relationships

docs.title: 6.2 Template Reference
docs.text: Use README_MODULE_TEMPLATE.md as the base for all module READMEs.

docs.title: 6.3 README Must Grow With Code
docs.text:
The README is a living document. It MUST be updated whenever code changes:

Code Change README Update Required New function/class added Add to Usage section with example New CLI command Add to CLI section with --help output New config option Document in Configuration section New dependency Add to Installation section Breaking change Update all affected examples New feature Add description and usage example

Rules:

  1. Every PR/commit that adds functionality MUST update the README
  2. Examples must be runnable — copy-paste should work
  3. Keep examples minimal — show the simplest case first
  4. Document edge cases — mention limitations and gotchas

docs.title: 6.4 TODO Sections in README
docs.text:
READMEs can have <!-- TODO: ... --> comments or explicit TODO sections for planned features:

## Usage

### Current Features

- `fetch --symbol BTCUSDT` — Fetch OHLCV data

### Planned Features

<!-- TODO: Document these when implemented -->

- `fetch --live` — Real-time streaming (not yet implemented)
- `fetch --cache` — Local caching layer (pending)

Or use inline TODOs:

## Installation

\```bash
pip install -e .
\```

<!-- TODO: Add Docker installation instructions -->
<!-- TODO: Document environment variables -->

docs.title: 6.5 README Update Checklist
docs.text:
When adding new code, update these README sections:

  • [ ] Description — Does it still accurately describe the module?
  • [ ] Current Sprint — Update status percentage and next action
  • [ ] Installation — Any new dependencies?
  • [ ] Usage — Add examples for new functions/commands
  • [ ] CLI — Document new commands with --help output
  • [ ] Configuration — New config options?
  • [ ] Testing — New test commands?
  • [ ] API Reference — New public functions/classes?

docs.title: 7. Markdown Formatting (Linting Compliance)
docs.text:
See MARKDOWN-STYLE.md for complete Markdown formatting rules.

All Markdown files MUST pass markdownlint without warnings.

docs.title: 7.1 Quick Reference
docs.text:
The Golden Rule: Always add a blank line before and after:

  • Headings (#, ##, ###, etc.)
  • Lists (-, *, 1.)
  • Code blocks (```)
  • Tables
  • Blockquotes (>)

docs.title: 7.2 Most Common Errors
docs.text: Error Fix MD022 Add blank line before AND after heading MD032 Add blank line before AND after list MD031 Add blank line before AND after code block MD041 Start file with # Title MD040 Add language after (e.g.,python)

docs.title: 8. Git Workflow
docs.text:
See GIT-COMMITS.md for complete git workflow guide.

This document covers: - Commit message format (Conventional Commits) - Branch strategy and when to branch - Quality gates before commit - How to handle submodules correctly - Recovery steps when things go wrong

docs.title: 8.1 Quick Reference
docs.text:
Commit Format: <type>(<scope>): <description>

Types: feat, fix, docs, test, refactor, style, chore

Quality Gates (must pass before commit):

pytest --cov=src --cov-fail-under=100
mypy src --strict
ruff check src

docs.title: 8.2 Self-Improving Code Flow
docs.text:
When the coder-node modifies code:

  1. Write code changes to the appropriate module
  2. Run unit tests, property tests, integration tests
  3. If all pass → commit changes locally
  4. Never auto-push to remote — user reviews and pushes manually

For detailed branching strategy, commit grouping, and submodule handling, see GIT-COMMITS.md.

docs.title: 10. Iterative Development & Skeleton Code
docs.text: When working iteratively, it's often useful to build skeleton functions first — functions that accept inputs and return dummy outputs, throw exceptions, or log placeholder messages.

docs.title: 10.1 TODO Comments
docs.text:
ALWAYS mark incomplete code with # TODO: comments. This makes it easy to track what's pending.

# Good: Clear TODO with context
def calculate_portfolio_risk(positions: list[Position]) -> RiskMetrics:
    """Calculate risk metrics for a portfolio of positions."""
    # TODO: Implement VaR calculation
    # TODO: Add correlation matrix support
    # TODO: Handle edge case of empty positions
    raise NotImplementedError("calculate_portfolio_risk not yet implemented")


def fetch_market_data(symbol: str, timeframe: str) -> pl.DataFrame:
    """Fetch OHLCV data for a symbol."""
    # TODO: Connect to actual data source (Binance API)
    # TODO: Add caching layer
    logger.warning("fetch_market_data is a stub - returning dummy data")
    return pl.DataFrame({
        "timestamp": [],
        "open": [],
        "high": [],
        "low": [],
        "close": [],
        "volume": [],
    })

docs.title: 10.2 Skeleton Patterns
docs.text:
Choose the appropriate pattern based on context:

Pattern When to Use Example raise NotImplementedError Function is critical, must be implemented before use Core business logic logger.warning() + dummy return Function can be skipped temporarily Optional features pass with TODO Empty class methods or abstract stubs Interface definitions 
# Pattern 1: NotImplementedError for critical functions
def train_model(features: pl.DataFrame, labels: pl.Series) -> Model:
    # TODO: Implement model training pipeline
    # TODO: Add hyperparameter tuning
    raise NotImplementedError("train_model pending implementation")


# Pattern 2: Warning + dummy return for optional features
def send_notification(message: str) -> bool:
    # TODO: Integrate with Telegram/Discord
    logger.warning("send_notification is a stub", message=message)
    return True  # Pretend success


# Pattern 3: Pass for interface stubs
class BaseAgent:
    def process(self, state: AgentState) -> AgentState:
        # TODO: Implement in subclass
        pass

docs.title: 10.3 Rules for Skeleton Code
docs.text:
1. Every skeleton MUST have a # TODO: comment explaining what needs to be done
  2. Be specific — # TODO: Add error handling is better than # TODO: fix this
  3. Include context — mention related functions, APIs, or requirements
  4. Use consistent format — # TODO: (uppercase, with colon)
  5. Track in CHANGELOG — note that skeleton was added, pending full implementation

docs.title: 10.4 Finding TODOs
docs.text:
Use grep to find all pending work:

# Find all TODOs in a module
grep -rn "# TODO:" src/

# Count TODOs
grep -rc "# TODO:" src/ | grep -v ":0$"

docs.title: 11. Work Plans for Medium/Large Changes
docs.text:
See WORK-PLANS.md for the complete guide on creating and executing work plans.

This document covers:

  • When to create a plan (triggers and thresholds)
  • Two-phase workflow (Creation → Execution)
  • Plan template with all required sections
  • Findings format and user decision process
  • Task list rules and granularity
  • Execution rules (what you CAN and CANNOT do)
  • Progress tracking and archival
  • Human-AI collaboration patterns
  • Common mistakes and how to avoid them

docs.title: 11.1 Quick Reference
docs.text:
When to create a plan:

  • Changes affecting >5 files
  • Cross-module changes
  • Architecture changes
  • Breaking changes
  • Coordinated updates (docs + code + tests + configs)

Two phases:

  1. Phase 1: Creation — Plan is flexible, iterate with user
  2. Phase 2: Execution — Plan is FROZEN, execute exactly as written

During execution:

  • ✅ CAN: Mark tasks done [x], update progress %, add notes
  • ❌ CANNOT: Modify tasks, add/remove tasks, change order

If plan is wrong: Create a NEW plan, don't modify existing one.

docs.title: 11.2 Plan Location
docs.text:
• Active plans: docs/plans/PLAN_NAME.md
  • Completed plans: docs/plans/archived/

docs.title: 11.3 Plan Template
docs.text: See 11-example-plans

docs.title: 12. Critical Rule: Read Before Editing
docs.text:
NEVER edit a file without reading it completely first.

This applies to: - Documentation files (README, CHANGELOG, etc.) - Configuration files (YAML, .env, etc.) - Code files (Python, etc.)

Why: - Prevents duplication - Maintains coherence - Preserves context - Avoids conflicts - Ensures logical flow

Process: 1. Read entire file using read_file tool 2. Understand structure and existing content 3. Identify where new content fits logically 4. Make coherent edit that integrates smoothly

Bad example:

# Just append to end without reading
grep -n "def main" file.py  # Find line number
sed -i '50a new_function()' file.py  # Insert blindly

Good example:

# Read entire file first
read_file("file.py")
# Understand: main() is at line 50, imports at top, helpers at bottom
# Edit: Add new import at top, new helper before main()
edit(file_path, old_string=..., new_string=...)

docs.title: 13. Critical Rule: No Legacy/Deprecated Code
docs.text:
NEVER leave old, deprecated, or legacy code in the codebase unless explicitly requested by the user.

Rules:

  • The codebase must be as clean as possible at all times
  • Every single function must have a use (no dead code)
  • Do not keep code "for compatibility" or "just in case" unless user explicitly asks
  • When refactoring, remove the old implementation completely
  • If replacing a function, delete the old one (don't rename to _old or _deprecated)

Exceptions (ONLY if user explicitly requests):

  • User says "keep the old version for reference"
  • User says "leave it for backward compatibility"
  • User explicitly asks to preserve something

When in doubt: Ask the user if old code should be kept or removed.

docs.title: 14. Opportunistic Improvements
docs.text:
When viewing code for a task, you may notice improvements unrelated to the current task.

DO make the improvement if:

  • It's a small, obvious fix (typo, formatting, naming consistency)
  • It aligns with patterns established elsewhere in the codebase
  • It doesn't contradict any documented rules
  • You're confident it's correct

ASK the user first if:

  • The change is significant or architectural
  • You're unsure if it contradicts something
  • It might have side effects
  • It's a recommendation but you have doubts

DON'T:

  • Make large changes without asking
  • Change things without understanding why
  • Refactor entire files when the task was small
  • Make changes you can't explain

Balance: Finding improvements while viewing code is good, but don't go overboard changing things the user didn't ask for.

docs.title: 15. Documentation May Be Outdated
docs.text:
Reality: Despite best efforts, documentation can become outdated, inconsistent, or incompatible between all of them and the actual code.

Examples:

  • Module READMEs may not reflect current implementation
  • Config docs may lag behind new configuration system
  • Examples may use old patterns

When you notice inconsistencies:

  1. Small inconsistencies: Fix them opportunistically (see Section 14)
  2. Large inconsistencies: Ask the user before making changes
  3. Code vs Docs conflict: Code is usually the source of truth, but ask if unsure

This rule supersedes others: If docs say one thing but code does another, don't blindly follow docs. Investigate and ask.

docs.title: 16. Quick Reference Checklist
docs.text:
Before submitting any code:

  • [ ] Language: All code, comments, docstrings in English
  • [ ] Style: PEP 8 compliant, 120 char max line length
  • [ ] Docstrings: Google style format on all public functions/classes
  • [ ] Type Hints: All function signatures typed (mypy --strict passes)
  • [ ] Tests: 100% coverage (pytest --cov-fail-under=100 passes)
  • [ ] Linting: Zero errors (ruff check passes)
  • [ ] Logging: Using structlog via common.get_logger()
  • [ ] Data: Using Polars (not pandas)
  • [ ] CLI: Using Typer
  • [ ] CHANGELOG: Updated with this change
  • [ ] Markdown: Passes markdownlint (blank lines around headings, lists, code blocks)
  • [ ] No Legacy: No deprecated or unused code left behind

docs.title: Configuration Guide
docs.text: Rainbow Trader's configuration system with clear priority rules.

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
• Configuration Guide
  • Table of Contents
  • Quick Start
  • Priority Order
    • Visual Flow
  • Configuration Sources
    • 1. Environment Variables (.env)
    • 2. YAML Config Files
    • 3. CLI Flags
    • 4. DEFAULTS Class Variable
  • BaseConfig Classes
    • Creating a Config Class
    • Using a Config Class
    • Automatic Naming
    • Nested Configs
    • Dict of Configs with Inheritance
  • Naming Conventions
    • Environment Variables
    • CLI Flags
  • Missing Fields = Error
  • deep_merge Behavior
  • Examples
    • Example 1: Simple Module Config
    • Example 2: Enum Conversion
    • Example 3: Full Priority Chain
  • Files Reference

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
# 1. Copy environment template
cp .env.example .env

# 2. Edit your values
nano .env

# 3. Run any module (config loads automatically)
python -m database migrate

docs.title: Priority Order
docs.text:
Configuration values are merged from multiple sources. Higher priority wins.

Priority Source Example 1 kwargs to Config class LogConfig.from_config(config_obj, level="DEBUG") 2 CLI --set flags --set log.level=DEBUG 3 Config file (-c/--config) --config custom.yaml 4 Environment variables LOG__LEVEL=DEBUG 5 YAML file per config class config/log.yaml 6 DEFAULTS class variable DEFAULTS = {"level": "INFO"}

docs.title: Visual Flow
docs.text:
ctx.obj = {
        ()
                            │
                            ▼
                ┌───────────────────────┐
                │  1. CLI --set flags   │  ◄── Highest priority
                │  2. Config file       │
                │  3. Env vars + .env   │
                └───────────────────────┘
                            │
                            ▼
                       config_obj
                            │
                            ▼
                  BaseConfig.__init__()
                            │
                            ▼
                ┌───────────────────────┐
                │  1. DEFAULTS          │  ◄── Lowest priority
                │  2. YAML file         │
                │  3. config_obj        │
                │  4. kwargs            │  ◄── Highest priority
                └───────────────────────┘
                            │
                            ▼
                    Final Config Object

docs.title: 1. Environment Variables (.env)
docs.text:
Located at project root. Use __ (double underscore) for nesting.

# .env
LOG__LEVEL=INFO
LOG__FORMAT=TXT
LOG__TARGET=TERMINAL
DATABASE__SQLITE_PATH=rainbow_trader.sqlite
FOLDERS__CONFIG=./config
FOLDERS__DATABASE=./database
FOLDERS__DATA=./data

# API Keys
KEYS__OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...

Mapping:

Env Variable Becomes In YAML LOG__LEVEL {"log": {"level": ...}} log.level LOG__FORMAT {"log": {"format": ...}} log.format LOG__TARGET {"log": {"target": ...}} log.target DATABASE__SQLITE_PATH {"database": {"sqlite_path": ...}} database.sqlite_path FOLDERS__DATA {"folders": {"data": ...}} folders.data KEYS__OPENAI_API_KEY {"keys": {"openai_api_key": ...}} keys.openai_api_key

docs.title: 2. YAML Config Files
docs.text:
Each BaseConfig subclass automatically loads its YAML file from config/.

Config Class YAML File Section Key LogConfig config/log.yaml log DatabaseConfig config/database.yaml database DataProviderConfig config/data-provider.yaml data_provider

Example config/log.yaml:

log:
  level: INFO
  format: TXT

docs.title: 3. CLI Flags
docs.text:
Override any value at runtime:

# Single override
python -m database --set log.level=DEBUG migrate

# Multiple overrides
python -m database --set log.level=DEBUG --set log.format=json migrate

# Use custom config file
python -m database --config custom.yaml migrate

# Inline JSON
python -m database --config '{"log": {"level": "DEBUG"}}' migrate

docs.title: 4. DEFAULTS Class Variable
docs.text:
Lowest priority. Defined in each config class:

class LogConfig(BaseConfig):
    DEFAULTS: ClassVar[dict[str, Any]] = {
        "level": "INFO",
        "format": "txt",
    }

    level: str
    format: str

docs.title: BaseConfig Classes
docs.text: All configuration classes inherit from BaseConfig (Pydantic BaseModel).

docs.title: Creating a Config Class
docs.text:
from typing import Any, ClassVar
from rainbow.common.types.configs.base_config import BaseConfig

class MyConfig(BaseConfig):
    """My module configuration."""

    # Default values (lowest priority)
    # Fields listed here will NOT fail validation
    DEFAULTS: ClassVar[dict[str, Any]] = {
        "timeout": 30,   # ← Has default, won't fail
        "retries": 3,    # ← Has default, won't fail
    }

    # Fields (type hints required)
    timeout: int         # ← Covered by DEFAULTS
    retries: int         # ← Covered by DEFAULTS
    api_key: str         # ← NOT in DEFAULTS = ValidationError if missing!

What happens:

  • timeout → Uses 30 from DEFAULTS if not provided elsewhere
  • retries → Uses 3 from DEFAULTS if not provided elsewhere
  • api_key → Raises ValidationError if not in env, YAML, CLI, or config_obj

docs.title: Using a Config Class
docs.text:
from rainbow.common.helpers import Config

# Load merged config from all sources
config_obj = ctx.obj = {
        ()

# Create typed config instance
my_config = MyConfig.from_config(config_obj)

# Access values with type safety
print(my_config.timeout)  # int
print(my_config.api_key)  # str

docs.title: Automatic Naming
docs.text:
Class names are automatically converted:

Class Name Section Key YAML File LogConfig log log.yaml DatabaseConfig database database.yaml DataProviderConfig data_provider data-provider.yaml MyCustomConfig my_custom my-custom.yaml

docs.title: Nested Configs
docs.text:
Parent configs can contain child configs:

class ChildConfig(BaseConfig):
    DEFAULTS: ClassVar[dict[str, Any]] = {"value": 100}
    value: int

class ParentConfig(BaseConfig):
    DEFAULTS: ClassVar[dict[str, Any]] = {"name": "parent"}
    name: str
    child: ChildConfig  # Automatically initialized

docs.title: Dict of Configs with Inheritance
docs.text:
For dict[str, BaseConfig] fields, use InheritFrom to share defaults and KeyToType for specific types:

from typing import Annotated
from rainbow.common.types.configs.base_config import BaseConfig, InheritFrom, KeyToType

# Base with shared fields
class BaseProviderConfig(BaseConfig):
    retry_max_attempts: int
    retry_backoff_secs_base: int

# Specific providers can add their own fields
class BinanceProviderConfig(BaseProviderConfig):
    api_key: str | None = None

class BybitProviderConfig(BaseProviderConfig):
    testnet: bool = False

# Map YAML keys to specific types
PROVIDER_TYPE_MAP = {
    "binance": BinanceProviderConfig,
    "bybit": BybitProviderConfig,
}

class DataProviderConfig(BaseConfig):
    base_provider: BaseProviderConfig  # Shared defaults

    # InheritFrom: each provider inherits from "base_provider"
    # KeyToType: "binance" → BinanceProviderConfig, "bybit" → BybitProviderConfig
    providers: Annotated[
        dict[str, BaseProviderConfig],
        InheritFrom("base_provider"),
        KeyToType(PROVIDER_TYPE_MAP)
    ]

YAML example:

data_provider:
  base_provider:
    retry_max_attempts: 5
    retry_backoff_secs_base: 2

  providers:
    binance:
      retry_max_attempts: 3  # Override base
      api_key: "xxx"         # BinanceProviderConfig-specific
    bybit:
      testnet: true          # BybitProviderConfig-specific
      # retry_* inherited from base_provider

Result:

  • binance: BinanceProviderConfig(retry_max_attempts=3, retry_backoff_secs_base=2, api_key="xxx")
  • bybit: BybitProviderConfig(retry_max_attempts=5, retry_backoff_secs_base=2, testnet=True)

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text:
# Pattern: SECTION__KEY=value
LOG__LEVEL=DEBUG
DATABASE__SQLITE_PATH=rainbow_trader.sqlite
FOLDERS__DATA=./data
KEYS__OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...

docs.title: CLI Flags
docs.text:
# Pattern: --set section.key=value
--set log.level=DEBUG
--set database.sqlite_path=rainbow_trader.sqlite
--set keys.openai_api_key=sk-...

docs.title: Missing Fields = Error
docs.text:
CRITICAL: If a required field has no value from ANY source, Pydantic raises ValidationError.

class MyConfig(BaseConfig):
    # NO DEFAULTS defined

    required_field: str      # MUST come from somewhere
    optional_field: str | None = None  # Has Python default, OK

# This FAILS with ValidationError
MyConfig.from_config()
# Error: "required_field" is missing

# This WORKS
config_obj = {"my": {"required_field": "value"}}
MyConfig.from_config(config_obj)

Why this matters:

  • No silent failures with undefined values
  • Users know exactly which fields need configuration
  • Configuration is explicit, never implicit

Sources that satisfy a required field:

  1. kwargs to constructor
  2. CLI --set flag
  3. Config file (YAML/JSON)
  4. Environment variable
  5. YAML file for the config class
  6. DEFAULTS class variable
  7. Python default value on the field itself

docs.title: deep_merge Behavior
docs.text:
When merging configs, deep_merge has specific rules:

Scenario Result Empty dict {} Does NOT clear existing values Explicit None DOES overwrite (when intentional) Missing key Preserves existing value

Why this matters for Optional fields:

class SimulationConfig(BaseConfig):
    random_seed: int | None  # None = use random, int = reproducible

# DEFAULTS
{"simulation": {"random_seed": 123}}

# User config with explicit None
{"simulation": {"random_seed": None}}

# Result: random_seed = None (user's choice wins)

docs.title: Example 1: Simple Module Config
docs.text:
# my_module/config.py
from typing import Any, ClassVar
from rainbow.common.types.configs.base_config import BaseConfig

class MyModuleConfig(BaseConfig):
    DEFAULTS: ClassVar[dict[str, Any]] = {
        "batch_size": 32,
        "learning_rate": 0.001,
    }

    batch_size: int
    learning_rate: float
    model_path: str  # Required - no default

# config/my-module.yaml
my_module:
  model_path: ./models/default.pt

# Override at runtime
python -m my_module --set my_module.batch_size=64 train

docs.title: Example 2: Enum Conversion
docs.text:
from enum import Enum

class LogLevel(str, Enum):
    DEBUG = "DEBUG"
    INFO = "INFO"
    WARNING = "WARNING"
    ERROR = "ERROR"

class LogConfig(BaseConfig):
    DEFAULTS: ClassVar[dict[str, Any]] = {"level": "INFO"}

    level: LogLevel  # Automatic case-insensitive conversion

# All of these work:
LOG__LEVEL=debug
LOG__LEVEL=DEBUG
LOG__LEVEL=Debug

docs.title: Example 3: Full Priority Chain
docs.text:
# 1. DEFAULTS (lowest)
DEFAULTS = {"level": "INFO"}

# 2. YAML file
# config/log.yaml
log:
  level: WARNING

# 3. Environment variable
LOG__LEVEL=ERROR

# 4. CLI flag (highest)
--set log.level=DEBUG

# Result: level = "DEBUG"

docs.title: Files Reference
docs.text: File Purpose .env Environment variables (gitignored) .env.example Template for .env config/*.yaml Default config values */DEFAULTS Python defaults in config classes

docs.title: Data Storage
docs.text: Single Source of Truth for data directory structure, caching strategy, and artifact management.

docs.title: 📑 Table of Contents
docs.text:
• Data Storage
  • 📑 Table of Contents
  • 📚 Related Documentation
  • Overview
  • Directory Structure
  • Artifact Caching Strategy
    • 1. Base Data (OHLCV)
    • 2. Derived Features (Indicators/Norm)
    • 3. Labels
    • 4. Windows (FAT/SMALL)
  • Global Parquet Cache
  • Parquet Naming Convention
  • Feature to Parquet Lookup
  • Database Registration

docs.title: 📚 Related Documentation
docs.text: Document Description MASTER-PLAN.md System architecture DATABASE-SCHEMA.md Database schema (parquet_files, labels_files tables) CONFIGURATION.md FOLDERS__DATA, FOLDERS__DATABASE, DATABASE__SQLITE_PATH

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The data/ directory holds all heavy artifacts and runtime data. It is NOT versioned in Git.

  • Location: ./data/ by default (configurable via FOLDERS__DATA env var)
  • Purpose: Store OHLCV, indicators, labels, windows, checkpoints, logs
  • Managed by: housekeeping module for cleanup and archival

docs.title: Directory Structure
docs.text:
data/                                 # Default: ./data/ (configurable via FOLDERS__DATA)
├── OHLCV/                            # GLOBAL cache — raw market data
│   └── {symbol}/{timeframe}/         # e.g., BTCUSDT/5m/raw.parquet
├── indicators/                       # GLOBAL cache — computed indicators
│   └── {symbol}/{timeframe}/         # e.g., BTCUSDT/5m/RSI_14_raw.parquet
├── labels/                           # Labels per labeling config
│   └── {label_cache_key}/            # hash(labeling_config, symbol, timeframe, range)
├── fat_windows/                      # FAT windows per experiment
│   └── {fat_cache_key}/              # hash(features, window_size, labeling_config_hash)
├── small_windows/                    # SMALL windows per trial config
│   └── {small_cache_key}/            # hash(feature_subset, window_size, batch_size)
├── checkpoints/                      # Model checkpoints + Optuna storage
│   └── {timestamp}-experiment-{id}/  # One folder per experiment
│       ├── optuna_study.sqlite       # One Optuna DB per experiment
│       └── {suffix}/phase-{N}/       # Suffix + phase namespaces
│           └── trial_{id}/            # Checkpoint files per trial
│               ├── trial.json         # Trial metadata snapshot (ordered fields)
│               └── epoch_*.pt, best.pt
├── plans/                            # Plan JSON files (SOURCE OF TRUTH)
│   ├── experiments/{experiment_id}.json
│   ├── execution/{execution_plan_id}.json
│   └── master_execution_plan.json
├── live/                             # Live trading logs and data
│   └── {execution_plan_id}/{live_session_id}/
├── archived/                         # Old experiments (managed by housekeeping)
└── logs/                             # System logs

database/                             # Folder configurable via FOLDERS__DATABASE
└── rainbow_trader.sqlite             # The actual database file
                                      # Filename configurable via DATABASE__SQLITE_PATH

docs.title: 1. Base Data (OHLCV)
docs.text:
Unique, update-only. One file per symbol/timeframe.

  • Path: OHLCV/raw/{symbol}/{timeframe}/data.parquet
  • Behavior: When new data arrives: append or merge. Never duplicate.

docs.title: 2. Derived Features (Indicators/Norm)
docs.text:
Versioned by config hash.

  • Path: indicators/{symbol}/{timeframe}/{indicator_name}_{hash}.parquet
  • Reusability: Reusable across experiments if config matches.

docs.title: 3. Labels
docs.text:
Versioned by labeling config hash.

  • Path: labels/{label_cache_key}/
  • Cache key: label_cache_key = hash(labeling_config, base_symbol, base_timeframe, time_range)
  • Reusability: Reusable across experiments with same labeling config.

docs.title: 4. Windows (FAT/SMALL)
docs.text:
LRU Cache, purgeable by housekeeping.

  • FAT: fat_windows/{fat_cache_key}/
  • Cache key: fat_cache_key = hash(features, window_size, label_cache_key)
  • SMALL: small_windows/{small_cache_key}/
  • Cache key: small_cache_key = hash(feature_subset, window_size, batch_size)
  • Both are reusable across experiments with matching config.
  • Cleanup: Housekeeping task can delete old windows not accessed in X days.

docs.title: Global Parquet Cache
docs.text:
OHLCV and indicators are GLOBAL (not per-experiment):

  • Can be reused, extended, completed by ANY process
  • Each feature has its own parquet file(s)
  • Same indicator with different params = different parquet files

docs.title: Parquet Naming Convention
docs.text:
data/OHLCV/{symbol}/{timeframe}/{pipeline}.parquet
  e.g., OHLCV/BTCUSDT/5m/raw.parquet
  e.g., OHLCV/BTCUSDT/5m/clean.parquet
  e.g., OHLCV/BTCUSDT/5m/relative.parquet
  e.g., OHLCV/BTCUSDT/5m/normalized.parquet

data/indicators/{symbol}/{timeframe}/{indicator}_{params}_{pipeline}.parquet
  e.g., indicators/BTCUSDT/5m/RSI_14_raw.parquet
  e.g., indicators/BTCUSDT/5m/RSI_14_normalized.parquet
  e.g., indicators/BTCUSDT/1h/MACD_12_26_9_raw.parquet

docs.title: Feature to Parquet Lookup
docs.text:
Each feature in features_config contains enough info to find its parquet:

  • symbol, timeframe, type, source, params, pipeline

Process:

  1. Check if parquet exists and covers required time range
  2. If missing or incomplete: fetch data, compute, save to global cache

docs.title: Database Registration
docs.text:
All parquets are tracked in the parquet_files table with time ranges covered.

This allows efficient lookup: "do I have BTCUSDT/5m/RSI_14 for 2024-01-01 to 2024-06-01?"

See DATABASE-SCHEMA.md for table schema.

docs.text:
Single Source of Truth for all database tables, columns, and conventions.

This document defines the complete database schema for Rainbow Trader.

docs.title: 1. Schema Conventions
docs.text:
Applies to all tables:

  • Primary key column named id (int, autoincrement).
  • Foreign keys use snake_case with _id suffix (int) referencing the parent table's id.
  • Timestamps: created_at, updated_at; optional completed_at, scored_at, processed_at, etc., as needed.
  • Timestamp format: All timestamps use ISO 8601 format (YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SSZ), e.g., "2025-01-15T10:30:00Z".
  • JSON payload columns use _json suffix when storing structured blobs.
  • Paths use path (not file_path) and hashes use _hash.

docs.title: 2.1 experiments
docs.text:
Column Type Description id int (PK) Autoincrement name str Human-friendly name experiment_type str "trading" or "regime" status str pending, data_fetching, labeling, fat_building, training, scoring, completed, failed, stopping, stopped plan_hash str (nullable) Hash of ExperimentPlan experiment_config_json JSON (nullable) Full ExperimentPlan (planned) experiment_config_path str (nullable) Path to JSON plan (planned) n_trials_total int (nullable) Planned number of trials (planned) n_trials_completed int (nullable) Completed trials count (planned) n_trials_scored int (nullable) Scored trials count (planned) best_trial_id int (nullable, FK → trials.id) Best trial (planned) best_score float (nullable) Highest final_score (planned) created_at datetime When created updated_at datetime When updated

Relationships: experiments.id is referenced by trials.experiment_id, events.experiment_id, parquet_files.experiment_id, labels_files.experiment_id, execution_plans.experiment_id, backtest_sessions.experiment_id.

docs.title: 2.2 trials
docs.text:
Column Type Description id int (PK) Autoincrement trial_uuid str (unique) UUID for the trial experiment_id int (FK → experiments.id) Owning experiment fat_parquet_id int (nullable, FK → parquet_files.id) FAT parquet for this trial small_parquet_id int (nullable, FK → parquet_files.id) SMALL parquet for this trial optuna_study_name str Optuna study name optuna_trial_number int Optuna trial number suffix str Suffix label (default: "base") phase int Optimization phase number window_size int Window size selected_columns JSON Selected columns and weights model_hyperparameters JSON Hyperparameters chosen by Optuna objective_value float (nullable) Objective metric value objective_value_raw float (nullable) Raw objective value before phase weighting last_epoch_objective_value float (nullable) Objective value from the latest completed validation epoch best_epoch_objective_value float (nullable) Best objective value across validation epochs seen during training stage1_result float (nullable) Stage 1 objective value (training_weights only) stage2_result float (nullable) Stage 2 objective value (training_weights only) stage3_return_pct float (nullable) Stage 3 return percentage stage3_total_pnl float (nullable) Stage 3 total PnL stage3_trades_count int (nullable) Stage 3 trades count training_metrics JSON (nullable) Training/validation metrics status str pending, running, completed, failed, pruned error_message str (nullable) Error details for failed trials started_at datetime (nullable) When the trial started completed_at datetime (nullable) When the trial completed created_at datetime When created updated_at datetime When updated

Relationships: trials.id is referenced by events.trial_id, parquet_files.trial_id, backtest_trials.trial_id, backtest_sessions.trial_id, trades.trial_id.

Constraints: Unique (experiment_id, suffix, phase, optuna_trial_number).

docs.title: 2.3 events
docs.text:
Column Type Description id int (PK) Autoincrement integer event_uuid str (unique, nullable) UUID for cross-system dedupe (optional now, recommended) event_type str e.g., "TRIAL_SCORED", "DATA_FETCH_DONE" experiment_id int (nullable, FK) For routing trial_id int (nullable, FK) For trial-specific events thread_id str (nullable) LangGraph thread to resume (default rainbow_main) payload JSON Event-specific data processed bool Whether Event Bridge has handled it processed_at datetime (nullable) When marked processed created_at datetime When event occurred (or inserted) updated_at datetime Touch-on-update

Relationships: experiment_id → experiments.id; trial_id → trials.id.

For event types and payloads, see EVENTS.md.

docs.title: 3.1 parquet_files
docs.text: Column Type Description id int (PK) Autoincrement path str Absolute path to file category str "OHLCV", "indicator", "FAT", "SMALL", "labels" config_hash str (nullable) Hash of config used to build config_json JSON (nullable) Full config snapshot symbol str (nullable) For OHLCV/indicator files timeframe str (nullable) For OHLCV/indicator files experiment_id int (nullable, FK → experiments.id) Link to experiment trial_id int (nullable, FK → trials.id) Link to trial time_range_start datetime (nullable) Period start time_range_end datetime (nullable) Period end created_at datetime When created updated_at datetime When updated version int (nullable) File version

docs.title: 3.2 labels_files
docs.text: Column Type Description id int (PK) Autoincrement experiment_id int (FK → experiments.id) Link to experiment path str Path to parquet file label_type str "trading" or "regime" labeling_config_hash str (nullable) Hash of labeling_config (planned) count_long int (nullable) Planned count_short int (nullable) Planned count_none int (nullable) Planned count_total int (nullable) Planned time_range_start datetime (nullable) Planned time_range_end datetime (nullable) Planned created_at datetime When created updated_at datetime When updated

docs.title: 4.1 backtest_trials
docs.text: Column Type Description id int (PK) Autoincrement trial_id int (FK → trials.id) Link to trial metrics JSON (nullable) Aggregated metrics (current) sharpe_ratio float (nullable) Planned convenience column profit_factor float (nullable) Planned convenience column max_drawdown float (nullable) Planned convenience column win_rate float (nullable) Planned convenience column total_trades int (nullable) Planned convenience column total_pnl float (nullable) Planned convenience column avg_trade_duration float (nullable) Planned convenience column (seconds) created_at datetime When created updated_at datetime When updated

docs.title: 4.2 backtest_trades
docs.text: Column Type Description id int (PK) Autoincrement backtest_id int (FK → backtest_trials.id) Link to backtest side str "buy" or "sell" pnl float Profit/loss created_at datetime When created updated_at datetime When updated

docs.title: 4.3 backtest_sessions
docs.text:
Column Type Description id int (PK) Autoincrement backtest_session_uuid str (unique) UUID for the backtest session trial_id int (FK → trials.id) Link to trial experiment_id int (FK → experiments.id) Link to experiment backtest_type str mini, complete_post_optuna, manual status str running, completed, failed range_start datetime Backtest range start range_end datetime Backtest range end initial_equity float (nullable) Starting equity final_equity float (nullable) Final equity total_pnl float (nullable) Total PnL sharpe_ratio float (nullable) Sharpe ratio trades_count int (nullable) Number of trades win_rate float (nullable) Win rate started_at datetime (nullable) When the backtest started completed_at datetime (nullable) When the backtest finished created_at datetime When created updated_at datetime When updated

Relationships: backtest_sessions.id is referenced by trades.backtest_session_id.

docs.title: 5.1 analyst_summaries
docs.text: Column Type Description id int (PK) Autoincrement experiment_id int (FK → experiments.id) Link to experiment trial_id int (nullable, FK → trials.id) Link to trial (for trial summaries) summary_type str "trial" or "experiment" summary_json JSON Structured summary regime_tags JSON (nullable) Regime tags for experiment recommendations_json JSON (nullable) Recommendations created_at datetime When created updated_at datetime When updated

docs.title: 6.1 execution_plans
docs.text:
Static definition of an execution plan. Runtime state is tracked in live_sessions.

Column Type Description id int (PK) Autoincrement execution_plan_id str (unique) Human-readable ID (e.g., "exec_001") experiment_id int (FK → experiments.id) Link to experiment trial_id int (nullable, FK → trials.id) Link to trial target_symbol str Trading symbol target_timeframe str Trading timeframe execution_plan_json JSON Full ExecutionPlan config execution_plan_path str (nullable) Path to JSON file created_at datetime When created updated_at datetime When updated

Note: mode (live/sim) is NOT stored here. It is derived from MasterExecutionPlan.slots which specifies whether this plan is in live_execution_plan_id or sim_execution_plan_ids.

Note: status is NOT stored here. Runtime state is tracked in live_sessions.state.

docs.title: 6.2 master_execution_plans
docs.text:
Static definition of a master execution plan. Can have multiple plans, but only one ACTIVE at a time.

Column Type Description id int (PK) Autoincrement master_execution_plan_id str (unique) Human-readable ID (e.g., "master_001") version_id int Increments on each update slots_json JSON Slots config: {symbol_TF: {live_execution_plan_id, sim_execution_plan_ids[]}} global_rules_json JSON (nullable) Global rules created_at datetime When created updated_at datetime When updated

Note: Runtime state (ACTIVE, etc.) is NOT stored here. The active master is determined by which one has running live_sessions.

Slots JSON structure:

{
  "BTCUSDT_15m": {
    "live_execution_plan_id": "exec_001",
    "sim_execution_plan_ids": ["exec_002", "exec_003"]
  },
  "ETHUSDT_1h": {
    "live_execution_plan_id": "exec_004",
    "sim_execution_plan_ids": []
  }
}

docs.title: 7.1 live_trades
docs.text:
Column Type Description id int (PK) Autoincrement execution_plan_id int (nullable, FK → execution_plans.id) Link to execution plan live_session_id int (FK → live_sessions.id) Link to live session symbol str Trading symbol side str "buy" or "sell" entry_timestamp datetime Entry time exit_timestamp datetime (nullable) Exit time entry_price float Entry price exit_price float (nullable) Exit price size float Position size realized_pnl float (nullable) Realized profit/loss unrealized_pnl float (nullable) Unrealized profit/loss status str "open", "closed" exchange_order_id str Exchange order ID created_at datetime When created updated_at datetime When updated

Note: Current ORM uses the unified trades table for both backtest and live runs.

docs.title: 7.2 live_sessions
docs.text:
Dynamic runtime state for each running execution. One LiveSession per running bot (live or sim).

Column Type Description id int (PK) Autoincrement live_session_id str (unique) UUID auto-generated on start master_execution_plan_id str (FK → master_execution_plans.master_execution_plan_id) Which master plan execution_plan_id str (FK → execution_plans.execution_plan_id) Which execution plan slot str Slot identifier (e.g., "BTCUSDT_15m") mode str "live" or "sim" (derived from master at creation) state str "RUNNING", "PAUSED", "STOPPED" started_at datetime Live session start stopped_at datetime (nullable) Live session end total_trades int (nullable) Number of trades (updated on stop) total_pnl float (nullable) Total profit/loss (updated on stop) metrics_json JSON (nullable) Additional metrics created_at datetime When created updated_at datetime When updated

Constraints: - Only ONE live_session with mode='live' and state='RUNNING' per slot - Multiple live_session with mode='sim' and state='RUNNING' allowed per slot

Identification rules: - Live mode: unique per slot → can identify with slot + mode='live' - Sim mode: multiple per slot → MUST identify with live_session_id or execution_plan_id

docs.title: 7.3 trades
docs.text:
Unified trade records for both backtest and live trading.

Column Type Description id int (PK) Autoincrement trial_id int (FK → trials.id) Trial used for this trade live_session_id int (nullable, FK → live_sessions.id) Live session linkage backtest_session_id int (nullable, FK → backtest_sessions.id) Backtest session linkage side str "LONG" or "SHORT" size float Position size entry_price float Entry price exit_price float (nullable) Exit price stop_loss float Stop loss price take_profit float Take profit price pnl float Profit/loss reason str (nullable) Close reason opened_at datetime Open timestamp closed_at datetime (nullable) Close timestamp is_open bool True if open, False if closed is_backtest bool True for backtest trades, False for live trailing_stop_distance float (nullable) Trailing stop distance trailing_stop_activation float (nullable) Trailing stop activation highest_price float (nullable) Highest price seen (LONG) lowest_price float (nullable) Lowest price seen (SHORT) created_at datetime When created updated_at datetime When updated

Constraints:

  • Exactly one of live_session_id or backtest_session_id must be set
  • trial_id is required for all trades

docs.title: 8.1 regimes
docs.text: Column Type Description id int (PK) Autoincrement experiment_id int (nullable, FK → experiments.id) Source experiment (if ML-based) symbol str Symbol timeframe str Timeframe start_timestamp datetime Period start end_timestamp datetime Period end regime_label str e.g., "bull", "bear", "range", "high_vol" confidence float (nullable) Confidence score created_at datetime When created updated_at datetime When updated

docs.title: 9.1 Checkpointer Tables
docs.text:
Managed by LangGraph Checkpointer:

  • checkpoints: Graph state checkpoints
  • checkpoint_blobs: Binary data for checkpoints
  • checkpoint_writes: Write operations log

docs.title: 9.2 Store Tables
docs.text:
Managed by LangGraph Store:

  • store_items: Key-value items for agent long-term memory
  • store_vectors: Vector embeddings for semantic search (if using vector store)

docs.title: Option 1: Using docker-compose (Recommended, single stack)
docs.text:
# Build the image
docker-compose build
docker-compose build --no-cache

# Start the container (auto-installs all modules on first run)
docker-compose up -d

# Access the container
docker-compose exec rainbow-trader bash

# Stop the container
docker-compose down
docker-compose down && docker network rm rainbow-trader_default 2>/dev/null

docs.title: Option 2: Using docker directly
docs.text:
# Build the image
docker build -t rainbow-trader .

docs.title: Auto-Install Modules
docs.text:
The container automatically installs all dependencies and modules when it starts for the first time:

  1. Root requirements from requirements.txt (LangGraph, Optuna, Polars, etc.)
  2. All modules in editable mode (pip install -e)

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
• On first container start, the entrypoint runs /install-modules.sh
  • A marker file (.modules_installed) is created in the venv to track installation status
  • Subsequent container starts skip installation (faster startup)

docs.title: Force Reinstall
docs.text:
If you need to reinstall all modules (e.g., after adding a new module or updating dependencies):

# Option 1: Set environment variable
FORCE_REINSTALL=1 docker-compose up -d

# Option 2: Remove the marker file
docker-compose exec rainbow-trader rm /workspace/.venv/.modules_installed
docker-compose restart rainbow-trader

# Option 3: Remove the entire venv volume (clean install)
docker-compose down
docker volume rm rainbow-trader_rainbow-venv
docker-compose up -d

docs.title: Available CLIs After Installation
docs.text:
Once installed, all module CLIs are available:

# Inside the container
python -m common --help
python -m database --help
python -m data_fetcher --help
python -m labeling_service --help
python -m window_preparer --help
python -m model_trainer --help
python -m backtesting_engine --help
python -m live_trading_runner --help
python -m bot_logic --help
python -m housekeeping --help
python -m control_panel --help
python -m graph_orchestrator --help

docs.title: Module Installation Order
docs.text:
Modules are installed in dependency order:

  1. common (foundation - env, logging, config)
  2. database (SQLAlchemy ORM, migrations)
  3. data-processing (numeric operations)
  4. data-fetcher (OHLCV download)
  5. labeling-service (label generation)
  6. window-preparer (FAT/SMALL windows)
  7. model-trainer (Optuna training)
  8. backtesting-engine (offline simulation)
  9. live-trading-runner (real-time execution)
  10. bot-logic (order decisions)
  11. housekeeping (maintenance)
  12. control-panel (web dashboard)
  13. graph-orchestrator (LangGraph agents)

## Features

- **Security**: Isolated environment with Python 3.11-slim
- **All repos**: All subrepos are copied inside the container
- **Single compose file**: No dev/prod overrides yet (TODO for later phases)
- **VSCode extensions**: Extensions persist across container restarts using named volumes
- **GPG signing**: Your GPG keys are mounted for commit signing (read-only)
- **Persistent data**: `./data` and `./database` on the host are bind-mounted into the container

## Container Structure

```text
/workspace/
├── README.md
└── .gitmodules

docs.title: GitHub Access (Secure Setup)
docs.text: The container uses a limited GitHub token for secure access. This token only has access to this specific repository.

docs.title: Setup (One-time)
docs.text:
1. Create a fine-grained token at https://github.com/settings/tokens?type=beta
  2. Click "Generate new token"
  3. Token name: rainbow-trader-container
  4. Expiration: 90 days (recommended)
  5. Repository access: Only select repositories → rainbow-trader
  6. Permissions:
    • Contents: Read and write
    • Metadata: Read-only

  7. Click "Generate token" and copy it

  8. Create .env file in the project root:

bash cp .env.example .env # Edit .env and paste your token

  1. Restart the container:

bash docker-compose down docker-compose build docker-compose up -d

That's it! Git is automatically configured inside the container. No manual commands needed.

docs.title: How it works
docs.text:
• The token is passed as an environment variable
  • The container automatically configures Git credentials on startup
  • The token only works for the rainbow-trader repository
  • You can revoke the token anytime from GitHub settings

docs.title: Verify inside container
docs.text:
# Test Git access
git status
git pull

# Verify GPG signing (if enabled in your .gitconfig)
gpg --list-keys
git log --show-signature -1

# Disable GPG signing (GPG keys are read-only for security)
git config --local commit.gpgsign false

docs.title: Working with Git
docs.text:
# Make changes to code
git add .
git commit -m "Your commit message"
git push origin main

# Pull latest changes
git pull origin main

docs.title: VSCode Extensions
docs.text:
VSCode extensions and Codex configuration are persisted using Docker named volumes. This means:

  • Extensions installed in the container will persist across restarts
  • Codex extension configuration (in ~/.codex) persists across restarts
  • You only need to install and configure extensions once
  • To reset extensions, remove the volumes: docker volume rm rainbow-trader_vscode-server-extensions rainbow-trader_vscode-server-extensions-insiders rainbow-trader_codex-config

docs.title: Installing Extensions
docs.text:
When you first connect VSCode to the container, install the extensions you need:

  • GitHub Copilot: GitHub.copilot (if you have access)
  • OpenAI ChatGPT: openai.chatgpt (pre-release version)
  • Any other extensions you use regularly

The Codex extension configuration will be automatically saved in the persistent volume.

docs.title: Security Considerations
docs.text:
The container has security restrictions to provide isolation:

  • No privilege escalation: no-new-privileges prevents gaining additional privileges
  • Minimal capabilities: Only essential Linux capabilities are enabled (CHOWN, SETUID, SETGID)
  • Read-only mounts: Git config and credentials are mounted read-only

docs.title: What the container CAN do
docs.text:
• Read/write to the data folder (shared with host)
  • Push/pull to GitHub using your credentials
  • Install packages and modify files inside the container
  • Access all code in /workspace

docs.title: What the container CANNOT do
docs.text:
• Access files outside mounted volumes
  • Escalate privileges beyond the container
  • Directly modify your Ubuntu system files
  • Access your network without Docker network rules

docs.title: Recommendations for AI agents
docs.text:
• The limited GitHub token restricts access to only this repository
  • Monitor the data folder for unexpected changes
  • Review Git commits: git log -n 5
  • Revoke the token anytime from GitHub settings if needed
  • Token expiration (90 days) limits potential misuse window

docs.title: The Problem
docs.text:
The container runs as root (UID 0) for security isolation. When files are created inside the container, they're owned by root. This causes permission issues when you try to modify those files from your local Ubuntu system.

Example scenario:

  1. AI agent creates files in container → owned by root
  2. You run git pull locally → Permission denied (your user can't modify root files)

docs.title: The Solution
docs.text:
When you encounter permission errors during git pull or file operations:

# Navigate to the affected subrepo
cd <sub repo X>

# Fix permissions (requires sudo password)
sudo chown -R $USER:$USER .

# Clean untracked files and reset
git clean -fdx && git reset --hard HEAD

# Now pull works
git pull

docs.title: Why Not Run Container as Your User?
docs.text:
Security by design: The container is a sandbox for AI agents. Running as root inside the container:

  • ✅ Isolates the AI agent from your system
  • ✅ Prevents the agent from accessing your user files
  • ✅ Limits damage if the agent misbehaves
  • ✅ Docker's security features contain root within the container

The permission issue is a feature, not a bug - it's the trade-off for keeping your system safe.

docs.title: Workflow Recommendation
docs.text:
1. Work in container: Let AI agent create/modify files
  2. Commit & push from container: git add . && git commit && git push
  3. Pull locally when needed: Use the permission fix commands above
  4. Review changes: Always check git log and git diff before pulling

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• To update code in the container, rebuild the image with docker-compose build
  • Git credentials and config are mounted read-only for security
  • Changes to code inside the container can be committed and pushed to GitHub using HTTPS
  • VSCode extensions are stored in named volumes and persist across container restarts
  • Data and database artifacts persist on the host under ./data and ./database (bind mounts)
