docs.title: 🤖 Agent Summary
docs.text: Keywords: events, Event Bridge, event types, payload, DATA_FETCH_DONE, LABELS_DONE, FAT_WINDOWS_DONE, SMALL_WINDOWS_PROGRESS, SMALL_WINDOWS_DONE, TRAINING_PROGRESS, TRIAL_CONFIG_READY, TRIAL_TRAINING_DONE, TRIAL_PRUNED, TRIAL_SCORED, ANALYST_TRIAL_SUMMARY_READY, ALL_TRIALS_DONE, ANALYST_EXPERIMENT_SUMMARY_READY, BACKTEST_TRIAL_DONE, EXECUTION_PLAN_CREATED, MASTER_EXEC_PLAN_UPDATED, LIVE_EXEC_SUMMARY, LIVE_TRADING_STARTED, LIVE_TRADING_STOPPED, LIVE_ANOMALY_DETECTED, EXPERIMENT_STOPPING, TRIAL_STOPPING, TRIAL_CANCELLED, EXPERIMENT_STOPPED, MODULE_RESTARTED, MIGRATION_DONE, CODE_DEPLOYED, HOUSEKEEPING_DONE, EVENT_BRIDGE_ERROR, emit_event, processed, processed_at, thread_id, experiment_id, trial_id, event_uuid, polling, graph.invoke, Command(resume=...), ISO 8601, *_DONE convention

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: All events are stored in events table with JSON payload. Event types MUST use the names below (prefer *_DONE for completion signals). Payloads should include the minimal keys listed so downstream consumers and the control panel stay stable. Dummy runs are acceptable as long as the payload shape is correct.

docs.title: 2. Canonical Event Types
docs.text:
Use exactly these names (case-sensitive):

Data Preparation:

  • DATA_FETCH_DONE, LABELS_DONE, FAT_WINDOWS_DONE, SMALL_WINDOWS_DONE

Per-Trial:

  • TRIAL_CONFIG_READY, SMALL_WINDOWS_PROGRESS, SMALL_WINDOWS_DONE, TRAINING_PROGRESS, TRIAL_TRAINING_DONE, TRIAL_PRUNED, TRIAL_SCORED, ANALYST_TRIAL_SUMMARY_READY

Backtest:

  • BACKTEST_TRIAL_DONE

Experiment Closure:

  • ALL_TRIALS_DONE, ANALYST_EXPERIMENT_SUMMARY_READY

Live Execution:

  • EXECUTION_PLAN_CREATED, MASTER_EXEC_PLAN_UPDATED, LIVE_EXEC_SUMMARY, LIVE_TRADING_STARTED, LIVE_TRADING_STOPPED, LIVE_ANOMALY_DETECTED

Stop/Cancel:

  • EXPERIMENT_STOPPING, TRIAL_STOPPING, TRIAL_CANCELLED, EXPERIMENT_STOPPED

System:

  • MODULE_RESTARTED, MIGRATION_DONE, CODE_DEPLOYED

Housekeeping:

  • HOUSEKEEPING_DONE, EVENT_BRIDGE_ERROR

Do NOT use deprecated/alias names:

Deprecated Use Instead DATA_FETCH_COMPLETE DATA_FETCH_DONE LABELING_COMPLETE LABELS_DONE BACKTEST_COMPLETE BACKTEST_TRIAL_DONE TRAINING_COMPLETE TRIAL_TRAINING_DONE (or TRIAL_SCORED as the final signal)

docs.title: 3. Event Structure
docs.text:
class Event:
    id: int                 # PK (autoincrement)
    event_uuid: str | None  # Optional UUID for dedupe
    event_type: str         # e.g., "TRIAL_SCORED"
    experiment_id: int | None  # For routing
    trial_id: int | None       # For trial events
    thread_id: str | None      # LangGraph thread to resume
    created_at: str            # ISO 8601
    payload: dict              # event-specific data
    processed: bool            # whether Event Bridge has processed it
    processed_at: str | None   # when bridge handled it

docs.title: 4. Payload Expectations
docs.text:
Minimal payload expectations (applies to dummy and real):

  • Include identifiers required by the event type (experiment_id, trial_id, etc.).
  • Include the numeric metrics listed per type (e.g., n_windows, sharpe_ratio, final_score).
  • For timing fields, use ISO 8601 strings (see DATABASE-SCHEMA.md for timestamp conventions).
  • Add note/source in payload for debugging when emitting from stubs.

docs.title: 5. Event Types (Detailed)
docs.text: Use these exact event_type strings (case-sensitive). Prefer *_DONE for completion markers.

docs.title: 5.1 Data Preparation (Sequential)
docs.text: Event Payload Description EXPERIMENT_PLAN_CREATED {experiment_id, experiment_type, n_trials_planned} New experiment plan created DATA_FETCH_DONE {experiment_id, symbols_fetched, time_range} OHLCV data fetched LABELS_DONE {experiment_id, count_long, count_short, count_none, count_total} Labels generated FAT_WINDOWS_DONE {experiment_id, n_windows, feature_count, fat_hash} FAT windows built

docs.title: 5.2 Per-Trial (Parallel Across Trials)
docs.text: Event Payload Description TRIAL_CONFIG_READY {experiment_id, trial_id, model_type, feature_count, window_size, batch_size} Trial config generated SMALL_WINDOWS_PROGRESS {experiment_id, trial_id, progress_percent, columns_processed, total_columns, cache_key} SMALL windows progress SMALL_WINDOWS_DONE {experiment_id, trial_id, cache_key, reused: bool} SMALL windows ready TRAINING_PROGRESS {experiment_id, trial_id, epoch, total_epochs, loss, val_loss, val_accuracy, progress_percent} Training progress TRIAL_TRAINING_DONE {experiment_id, trial_id, objective_value, duration_seconds} Training complete TRIAL_PRUNED {experiment_id, trial_id, reason, step_pruned_at} Trial pruned early (NOT scored) BACKTEST_TRIAL_DONE {experiment_id, trial_id, sharpe_ratio, total_trades, profit_factor} Backtest complete TRIAL_SCORED {experiment_id, trial_id, final_score, rank} Final score computed ANALYST_TRIAL_SUMMARY_READY {experiment_id, trial_id, summary_path, highlights: list} Analyst summary for individual trial

docs.title: 5.3 Experiment Closure
docs.text:
Event Payload Description ALL_TRIALS_DONE {experiment_id, n_scored, n_pruned, n_cancelled, best_trial_id, best_score} All trials finished ANALYST_EXPERIMENT_SUMMARY_READY {experiment_id, summary_path, recommended_trials: list, regime_tags: list} Analyst summary for complete experiment

Note on pruned trials:

  • n_trials_completed counts only status == "completed" trials
  • Pruned trials are NOT counted in n_trials_completed and do NOT receive a final_score
  • Use derived output-only totals when needed: finished = completed + pruned + failed
  • Pruned trials are excluded from ranking and best trial selection

docs.title: 5.4 Stop/Cancel (Triggered by Director Tools)
docs.text: Event Payload Description EXPERIMENT_STOPPING {experiment_id, reason, requested_by} Stop requested TRIAL_STOPPING {experiment_id, trial_id, reason, requested_by} Trial stop requested TRIAL_CANCELLED {experiment_id, trial_id, final_status: "cancelled"} Trial cancelled EXPERIMENT_STOPPED {experiment_id, n_completed, n_cancelled, reason} Experiment stopped

docs.title: 5.5 Live Execution
docs.text: Event Payload Description EXECUTION_PLAN_CREATED {execution_plan_id, experiment_id, trial_id, mode, target_symbol} New execution plan MASTER_EXEC_PLAN_UPDATED {version_id, state, changed_slots: list} MasterExecutionPlan updated LIVE_EXEC_SUMMARY {execution_plan_id, period_id, period_start, period_end, n_trades, pnl, sharpe} Periodic live summary LIVE_TRADING_STARTED {execution_plan_id, mode} Live trading started LIVE_TRADING_STOPPED {execution_plan_id, reason} Live trading stopped LIVE_ANOMALY_DETECTED {execution_plan_id, anomaly_type, details} Anomaly detected in live trading

docs.title: 5.6 System Events
docs.text: Event Payload Description MODULE_RESTARTED {module_name, reason} Module service restarted MIGRATION_DONE {note} All DB migrations completed (emitted by database module) CODE_DEPLOYED {module_name, commit_hash} Code changes deployed

docs.title: 6.1 How It Works
docs.text:
A background process in the orchestrator that:

  1. Polls events table for unprocessed events.
  2. Finds the corresponding Graph thread.
  3. Resumes the Graph: graph.invoke(Command(resume=event_data), config={"thread_id": ...})
  4. Marks the event as processed.

docs.title: 6.2 Implementation
docs.text:
async def event_bridge_loop(graph, poll_interval=1.0):
    while True:
        with get_db_session() as db_session:
            events = db_session.query(Event).filter_by(processed=False).order_by(Event.created_at).all()
            for event in events:
                try:
                    await graph.ainvoke(
                        Command(resume={"event_type": event.event_type, "payload": event.payload}),
                        config={"configurable": {"thread_id": event.thread_id}}
                    )
                    event.processed = True
                    db_session.commit()
                except Exception as e:
                    log.error(f"Failed to process event {event.id}: {e}")
                    # Mark as processed to avoid infinite retry, create error event
                    event.processed = True
                    create_error_event(event, e)
                    db_session.commit()
        await asyncio.sleep(poll_interval)

docs.title: 6.3 Implementation Notes
docs.text:
Polling:

  • Query: SELECT * FROM events WHERE processed = false ORDER BY created_at ASC
  • Optional: filter by whitelist of event types

Thread Routing:

  • Use event.thread_id if present, otherwise default to "rainbow_main"
  • Multi-threading support planned for future

After Processing:

  • On success: set processed = true and processed_at = now()
  • On error: set processed = true (to avoid infinite retry) and emit EVENT_BRIDGE_ERROR event

Testing Mode:

  • Modules can insert DONE events directly (with correct payload shape) to unlock graph flow without doing real work

Database Schema:

  • See DATABASE-SCHEMA.md Section 2.3 for the complete events table definition

docs.title: ❌ NEVER use git reset --hard
docs.text:
# ❌ WRONG - Destroys untracked files permanently
git reset --hard HEAD~1

# ✅ CORRECT - Use soft reset instead
git reset --soft HEAD~1

Why: git reset --hard DESTROYS:

  • All untracked files
  • All uncommitted changes
  • Hours of work with NO way to recover

When to use: ONLY if user explicitly requests it with full understanding of data loss.

docs.title: 🚫 Problem: MCP Git Tool Commits Submodule Internal Files
What Happened
docs.text:
When using MCP's mcp2_git_add and mcp2_git_commit tools, they can accidentally commit: - .git files inside submodules (should be directories, not files) - .gitignore, .mypy_cache, .pytest_cache from submodules - Other internal submodule files

This breaks the submodule structure - git sees them as regular files instead of submodule references.

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
git add rainbow-*
# Error: You have both modules/common and modules/common/.git
# Error: You have both modules/common and modules/common/.gitignore

docs.title: ✅ SAFE MCP Tools (Use These)
docs.text:
# These work correctly with submodules
mcp2_git_status(repo_path="/path/to/submodule")
mcp2_git_add(repo_path="/path/to/submodule", files=["."])
mcp2_git_commit(repo_path="/path/to/submodule", message="...")
mcp2_git_log(repo_path="/path/to/submodule")

docs.title: ❌ AVOID for Main Repo with Submodules
docs.text:
# These can commit internal submodule files by mistake
mcp2_git_add(repo_path="/main/repo", files=["rainbow-*"])  # ❌ Risky!

# Instead, use shell commands for main repo:
run_command("git add modules/common modules/database")      # ✅ Safe

Rule: Use MCP git tools for individual submodules, use shell commands for main repo.

docs.title: Step 1: Analyze Changes First
docs.text:
# See what changed
git status

# Review diffs to understand changes
git diff path/to/file

# Group related changes mentally
# Example groups:
# - Configuration files (.env.example, CONFIGURATION.md)
# - Documentation (README.md, MASTER-PLAN.md)
# - Core code (config_loader.py, runtime.py)
# - Tests (test_*.py)

docs.title: Step 2: Commit Submodules in Logical Groups
docs.text:
Group by functionality, not "all at once"!

# Group 1: Core config system (modules/common)
cd modules/common
git add src/common/config_loader.py src/common/log_format.py
git commit -m "feat(config): add automatic env var mapping"

git add src/common/runtime.py src/common/__init__.py
git commit -m "feat(config): update runtime with LogFormat enum"

git add tests/test_config.py tests/test_paths.py
git commit -m "test: update tests for new variable names"
cd ..

# Group 2: Database module
cd modules/database
git add src/database/session.py
git commit -m "docs: update comments to use DATABASE__SQLITE_PATH"

git add src/database/cli.py src/database/defaults.py
git commit -m "feat(config): add --set flag and empty defaults"
cd ..

# Group 3: Similar modules (batch if changes are identical)
for module in backtesting-engine bot-logic window-preparer; do
  cd rainbow-$module
  git add src/*/cli.py src/*/defaults.py .env.example
  git commit -m "feat(config): standardize configuration files"
  cd ..
done

docs.title: Step 3: Commit Main Repo in Logical Groups
docs.text:
# Group 1: New documentation
git add CONFIGURATION.md CONFIG_CLEANUP_PLAN_V3.md
git commit -m "docs: add comprehensive configuration guide"

# Group 2: Update existing docs
git add README.md README_MODULE_TEMPLATE.md MASTER-PLAN.md CODE-GENERATION.md
git commit -m "docs: update documentation with configuration links"

# Group 3: Root config files
git add .env.example .gitignore
git commit -m "chore: update root configuration files"

# Group 4: Submodule references (LAST!)
git add modules/common modules/database modules/backtesting-engine
git add modules/bot-logic modules/window-preparer
git commit -m "chore: update submodule references to config changes"

# Group 5: More submodule references
git add modules/labeling-service modules/live-trading-runner
git add modules/model-trainer modules/data-fetcher
git commit -m "chore: update remaining submodule references"

Key: When you git add modules/common, git should only add the commit hash reference, NOT the internal files.

docs.title: 🔧 How to Fix When Things Go Wrong
Scenario: Accidentally Committed Submodule Internal Files
Step 1: Soft Reset (NEVER --hard!)
docs.text:
# Undo the bad commits but keep all changes
git reset --soft HEAD~2  # Go back 2 commits (adjust number as needed)

docs.title: Step 2: Clear the Staging Area
docs.text:
# Remove ALL staged files
git reset HEAD .

docs.title: Step 3: Remove Problematic Files from Git Cache
docs.text:
# Remove internal submodule files from git's index
git rm --cached -r rainbow-*/.git
git rm --cached -r rainbow-*/.gitignore
git rm --cached -r rainbow-*/.mypy_cache
git rm --cached -r rainbow-*/.pytest_cache
git rm --cached -r rainbow-*/.coverage
git rm --cached -r rainbow-*/.env
git rm --cached -r rainbow-*/src/*.egg-info

docs.title: Step 4: Re-add Files Correctly
docs.text:
# Add root files
git add .env.example CONFIGURATION.md README.md MASTER-PLAN.md CODE-GENERATION.md

# Add submodule references (NOT their internal files)
git add modules/backtesting-engine modules/bot-logic modules/common
git add modules/control-panel modules/data-processing modules/database
git add modules/window-preparer modules/graph-orchestrator modules/housekeeping
git add modules/labeling-service modules/live-trading-runner modules/model-trainer
git add modules/data-fetcher

docs.title: Step 5: Verify Before Committing
docs.text:
# Check what's staged - should NOT see internal submodule files
git status --short

# Should see:
# M  modules/common          ✅ (just the reference)
# NOT:
# A  modules/common/.git     ❌ (internal file - BAD!)

docs.title: Step 6: Commit Correctly
docs.text: git commit -m "feat: your commit message"

docs.title: 📋 Checklist: Before Every Commit
docs.text:
• [ ] All submodules committed first
  • [ ] git status shows only submodule references (M rainbow-*)
  • [ ] NO internal files staged (no .git, .gitignore, .mypy_cache, etc.)
  • [ ] Verified with git status --short
  • [ ] Using git reset --soft if need to undo (NEVER --hard)

docs.title: Safe Git Commands
docs.text:
git reset --soft HEAD~1   # Undo commit, keep changes
git reset HEAD .          # Unstage everything
git rm --cached file      # Remove from staging, keep file
git status --short        # Quick status check

docs.title: Dangerous Git Commands (Ask First!)
docs.text:
git reset --hard          # ⚠️ DESTROYS untracked files
git clean -fd             # ⚠️ DELETES untracked files
git checkout -- .         # ⚠️ DISCARDS uncommitted changes

docs.title: 🔍 Verification Commands
docs.text:
# Check submodule status
git submodule status

# Check what's staged
git status --short

# Check submodule commits
git log --oneline -5

# Check if submodules are clean
for module in rainbow-*; do 
  echo "=== $module ===" 
  cd $module && git status --short && cd ..
done

docs.title: Commit Philosophy
docs.text:
1. Small, logical commits - Group related changes, not everything at once
  2. Understand before committing - Use git diff and git status to analyze
  3. Tell a story - Each commit should represent one logical change
  4. Commit messages matter - Clear, descriptive messages help future you

docs.title: Commit Grouping Examples
docs.text:
❌ BAD: One giant commit

git add .
git commit -m "fix: update everything"  # What changed? No idea!

✅ GOOD: Logical groups

# Commit 1: Core functionality
git add src/common/config_loader.py
git commit -m "feat(config): add automatic env var mapping with __"

# Commit 2: Supporting changes
git add src/common/runtime.py src/common/__init__.py
git commit -m "feat(config): integrate LogFormat enum in runtime"

# Commit 3: Tests
git add tests/test_config.py tests/test_paths.py
git commit -m "test: update for new variable naming convention"

# Commit 4: Documentation
git add CONFIGURATION.md README.md
git commit -m "docs: add configuration guide and update README"

docs.title: General Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Always commit submodules first, then main repo
  2. Never use --hard reset without explicit permission
  3. Verify staging area before committing (git status --short)
  4. Use git rm --cached to remove files from staging without deleting them
  5. Keep untracked files (.mcp/, CONFIG_CLEANUP_PLAN.md, etc.) untracked
  6. Test in a branch if unsure about git operations
  7. Use MCP git tools for submodules, shell commands for main repo
  8. Review diffs before committing (git diff --staged)

docs.title: 📝 Commit Message Format
docs.text:
Follow Conventional Commits:

<type>(<scope>): <description>

[optional body]

[optional footer]

docs.title: Commit Types
docs.text: Type Description Example feat New feature feat(config): add automatic env var mapping fix Bug fix fix(database): handle empty query results docs Documentation only docs(readme): update installation guide test Adding or updating tests test(config): add precedence tests refactor Code restructuring refactor(core): simplify indicator pipeline style Formatting, no logic change style: fix indentation in cli.py chore Maintenance tasks chore: update submodule references

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
# Good commit messages
feat(config): add --set flag support with dot notation
fix(model-trainer): handle empty feature subset gracefully
docs(git): add comprehensive commit guide
test(backtesting): add property tests for trade simulation
chore: update all submodule references to latest

# Bad commit messages
fix: stuff
update files
changes
WIP

docs.title: Branch Types
docs.text: Branch Purpose Protection main Production-ready code Protected, requires PR develop Integration branch Semi-protected feature/<name> New features Delete after merge fix/<name> Bug fixes Delete after merge docs/<name> Documentation Delete after merge

docs.title: When to Branch
docs.text: Change Size Strategy Small fixes (< 50 lines) Commit to current branch locally Medium features (50-200 lines) Create feature/<name> branch, commit locally Large features (> 200 lines) Create feature/<name> branch + PR required Architecture changes Create feature/<name> branch + PR + review required

docs.title: PR Requirements
docs.text:
• Major changes (new features, architecture changes) require PRs
  • All commits should pass quality gates before push
  • User reviews and pushes manually (never auto-push)

docs.title: ✅ Quality Gates Before Commit
docs.text:
Every code change MUST pass these checks without errors or warnings:

# 1. Tests with 100% coverage
pytest --cov=src --cov-fail-under=100

# 2. Type checking (strict mode)
mypy src --strict

# 3. Linting and formatting
ruff check src

All three must pass before committing.

Remember: When in doubt, ask before running destructive git commands!

docs.title: 1. The Golden Rule
docs.text:
ALWAYS add a blank line before and after:

  • Headings (#, ##, ###, etc.)
  • Lists (-, *, 1.)
  • Code blocks (```)
  • Tables
  • Blockquotes (>)

This is the cause of 90% of markdownlint errors.

docs.title: 2. Common Errors and Fixes
docs.text:
Error Rule Problem Fix MD009 no-trailing-spaces Trailing spaces at end of line Remove trailing whitespace MD012 no-multiple-blanks Multiple consecutive blank lines Keep only one blank line MD022 blanks-around-headings No blank line around heading Add blank line before AND after heading MD026 no-trailing-punctuation Heading ends with : or ? Remove trailing punctuation from heading MD031 blanks-around-fences No blank line around code block Add blank line before AND after code block MD032 blanks-around-lists No blank line around list Add blank line before AND after list MD034 no-bare-urls URL without angle brackets Wrap URL: <https://example.com> MD040 fenced-code-language Code block has no language Add language: ```python, ```bash, ```text MD041 first-line-heading File doesn't start with # Start file with # Title MD047 single-trailing-newline File doesn't end with newline Add empty line at end of file

Disabled rules (project-wide):

Rule Reason MD013 Line length — we allow long lines MD024 Duplicate headings — allowed in different sections MD033 Inline HTML — sometimes needed for formatting MD036 Emphasis as heading — we use **bold** for emphasis

docs.title: 3. Headings
docs.text:
✅ CORRECT:

# Title

Some paragraph text here.

## Section Title

More content here.

### Subsection

Details here.

❌ WRONG:

# Title
Some paragraph text here.
## Section Title
More content here.

Rules:

  • First line MUST be a top-level heading (# Title)
  • Blank line BEFORE every heading
  • Blank line AFTER every heading
  • Heading levels increment by one (don't skip from # to ###)

docs.title: 4. Lists
docs.text:
✅ CORRECT:

Introduction paragraph.

- Item 1
- Item 2
- Item 3

Conclusion paragraph.

❌ WRONG:

Introduction paragraph.
- Item 1
- Item 2
- Item 3
Conclusion paragraph.

Rules:

  • Blank line BEFORE the first list item
  • Blank line AFTER the last list item
  • Use - for unordered lists (not *)
  • Use 1. for ordered lists

Nested lists:

Parent paragraph.

- Item 1
  - Nested item 1.1
  - Nested item 1.2
- Item 2

Next paragraph.

docs.title: 5. Code Blocks
docs.text:
✅ CORRECT:

Here is an example:

```python
print("Hello")
```

And here is the result.

❌ WRONG:

Here is an example:
```python
print("Hello")
```
And here is the result.

Rules:

  • Blank line BEFORE the opening ```
  • Blank line AFTER the closing ```
  • ALWAYS specify the language after `` (usetext` if no specific language)

Common languages: python, bash, yaml, json, markdown, text, sql

docs.title: 6. Tables
docs.text:
✅ CORRECT:

Description of the table:

| Column 1 | Column 2 |
|----------|----------|
| Value 1  | Value 2  |

Next paragraph.

❌ WRONG:

Description of the table:
| Column 1 | Column 2 |
|----------|----------|
| Value 1  | Value 2  |
Next paragraph.

docs.title: 7. Blockquotes
docs.text:
✅ CORRECT:

Introduction.

> This is a quote.
> It can span multiple lines.

Conclusion.

❌ WRONG:

Introduction.
> This is a quote.
Conclusion.

docs.title: 8. Other Rules
docs.text: Rule Description MD001 Heading levels should increment by one level at a time MD003 Use consistent heading style (ATX: #) MD009 No trailing spaces at end of lines MD010 No hard tabs (use spaces) MD012 No multiple consecutive blank lines MD047 Files should end with a single newline

docs.text: See WORK-PLANS.md

docs.title: 9.2 CHANGELOG Entry Template
docs.text:
## CHANGE [YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM] - Brief Description

### Changes

- **Added/Modified/Removed:** `path/to/file.py`
  - Description of what was changed

### Verification

```bash
pytest tests/test_module.py -v

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Any relevant context

---

## 10. Quick Checklist

Before committing any `.md` file:

- [ ] File starts with `# Title`
- [ ] Blank line before EVERY heading
- [ ] Blank line after EVERY heading
- [ ] Blank line before EVERY list
- [ ] Blank line after EVERY list
- [ ] Blank line before EVERY code block
- [ ] Blank line after EVERY code block
- [ ] All code blocks have a language specified
- [ ] File ends with a single newline
- [ ] No trailing spaces
- [ ] No multiple consecutive blank lines

**Run linter:**

```bash
# PyMarkdownLnt (Python-based, installed globally)
pymarkdown scan docs/*.md

# Check specific file
pymarkdown scan docs/MASTER-PLAN.md

# Fix auto-fixable issues
pymarkdown fix docs/*.md

docs.title: Exclusions
docs.text:
• docs/plans/archived/ — Old/completed plans
  • */_OLD/* — Any _OLD subfolder (legacy files)

docs.title: 11.1 PyMarkdownLnt (Linter)
docs.text:
Python-based markdown linter. Checks for style violations.

Installation:

pip install pymarkdownlnt

Usage:

# Scan all docs (excluding archived and _OLD)
pymarkdown scan docs/*.md docs/plans/*.md --ignore-path docs/plans/archived --ignore-path '*/_OLD/*'

# Scan with specific rules disabled
pymarkdown --disable-rules MD013,MD033 scan docs/*.md

# Auto-fix issues
pymarkdown fix docs/*.md

docs.title: 11.2 mdformat (Formatter)
docs.text:
Python-based markdown formatter. Auto-formats files.

Installation:

pip install mdformat mdformat-gfm mdformat-tables

Usage:

# Format a file (in-place)
mdformat docs/MASTER-PLAN.md

# Format all docs
mdformat docs/*.md

# Check without modifying (dry-run)
mdformat --check docs/*.md

Plugins:

  • mdformat-gfm — GitHub Flavored Markdown support
  • mdformat-tables — Table formatting

docs.title: 1. System Identity
docs.text: Rainbow Trader is an autonomous research–training–execution system for quantitative trading.

docs.title: Core Philosophy
docs.text:
1. Brains vs. Brawn: We separate Cognitive Logic (LangGraph Agents) from Heavy Compute (Optimized Python Binaries).
  2. Stateful Orchestration: The system is a persistent LangGraph, not a fragile script loop. It survives crashes and resumes exactly where it left off via Checkpointers.
  3. Event-Driven Bridge: Compute modules signal completion via DB events, which wake up the suspended Graph.
  4. Reproducibility: Everything is reproducible via commit hashes, plan hashes, and immutable datasets.

docs.title: Core Ideas
docs.text:
• Experiments are defined in structured configs (ExperimentPlan)
  • Data pipelines build reusable FAT and SMALL windows
  • Optuna-based training explores features and hyperparameters
  • Models are evaluated via backtesting and live execution
  • Multi-agent LLMs (director, planner, analyst, etc.) decide what to try next
  • Everything is reproducible via commit hashes, plan hashes, and metadata

docs.title: Experiment Types
docs.text:
• experiment_type = "trading" → model outputs trading signals
  • experiment_type = "regime" → model outputs market regime labels

docs.title: 2. Architecture Overview
docs.text: The system is divided into three layers:

docs.title: 2.1 Cognitive Layer (The Brain)
docs.text:
• graph-orchestrator: A LangGraph application that manages all agent workflows.
  • Agents as Nodes: Director, Planner, Analyst, Coder, etc. are nodes in the graph.
  • Persistence: State is checkpointed to SQLite/Postgres after every step.

docs.title: 2.2 Compute Layer (The Muscle)
docs.text:
• Standalone Modules: model-trainer, backtesting-engine, data-fetcher, etc.
  • Invoked as Tools: The Graph triggers them via wrapper functions.
  • No LLM Logic: Pure Python, optimized for speed and determinism.

docs.title: 2.3 Data Layer
docs.text:
• database: SQLite (dev) / Postgres (prod) for all structured data.
  • data: Filesystem for heavy artifacts (parquets, checkpoints, logs).
  • The Store: LangGraph's BaseStore for agent long-term memory (knowledge base).

docs.title: 2.4 Communication Patterns
docs.text: From To Mechanism Agent → Agent Graph State (messages) Automatic via LangGraph edges Agent → Module Tool Wrapper (Python function) trigger_training(exp_id) Module → Graph Events Table + Event Bridge INSERT INTO events → graph.invoke(Command(resume=...)) User → Agent Control Panel → Graph API POST /threads/{id}/runs with Command

docs.title: 2.5 Repository Structure
docs.text:
All code modules are git submodules within rainbow-trader/:

rainbow-trader/                               # Parent repo
├── modules/graph-orchestrator/               # The Brain — LangGraph orchestration
├── modules/common/                           # Shared non-numeric utils (env, logging, parquet, config)
├── modules/data-processing/          # Shared numeric logic (cleaning, normalization, indicators)
├── modules/labeling-service/                 # Label generation (trading/regime)
├── modules/data-fetcher/                 # data download (batch + streaming)
├── modules/window-preparer/               # Window preparation module (internal FAT + SMALL flows)
├── modules/model-trainer/                    # Optuna + model training
├── modules/bot-logic/                        # Order decision engine
├── modules/backtesting-engine/               # Offline simulation
├── modules/live-trading-runner/              # Real-time trading execution
├── modules/housekeeping/                     # Scheduled maintenance tasks
├── modules/database/                         # Python package for DB models (code only)
└── modules/control-panel/                    # Web dashboard + CLI

Note: Each module's internal structure is detailed in Section 4.x.

docs.title: 2.6 Data Storage Structure
docs.text:
See DATA-STORAGE.md for complete data storage documentation.

This document contains:

  • Directory structure (data/, database/)
  • Artifact caching strategy (OHLCV, indicators, labels, windows)
  • Global parquet cache rules
  • Parquet naming conventions
  • Feature to parquet lookup

docs.title: 2.7 Repository Organization & Git Submodules
docs.text:
Rainbow Trader uses a monorepo with git submodules. Each module is an independent git repository linked as a submodule.

Benefits:

  • Independent versioning per module
  • Separate CI/CD pipelines
  • Clear ownership boundaries
  • Modules can be developed in isolation

Submodule configuration: See .gitmodules file in the repository root.

Standard module structure:

rainbow-{module-name}/
├── .gitignore
├── CHANGELOG.md          # Change tracking (MANDATORY - see Section 5.4)
├── README.md
├── pyproject.toml
├── requirements.txt
├── src/{module_name}/
├── tests/
└── config/

docs.title: 2.8 Development Environment & Installation
docs.text:
Prerequisites:

  • Docker Engine 24.0+ and Docker Compose v2.20+
  • Git 2.30+
  • Python 3.11+ (for local development without Docker)

Quick Start:

# Clone with submodules
git clone --recurse-submodules https://github.com/manupadillaph/rainbow-trader.git
cd rainbow-trader

# Setup environment
cp .env.example .env
# Edit .env with API keys

# Start with Docker
docker compose build
docker compose up -d
docker compose exec rainbow-trader bash

Key configuration files:

File Purpose Dockerfile Container definition docker-compose.yml Service orchestration with security settings .env.example Environment variables template requirements.txt Python dependencies .gitignore Git exclusions (includes *.parquet, etc.)

Detailed Docker usage: See DOCKER_USAGE.md

docs.title: 2.9 Environment Variables & Secrets
docs.text:
See CONFIGURATION.md for the complete configuration system documentation.

Key points:

  • 7-level precedence: CLI flags → YAML inline → YAML file → Module env vars → Global env vars → Module YAML → Python defaults
  • Global variables: LOG__LEVEL, DATABASE__SQLITE_PATH, FOLDERS__DATA, FOLDERS__DATABASE
  • Module-specific: {MODULE}__SECTION__KEY format (e.g., MODEL_TRAINER__BATCH_SIZE=128)
  • Secrets: Exchange credentials via env vars, referenced by credentials_key in ExecutionPlan

Each module's .env.example documents its specific variables.

docs.title: 3. The Brain: graph-orchestrator
docs.text: This is the Cognitive Layer. A LangGraph application serving as the central control plane.

docs.title: 3.1 Tech Stack
docs.text:
• LangGraph v1.0: For state machine, persistence, streaming, and agent routing.
  • LangChain: For LLM interaction, Tool definitions, and prompt management.
  • LangSmith: For observability (token counting, latency, traces, debugging).
  • FastAPI: Exposes the Graph as a REST API (for Control Panel and Event Bridge).

docs.title: 3.2 Orchestrator Folder Structure
docs.text:
graph-orchestrator/
├── config/
│   ├── agents.yaml              # System prompts & persona configs per agent
│   ├── models.yaml              # Tier definitions (Fast/Smart/Genius)
│   └── graph_config.yaml        # Checkpointer, recursion limits, HITL rules
├── src/
│   ├── agents/                  # One folder per agent (director/, planner/, analyst/, coder/, doc/, research/, issues/)
│   ├── graph/                   # state.py, workflow.py, events_bridge.py
│   ├── tools/                   # wrappers.py, system.py, hitl.py, deployment.py
│   ├── store/                   # memory.py (long-term memory config)
│   └── main.py                  # FastAPI entry point
├── tests/
└── pyproject.toml

docs.title: 3.3 Graph State (Short-term Memory)
docs.text:
The shared state across all nodes in a single thread (conversation/workflow).

from typing import TypedDict, Annotated, List, Optional, Dict, Any
from langchain_core.messages import BaseMessage
from langgraph.graph.message import add_messages

class TodoItem(TypedDict):
    task: str           # e.g., "Analyze experiment results"
    status: str         # "pending", "in_progress", "completed"

class ActiveOperation(TypedDict):
    task_type: str       # "training", "data_fetch", "labeling", etc.
    experiment_id: str
    trial_id: Optional[str]
    started_at: str     # ISO 8601

class AgentState(TypedDict):
    # Core conversation history (auto-appended by LangGraph)
    messages: Annotated[List[BaseMessage], add_messages]

    # Current context
    current_experiment_id: Optional[str]
    current_trial_id: Optional[str]
    current_plan_id: Optional[str]

    # Active async operations (modules running in background)
    active_operations: List[ActiveOperation]

    # Agent's task list (within a single invocation)
    todos: List[TodoItem]

    # Budget tracking snapshot
    budget_stats: Dict[str, float]  # {"daily_spend": 1.23, "limit": 5.0}

    # Explicit routing override (if set, next node is forced)
    next_node: Optional[str]

    # Error tracking for retry logic
    error_count: int

Thread Policy:

Rainbow Trader uses a single global thread for the entire system:

RAINBOW_THREAD_ID = "rainbow_main"  # Global thread for all operations

Why one thread:

  • The system is ONE autonomous agent, not multiple users/sessions
  • Graph State tracks active_experiments[] to manage multiple experiments
  • Event Bridge always resumes the same thread
  • Simpler architecture: all state in one place

Parallelism Architecture:

Component Instance Count Notes Orchestrator 1 (singleton) Single LangGraph instance Agent Nodes 1 each (singleton) director, planner, analyst, coder, etc. Compute Modules Multiple (parallel) Each can spawn multiple processes

Compute Module Parallelism:

  • data-fetcher: Multiple symbols/timeframes in parallel (with file locking)
  • labeling-service: Multiple experiments in parallel
  • window-preparer: Multiple experiments and trials in parallel
  • model-trainer: Multiple trials in parallel (via max_concurrent_trials)
  • backtesting-engine: Multiple trials in parallel
  • live-trading-runner: Multiple bots in parallel (one per ExecutionPlan)

Example: The director-node (singleton) calls trigger_training(exp_123), which spawns 5 parallel training processes in model-trainer.

docs.title: 3.4 Tiers & Cost Management
docs.text:
Logic to select models based on task complexity and budget.

Tiers Definition (config/models.yaml):

tiers:
  tier_1_fast:
    provider: "ollama"
    model: "llama3"
    cost_per_1k_tokens: 0.0
    context_window: 8000
    use_cases:
      - "simple_summaries"
      - "json_formatting"
      - "simple_checks"
      - "context_compression"

  tier_2_smart:
    provider: "openai"
    model: "gpt-4o-mini"
    cost_per_1k_tokens: 0.00015
    context_window: 128000
    use_cases:
      - "analyst_reports"
      - "standard_planning"
      - "doc_generation"
      - "issue_analysis"

  tier_3_genius:
    provider: "anthropic"
    model: "claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022"
    cost_per_1k_tokens: 0.003
    context_window: 200000
    use_cases:
      - "director_decisions"
      - "complex_code_generation"
      - "root_cause_analysis"
      - "experiment_design"

budget:
  daily_hard_limit_usd: 5.0
  alert_threshold_usd: 4.0
  on_limit_reached: "downgrade_to_tier_1" # or "pause_non_essential" or "stop_system"

node_tier_assignments:
  director: "tier_3_genius"
  planner: "tier_3_genius"
  analyst: "tier_2_smart"
  coder: "tier_3_genius"
  doc: "tier_2_smart"
  research: "tier_2_smart"
  issues: "tier_2_smart"

Budget Guardrails Implementation:

def check_budget_before_llm_call(state: AgentState, node_name: str) -> str:
    """Returns the tier to use, potentially downgrading if over budget."""
    current_spend = get_daily_spend_from_db()
    config = load_models_config()

    if current_spend >= config["budget"]["daily_hard_limit_usd"]:
        if config["budget"]["on_limit_reached"] == "downgrade_to_tier_1":
            return "tier_1_fast"
        elif config["budget"]["on_limit_reached"] == "stop_system":
            raise BudgetExceededError("Daily budget exhausted")

    if current_spend >= config["budget"]["alert_threshold_usd"]:
        # Log warning, maybe notify user
        log.warning(f"Budget alert: ${current_spend} of ${config['budget']['daily_hard_limit_usd']}")

    return config["node_tier_assignments"].get(node_name, "tier_2_smart")

docs.title: 3.5 Graph Configuration (config/graph_config.yaml)
docs.text:
graph:
  recursion_limit: 100

checkpointer:
  type: "sqlite" # or "postgres" for production
  connection_string: "sqlite:///database/checkpoints.sqlite"
  # For Postgres: "postgresql://user:pass@localhost/rainbow_trader"

hitl:
  enabled: true
  interrupt_before:
    - "stop_experiment_tool"
    - "update_master_execution_plan_tool"
    - "deploy_to_live_tool"
  approval_timeout_hours: 24
  on_timeout: "reject" # or "approve" for auto-approve

concurrency:
  max_concurrent_experiments: 3
  max_concurrent_trials_per_experiment: 10
  max_concurrent_live_bots: 5

error_handling:
  max_consecutive_agent_errors: 3
  on_agent_error: "retry_then_notify"
  on_module_error: "stop_and_notify"

context:
  max_context_tokens: 100000
  reserve_for_response: 8000

docs.title: 3.6 Coding Standards & Quality Requirements
docs.text:
📖 Full Reference: See CODE-GENERATION.md for complete code generation standards, including:

  • Language & style guide (PEP 8, 120 char limit)
  • Docstrings format (Google style)
  • Type hints requirements (mypy --strict)
  • Quality gates (pytest, mypy, ruff)
  • Testing requirements (100% coverage)
  • Logging standards (structlog)
  • Required libraries (Polars, Typer, etc.)
  • CHANGELOG.md format and rules
  • README.md template, format and rules
  • Markdown formatting (markdownlint compliance)
  • Git workflow and commit messages

Quick Reference — Quality Gates (Before Commit):

pytest --cov=src --cov-fail-under=100
mypy src --strict
ruff check src

All code must pass these checks without errors or warnings.

docs.title: 4. Compute Modules (The Muscle)
docs.text:
Critical: These are NOT Agents. They are Tools triggered by the Graph. They run as independent processes (subprocesses or separate containers). They communicate back via the events table.

All modules are Python packages that import each other directly. There are no HTTP APIs between modules — they communicate via:

  • Direct function calls (synchronous) within the same process
  • Shared database (SQLite/Postgres)
  • Shared data (filesystem)
  • Events table (async coordination with the Graph)

docs.title: 4.0 common
docs.text:
Shared non-numeric utilities used by ALL modules. This is the foundation layer.

Package: common

Provides:

  • Environment: Load from .env, .env.local, with type-safe access
  • Logging: Structured logging setup with structlog
  • Parquet: Read/write helpers using Polars
  • Config: YAML/JSON config loading and validation
  • Paths: Resolve data/, database/, plans/ paths from env vars

Structure:

modules/common/
├── src/common/
│   ├── __init__.py
│   ├── env.py              # load_env(), get_env(), get_env_bool(), get_env_int()
│   ├── logging.py          # setup_logging(), get_logger()
│   ├── parquet.py          # read_parquet(), write_parquet(), list_parquet_files()
│   ├── config.py           # load_yaml(), load_json(), validate_config()
│   └── paths.py            # get_data_path(), get_db_path(), resolve_path()
├── tests/
├── pyproject.toml
└── requirements.txt

Note: This is reference documentation. For detailed and up-to-date information, see the module's README.md.

Configuration:

  • No CLI (library only)
  • Provides load_cli_config() and bootstrap_runtime() for other modules
  • Global defaults will be defined in src/common/defaults.py
  • See CODE-GENERATION.md for configuration system details

Usage by other modules:

from common import load_env, get_logger, read_parquet, get_data_path
from common.config import load_cli_config, bootstrap_runtime

# Load environment
load_env()  # Loads .env and .env.local

# Get logger
logger = get_logger(__name__)

# Read parquet with Polars
df = read_parquet(get_data_folder("OHLCV", "BTCUSDT", "5m", "raw.parquet"))

Key functions:

# env.py
def load_env() -> None:
    """Load .env and .env.local files."""

def get_env(key: str, default: str | None = None) -> str:
    """Get environment variable with optional default."""

def get_env_bool(key: str, default: bool = False) -> bool:
    """Get boolean environment variable."""

def get_env_int(key: str, default: int = 0) -> int:
    """Get integer environment variable."""

# logging.py
def setup_logging(level: str = "INFO") -> None:
    """Configure structlog for the application."""

def get_logger(name: str) -> structlog.BoundLogger:
    """Get a configured logger instance."""

# parquet.py
def read_parquet(path: Path) -> pl.DataFrame:
    """Read parquet file into Polars DataFrame."""

def write_parquet(df: pl.DataFrame, path: Path) -> None:
    """Write Polars DataFrame to parquet file."""

# paths.py
def get_data_folder(*path_segments: str, create: bool = False) -> Path:
    """Build a data folder path from FOLDERS__DATA or default."""

def get_database_folder() -> Path:
    """Build the database folder path from FOLDERS__DATABASE or default."""

docs.title: 4.1 data-processing
docs.text:
Single shared library for deterministic numeric logic:

  • Cleaning
  • Normalization
  • Price relatives
  • Indicators (raw, relative, normalized)
  • Hashing of configs and feature sets
  • Helper utilities for OHLCV arrays and windows

Hash algorithms for cache lookup:

All hashes are deterministic and do NOT include experiment_id to enable reuse across experiments.

def compute_label_cache_key(labeling_config: dict, base_symbol: str, base_timeframe: str, time_range: dict) -> str:
    """
    Hash for labels cache. Same labeling config + symbol + timeframe + range = reusable labels.
    """
    data = {
        "labeling_config": labeling_config,  # includes horizon, thresholds (sampling_config is separate)
        "base_symbol": base_symbol,
        "base_timeframe": base_timeframe,
        "time_range": time_range  # {"start": ..., "end": ...}
    }
    json_str = json.dumps(data, sort_keys=True)
    return hashlib.sha256(json_str.encode()).hexdigest()[:16]


def compute_fat_cache_key(features_config: List[dict], window_size: int, label_cache_key: str) -> str:
    """
    Hash for FAT window cache.
    FAT windows depend on: all features, max window_size, and which labels (timestamps).
    """
    data = {
        "features": sorted([f["name"] for f in features_config]),
        "window_size": window_size,
        "label_cache_key": label_cache_key  # links to specific labels/timestamps
    }
    json_str = json.dumps(data, sort_keys=True)
    return hashlib.sha256(json_str.encode()).hexdigest()[:16]


def compute_small_cache_key(feature_subset: List[str], window_size: int, batch_size: int) -> str:
    """
    Hash for SMALL window cache.
    SMALL windows depend on: feature subset, window size, AND batch_size (affects file organization).
    """
    data = {
        "features": sorted(feature_subset),
        "window_size": window_size,
        "batch_size": batch_size
    }
    json_str = json.dumps(data, sort_keys=True)
    return hashlib.sha256(json_str.encode()).hexdigest()[:16]

Cache key summary:

Cache Type Key Components Reusable Across Labels labeling_config, base_symbol, base_timeframe, time_range Experiments with same labeling FAT Windows features, window_size, label_cache_key Experiments with same features + labels SMALL Windows feature_subset, window_size, batch_size Trials with same feature/window/batch config

Same code paths used in:

  • labeling-service — computes label_cache_key
  • window-preparer — computes fat_cache_key and small_cache_key
  • live-trading-runner — reuses same hash logic for consistency

Note: This is reference documentation. For detailed and up-to-date information, see the module's README.md.

Configuration:

  • No CLI (library only)
  • Pure deterministic functions
  • No configuration files needed

docs.title: 4.2 labeling-service
docs.text:
Label generation (trading/regime) for datasets. Runs BEFORE window-preparer.

See modules/labeling-service/README.md for complete documentation.

  • Tool Wrapper: trigger_labeling(experiment_id: str) -> str
  • Event Emitted: LABELS_DONE
  • Outputs: labels_directional.parquet, labels_selected.parquet (cached by label_cache_key)

docs.title: 4.3 data-fetcher
docs.text:
Downloads and caches OHLCV (batch + streaming modes).

See modules/data-fetcher/README.md for complete documentation.

  • Tool Wrapper: trigger_data_fetch(experiment_id: str) -> str
  • Event Emitted: DATA_FETCH_DONE
  • Outputs: data/OHLCV/{symbol}/{timeframe}/raw.parquet

docs.title: 4.4 window-preparer
docs.text:
Builds FAT windows (large feature matrices). Runs AFTER labeling-service.

See modules/window-preparer/README.md for complete documentation.

  • Tool Wrapper: trigger_fat_build(experiment_id: str) -> str
  • Event Emitted: FAT_WINDOWS_DONE

  • Outputs: data/fat_windows/{fat_cache_key}/

  • OFF mode: Slices FAT windows for training trials (cached by small_cache_key)

  • LIVE mode: Builds windows from raw OHLCV in real-time (in memory only)
  • Outputs: data/small_windows/{small_cache_key}/

docs.title: 4.5 model-trainer
docs.text:
Central training engine using Optuna.

See modules/model-trainer/README.md for complete documentation.

  • Tool Wrapper: trigger_training(experiment_id: str) -> str
  • Events Emitted: TRIAL_SCORED (per trial), ALL_TRIALS_DONE (when complete)
  • Outputs: data/checkpoints/{experiment_id}/{trial_id}.*
  • Supports: experiment_type = "trading" and experiment_type = "regime"

docs.title: 4.6 bot-logic
docs.text:
Trading decision module with explicit strategy/risk separation.

See modules/bot-logic/README.md for complete documentation.

  • Inputs: Model predictions + bot_config (strategy_config, risk_manager_config, order_preferences_config)
  • Flow: generate_exit_orders -> generate_entry_signals -> RiskManager.evaluate_entries
  • Outputs: Strategy close orders and risk-approved open orders with typed metadata
  • Shared by: backtesting-engine, live-trading-runner

docs.title: 4.7 backtesting-engine
docs.text:
Offline evaluation engine for trading. Does NOT use labels.

See modules/backtesting-engine/README.md for complete documentation.

  • Event Emitted: BACKTEST_TRIAL_DONE(experiment_id, trial_id)
  • Outputs: backtest_trials, backtest_trades tables
  • Key distinction: Measures trading performance (Sharpe, drawdown), not label accuracy

docs.title: 4.8 live-trading-runner
docs.text:
Real-time trading and simulation engine. Orchestrated by the Graph.

See modules/live-trading-runner/README.md for complete documentation.

  • Current scope (implemented): ExecutionPlan-first runtime foundation (no MasterExecutionPlan runtime dependency).
  • Control commands: start, stop, pause, resume, reload, status, force-close.
  • Events emitted: LIVE_TRADING_STARTED, LIVE_EXEC_SUMMARY, LIVE_TRADING_STOPPED, LIVE_ANOMALY_DETECTED.
  • Modes: LIVE_REAL (real orders) or LIVE_SIM (simulated execution adapter).
  • Persistence outputs: live_sessions runtime audit + trades lineage + summary/anomaly events.

docs.title: 4.9 database
docs.text:
SQLite database (dev) / Postgres (prod) + shared Python package for DB access.

See modules/database/README.md for module documentation. See DATABASE-SCHEMA.md for complete schema documentation.

  • Holds: Experiments, trials, events, execution plans, backtest results, live trades, LangGraph tables
  • Migrations: Alembic-managed schema changes

docs.title: 4.10 control-panel
docs.text:
Web dashboard and CLI for system monitoring and manual intervention.

See modules/control-panel/README.md for complete documentation.

  • Stack: FastAPI + HTMX/Tailwind + Typer CLI
  • Capabilities: HITL interface, system monitoring, graph visibility, live trading dashboard
  • Runs on: http://localhost:8080 (configurable)

docs.title: 4.11 housekeeping
docs.text:
Scheduled maintenance module for cache cleanup, data archival, and system health.

See modules/housekeeping/README.md for complete documentation.

  • NOT a Tool Wrapper — runs independently on a schedule (cron or systemd timer)
  • Tool Wrapper: trigger_housekeeping(task: str) — for on-demand cleanup
  • Responsibilities: Cache cleanup, experiment archival, log rotation, DB maintenance, health checks

docs.title: 4.12 CLI Configuration System
docs.text:
Configuration details now live in CONFIGURATION.md. This section remains intentionally brief to keep MASTER-PLAN focused on architecture.

  • All modules share the standardized loader in modules/common (load_cli_config + bootstrap_runtime) with the 7-level precedence defined in the configuration guide.
  • CLI entrypoints should expose --config and --set for overrides; module-specific commands stay documented in each module's README and --help.
  • Each module keeps .env.example, config/cli_defaults.yaml, and src/{module}/defaults.py in sync with the centralized guide instead of duplicating variables here.

docs.title: 5. Agent Nodes
docs.text: Agents are nodes in the graph-orchestrator. They communicate via the Graph State.

docs.title: 5.1 director-node (Router/Supervisor)
docs.text:
The Boss. Coordinates the high-level workflow. Decides "What next?".

Model Tier: Tier 3 (Genius) — requires maximum intelligence.

Triggered when:

  • User sends a message to the Graph
  • Event Bridge resumes the Graph after a module completes
  • Another node routes back to Director

Responsibilities:

  • Reads incoming messages/events
  • Decides next action:
  • New ExperimentPlans → delegates to planner-node
  • Analyze results → delegates to analyst-node
  • Fix code issue → delegates to coder-node
  • Stop or modify running work → uses tools directly
  • Improve system → delegates to research-node then coder-node
  • Uses summaries from The Store for context
  • Reads high-level state from long-term memory

Self-Improving Code Flow: See PIPELINE-FLOWS.md Section 8 for the complete flow diagram.

Tools available:

  • stop_experiment(experiment_id, reason) → sets experiments.status = "stopping" + emits event
  • stop_trial(experiment_id, trial_id, reason) → sets trials.status = "stopping" + emits event
  • prioritize_experiment(experiment_id) → adjusts experiment priority
  • update_master_execution_plan(changes) → modifies MasterExecutionPlan (HITL required)
  • read_from_store(namespace, query) → semantic search in long-term memory
  • write_to_store(namespace, key, value) → save insight to long-term memory

Routing (Conditional Edges):

  • If task requires planning → planner-node
  • If task requires analysis → analyst-node
  • If task requires coding → coder-node
  • If task requires research → research-node
  • If task is complete → END

docs.title: 5.2 planner-node
docs.text:
The Architect. Generates structured plans.

Model Tier: Tier 3 (Genius).

Generates:

  • ExperimentPlan (trading and regime)
  • ExecutionPlan (for live or simulation)
  • Proposals to update MasterExecutionPlan

Strategy:

  • Phased Experimentation:
  • Phase 1 (Discovery): Short period (3m), broad search (many models/features).
  • Phase 2 (Refinement): Medium period (6m), focused search on best families.
  • Phase 3 (Validation): Long period (1y+), narrow tuning, rolling windows.

Tools available:

  • create_experiment_config(config) → creates ExperimentPlan JSON + DB record
  • create_execution_plan(experiment_id, trial_id, mode) → creates ExecutionPlan
  • read_from_store(namespace, query) → get insights from past experiments
  • get_experiment_summary(experiment_id) → read analyst summaries

Uses:

  • Summaries from analyst-node
  • database contents
  • research-node findings

Returns to: director-node (for approval and next steps)

docs.title: 5.3 analyst-node (formerly supervisor-agent)
docs.text:
The Eye. Analyzes results and generates insights. Does NOT compute scores.

Model Tier: Tier 2 (Smart).

NOTE: Scoring is done by model-trainer (deterministic). Analyst-node does NOT compute scores.

Triggered by Graph when:

  • TRIAL_SCORED(experiment_id, trial_id) event arrives — via Event Bridge
  • TRIAL_SUMMARY_READY(experiment_id, trial_id) event arrives — via Event Bridge
  • ALL_TRIALS_DONE(experiment_id) event arrives — via Event Bridge
  • LIVE_EXEC_SUMMARY event arrives — for live trading analysis

Trial-level responsibilities (after each TRIAL_SCORED):

  • Generate trial summary (human-readable) and emit TRIAL_SUMMARY_READY
  • Monitor training progress across trials
  • Can recommend intervention: If trials are consistently failing:
  • Returns message to director-node with recommendation
  • Director decides whether to use stop_experiment tool
  • Track trial completion rate and estimated time remaining
  • Store trial summary in The Store

Experiment-level responsibilities (after ALL_TRIALS_DONE):

  • Generate experiment summary (comprehensive analysis)
  • Identify patterns across ALL trials:
  • Which features performed best
  • Which model types worked
  • Which hyperparameters mattered
  • Assign regime tags to experiment period
  • Compare with historical experiments (from The Store)
  • Recommend best trials for deployment
  • Store experiment summary in The Store
  • Return final report to director-node

Live-level responsibilities:

  • Analyze live trading performance
  • Detect regime changes
  • Alert on anomalies
  • Recommend adjustments

Tools available:

  • read_trials_metrics(experiment_id) → get all trial scores
  • read_backtest_results(trial_id) → get backtest metrics
  • write_to_store(namespace, key, value) → save summary/insight
  • read_from_store(namespace, query) → compare with past

Returns to: director-node

docs.title: 5.4 coder-node
docs.text:
The Builder. Reads documentation and modifies code.

Model Tier: Tier 3 (Genius).

📖 Code Standards: All code generated by coder-node MUST follow CODE-GENERATION.md.

Modifies code in modules:

  • graph-orchestrator
  • data-processing
  • labeling-service
  • model-trainer
  • bot-logic
  • etc.

Responsibilities:

  • Adds tests and implementations
  • Aims for high coverage and deterministic behaviors
  • Constraint: Cannot invent logic not in docs/specs
  • Ensures all changes pass tests before deployment
  • Handles database migrations when schema changes

Tools available:

  • read_file(path) → read source file
  • write_file(path, content) → write/update source file
  • search_codebase(query) → find relevant code
  • run_tests(module) → execute pytest --cov=src --cov-fail-under=100
  • run_mypy(module) → execute mypy src --strict
  • run_ruff(module) → execute ruff check src
  • run_e2e_tests() → execute end-to-end tests
  • git_commit(message) → commit changes locally (after all checks pass)
  • run_migrations() → apply Alembic migrations to DB
  • restart_module(module_name) → restart a specific module service
  • check_module_health(module_name) → verify module is running correctly

Quality Gates Before Commit:

See CODE-GENERATION.md Section 2 for complete requirements.

  1. run_tests(module) → must pass with 100% coverage
  2. run_mypy(module) → must pass with no errors and no warnings
  3. run_ruff(module) → must pass with no errors and no warnings
  4. run_e2e_tests() → must pass with no errors and no warnings
  5. Only then: git_commit(message) → commit locally

Git Workflow:

See GIT-COMMITS.md for complete git workflow guide, including:

  • Commit message format (Conventional Commits)
  • Branch strategy and when to branch
  • How to handle submodules correctly
  • Commit grouping philosophy (small, logical commits)
  • Recovery steps when things go wrong

Quick reference:

  • Small changes: commit locally, do NOT push automatically
  • Large features: create feature branch, commit locally
  • All commits require director-node approval before push
  • PRs for major changes (new features, architecture changes)
  • Never auto-push to remote — user reviews and pushes manually

Change Tracking (MANDATORY):

See CODE-GENERATION.md Section 5 for complete format and rules.

Every code change MUST be documented in CHANGELOG.md at the root of the modified module.

Returns to: director-node

docs.title: 5.5 doc-node
docs.text:
The Librarian. Maintains documentation.

Model Tier: Tier 2 (Smart).

Maintains:

  • README files
  • Module-level documentation
  • API references
  • Architecture documents

Consistency Check:

  • Validates that implemented code matches the documentation.
  • Raises alerts if discrepancies are found.
  • Principle: "Code Agent never invents features. Everything flows through documentation first."

Keeps docs consistent with the current implementation.

Returns to: director-node

docs.title: 5.6 research-node
docs.text:
The Scout. Scans external information.

Model Tier: Tier 2 (Smart).

Scans external information:

  • Papers
  • Blogs
  • Code repos
  • Strategies

Writes findings for:

  • New model families
  • New features
  • New labeling methods
  • New risk management practices

Tools available:

  • web_search(query) → search the web
  • write_to_store(namespace, key, value) → save research finding

Returns to: director-node

docs.title: 5.7 issues-node
docs.text:
The Detective. Scans logs and error reports.

Model Tier: Tier 2 (Smart).

Identifies:

  • Pipeline failures
  • Misconfigurations
  • Inconsistent docs vs code

Generates structured problem analysis for director-node and proposes fixes.

Tools available:

  • read_logs(module, time_range) → get recent logs
  • read_events(event_type, status="failed") → get failed events

Returns to: director-node

docs.title: 6. Plans
docs.text:
See PLANS-SCHEMA.md for complete plan schema documentation.

This document contains:

  • ExperimentPlan — Full schema for experiment configuration
  • ExecutionPlan — Full schema for live trading configuration
  • MasterExecutionPlan — Multi-bot orchestration schema

Summary:

Plan Type Storage Purpose ExperimentPlan plans/experiments/{id}.json Dataset + training + backtest config ExecutionPlan plans/execution/{id}.json Live trading config for one symbol MasterExecutionPlan plans/master_execution_plan.json Multi-bot orchestration

docs.title: 7. Data Storage
docs.text:
See DATA-STORAGE.md for complete data storage documentation.

This document contains:

  • Directory Structure — data/ folder layout
  • Artifact Caching Strategy — OHLCV, indicators, labels, windows
  • Global Parquet Cache — Reusability rules
  • Parquet Naming Convention — File naming patterns
  • Feature to Parquet Lookup — How modules find data

docs.title: 8. Database Tables
docs.text:
See DATABASE-SCHEMA.md for complete database schema documentation.

This document contains:

  • Schema Conventions — Naming rules for columns, FKs, timestamps
  • Core Tables — experiments, trials, events
  • Data Storage Tables — parquet_files, labels_files
  • Backtest Tables — backtest_trials, backtest_trades
  • Analysis Tables — analyst_summaries
  • Execution Tables — execution_plans, master_execution_state
  • Live Trading Tables — live_trades, live_sessions
  • Regime Tables — regimes
  • LangGraph Tables — Checkpointer and Store tables

docs.title: 9. Events & The Event Bridge
docs.text:
See EVENTS.md for complete event documentation.

This document contains:

  • Canonical Event Types — All valid event type names
  • Event Structure — Event class definition
  • Payload Expectations — Required payload fields
  • Event Types (Detailed) — Full payload specs per event type
  • Data Preparation events
  • Per-Trial events
  • Experiment Closure events
  • Stop/Cancel events
  • Live Execution events
  • System events
  • The Event Bridge — Implementation and notes

docs.title: 10. Full Pipeline Workflows
docs.text:
See PIPELINE-FLOWS.md for complete workflow diagrams.

This document contains all pipeline flows with detailed ASCII diagrams:

  • 1. Experiment Flow — Full experiment lifecycle from plan creation to analysis
  • 2. Per-Trial Flow — SMALL windows, training, backtest, scoring
  • 3. Experiment to Execution — ExecutionPlan and MasterExecutionPlan creation
  • 4. Live Trading — Real-time trading loop with bot decision flow and runtime controls
  • 5. Stop Experiment — Graceful experiment termination
  • 6. Stop Trial — Individual trial cancellation
  • 7. User Intervention — Manual control via Control Panel/CLI
  • 8. Self-Improving Code — Automated code improvement cycle

docs.title: 11.1 Graph State (Short-term Memory)
docs.text:
• Mechanism: LangGraph StateGraph with messages list.
  • Content: Current conversation history, active plan, recent tool outputs.
  • Persistence: Checkpointed to DB after every step via Checkpointer.
  • Benefit: Safe to restart orchestrator at any time. Resumes exactly where it left off.

docs.title: 11.2 The Store (Long-term Memory)
docs.text:
• Mechanism: LangGraph BaseStore (KV Store with optional semantic search).
  • Namespaces:
  • ("insights", "experiments"): Lessons learned from experiments.
  • ("insights", "features"): Which features work best.
  • ("user", "preferences"): User-specified preferences.
  • ("system", "errors"): Common failure patterns.
  • Usage: Agents query the Store to retrieve context before acting.
# Writing to Store
store.put(("insights", "experiments"), experiment_id, {
    "summary": "XGBoost outperformed LSTM on BTC/5m",
    "best_features": ["RSI_14", "MACD_signal"],
    "regime": "high_volatility"
})

# Reading from Store (semantic search)
results = store.search(("insights", "experiments"), query="What works in high volatility?")

docs.title: 11.3 Checkpointer (Persistence)
docs.text:
• Tool: SqliteSaver (dev) / PostgresSaver (prod).
  • Function: Saves the entire Graph state after every node execution.
  • Benefit: "Time Travel" debugging, resumable workflows, crash recovery.

docs.title: 11.4 Human-in-the-Loop (HITL)
docs.text:
• Mechanism: interrupt_before on sensitive tools.
  • Workflow:
  • Agent proposes action.
  • Graph hits interrupt, state saved.
  • Control Panel shows approval request.
  • User approves/rejects.
  • Control Panel sends Command(resume=decision).
  • Graph executes or skips tool.

docs.title: 11.5 Context Window Management
docs.text:
Before invoking any agent node:

  1. Calculate available tokens: max_context - reserve_for_response
  2. Priority-based loading: Current task > Recent messages > Summaries > Historical
  3. If exceeds budget: Summarize older items using Tier 1 model.
  4. Large tool results: Write to file, return reference.

docs.title: 11.6 Error Recovery
docs.text:
Orchestrator crash recovery:

  1. Checkpointer has saved state after every step.
  2. On restart, read last checkpoint from DB.
  3. Resume from exact point of failure.
  4. Event Bridge resumes processing unprocessed events.

Agent error handling:

  • Retry up to max_consecutive_agent_errors.
  • If still failing: Create alert for USER, pause workflow.

docs.title: 12.1 Data Types
docs.text: Field Type Format Example timestamp ISO 8601 string "2025-01-15T10:30:00Z" experiment_id UUID string "exp_a1b2c3d4" trial_id UUID string "trial_x1y2z3" execution_plan_id UUID string "exec_m1n2o3" thread_id UUID string "thread_abc123" duration_seconds float 3600.5 probability float [0.0, 1.0] 0.75 percentage float (as decimal) 0.02 (= 2%) score float 0.85 hash hex string (16 chars) "a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8" status enum string "pending", "training", "completed" file_path relative to data/ "checkpoints/exp_abc/trial_xyz.pt"

docs.title: 12.2 Naming Conventions
docs.text: Element Convention Example Database tables snake_case, plural experiments, trials, backtest_trades Database columns snake_case experiment_id, created_at, final_score Python modules snake_case model-trainer, bot_logic Config keys snake_case max_concurrent_experiments, window_size Event types UPPER_SNAKE_CASE TRIAL_SCORED, ALL_TRIALS_DONE Agent nodes kebab-case director-node, analyst-node Feature names {symbol}_{tf}_{source}_{pipeline} BTCUSDT_5m_close_normalized File storage dirs snake_case fat_windows, small_windows

docs.title: 12.3 Acronyms
docs.text:
• OHLCV — Open, High, Low, Close, Volume
  • FAT — Full-feature Array for Training
  • SMALL — Selected-feature Matrix for Applied Learning Loops
  • SL/TP — Stop Loss / Take Profit
  • HITL — Human-in-the-Loop
  • LLM — Large Language Model

docs.title: 13.1 Test Categories
docs.text:
1. Hard Tests (Pipeline Blockers):

  2. Must always pass. If failed: Pipeline STOPs.

  3. Examples: Schema validation, No duplicate timestamps, Price validity (Low ≤ High), No NaNs/Inf.

  4. Warning Tests (Monitoring):

  5. Log warning but continue pipeline.
  6. Examples: Data gaps > X hours, Outlier ratio > Y%, Low trading opportunity count.

docs.title: 13.2 Module-Specific Gates
docs.text:
• data-fetcher: Volume consistency, Completeness ratio.
  • cleaning (core-lib): Max synthetic ratio, Max outlier ratio.
  • indicators: Bounded range check (RSI in [0, 100]), Consistency check.
  • labeling: Label distribution balance, Min trading opportunities.
  • Windows (FAT/SMALL): Row count matches label count, No look-ahead bias.

docs.title: 13.3 Graph Tests
docs.text:
• LangSmith Traces: Verify routing logic is correct.
  • Unit Tests: For each node's decision logic.
  • Integration Tests: End-to-end workflow with mock modules.

docs.title: 14. Final Notes
docs.text:
• bot-logic is explicitly responsible for transforming model outputs + bot_config into concrete orders through the bot decision flow (generate_exit_orders -> generate_entry_signals -> RiskManager.evaluate_entries).

  • ExperimentPlan and ExecutionPlan both contain bot_config (for backtesting and live/Sim).

  • OHLCV and indicators are stored in the structured layout under data/OHLCV and data/indicators.

  • FAT and SMALL windows are cached to enable re-use across trials and experiments:

  • FAT windows: per experiment, full feature set

  • SMALL windows: per trial config (feature_subset, window_size, batch_size)

  • All major steps emit explicit events so the Event Bridge can resume the Graph.

  • batch_size is a tunable parameter in dataset_config, and Optuna can optimize it via batch_size_range.

  • Feature hash applies to both FAT and SMALL windows for cache lookup.

Key Architectural Points:

  1. The Graph is the Brain: LangGraph manages all agent workflows, persistence, and HITL.
  2. Modules are the Muscle: Heavy compute runs as separate processes (subprocesses), communicates via events.
  3. Events are the Bridge: events table + Event Bridge connects async modules to the stateful Graph.
  4. The Store is the Memory: Long-term knowledge lives in LangGraph Store, agents query it before acting.
  5. Checkpointer is the Safety Net: Crash recovery is native, no custom checkpointing needed.
  6. Live-trading-runner is Orchestrated: The Graph starts/stops/reloads it, not manual.

Director-node is the decision maker:

  • Triggered by Event Bridge or user input.
  • Reads messages/events, analyzes, decides.
  • Uses tools to execute decisions.
  • Delegates to specialist nodes via routing edges.
  • Can trigger self-improving code flow (research → coder → deploy).

Self-Improving System: See PIPELINE-FLOWS.md Section 8 for the complete flow.

Trainer-engine polls DB for status:

  • Checks trials.status periodically during training.
  • If status = "stopping" → graceful stop → status = "cancelled".
  • No need to listen to events for stop signals.

The system is ready for the orchestrator and agents to start generating code and documentation per repo.
