docs.title: D. Removed-event registry (kept documented for possible future reimplementation)
docs.text: Event or family Decision Evidence from current repo Reason for removal TRIAL_CONFIG_READY Remove Only found in events.py; no real emitter callsite found No requester, no consumer contract, no lifecycle value TRIAL_SCORED Remove from canonical set Found in events.py, config/auto-routes.yaml, docs/MASTER-PLAN.md, modules/model-trainer/README.md, and database tests, but no runtime emitter callsite was found in active module code Ranking/result persistence belongs in DB state or a future artifact/result flow; today it adds lifecycle noise without a clean requester/emitter/consumer contract ANALYST_TRIAL_SUMMARY_READY Remove from canonical set Only found in events.py; no requester/emitter callsite found outside declarations If summaries are evented later, they need a real artifact flow or should remain plain files/DB artifacts ANALYST_EXPERIMENT_SUMMARY_READY Remove from canonical set Only found in events.py; no requester/emitter callsite found outside declarations Same problem as trial summary EXPERIMENT_STOPPING Remove from canonical set Only found in events.py; no stop orchestration callsite found “Experiment” is too vague as a stop target; stop flows must be domain-specific (TRAINING_*, BACKTEST_*, LIVE_TRADING_*) EXPERIMENT_STOPPED Remove from canonical set Only found in events.py; no real emitter/consumer flow found Same ambiguity as EXPERIMENT_STOPPING TRIAL_STOPPING, TRIAL_CANCELLED Remove from canonical set Only found in events.py; no concrete stop/cancel flow found Replace with normalized terminal semantics in TRIAL_DONE if needed HOUSEKEEPING_DONE Remove from canonical set One emitter exists in housekeeping.run_all(), but that method is still TODO/pending implementation and there is no REQUESTED/STARTED/PROGRESS flow A single maintenance DONE event is not enough to justify inclusion in the final taxonomy SYSTEM_TASK_* Move out of canonical set Only found in events.py; no real emitter callsites found in modules Internal observability, not business execution taxonomy GRAPH_THREAD_* Move out of canonical set Declared in events.py; not part of domain job lifecycles Internal graph runtime telemetry AGENT_* Move out of canonical set Declared in events.py; not part of domain job lifecycles Agent telemetry, not business execution taxonomy STORE_READ, STORE_WRITE Move out of canonical set Declared in events.py; no domain lifecycle Internal memory/store observability EVENT_BRIDGE_ERROR Move out of canonical set Declared in events.py; bridge/runtime concern only Infra error telemetry, not a domain flow MASTER_EXEC_PLAN_UPDATED Remove from canonical set Only found in events.py; no real emitter callsite found No clear request/update/delete lifecycle and no concrete consumer contract EXPERIMENT_PLAN_CREATED, EXECUTION_PLAN_CREATED Remove from canonical set for now Declared in events.py, but not part of the module-job lifecycles above Artifact/materialization events can be reconsidered later in a separate artifact lifecycle spec DATA_PIPELINE_REQUESTED, DATA_PIPELINE_DONE Remove Convenience meta-flow only, no clear executor lifecycle Too ambiguous versus the explicit subflows we already have TRIAL_TRAINING_DONE, TRIAL_COMPLETED, TRIAL_PRUNED, TRIAL_FAILED, BACKTEST_TRIAL_DONE, ALL_TRIALS_DONE, LIVE_EXEC_SUMMARY, LIVE_TRADING_STOPPED, LIVE_ANOMALY_DETECTED, DATA_PROCESSED, DATA_PROCESSED_DONE Remove / replace Legacy declarations in events.py conflict with the normalized target families above Replace with TRIAL_DONE, BACKTEST_DONE, LIVE_TRADING_PROGRESS, LIVE_TRADING_DONE, LIVE_TRADING_ANOMALY_DETECTED, DATA_PROCESSING_*, and TRAINING/PHASE terminal events

docs.title: 📦 Current Payload Catalog (Declared Inventory, To Prune)
docs.text:
Important: The canonical target set is the section above. The payload inventory below is still the broader declared inventory from events.py plus the normalized target payloads we want to implement. It must be pruned to match the canonical set during the code implementation pass. Any removed event that still appears below is documented only as historical inventory, not as an implementation target.

Catalog rule: All payload models inherit BaseEventPayload, so every payload may also include optional source and note. If an event remains in the final EventType, it must have a concrete payload contract here.

docs.title: Optimization hierarchy
docs.text:
• TRAINING_REQUESTED → TrainingRequestedPayload
  • Required: experiment_id, trial_id, hyperparameters
  • Optional: none
  • TRAINING_STARTED → TrainingStartedPayload
  • Required: experiment_id, total_phases
  • Optional: resume, suffix, copy_source_suffix, starting_phase, all_phases
  • PHASE_STARTED → PhaseStartedPayload
  • Required: experiment_id, phase_number, total_phases, planned_trials
  • Optional: resumed_from_checkpoint, source_suffix
  • TRIAL_CONFIG_READY → TrialConfigReadyPayload
  • Required: experiment_id, trial_id, model_type, feature_count, window_size, batch_size
  • Optional: phase_number
  • TRIAL_STARTED → TrialStartedPayload
  • Required: experiment_id, trial_id, trial_number
  • Optional: phase_number
  • TRIAL_PROGRESS → TrialProgressPayload
  • Required: experiment_id, trial_id, trial_number, phase_number, epoch, total_epochs, progress_percent
  • Optional: epoch_objective_value, best_score_so_far
  • TRAINING_PROGRESS → TrainingProgressPayload
  • Required: experiment_id, trial_id, phase_number, epoch, total_epochs, loss, val_loss, val_accuracy, f1_macro, recall, precision, entry_count, entry_rate_over_total_predictions, entry_f1, epoch_objective_value, progress_percent
  • Optional: trial_number, total_phases, trials_completed_in_phase, total_trials_in_phase
  • TRIAL_DONE → TrialDonePayload
  • Required: experiment_id, trial_id, trial_number, outcome, duration_seconds
  • Optional: phase_number, score, reason, step_pruned_at, error_message, error_type, is_recoverable
  • PHASE_PROGRESS → PhaseProgressPayload
  • Required: experiment_id, phase_number, total_phases, trials_completed, total_trials, progress_percent
  • Optional: current_trial_id, current_trial_number, last_trial_outcome, last_trial_score
  • PHASE_DONE → PhaseDonePayload
  • Required: experiment_id, phase_number, n_scored, n_pruned, n_cancelled
  • Optional: total_phases, best_trial_id, best_score
  • TRIAL_SCORED → TrialScoredPayload (historical/deferred only — removed from final canonical target set)
  • Required: experiment_id, trial_id, final_score, rank
  • Optional: phase_number
  • TRAINING_DONE → TrainingDonePayload
  • Required: experiment_id, total_phases, completed_phases, status
  • Optional: best_trial_id, best_score, duration_seconds

docs.title: Backtest family
docs.text:
• BACKTEST_REQUESTED → BacktestRequestedPayload
  • Required: experiment_id, trial_id, backtest_config
  • Optional: none
  • BACKTEST_STARTED → BacktestStartedPayload
  • Required: experiment_id, trial_id, backtest_session_id, backtest_type, range_start, range_end, initial_equity
  • Optional: checkpoint_selection_mode, checkpoint_selection_epoch
  • BACKTEST_PROGRESS → BacktestProgressPayload
  • Required: experiment_id, trial_id, backtest_session_id, processed_timestamps, total_timestamps, progress_percent
  • Optional: equity, open_positions, closed_trades_count
  • BACKTEST_DONE → BacktestDonePayload
  • Required: experiment_id, trial_id, processed_timestamps, total_timestamps, total_pnl, return_pct, sharpe_ratio, trades_count, winning_trades, losing_trades, win_rate, avg_win, avg_loss, equity, unrealized_pnl, equity_mark_to_market, open_positions, benchmark_return, alpha, pruned
  • Optional: pruning_reason, prediction_health

docs.title: 📝 Notes
docs.text:
• 2026-03-19 — Taxonomy discussion took several sessions. Key confusion was Training vs Trial — user confirmed Training CONTAINS phases which contain trials. Trial = single Optuna iteration. Training = full run_optimization() call.
  • 2026-03-19 — Cascade rule: every epoch emits 3 events (TRIAL_PROGRESS + PHASE_PROGRESS + TRAINING_PROGRESS). Every trial-end emits TRIAL_DONE + PHASE_PROGRESS + TRAINING_PROGRESS. Every phase-end emits PHASE_DONE + TRAINING_PROGRESS.
  • 2026-03-19 — User confirmed: TRIAL_COMPLETED, TRIAL_PRUNED, TRIAL_FAILED all become TRIAL_DONE with outcome field in payload.
  • 2026-03-19 — User confirmed: ALL_TRIALS_DONE was misnamed — it is actually end-of-phase (has phase_number in payload). Rename to PHASE_DONE.
  • 2026-03-20 — User corrected the live family to follow the same naming convention as the other modules: LIVE_TRADING_REQUESTED → LIVE_TRADING_STARTED → LIVE_TRADING_PROGRESS → LIVE_TRADING_DONE, plus LIVE_TRADING_ANOMALY_DETECTED.
  • 2026-03-20 — User required the audit inventory to be lowered into this plan as explicit KEEP / RENAME / DELETE / NEW decisions.
  • 2026-03-20 — User required the full payload catalog to be part of this plan, not deferred to implementation.

docs.title: Event Taxonomy — Simple View
docs.text: What this is: A child-friendly view of the event refactor. Source of truth: EVENT_TAXONOMY_PLAN.md Generated: 2026-03-24

docs.title: 1. Final Events (what will exist after the refactor)
docs.text: Every module job follows the same pattern: REQUESTED → STARTED → PROGRESS → DONE.

docs.title: Data Fetch
docs.text: Event Who emits it DATA_FETCH_REQUESTED Orchestrator DATA_FETCH_STARTED Data-Fetcher DATA_FETCH_PROGRESS Data-Fetcher DATA_FETCH_DONE Data-Fetcher

docs.title: Labels
docs.text: Event Who emits it LABELS_REQUESTED Orchestrator LABELS_STARTED Labeling-Service LABELS_PROGRESS Labeling-Service LABELS_DONE Labeling-Service

docs.title: FAT Windows
docs.text: Event Who emits it FAT_WINDOWS_REQUESTED Orchestrator FAT_WINDOWS_STARTED Window-Preparer FAT_WINDOWS_PROGRESS Window-Preparer FAT_WINDOWS_DONE Window-Preparer

docs.title: SMALL Windows
docs.text: Event Who emits it SMALL_WINDOWS_REQUESTED Orchestrator SMALL_WINDOWS_STARTED Window-Preparer SMALL_WINDOWS_PROGRESS Window-Preparer SMALL_WINDOWS_DONE Window-Preparer

docs.title: Data Processing
docs.text: Event Who emits it DATA_PROCESSING_REQUESTED Orchestrator DATA_PROCESSING_STARTED Data-Processing DATA_PROCESSING_PROGRESS Data-Processing DATA_PROCESSING_DONE Data-Processing

docs.title: Training (container — contains Phases, which contain Trials)
docs.text: Event Who emits it Notes TRAINING_REQUESTED Orchestrator TRAINING_STARTED Model-Trainer TRAINING_PROGRESS Model-Trainer Cascaded up from every epoch + every trial end + every phase end TRAINING_DONE Model-Trainer NEW — emitted when all phases finish

docs.title: Phase (nested inside Training)
docs.text: Event Who emits it Notes PHASE_STARTED Model-Trainer NEW PHASE_PROGRESS Model-Trainer NEW — cascaded up from every epoch + every trial end PHASE_DONE Model-Trainer NEW — replaces old ALL_TRIALS_DONE

docs.title: Trial (nested inside Phase)
docs.text: Event Who emits it Notes TRIAL_STARTED Model-Trainer Already exists TRIAL_PROGRESS Model-Trainer NEW — emitted every epoch TRIAL_DONE Model-Trainer NEW — replaces old TRIAL_COMPLETED + TRIAL_PRUNED + TRIAL_FAILED (outcome in payload)

docs.title: Backtest
docs.text: Event Who emits it BACKTEST_REQUESTED Orchestrator BACKTEST_STARTED Backtesting-Engine BACKTEST_PROGRESS Backtesting-Engine BACKTEST_DONE Backtesting-Engine

docs.title: Live Trading
docs.text: Event Who emits it Notes LIVE_TRADING_REQUESTED Orchestrator NEW LIVE_TRADING_STARTED Live-Trading-Runner Already exists LIVE_TRADING_PROGRESS Live-Trading-Runner Replaces old LIVE_EXEC_SUMMARY LIVE_TRADING_DONE Live-Trading-Runner Replaces old LIVE_TRADING_STOPPED LIVE_TRADING_ANOMALY_DETECTED Live-Trading-Runner Replaces old LIVE_ANOMALY_DETECTED

docs.title: HITL (Human-in-the-Loop)
docs.text:
Event Who emits it HITL_REQUEST Orchestrator HITL_DECISION Control-Panel HITL_APPROVED Control-Panel HITL_REJECTED Control-Panel

Total canonical events: 45

docs.title: 2. Renames (old name → new name)
docs.text: Old name (in code today) New name Why ALL_TRIALS_DONE PHASE_DONE It was actually end-of-phase, not end-of-training TRIAL_COMPLETED TRIAL_DONE Unified terminal event with outcome in payload TRIAL_PRUNED TRIAL_DONE Same — outcome="pruned" in payload TRIAL_FAILED TRIAL_DONE Same — outcome="failed" in payload BACKTEST_TRIAL_DONE BACKTEST_DONE Follow {OPERATION}_DONE convention DATA_PROCESSED_DONE DATA_PROCESSING_DONE Follow {OPERATION}_DONE convention LIVE_EXEC_SUMMARY LIVE_TRADING_PROGRESS Was the heartbeat — now follows naming convention LIVE_TRADING_STOPPED LIVE_TRADING_DONE Terminal event must be _DONE LIVE_ANOMALY_DETECTED LIVE_TRADING_ANOMALY_DETECTED Must share the LIVE_TRADING_ prefix

docs.title: Renames in docs only (these names exist in EVENTS.md/MASTER-PLAN.md but not in code)
docs.text: Doc-only name Correct name BACKTEST_COMPLETE BACKTEST_DONE DATA_FETCH_COMPLETE DATA_FETCH_DONE LABELING_COMPLETE LABELS_DONE TRAINING_COMPLETE TRAINING_DONE TRIAL_SUMMARY_READY removed (was never real)

docs.title: 3. Deletions from code (events in EventType enum today that will be removed)
docs.text: Event to delete Why TRIAL_TRAINING_DONE Dead code — never emitted. Covered by TRIAL_DONE TRIAL_COMPLETED Replaced by TRIAL_DONE (outcome="completed") TRIAL_PRUNED Replaced by TRIAL_DONE (outcome="pruned") TRIAL_FAILED Replaced by TRIAL_DONE (outcome="failed") TRIAL_CANCELLED Folded into TRIAL_DONE (outcome="cancelled") TRIAL_STOPPING No real stop flow exists — removed ALL_TRIALS_DONE Renamed to PHASE_DONE BACKTEST_TRIAL_DONE Renamed to BACKTEST_DONE DATA_PROCESSED Legacy/deprecated — replaced by DATA_PROCESSING_DONE DATA_PROCESSED_DONE Renamed to DATA_PROCESSING_DONE DATA_PIPELINE_REQUESTED Dead code — never emitted, redundant concept DATA_PIPELINE_DONE Dead code — never emitted, redundant concept LIVE_EXEC_SUMMARY Renamed to LIVE_TRADING_PROGRESS LIVE_TRADING_STOPPED Renamed to LIVE_TRADING_DONE LIVE_ANOMALY_DETECTED Renamed to LIVE_TRADING_ANOMALY_DETECTED TRIAL_SCORED No runtime emitter — ranking belongs in DB, not events TRIAL_CONFIG_READY No requester/consumer contract ANALYST_TRIAL_SUMMARY_READY No emitter callsite — future artifact flow ANALYST_EXPERIMENT_SUMMARY_READY No emitter callsite — future artifact flow EXPERIMENT_STOPPING Too vague — stop flows must be domain-specific EXPERIMENT_STOPPED Too vague — same reason EXECUTION_PLAN_CREATED Artifact event — no lifecycle contract EXPERIMENT_PLAN_CREATED Artifact event — no lifecycle contract MASTER_EXEC_PLAN_UPDATED No emitter — no lifecycle contract HOUSEKEEPING_DONE No REQUESTED/STARTED/PROGRESS flow — incomplete lifecycle

docs.title: Integración de análisis de sentimiento en Rainbow Trader
Supuestos y alcance
docs.text:
Asumo que Rainbow Trader está implementado en Python, que no hay una nube definida (objetivo: despliegue flexible on‑prem o en cloud), y que el sistema opera un pipeline automatizado de trading con ingestión continua de señales. También asumo que el análisis de sentimiento se usará como feature (o conjunto de features) para modelos predictivos y/o reglas de ejecución, y que el sistema requiere baja latencia y trazabilidad.

A lo largo del informe separo: (a) cómputo de sentimiento (modelo), (b) fuentes de texto (datasets/APIs), (c) operacionalización (streaming, serving, despliegue) y (d) validación (métricas, backtesting, controles).

docs.title: Resumen ejecutivo
docs.text:
El camino más directo para incorporar sentimiento con coste bajo y control de licencias es un enfoque por capas: modelo financiero “ligero” para inferencia continua (p. ej., FinBERT) + modelo LLM financiero solo para tareas de mayor valor (resumen, extracción de “drivers” y calibración), alimentados por un bus de eventos. citeturn1view0turn2view0turn30search11turn14search0

Para señales en tiempo real, la decisión dominante es qué fuentes puedes usar sin bloquearte por licencias o términos: varias fuentes sociales tienen restricciones fuertes para scraping y para entrenamiento, y conviene diseñar el sistema para almacenar IDs y metadatos, no redistribuir contenido completo, y desacoplar ingestión de inferencia para controlar coste y latencia. citeturn13search4turn9view3turn11view0

En open‑source, la prioridad práctica es: - FinBERT (ProsusAI) para clasificación rápida y estable en CPU/GPU, con licencia permisiva. citeturn1view0turn2view0 - FinGPT (AI4Finance) para sentimiento con LLM y para tareas auxiliares (resumen/explicación), con mantenimiento activo y licencia MIT. citeturn30search11turn14search3turn15view0 - PIXIU / FinMA (The‑FinAI) para una base LLM financiera y benchmarks abiertos; útil si el proyecto quiere un backbone LLM propio. citeturn14search0turn14search10turn14search14

En datos, si necesitas “news” con cobertura global y sin fricción de licencias, GDELT suele ser el punto de arranque; para datasets etiquetados, Financial PhraseBank, FiQA y el dataset de sentimiento de FinGPT cubren entrenamiento y evaluación, aunque con licencias distintas (varias no permiten uso comercial). citeturn6search0turn17view0turn15view0turn6search8

En comercial, para acelerar prototipos: Alpha Vantage da un punto de entrada con límites y precios publicados; EODHD ofrece “news + sentiment score” en planes desde el rango bajo; Quartr aporta material de investor relations y límites públicos; y sec‑api.io puede facilitar ingestión de filings a escala (útil para sentimiento en reportes). citeturn19view1turn20search12turn25view2turn26view1turn18search2

docs.title: Tabla comparativa de opciones recomendadas
docs.text:
La tabla enfatiza: framework, licencia, señales de mantenimiento, y encaje en pipelines de trading (latencia/coste). Donde la fuente pública no publica métricas de latencia o accuracy de forma comparable, lo marco como “medir en tu entorno”.

Prioridad Repo / Modelo Tipo Framework / stack Licencia Madurez y mantenimiento Encaje en trading Fuente 1 ProsusAI/finBERT Modelo finanzas (BERT) PyTorch + BERT Apache‑2.0 Repo estable; commits históricos (último commit visible 2022) Inferencia rápida; buen baseline; fácil ONNX/quant citeturn1view0turn2view0 2 AI4Finance‑Foundation/FinGPT LLM + pipeline de sentimiento PyTorch; LoRA; datasets HF MIT Proyecto activo; listado org con actividad 2026 Mejor para tasks de alto valor; coste GPU; usar por lotes/micro‑batch citeturn30search11turn14search3turn15view0 3 The‑FinAI/PIXIU + FinMA LLM financiero + benchmark LLaMA‑family fine‑tune; HF models MIT Repo con enfoque benchmark + modelos Útil si quieres backbone LLM propio y evaluación formal citeturn14search0turn14search10turn14search14 4 AI4Finance‑Foundation/FinNLP Ingestión/corpus financiero Python pipelines MIT Repo con pipelines de datos Soporte de data‑ops para señales (news/social) citeturn30search3turn30search5turn0search14 5 HuggingFace Transformers Runtime NLP Python; PyTorch/TF Apache‑2.0 Estándar de facto Base para serving; integra con ONNX/vLLM citeturn32search0turn32search8 6 vLLM Serving LLM Python serving (Apache‑style, ver LICENSE) Repo y docs activos Útil si FinGPT/FinMA se sirven como LLM; medir latencia citeturn32search2turn32search6turn32search30 7 BentoML Serving modelos Python; APIs Apache‑2.0 Proyecto activo Empaquetado/serving reproducible; útil on‑prem/cloud citeturn32search1turn32search5turn32search13 8 Feast Feature store Python Apache‑2.0 Proyecto activo; docs 2026 Mantiene consistencia offline/online (evita skew) citeturn32search3turn32search7turn32search27turn32search31

docs.title: Notas de selección y patrón recomendado
docs.text:
Baseline operativo (recomendado): comenzar con FinBERT para scoring (positivo/neutral/negativo) y, si el proyecto necesita extracción de “drivers” (tema, entidad, riesgo, guidance), añadir FinGPT/FinMA como segundo nivel con inferencia condicionada (solo ante eventos relevantes). citeturn1view0turn14search3turn14search14

Observación de mantenimiento: ProsusAI/finBERT muestra estabilidad con menor movimiento reciente; FinGPT aparece con actividad reciente en la organización; en producción esto suele traducirse en menor riesgo técnico en el baseline y mayor velocidad de iteración en el LLM. citeturn2view0turn30search11

docs.title: “Sample code” mínimo por modelo (Python)
docs.text:
FinBERT (Transformers):

from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForSequenceClassification
import torch

MODEL_ID = "ProsusAI/finbert"  # weights en Hugging Face; revisar licencia y modelo card
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(MODEL_ID)
model = AutoModelForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained(MODEL_ID)

LABELS = ["negative", "neutral", "positive"]

def finbert_score(text: str) -> dict:
    inputs = tokenizer(text, return_tensors="pt", truncation=True, max_length=256)
    with torch.no_grad():
        logits = model(**inputs).logits[0]
        probs = torch.softmax(logits, dim=-1).tolist()
    return {"label": LABELS[int(torch.argmax(logits))], "probs": dict(zip(LABELS, probs))}

FinGPT dataset para fine‑tuning/validación (HF Datasets):

from datasets import load_dataset

ds = load_dataset("flwrlabs/fingpt-sentiment-train")  # licencia MIT en el dataset card
# columnas: instruction, input, output
row = ds["train"][0]
print(row["input"], row["output"])

citeturn15view0

docs.title: Observaciones de licencias y “data hygiene”
docs.text:
• Varios datasets académicos clave restringen uso comercial (ej., PhraseBank CC BY‑NC‑SA; algunas variantes de FiQA aparecen como CC BY‑NC en repositorios derivados). citeturn6search0turn16view0
  • En earnings calls, aunque existan “dataset cards” con licencias permisivas, el contenido puede derivar de fuentes con derechos; conviene mantener un análisis legal propio y preferir material de investor relations con permisos explícitos o datos de fuentes que publiquen términos claros. citeturn27view2turn18search29

docs.title: Arquitecturas de integración para pipelines de trading y streaming
Patrón de componentes
docs.text: 1) Ingestión: conectores por fuente (news, filings, social, transcripts). 2) Normalización: limpieza, idioma, deduplicación, extracción de entidades (ticker/empresa), hashing para idempotencia. 3) Scoring: modelo de sentimiento (FinBERT) en servicio de baja latencia; LLM financiero (FinGPT/FinMA) como servicio condicional. citeturn1view0turn14search3turn14search0 4) Feature store: persistir features con timestamps y claves (ticker, fuente, evento). citeturn32search27turn32search3 5) Estrategia: modelo de “alpha/forecast” y motor de ejecución; consumir features con ventanas temporales (p. ej., EWMA de sentimiento). 6) Observabilidad: latencia por etapa, drift del sentimiento, distribución por fuente, auditoría de decisiones.

docs.title: Código (Python): ejemplo de “sentiment microservice” en streaming
docs.text:
El siguiente patrón asume un bus (p. ej., Kafka/Redis Streams) y separa ingestión de inferencia. El objetivo es idempotencia (clave por evento) y control de latencia.

import json
import time
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Any, Dict

from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForSequenceClassification
import torch

MODEL_ID = "ProsusAI/finbert"
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(MODEL_ID)
model = AutoModelForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained(MODEL_ID)
LABELS = ["negative", "neutral", "positive"]

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class TextEvent:
    event_id: str
    ts_unix_ms: int
    symbol: str
    source: str
    text: str

def score_finbert(text: str) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    inputs = tokenizer(text, return_tensors="pt", truncation=True, max_length=256)
    with torch.no_grad():
        logits = model(**inputs).logits[0]
        probs = torch.softmax(logits, dim=-1)
        idx = int(torch.argmax(probs))
    return {
        "label": LABELS[idx],
        "p_negative": float(probs[0]),
        "p_neutral": float(probs[1]),
        "p_positive": float(probs[2]),
    }

def process_event(evt: TextEvent) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    s = score_finbert(evt.text)
    return {
        "event_id": evt.event_id,
        "ts_unix_ms": evt.ts_unix_ms,
        "symbol": evt.symbol,
        "source": evt.source,
        "sentiment": s,
        "model": {"id": MODEL_ID, "type": "finbert"},
        "processed_at_ms": int(time.time() * 1000),
    }

# Nota: integrar aquí tu consumer/producer (Kafka, Redis Streams, NATS).
# Recomendación: commit offset SOLO tras persistir resultado (feature store / TSDB).

citeturn1view0

docs.title: Diseño para latencia
docs.text:
• Modelo: correr FinBERT en CPU con batch pequeño (micro‑batch) suele ser estable; reservar GPU para LLM o para ráfagas.
  • Caching: reutilizar scoring para textos duplicados (hash + ventana temporal), alineado con deduplicación de la fuente (ej. X documenta deduplicación de cobro; tú puedes deduplicar para cómputo). citeturn11view0
  • Degradación controlada: si la cola crece, bajar granularidad (p. ej., solo titulares vs artículo completo) o activar “sampling”.

docs.title: Evaluación, métricas y procedimientos de prueba
Métricas de NLP
docs.text:
Para clasificación discreta: accuracy, macro‑F1 y calibración (ECE/Brier). Para score continuo (FiQA), correlación y MAE respecto a score objetivo. FiQA publica un score de sentimiento continuo en su dataset card, lo que permite diseñar umbrales y “bucketización” de señal. citeturn17view0

Para modelos tipo FinGPT, los trabajos y repos suelen reportar F1 medio en sub‑datasets de sentimiento; el propio material de benchmark describe agregación por mean F1 para SA. citeturn14search13

docs.title: Métricas “finance‑first” (validación de señal)
docs.text:
El objetivo en trading no es solo “acertar etiqueta”, sino aportar señal con control de riesgos. Un procedimiento mínimo:

1) Alineación temporal: timestamp del texto vs timestamp de mercado (evitar look‑ahead). 2) Event study: medir retorno y volatilidad post‑evento por buckets de sentimiento (p. ej., +30m, +1d). 3) Information Coefficient (IC): correlación entre sentiment feature y retorno futuro en ventanas; reportar IC medio y t‑stat por walk‑forward. 4) Backtest: comparar estrategia base vs con sentimiento, con comisiones, slippage y límites de liquidez. 5) Robustez: sensibilidad por fuente (news vs social), por régimen (alta volatilidad vs baja), por sector y por idioma.

Cuando uses proveedores que construyen índices/factores con sentimiento (ej. metodología del índice de sentimiento basado en conteos pos/neg y ventana móvil), puedes contrastar tu feature engineering con ese tipo de definiciones para pruebas de consistencia. citeturn20search5

docs.title: Procedimiento de test (mínimo) para producción
docs.text:
• Golden set: 1–2k textos por fuente (news/social/filings) con etiquetado manual interno (3 anotadores, acuerdo).
  • Drift: monitor de distribución de longitudes, idioma, entidades, y distribución de probas (p_positive, etc.).
  • Latencia: p50/p95 por etapa (ingest → score → persist → consumo por modelo).
  • Replay: reproducir una semana histórica completa con el mismo pipeline (misma lógica de dedup y ventanas).

docs.title: Riesgos, cumplimiento y consideraciones de licencias
Riesgo de abuso de mercado y manipulación
docs.text:
Si el sistema genera o amplifica señales basadas en contenido público, el riesgo no es solo “accuracy”, sino impacto de mercado y cumplimiento interno. En la UE, el marco de abuso de mercado define prohibiciones y conceptos (incluida manipulación) y existen documentos regulatorios y técnicos relacionados; en España la CNMV concentra recursos y referencias sobre abuso de mercado y regula/reporting (STOR). citeturn31search9turn31search0turn31search8turn31search24

Medidas prácticas: - Aislar el pipeline de sentimiento de cualquier componente que publique contenido o ejecute acciones que puedan interpretarse como “señales públicas”. - Mantener audit trail: texto fuente → score → feature → decisión → orden. - Controlar “feedback loops” (sentimiento generado por tu propio output).

docs.title: Privacidad, datos personales y anonimización
docs.text:
Para texto social, puede existir información personal. Las guías de la AEPD tratan la anonimización como proceso basado en riesgo y definen objetivos y límites; en un sistema de trading, esto se traduce en minimizar retención de contenido y aplicar anonimización/pseudonimización cuando sea posible. citeturn31search6turn31search2

En la UE/España, el RGPD está publicado en BOE (versión DOUE‑L); para un pipeline de NLP conviene evaluar base legal, minimización, retención y medidas técnicas. citeturn31search15

docs.title: Términos de plataformas: X y Reddit
docs.text:
• La guía de desarrolladores de X incluye restricciones explícitas (p. ej., prohibición de scraping y restricciones relacionadas con AI/ML training); esto impacta tu estrategia de dataset si pretendes entrenar con contenido de esa plataforma. citeturn13search4turn11view0
  • Reddit establece términos para su Data API; para entrenamiento, el texto indica restricciones sobre uso de contenido para entrenar modelos sin permisos. citeturn9view3

Diseño recomendado: - Almacenar solo IDs y features derivados (scores agregados), en lugar de texto completo. - Diseñar “data deletion compliance”: si una fuente exige borrado/actualización, tu sistema debe poder purgar.

docs.title: Licencias de modelos y repositorios
docs.text:
• FinBERT usa una licencia permisiva (Apache‑2.0) en repo. citeturn1view0
  • FinGPT y datasets asociados aparecen con licencia MIT en listados y dataset cards. citeturn30search11turn15view0
  • Transformers, vLLM, BentoML y Feast publican licencias tipo Apache‑2.0 en sus repos. citeturn32search0turn32search2turn32search5turn32search3

En Hugging Face, la licencia puede declararse en metadata (“model card” / “dataset card”); eso reduce fricción, pero no sustituye revisar derechos upstream de datos. citeturn32search8

docs.title: Próximos pasos y hoja de ruta de tres meses
Próximos pasos inmediatos
docs.text: 1) Definir universo de instrumentos y fuentes textuales por instrumento (por ejemplo, news global + filings + social limitado). 2) Seleccionar 2 modelos: FinBERT como baseline y FinGPT/FinMA como segundo nivel. citeturn1view0turn30search11turn14search0 3) Diseñar el “contract” de evento y feature (schemas), con timestamps y versionado. 4) Montar un entorno de evaluación: FiQA + PhraseBank + FinGPT dataset como base, y un golden set propio. citeturn17view0turn6search0turn15view0

docs.text:
Mes 1 (prototipo funcional): scoring FinBERT en streaming, persistencia de features, y evaluación inicial con FiQA/PhraseBank/FinGPT dataset. citeturn1view0turn17view0turn6search0turn15view0

Mes 2 (validación y robustez): backtests por régimen, análisis por fuente, calibración y control de latencia; aplicar deduplicación y políticas de retención. citeturn11view0turn17view0

docs.title: Loss, Scoring, Training & Optuna — Who Uses What and When
The Confusion
docs.text:
There are multiple "evaluation" concepts that overlap:

  • Loss function (FocalLoss / CrossEntropyLoss)
  • Eval metrics (val_loss, accuracy, f1, precision, recall)
  • Training objective (weighted combo of eval metrics — for pruning)
  • Stage objective (3-stage weighted combo — final trial score)

This doc clarifies exactly what uses what, when, and on which data.

docs.title: 1. Training Loop (per batch, per epoch)
docs.text:
File: model.py → model_train()

What happens per batch:

for batch in train_loader:
    outputs = model(batch_features)                          # forward pass
    loss = criterion(outputs, targets)                       # compute loss
    loss.backward()                                          # backpropagation
    optimizer.step()                                         # update weights

What is criterion?

MultiOutputCriterion — a weighted sum of 3 losses:

total_loss = w_cls * classification_loss(logits, labels)     # FocalLoss or CrossEntropyLoss
           + w_str * MSELoss(pred_strength, true_strength)   # regression
           + w_qua * MSELoss(pred_quality, true_quality)     # regression

What is classification_loss?

Created by loss_factory.py → create_classification_loss():

  • "cross_entropy" → nn.CrossEntropyLoss(weight=class_weights, label_smoothing=X)
  • "focal" → FocalLoss(gamma=X, weight=class_weights)

Data used: Training set only (rows 0 → train_end_row).

Output: train_loss = average of total_loss across all batches in the epoch.

Purpose: This is what makes the model learn. Backpropagation adjusts weights to minimize this loss.

docs.title: 2. Validation Evaluation (per epoch, after training)
docs.text:
File: model.py → model_evaluate()

What happens:

with torch.no_grad():                                        # no backpropagation
    for batch in val_loader:
        outputs = model(batch_features)
        loss = criterion(outputs, targets)                   # same criterion as training
        predictions = argmax(logits)                         # class predictions
        # accumulate: loss, correct, labels, predictions

After all batches:

val_loss     = average loss across all val batches
accuracy     = correct / total
f1_macro     = f1_score(labels, predictions, labels=[1,2])   # LONG/SHORT only, ignores NONE
precision    = precision_score(labels, predictions, labels=[1,2])
recall       = recall_score(labels, predictions, labels=[1,2])

Data used: Validation set only (rows train_end_row → val_end_row).

Output: EvalMetrics(val_loss, accuracy, f1_macro, precision, recall)

Purpose: See how the model performs on unseen data. NO weight updates happen here.

Important: val_loss uses the SAME MultiOutputCriterion as training (same FocalLoss/CrossEntropy + MSE for strength/quality). The metrics (f1, precision, recall) are computed SEPARATELY using sklearn — they have nothing to do with the loss function.

docs.title: 3. Optuna Pruning (per epoch)
docs.text:
File: model_training.py (training loop) + optuna_scoring.py → compute_training_objective()

What happens after each epoch:

eval_metrics = model_evaluate(model, val_loader, criterion)  # step 2 above
epoch_objective = compute_training_objective(eval_metrics, scoring_config)
trial.report(epoch_objective, step=epoch)                    # report to Optuna
if trial.should_prune():
    raise TrialPruned                                        # kill bad trials early

What is compute_training_objective()?

A weighted sum of the validation eval metrics:

objective = w_accuracy * accuracy
          + w_val_loss * val_loss      # note: weight is NEGATIVE (minimize)
          + w_f1       * f1_macro
          + w_precision * precision
          + w_recall   * recall

Weights come from scoring_config.training_weights.

Data used: Validation set (same as step 2).

Purpose: Give Optuna a single number per epoch to decide if the trial is worth continuing. This is a CHEAP metric (no backtest needed). Optuna's pruner compares this across trials to kill underperformers early.

This is NOT the final trial score. It's just for pruning decisions during training.

docs.title: 3.1. Best Epoch Tracking & Early Stopping (per epoch)
docs.text:
File: model_training.py (training loop)

What happens after each epoch (same epoch_objective from step 3):

epoch_objective = compute_training_objective(eval_metrics, scoring_config)

# Check if this epoch improved over the best so far
improved = best_epoch_objective is None or (
    epoch_objective > best_epoch_objective    # direction = "maximize" in scoring.yaml
)

if improved:
    best_epoch_objective = epoch_objective    # new best score
    best_epoch = epoch                        # remember this epoch
    # Snapshot all metrics at this epoch for logging
    best_epoch_train_loss = train_loss
    best_epoch_val_loss = val_loss
    best_epoch_val_accuracy = val_accuracy
    best_epoch_f1_macro = f1_macro
    best_epoch_recall = recall
    best_epoch_precision = precision
    patience_left = early_stopping_patience   # reset patience
    epochs_since_improvement = 0
else:
    patience_left -= 1
    epochs_since_improvement += 1

What determines best_epoch?

The training objective — the SAME weighted combination of validation metrics used for Optuna pruning (step 3 above). With the current scoring.yaml:

objective = +0.15 * accuracy
          - 0.15 * val_loss         # negative because direction=minimize
          + 0.30 * f1_macro
          + 0.20 * precision
          + 0.20 * recall

best_epoch = the epoch where this objective was highest (direction = "maximize").

It is NOT just val_loss. It's a weighted combination of ALL 5 validation metrics.

Data used: Validation set (same as steps 2 and 3).

Purpose: - Early stopping: When patience_left reaches 0, training stops (the model has not improved for N epochs). - Checkpoint selection: The model weights from best_epoch are saved as is_best=True — this is the model used for the final 3-stage evaluation. - Logging: best_val_loss and best_val_acc in the progress bar show the metrics AT the best epoch, not the current epoch's metrics.

Important: epochs_since_improve in the training log shows how many epochs since the training objective last improved. When this keeps growing, the model is overfitting and the val metrics are degrading.

docs.title: 4. Final Trial Score — 3-Stage Objective (once, after training completes)
docs.text:
File: optuna_scoring.py → compute_stage_results() + compute_stage_objective()

When: After all epochs finish (or early stopping triggers), ONCE per trial.

docs.title: Stage 1: Re-evaluate on Training Data
docs.text:
stage1_metrics = model_evaluate(model, train_loader, criterion)
stage1_result  = compute_training_objective(stage1_metrics, scoring_config)
  • Same model_evaluate() as step 2, but on training data
  • Checks if the model learned the training patterns
  • Uses training_weights to produce a single score

docs.title: Stage 2: Re-evaluate on Validation Data
docs.text:
stage2_metrics = model_evaluate(model, val_loader, criterion)
stage2_result  = compute_training_objective(stage2_metrics, scoring_config)
  • Same as the per-epoch validation, but final snapshot
  • Uses training_weights to produce a single score

docs.title: Stage 3: Mini-Backtest on Tail Data
docs.text:
stage3_metrics = run_stage3_backtest_mini(model, tail_frame, ...)
stage3_result  = compute_stage3_value(stage3_metrics, scoring_config)
  • Runs the actual backtesting engine on tail data (after validation period)
  • Produces: return_pct, sharpe_ratio, win_rate, alpha, trades_count, etc.
  • compute_stage3_value() normalizes these to [0,1] range using log-sigmoid for returns, clipping for sharpe, etc.

docs.title: Final Objective
docs.text:
objective = w_stage1 * stage1_result
          + w_stage2 * stage2_result
          + w_stage3 * stage3_result

Weights come from scoring_config.stage_weights.

This IS the final trial score that Optuna uses to rank trials and select the best one.

docs.title: Summary Table
docs.text: What Function Data Loss fn used? Metrics used Purpose Training model_train() Train set ✅ MultiOutputCriterion (backprop) train_loss only Learn weights Val eval model_evaluate() Val set ✅ MultiOutputCriterion (no backprop) val_loss + f1/prec/recall See performance Pruning compute_training_objective() Val set (from eval) ❌ (uses eval metrics) Weighted combo of val metrics Kill bad trials early Best epoch compute_training_objective() Val set (from eval) ❌ (uses eval metrics) SAME weighted combo as pruning Early stop + checkpoint Stage 1 model_evaluate() Train set ✅ MultiOutputCriterion Same as val eval Final train score Stage 2 model_evaluate() Val set ✅ MultiOutputCriterion Same as val eval Final val score Stage 3 run_stage3_backtest_mini() Tail set ❌ (backtest engine) return%, sharpe, win_rate, alpha Final backtest score Final objective compute_stage_objective() All 3 stages ❌ (combines stage scores) Weighted combo of stages Rank trials

docs.title: Loss Function vs Scoring Metrics
docs.text:
• Loss function (FocalLoss/CrossEntropy): Used by PyTorch for backpropagation. Tells the optimizer HOW to adjust weights. Also used in model_evaluate() to compute val_loss.
  • Scoring metrics (f1, precision, recall): Computed by sklearn AFTER predictions. NOT used for backpropagation. Used to evaluate quality of predictions.

docs.title: train_loss vs val_loss
docs.text:
• train_loss: Average MultiOutputCriterion loss over training batches. Includes dropout, batch norm in training mode.
  • val_loss: Average MultiOutputCriterion loss over validation batches. No dropout, batch norm uses global stats. Can be lower than train_loss (especially with FocalLoss + dropout=0 + few samples).

docs.title: Epoch Objective vs Final Objective
docs.text:
• Epoch objective (compute_training_objective): Cheap, per-epoch, val metrics only. Used to kill bad trials early.
  • Final objective (compute_stage_objective): Expensive, once per trial, includes backtest. Used to rank trials.

docs.title: Why val_loss can be < train_loss with FocalLoss
docs.text: FocalLoss applies (1-p_t)^γ weighting. During training, the model sees harder examples (more diverse training set) → higher focal weights → higher loss. During validation, if the model predicts the majority class well, p_t is high → focal weight is low → lower loss. This is expected behavior, not a bug.

docs.title: Mathematical Models Integration Research
docs.text: Status: Pending Implementation Priority: Medium Created: 2026-03-06

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Rainbow-Trader is primarily an ML/data-driven trading system, not traditional quantitative finance. However, certain mathematical models from quant finance could enhance features, risk management, and regime detection.

docs.title: Key Distinction
docs.text:
• Quant Finance: Pricing, hedging, arbitrage (Black-Scholes, derivatives)
  • Rainbow-Trader: ML-based signal prediction, systematic trading, backtesting

docs.title: 1. GARCH (Volatility Modeling)
docs.text:
Priority: ⭐⭐⭐ High

Purpose: Better volatility estimation than simple rolling std

Model:

σₜ² = ω + α·εₜ₋₁² + β·σₜ₋₁²

Implementation:

  • Use arch library: arch_model(returns, vol='Garch', p=1, q=1)
  • Add as feature alongside ATR
  • Improve stop-loss/take-profit dynamic sizing

Benefits:

  • More accurate volatility prediction
  • Better risk management
  • Conditional volatility (adapts to market conditions)

docs.title: 2. Hidden Markov Models (Regime Detection)
docs.text:
Priority: ⭐⭐⭐ High

Purpose: Formalize regime detection (trending up/ranging/trending down)

Model:

  • 3 states: bullish trend, ranging, bearish trend
  • Transition probabilities between states
  • Observation probabilities (returns, volatility)

Implementation:

  • Use hmmlearn library: GaussianHMM(n_components=3)
  • Replace/enhance current scripts/analysis/regime/regime_detector.py
  • Adjust strategy parameters per regime

Benefits:

  • Systematic regime classification
  • Strategy adaptation (trend-following vs mean-reversion)
  • Better context for ML predictions

docs.title: 3. Kelly Criterion (Position Sizing)
docs.text:
Priority: ⭐⭐ Medium

Purpose: Optimal position sizing based on win rate and risk/reward

Formula:

f* = (p·b - q) / b

where:
p = win probability (from backtesting)
q = loss probability (1-p)
b = avg_win / avg_loss ratio

Implementation:

  • Calculate from backtesting metrics
  • Use half-Kelly (f*/2) for conservative sizing
  • Dynamic position sizing per trade

Benefits:

  • Mathematically optimal capital allocation
  • Prevents over-leveraging
  • Maximizes long-term growth rate

docs.title: 4. Kalman Filter (Signal Smoothing)
docs.text:
Priority: ⭐⭐ Medium

Purpose: Reduce noise in price/indicator signals

Implementation:

  • Use pykalman library
  • Apply to price series or indicator outputs
  • Smooth noisy signals before ML input

Benefits:

  • Better trend detection
  • Reduced false signals
  • Cleaner features for ML model

docs.title: 5. Value at Risk (VaR)
docs.text:
Priority: ⭐ Low

Purpose: Quantitative risk measurement

Methods:

  • Parametric: -μₚ + z_α · σₚ
  • Monte Carlo: simulate scenarios
  • Historical: percentile of past losses

Implementation:

  • Calculate portfolio VaR
  • Set risk limits
  • Complement existing stop-loss

Benefits:

  • Formal risk quantification
  • Portfolio-level risk management
  • Regulatory/reporting compliance (if needed)

docs.title: Black-Scholes
docs.text:
• Only for options pricing
  • Rainbow-Trader focuses on spot trading
  • Not applicable

docs.title: Arbitrage Models
docs.text:
• Require ultra-low latency (HFT)
  • Not Rainbow-Trader's focus

docs.title: Markowitz Portfolio Optimization
docs.text:
• Only useful if trading multiple assets simultaneously
  • Current focus is single-asset strategies

docs.title: Implementation Priority
docs.text:
1. GARCH - Quick win, direct feature improvement
  2. HMM Regime Detection - Already have foundation in scripts/analysis/regime/regime_detector.py
  3. Kelly Criterion - Simple formula, big impact on risk management
  4. Kalman Filter - If noise is a problem in signals
  5. VaR - Nice to have, not critical

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
arch>=5.3.0          # GARCH models
hmmlearn>=0.3.0      # Hidden Markov Models
pykalman>=0.9.5      # Kalman Filter

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• GARCH: Bollerslev (1986) - Generalized Autoregressive Conditional Heteroskedasticity
  • HMM: Rabiner (1989) - A Tutorial on Hidden Markov Models
  • Kelly Criterion: Kelly (1956) - A New Interpretation of Information Rate
  • Kalman Filter: Kalman (1960) - A New Approach to Linear Filtering

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Rainbow-Trader's ML approach is valid and powerful
  • These models are enhancements, not replacements
  • Keep data-driven philosophy
  • Avoid over-engineering with unnecessary quant finance complexity

docs.title: Parallel Execution Safety Audit
docs.text: Date: 2026-02-20 Scope: Complete audit of rainbow-trader codebase for parallel trial execution (parallel_trials > 1) Context: With parallel_trials=2, Optuna uses joblib threading — two trials run simultaneously in the same Python process (shared memory space)

docs.title: Executive Summary
docs.text:
Status: ✅ System appears mostly safe for parallel execution Critical Issues: 0 High Priority: 1 Medium Priority: 3 Low Priority: 2

Key Finding: The codebase was designed with parallel execution in mind. Most shared resources use proper DB transactions, memory calculations account for parallel_trials, and file I/O uses unique paths per trial.

docs.title: 1.1 ✅ SAFE: Optuna Study SQLite Database
docs.text:
• Location: Per-study Optuna SQLite (optuna_study.sqlite)
  • Access Pattern: Both threads write to same SQLite file via Optuna's RDBStorage
  • Safety: ✅ Optuna's RDBStorage handles SQLite locking internally — safe for threading
  • Evidence: Standard Optuna design for n_jobs > 1, widely used in production

docs.title: 1.2 ✅ SAFE: Metadata Database (rainbow_trader.sqlite)
docs.text:
• Location: database/rainbow_trader.sqlite
  • Access Pattern: Both threads read/write experiment metrics, trial records
  • Safety: ✅ SQLAlchemy + SQLite handle concurrent access with transaction locking
  • Evidence:
  • All writes use database.session.commit() with proper transactions
  • SQLite SERIALIZED mode (default) ensures write serialization
  • No raw SQL without transactions

docs.title: 1.3 ⚠️ MEDIUM: best_trial_id / best_score Callback State
docs.text:
• Location: model_trainer.py:861-878 (handle_trial_complete closure)
  • Issue: last_best_trial_id and last_best_score are nonlocal variables shared between threads
  • Current Behavior: python nonlocal last_study_counters, last_best_trial_id, last_best_score # Both threads may update these simultaneously
  • Race Condition: Thread A and B both complete trials, both call sync_all_counters(), both update last_best_trial_id
  • Impact: LOW — only used for logging in callback, final values come from DB query at end
  • Recommendation: Make last_* variables thread-local or use threading.Lock() for correctness
  • Priority: MEDIUM (cosmetic issue, no data corruption)

docs.title: 1.4 ✅ SAFE: Trial Record Creation
docs.text:
• Location: helpers/objective.py (trial creation via repository)
  • Safety: Each trial gets unique trial_id from DB autoincrement before any parallel work
  • Evidence: Trial creation happens in study.optimize() callback before objective() runs

docs.title: 1.5 ✅ SAFE: Experiment Metrics Sync
docs.text:
• Location: sync_all_counters() in model_trainer.py:914
  • Safety: Reads from DB, calculates totals, writes back — all in transaction
  • Evidence: SQLite transaction isolation prevents dirty reads/writes

docs.title: 2.1 ✅ SAFE: RAM Budget Division for Parallel Trials
docs.text:
• Location: data_lazy_parquet_dataset.py:350-366
  • Implementation: ```python parallel_trials = kwargs.get("parallel_trials", 1) effective_workers = max(num_workers, 1) total_resource_consumers = parallel_trials * effective_workers

# Divide available RAM by total consumers ram_budget_per_worker = available_ram_gb / total_resource_consumers `` - **Safety**: ✅ Correctly divides RAM across parallel trials - **Verification**: Log line showsparallel_trials=2reducesram_budget_per_worker_mb` by ~50%

docs.title: 2.2 ✅ SAFE: FAT→SMALL Calibration RAM Division
docs.text:
• Location: fat_windows.py:769-857
  • Implementation: Uses memory_limit=50% of total available RAM
  • Safety: ✅ Each trial calibrates independently based on current available RAM
  • Note: Both trials may calibrate simultaneously, both reading psutil.virtual_memory().available
  • This is SAFE because each uses a % of current available (which decreases as trials consume)
  • Worst case: slight over-allocation, but Python's GC + OS paging handle this

docs.title: 3.1 ✅ SAFE: Small Window Parquet Files
docs.text:
• Location: small_window_preparer.py
  • Path Pattern: data/parquets/small_windows/{symbol}/{timeframe}/{timestamp}_{hash1}_{hash2}/
  • Safety: ✅ Each trial gets unique timestamp + hash-based path
  • Evidence: Log shows trial 20 writes to 2026-02-20T14-32-36Z_6646e70d_4f53cda1, trial 21 to 2026-02-20T14-32-36Z_ef034a16_4f53cda1
  • No Collision Risk: Hash includes selected columns, window size — guaranteed unique per trial

docs.title: 3.2 ✅ SAFE: Checkpoint Files
docs.text:
• Location: model_training.py checkpoint save
  • Path Pattern: data/checkpoints/{study_name}/{suffix}/phase-{N}/trial_{trial_id}/checkpoint_epoch_{E}.pt
  • Safety: ✅ trial_id is unique per trial (from DB), no collisions possible

docs.title: 3.3 ✅ SAFE: Log Files
docs.text:
• Location: Structured logging via structlog
  • Pattern: Single log file per training run (shared across trials)
  • Safety: ✅ structlog with file handler is thread-safe (uses locks internally)
  • Evidence: Interleaved logs from trial 20 and 21 in same file, no corruption

docs.title: 3.4 ⚠️ LOW: Temporary Parquet Directory
docs.text:
• Location: data/parquets/.tmp/
  • Issue: Both trials may create temp dirs with same timestamp (resolution: 1 second)
  • Current Mitigation: Each temp path includes hash, making collisions unlikely
  • Recommendation: Add microsecond timestamp or random suffix to temp paths
  • Priority: LOW (collision probability ~0 with hash, no data loss even if collision)

docs.title: 4.1 ✅ SAFE: Small Window Cleanup
docs.text:
• Location: housekeeping.py:953 — runs after each trial completes
  • Pattern: Deletes worst-performing small windows, keeps best N
  • Safety: ✅ Cleanup triggered by trial completion callback (sequential, not parallel)
  • Evidence: Each trial cleanup is logged separately, no overlapping cleanup operations

docs.title: 4.2 ✅ SAFE: Parquet Lock Timeout
docs.text:
• Location: housekeeping.py parquet lock cleanup
  • Safety: Uses DB transaction to check/delete locks atomically
  • Note: Lock timeout is 10 hours — parallel trials won't trigger lock cleanup on each other's files

docs.title: 5.1 ✅ SAFE: Backtest Execution
docs.text:
• Location: backtesting-engine module
  • Pattern: Each trial runs independent backtest with own BacktestEngine instance
  • Safety: ✅ No shared state between instances
  • Evidence: All state (positions, equity, trades) is instance-local in BacktestState

docs.title: 5.2 ✅ SAFE: Prediction Generation
docs.text:
• Location: Model predictions during backtest
  • Pattern: Each trial loads its own model, generates own predictions
  • Safety: ✅ PyTorch models are instance-local, no global state

docs.title: 6.1 ✅ SAFE: Bot Decision Making
docs.text:
• Location: bot-logic module
  • Pattern: Pure functions, no global state
  • Safety: ✅ All bot logic is stateless or instance-based
  • Evidence: BotConfig passed per-trial, no shared accumulators

docs.title: 7.1 ✅ FIXED: torch.compile Conflict
docs.text:
• Issue: Multiple trials compiling simultaneously caused FX-trace conflict → NaN crashes
  • Fix: torch._dynamo.config.suppress_errors = True set globally before study.optimize()
  • Status: ✅ RESOLVED — trials now fall back to eager mode on conflict
  • Evidence: Log line 38 confirms flag is set, no more NaN failures

docs.title: H1. Worker Restart Counter Thread Safety
docs.text:
• Location: model_trainer.py:1016-1042
  • Code: python trials_completed_in_phase = 0 # LOCAL variable for batch_start in range(...): study.optimize(..., n_trials=batch_n_trials) trials_completed_in_phase += batch_n_trials # Increment after batch
  • Issue: trials_completed_in_phase is a local variable, not shared between threads
  • Analysis: This is actually SAFE because:
  • Counter only tracks progress within the main thread's loop
  • Each study.optimize() call with n_jobs=2 spawns 2 worker threads
  • Workers complete → main thread continues loop → increments counter
  • Counter is never accessed by worker threads
  • Verdict: ✅ SAFE (false alarm)

docs.title: M1. Callback Closure Variable Race (Already Documented Above)
M2. Potential SQLite Lock Contention Under Heavy Load
docs.text:
• Scenario: With parallel_trials=8+, many concurrent DB writes
  • Risk: SQLite may return SQLITE_BUSY under extreme contention
  • Mitigation: Optuna's RDBStorage has retry logic
  • Recommendation: Monitor for SQLITE_BUSY errors in logs if scaling beyond 4 parallel trials
  • Priority: MEDIUM (not an issue at parallel_trials=2)

docs.title: M3. Memory Calibration Timing Race
docs.text:
• Location: Both trials may run cache calibration simultaneously
  • Scenario:
  • Trial A starts calibration, reads available_ram=18GB
  • Trial B starts calibration 0.1s later, reads available_ram=17GB (A consumed 1GB)
  • Both calculate cache sizes based on slightly stale RAM readings
  • Impact: Minor over-allocation possible, but Python GC + OS handle it
  • Verdict: ACCEPTABLE — real-world RAM is dynamic, calibrations are estimates
  • Priority: MEDIUM (document as expected behavior)

docs.title: L1. Temp Directory Timestamp Collision (Already Documented Above)
L2. Progress Bar Interleaving
docs.text:
• Location: tqdm progress bars from multiple trials
  • Issue: Two trials show progress bars simultaneously → visual clutter in terminal
  • Impact: Cosmetic only, no data issues
  • Recommendation: Use position parameter in tqdm to stack progress bars vertically
  • Priority: LOW (UX improvement)

docs.title: Immediate Actions
docs.text: None required — system is safe for current parallel_trials=2 configuration.

docs.title: Short-Term Improvements
docs.text:
1. Add threading.Lock to callback closure (M1) — 5min fix for correctness
  2. Add microseconds to temp parquet paths (L1) — 2min fix for robustness
  3. Monitor SQLite contention if scaling (M2) — add SQLITE_BUSY error logging

docs.title: Long-Term Considerations
docs.text:
1. Process-based parallelism: If scaling to parallel_trials > 4, consider switching from threading to multiprocessing
  2. Benefit: True parallelism (GIL bypass), isolated memory
  3. Cost: No shared memory, higher overhead
  4. Distributed Optuna: For parallel_trials > 8, use Optuna's distributed mode with Redis/RDB storage
  5. Explicit progress bar management: Use tqdm(position=worker_id) for clean terminal output

docs.title: Testing Recommendations
docs.text:
1. Stress test with parallel_trials=4 on larger experiments (100+ trials)
  2. Monitor DB file size growth — ensure no lock timeout issues
  3. Profile RAM usage during peak parallel execution
  4. Verify checkpoint integrity after parallel runs (no corrupted .pt files)

docs.title: Conclusion
docs.text:
The codebase is remarkably well-designed for parallel execution. The original architecture correctly: - Uses per-trial unique file paths - Divides RAM budgets across workers - Relies on DB transaction isolation - Avoids global mutable state

The single critical fix (torch.compile suppress_errors) has been implemented. All other findings are minor improvements or documentation notes.

System is PRODUCTION-READY for parallel_trials=2.

docs.title: Current Problem
docs.text: Model performs well on Nov 2024 - Jan 2025 (WR 85%) but degrades to 47% WR by Aug 2025.

docs.title: Root Cause
docs.text: Over-aggressive signal prediction due to class weight configuration.

docs.title: Current Settings
docs.text:
{
  "training_none_ratio": 2.5,
  "validation_none_ratio": 3.5,
  "class_weights": "auto"  // Results in [0.5714, 1.0, 1.0]
}

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
none_weight = max(0.5, 1.0 / (1.0 + training_ratio * 0.3))
# With training_ratio = 2.5:
# none_weight = 0.5714

docs.title: Effect
docs.text:
1. Sampling reduces NONEs: 83% → 71% of data
  2. Class weights penalize NONE less: 0.57 vs 1.0 for signals
  3. Model learns to over-predict signals: Finds patterns where none exist

docs.title: Why It Works Initially Then Fails
docs.text:
Good Period (Nov 2024 - Jan 2025): - Trending market with clear signals - Over-prediction = catching real opportunities - High win rate because signals were genuine

Bad Period (Feb 2025 - Aug 2025): - Choppy/ranging market - Over-prediction = false signals in noise - Low win rate because trading on randomness

docs.title: Option 1: Increase NONE Weight (Recommended)
docs.text:
Change:

# Current formula
none_weight = max(0.5, 1.0 / (1.0 + training_ratio * 0.3))

# Proposed formula
none_weight = max(0.7, 1.0 / (1.0 + training_ratio * 0.2))
# With training_ratio = 2.5:
# none_weight = max(0.7, 1.0 / 1.5) = max(0.7, 0.667) = 0.7

# Result: [0.7, 1.0, 1.0] instead of [0.5714, 1.0, 1.0]

Pros: - Simple one-line change - Makes model more conservative - Better precision, fewer false signals

Cons: - May reduce recall (miss some real signals) - Need to retrain to see effect

docs.title: Option 2: INCREASE Sampling Ratio (Keep More NONEs)
docs.text:
Change:

{
  "training_none_ratio": 3.5,  // Was 2.5 (INCREASE to keep MORE NONEs)
  "validation_none_ratio": 4.5  // Was 3.5 (INCREASE to keep MORE NONEs)
}

Effect: - Keeps MORE NONEs in dataset - 71% → 78% NONE (vs current 71%) - Model sees MORE "do nothing" examples

Calculation:

# With 1000 LONG + 1000 SHORT = 2000 signals
# Current ratio 2.5: 2000 * 2.5 = 5000 NONEs → 71% NONE
# New ratio 3.5: 2000 * 3.5 = 7000 NONEs → 78% NONE

Pros: - Model learns when NOT to trade (more conservative) - Reduces false signals in choppy markets - May improve precision

Cons: - Requires re-labeling with new sampling - Longer training time (more data) - May reduce recall (miss some real signals) - May bias towards inaction

docs.title: Option 3: Manual Class Weights
docs.text:
Change:

{
  "class_weights": [0.8, 1.0, 1.0]  // Manual instead of "auto"
}

Pros: - Direct control - Easy to experiment - No code changes needed

Cons: - Requires experimentation - Optimal values unclear - May need tuning per experiment

docs.title: Option 4: Focal Loss
docs.text:
Replace cross-entropy loss with Focal Loss:

# Focal Loss automatically down-weights easy examples
# Focuses learning on hard-to-classify cases
# Good for imbalanced datasets

focal_loss = FocalLoss(alpha=[0.7, 1.0, 1.0], gamma=2.0)

Pros: - State-of-art for imbalanced classification - Adaptive weighting during training - Better than fixed class weights

Cons: - Requires implementing new loss function - More hyperparameters to tune - Harder to debug

docs.title: Recommendation
docs.text:
Start with Option 1 (increase NONE weight to 0.7):

  1. Quick test: Change formula and retrain one trial
  2. Validate: Check if precision improves without losing recall
  3. Compare: Stage2 F1 should increase from 11% to >30%
  4. Backtest: Stage3 should be more stable across time

If that doesn't work well enough, combine with Option 2 (increase sampling ratio to 3.5 to keep more NONE examples).

docs.title: Step 1: Modify Class Weight Calculation
docs.text:
File: modules/model-trainer/src/rainbow/model_trainer/helpers/data_preparation.py

def calculate_class_weights(*, sampling_config: SamplingConfig) -> torch.Tensor:
    training_ratio = float(sampling_config.training_none_ratio)
    if training_ratio < 0:
        raise ValueError(...)

    # OLD: none_weight = max(0.5, 1.0 / (1.0 + training_ratio * 0.3))
    # NEW: Make NONE weight higher (less penalization of NONE class)
    none_weight = max(0.7, 1.0 / (1.0 + training_ratio * 0.2))

    weights = torch.tensor([none_weight, 1.0, 1.0], dtype=torch.float32)
    return weights

docs.title: Step 2: Test on Single Trial
docs.text:
# Run one trial with new weights
# Compare metrics:
# - Stage1 (training): expect accuracy ~93% (vs current 95.8%)
# - Stage2 (validation): expect F1 >30% (vs current 11%)
# - Stage3 (backtest): expect more stable WR across time

docs.title: Step 3: Full Optuna Study
docs.text: If initial trial shows improvement, run full study with new weights.

docs.title: Before (Current)
docs.text:
• Stage1: 95.8% acc, 92.9% F1 ← Overfitting
  • Stage2: 70.4% acc, 11.2% F1 ← Terrible generalization
  • Stage3: WR 85% → 47% ← Degradation over time

docs.title: After (Expected)
docs.text:
• Stage1: 92-94% acc, 90% F1 ← Less overfitting
  • Stage2: 75-80% acc, 35-45% F1 ← Better generalization
  • Stage3: WR 75% → 65% ← More stable, conservative

==================================================================================================== REGIME VARIABLE EXPLANATIONS ====================================================================================================

── VARIABLE 1: TREND (based on ADX — Average Directional Index) ── ADX measures trend STRENGTH (0-100), not direction. Threshold: ADX >= 25.0 = TRENDING | ADX <= 20.0 = RANGING

TRENDING — Strong directional movement. Price moves decisively in one direction. ADX >= 25. Market has clear momentum. Good for trend-following strategies. Example: sustained rally or sustained sell-off.

NEUTRAL — Moderate trend. ADX between 20-25. Market is transitioning — could be starting a trend or losing one. Ambiguous environment.

RANGING — No clear trend. ADX <= 20. Price oscillates sideways within a range. Good for mean-reversion strategies. Bad for trend-following. Example: consolidation phase, low-conviction market.

── VARIABLE 2: VOLATILITY (based on ATR/Price ratio vs rolling average) ── ATR ratio = ATR / Close price (percentage volatility). Compared to 2016-bar rolling average (~1 week of 5m data). Threshold: > 1.2x avg = HIGH | < 0.8x avg = LOW

HIGH_VOL — Volatility is 20%+ above its recent average. Big candles, wide ranges. Often during: news events, liquidation cascades, breakouts, panic. Risk: stop-losses get hit more. Reward: bigger moves to capture.

NORMAL_VOL— Volatility is within ±20% of its average. Typical market conditions. Most predictable environment for models. Patterns hold better.

LOW_VOL — Volatility is 20%+ below its recent average. Small candles, tight range. Often before: breakouts (volatility squeeze). During: low-liquidity periods. Risk: false signals from noise. Reward: tighter stops possible.

── VARIABLE 3: DIRECTION (based on +DI/-DI crossover + EMA fast/slow alignment) ── +DI > -DI AND EMA(14) > EMA(50) = BULLISH -DI > +DI AND EMA(14) < EMA(50) = BEARISH Otherwise = NEUTRAL (DI and EMA disagree)

BULLISH — Both indicators confirm upward pressure. Buyers dominate. +DI > -DI (more upward directional movement) AND fast EMA above slow EMA (short-term price above long-term).

NEUTRAL — Mixed signals. DI says one thing, EMA says another. Transitional phase — often at trend reversals or in choppy conditions. Model predictions are least reliable here.

BEARISH — Both indicators confirm downward pressure. Sellers dominate. -DI > +DI (more downward directional movement) AND fast EMA below slow EMA (short-term price below long-term).

==================================================================================================== ALL 27 REGIME COMBINATIONS (Trend | Volatility | Direction) ====================================================================================================

  1. TRENDING | HIGH_VOL | BULLISH Strong rally with big moves. Parabolic uptrend. Best for long trend-following. Example: bull market breakout, post-halving rally.

  2. TRENDING | HIGH_VOL | NEUTRAL Strong trend with high volatility but unclear direction. Whipsaws within a trend. Dangerous — trend is strong but direction keeps flipping. High risk.

  3. TRENDING | HIGH_VOL | BEARISH Crash / strong sell-off. Capitulation, liquidation cascades. Fast downward moves with high volatility. Worst for longs. Best for shorts.

  4. TRENDING | NORMAL_VOL | BULLISH Healthy uptrend with normal volatility. Steady buying pressure. Most consistent regime for bullish strategies. Good risk/reward.

  5. TRENDING | NORMAL_VOL | NEUTRAL Market trending but direction mixed. Possibly transitioning from bull to bear or vice versa. Signals may be unreliable.

  6. TRENDING | NORMAL_VOL | BEARISH Steady downtrend with normal volatility. Controlled selling. Bearish strategies work well. Less panic than HIGH_VOL crash.

  7. TRENDING | LOW_VOL | BULLISH Quiet uptrend — grinding higher on low volume/volatility. Often seen in late-stage rallies or slow recovery. Low conviction.

  8. TRENDING | LOW_VOL | NEUTRAL Trend detected but barely moving. Could be ADX lagging from previous trend. Direction unclear. Wait for confirmation.

  9. TRENDING | LOW_VOL | BEARISH Slow bleed — grinding lower on low volatility. Silent selling. Often before a capitulation event or in bear market exhaustion.

  10. NEUTRAL | HIGH_VOL | BULLISH Transition zone with high volatility and bullish bias. Could be starting a new uptrend after a range break. Volatile entry zone.

  11. NEUTRAL | HIGH_VOL | NEUTRAL Maximum uncertainty — moderate trend, high volatility, no directional bias. Avoid trading. Market is chaotic and unpredictable.

  12. NEUTRAL | HIGH_VOL | BEARISH Transition zone with high volatility and bearish bias. Could be starting a new downtrend. Potential breakdown.

  13. NEUTRAL | NORMAL_VOL | BULLISH Market in limbo — slight bullish lean. Not enough conviction for trend. Might be early stage of a move or late stage of a range. Average conditions.

  14. NEUTRAL | NORMAL_VOL | NEUTRAL Completely neutral — no trend, normal volatility, no direction. Maximum mean-reversion territory. Oscillator strategies may work.

  15. NEUTRAL | NORMAL_VOL | BEARISH Market in limbo with slight bearish lean. Watch for trend development. Range-bound with downward bias. Cautious environment.

  16. NEUTRAL | LOW_VOL | BULLISH Low energy with slight upward bias. Quiet accumulation phase. Volatility squeeze — could precede a breakout. Watch bandwidth.

  17. NEUTRAL | LOW_VOL | NEUTRAL Dead market — no trend, no volatility, no direction. Waiting for catalyst. Common during holidays or between events.

  18. NEUTRAL | LOW_VOL | BEARISH Low energy with slight downward bias. Quiet distribution phase. Like accumulation but for sellers. May precede a breakdown.

  19. RANGING | HIGH_VOL | BULLISH Choppy with big moves and upward bias. Wide range-bound market. Oscillating violently but buyers slightly winning. Difficult to trade.

  20. RANGING | HIGH_VOL | NEUTRAL Maximum chop — big moves in both directions with no trend or bias. Stop-loss killer. Avoid or use very wide stops. Market is noisy.

  21. RANGING | HIGH_VOL | BEARISH Choppy with big moves and downward bias. Sellers edge but within range. Volatile sideways with a bearish lean. Dangerous for any directional bet.

  22. RANGING | NORMAL_VOL | BULLISH Sideways market with normal volatility and slight upward bias. Classic consolidation with accumulation. Mean-reversion works. Watch for breakout.

  23. RANGING | NORMAL_VOL | NEUTRAL Textbook sideways — no trend, normal vol, no bias. Pure range trading. Buy support, sell resistance. Wait for break of range.

  24. RANGING | NORMAL_VOL | BEARISH Sideways market with normal volatility and slight downward bias. Distribution phase. Range-bound with sellers gaining. Watch for breakdown.

  25. RANGING | LOW_VOL | BULLISH Quiet accumulation — tight range, low volatility, buyers collecting. Classic pre-breakout setup. Bollinger squeeze. Often leads to explosive move up.

  26. RANGING | LOW_VOL | NEUTRAL Absolute calm — tight range, low volatility, no bias. Compression before expansion. Watch for any catalyst. Patience required.

  27. RANGING | LOW_VOL | BEARISH Quiet distribution — tight range, low volatility, sellers distributing. Pre-breakdown pattern. Compression may resolve downward.

docs.title: Graph Orchestrator Config Flow - Debug Trace
Overview
docs.text: This document traces the complete flow of configuration loading and runtime context creation from CLI invocation through tool execution.

docs.title: 1. Configuration Loading
docs.text:
• Source: .env file with KEYS__OPENAI_API_KEY and other env vars
  • Loaded by: ctx.obj = { () in CLI
  • Result: config_obj dict with all keys at root level

docs.title: 2. Config Object Structure
docs.text:
config_obj = {
    'keys': {
        'openai_api_key': 'sk-proj-...',
        'binance_api_key': '...',
        'binance_api_secret': '...'
    },
    'graph_orchestrator': {
        'host': '127.0.0.1',
        'port': 2024,
        'keys': {  # This gets merged from root 'keys'
            'openai_api_key': 'sk-proj-...'
        },
        ...
    },
    'data': {...},
    'database': {...},
    ...
}

docs.title: 3. GraphOrchestratorConfig Creation
docs.text:
• When: In GraphOrchestrator.__init__() (log 17)
  • How: get_graph_orchestrator_config(config_obj=config_obj)
  • Result: GraphOrchestratorConfig instance with all nested configs including keys_config
  • Key point: BaseConfig.deep_merge() with skip_empty=True preserves root-level keys

docs.title: 4. RuntimeContext Creation
docs.text:
• When: When tools are executed (log 25)
  • Contains:
  • database: Database instance
  • event_repository: EventRepository instance
  • data_path: Resolved data folder path
  • repo_root: Project root path
  • graph_orchestrator_config: Full GraphOrchestratorConfig with keys_config
  • Usage: Tools access context.graph_orchestrator_config for config needs

docs.title: Critical Fix: BaseConfig.deep_merge()
docs.text:
The key fix that makes this work is in config.py line 128:

# BEFORE (broken):
if isinstance(field_data, dict):
    child_config_obj[child_section_key] = field_data  # Overwrites with empty dict!

# AFTER (fixed):
if isinstance(field_data, dict) and field_data:
    existing = child_config_obj.get(child_section_key, {})
    child_config_obj[child_section_key] = Config.deep_merge(
        existing, field_data, skip_empty=True
    )

This ensures that when KeysConfig is created as a child of GraphOrchestratorConfig: - Root-level config_obj['keys'] is preserved - DEFAULTS with None values don't overwrite existing values - Final result: keys_config.openai_api_key has the actual value from .env

docs.title: Verification Commands
docs.text:
# See all debug logs
python -m rainbow.graph_orchestrator invoke -m "test" 2>&1 | grep -E "^\[|debug.*\["

# See only CLI phase
python -m rainbow.graph_orchestrator invoke -m "test" 2>&1 | grep "^\[CLI\]"

# See GraphOrchestrator init phase
