docs.title: Understanding Model Metrics: A Practical Guide for Trading ML
docs.text: Author: Manuel Padilla Date: January 2026 Purpose: Demystify ML and trading metrics to make better model selection decisions

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
• Understanding Model Metrics: A Practical Guide for Trading ML
  • Table of Contents
  • Introduction: Why So Many Metrics?
  • Classification Metrics (ML Fundamentals)
    • Accuracy
    • Loss
    • Precision
    • Recall
    • F1 Score
  • Trading-Specific Metrics
    • PnL (Profit and Loss)
    • Return Percentage
    • Win Rate
    • Sharpe Ratio
  • The Three Stages: What They Mean
    • Stage 1: Training Metrics
    • Stage 2: Validation Metrics
    • Stage 3: Backtest Metrics (Mini-Backtest)
  • Real Examples from Rainbow Trader
    • Example 1: Trial 85 (THE BEST) ⭐
    • Example 2: Trial 88 (DECEPTIVE)
    • Example 3: Trial 35 (CONSERVATIVE)
  • Decision Framework: How to Pick the Best Model
    • Step 1: Filter by Trading Metrics (Stage 3)
    • Step 2: Rank by Risk-Adjusted Return
    • Step 3: Check for Overfitting
    • Step 4: Consider the Phase
    • Quick Decision Table
  • Common Pitfalls
    • 1. "High Accuracy = Good Model"
    • 2. "Lowest Loss = Best Model"
    • 3. "More Trades = More Profit"
    • 4. "Stage 1 Metrics Matter"
    • 5. "Ignore Sharpe if PnL is Positive"
  • Summary Cheat Sheet
  • Conclusion

docs.title: Introduction: Why So Many Metrics?
docs.text:
When training a trading model, you'll encounter dozens of numbers. It's overwhelming at first. But here's the key insight:

Different metrics measure different aspects of model quality.

Think of it like evaluating a car:

  • Speed (0-100 km/h time) → How fast can it go?
  • Fuel efficiency (km/L) → How economical is it?
  • Safety rating (1-5 stars) → How safe is it?
  • Reliability (years without repair) → How dependable is it?

A car with amazing speed but terrible safety isn't "the best car" - it depends on what you need. Same with ML models.

For trading, we care about:

  1. Does the model predict correctly? (ML metrics)
  2. Does it make money? (Trading metrics)
  3. Is the performance consistent? (Risk-adjusted metrics)

docs.title: Classification Metrics (ML Fundamentals)
docs.text:
Our model predicts one of three classes:

  • HOLD (0): Do nothing
  • LONG (1): Buy / Go long
  • SHORT (2): Sell / Go short

docs.title: Accuracy
docs.text:
What it measures: The percentage of predictions that were correct.

Accuracy = Correct Predictions / Total Predictions

Example:

  • Model made 100 predictions
  • 65 were correct (predicted LONG when price went up, etc.)
  • Accuracy = 65% = 0.65

Intuition: "If the model says something, how often is it right overall?"

⚠️ The Problem with Accuracy:

Imagine 90% of the time the market is "HOLD". A dumb model that always predicts HOLD would have 90% accuracy! But it's useless for trading.

This is called the class imbalance problem. That's why we need other metrics.

When to use: As a quick sanity check, but never as the only metric.

docs.title: Loss
docs.text:
What it measures: How "wrong" the model's confidence scores are. Lower is better.

Intuition: Think of it as "regret" or "error magnitude".

If model predicts:  LONG with 90% confidence
But reality was:    SHORT

→ High loss! The model was very confident AND very wrong.

Types of loss:

  • Training loss: Error on data the model learned from
  • Validation loss (val_loss): Error on unseen data ← This one matters more!

Key insight:

  • If training_loss is low but val_loss is high → Overfitting (memorized training data)
  • Both should be similar for a good model

Range: Technically 0 to infinity, but in practice:

  • < 0.5: Excellent
  • 0.5 - 1.0: Good
  • 1.0 - 1.5: Acceptable

  • 1.5: Poor

docs.title: Precision
docs.text:
What it measures: When the model predicts a positive class (LONG or SHORT), how often is it actually correct?

Precision = True Positives / (True Positives + False Positives)

Trading intuition: "Of all the trades the model recommended, how many were actually good?"

Example:

  • Model predicted LONG 20 times
  • 14 times the price actually went up (True Positives)
  • 6 times the price went down (False Positives)
  • Precision = 14/20 = 70%

High precision means: Fewer false alarms. When the model says "trade", you can trust it more.

Low precision means: Many false signals. The model recommends trades that don't work out.

Use case: You want high precision when the cost of a wrong trade is high (big position sizes, high fees).

docs.title: Recall
docs.text:
What it measures: Of all the actual opportunities in the market, how many did the model catch?

Recall = True Positives / (True Positives + False Negatives)

Trading intuition: "Of all the good trading opportunities that existed, how many did the model identify?"

Example:

  • There were 30 actual LONG opportunities in the data
  • Model correctly predicted 18 of them
  • Recall = 18/30 = 60%

High recall means: The model doesn't miss opportunities. It catches most of the good trades.

Low recall means: The model is too conservative. It misses many good trades.

Use case: You want high recall when missing opportunities is costly (trending markets, limited time windows).

docs.title: F1 Score
docs.text:
What it measures: The harmonic mean of Precision and Recall. A balanced view.

F1 = 2 × (Precision × Recall) / (Precision + Recall)

Why harmonic mean? Because it penalizes extremes. If precision is 100% but recall is 0%, F1 = 0 (not 50%).

Trading intuition: "Is the model both careful (precision) AND aggressive enough to catch opportunities (recall)?"

Example calculations:

Scenario Precision Recall F1 Score Balanced 70% 70% 70% Conservative 90% 30% 45% Aggressive 30% 90% 45% Perfect 100% 100% 100%

Range: 0 to 1 (or 0% to 100%)

  • 0.5+: Decent
  • 0.6+: Good
  • 0.7+: Very good
  • 0.8+: Excellent (rare in trading)

F1 Macro: The average F1 across all classes (HOLD, LONG, SHORT). This is what we typically use because it treats each class equally.

docs.title: Trading-Specific Metrics
docs.text: ML metrics tell us if the model predicts correctly, but trading metrics tell us if it makes money.

docs.title: PnL (Profit and Loss)
docs.text:
What it measures: Total money gained or lost.

PnL = Sum of all trade profits - Sum of all trade losses

Units: Dollars (or whatever currency)

Example:

  • Started with $10,000
  • Made 5 winning trades: +$200, +$150, +$300, +$100, +$250 = +$1,000
  • Made 3 losing trades: -$100, -$200, -$150 = -$450
  • PnL = +$550

Intuition: The bottom line. Did we make or lose money?

⚠️ Limitation: PnL alone doesn't tell you:

  • How risky were the trades?
  • Was it one lucky trade or consistent?
  • What was the starting capital?

docs.title: Return Percentage
docs.text:
What it measures: PnL as a percentage of starting capital.

Return % = (PnL / Starting Capital) × 100

Example:

  • PnL = +$550
  • Starting Capital = $10,000
  • Return = 5.5%

Why it matters: Allows comparison across different account sizes.

Making $1,000 on a $10,000 account (10%) is more impressive than making $1,000 on a $1,000,000 account (0.1%).

docs.title: Win Rate
docs.text:
What it measures: Percentage of trades that were profitable.

Win Rate = Winning Trades / Total Trades

Example:

  • 87 total trades
  • 48 were profitable
  • Win Rate = 48/87 = 55.2%

Intuition: "Out of every 10 trades, how many make money?"

Important ranges:

  • < 40%: Poor (losing more often than winning)
  • 40-50%: Acceptable (if winners are bigger than losers)
  • 50-60%: Good
  • 60%+: Excellent

⚠️ Win Rate Trap:

A 90% win rate sounds amazing, but consider:

  • 9 winning trades of $10 each = +$90
  • 1 losing trade of $100 = -$100
  • Net PnL = -$10 😱

This is why we also need the risk/reward ratio and Sharpe ratio.

docs.title: Sharpe Ratio
docs.text:
What it measures: Risk-adjusted return. How much return per unit of risk?

Sharpe Ratio = (Average Return - Risk-Free Rate) / Standard Deviation of Returns

In practice for trading:

Sharpe Ratio ≈ Mean(daily returns) / StdDev(daily returns) × √252

Intuition: "Am I being compensated enough for the risk I'm taking?"

Example:

  • Strategy A: +20% return with 40% volatility → Sharpe ≈ 0.5
  • Strategy B: +10% return with 5% volatility → Sharpe ≈ 2.0

Strategy B is actually BETTER on a risk-adjusted basis, even though raw return is lower.

Ranges: | Sharpe Ratio | Interpretation | |--------------|----------------| | < 0 | Losing money | | 0 - 0.5 | Poor | | 0.5 - 1.0 | Acceptable | | 1.0 - 2.0 | Good | | 2.0 - 3.0 | Very Good | | > 3.0 | Excellent (suspicious - check for overfitting) |

Why it's crucial:

  • A strategy with Sharpe > 1 is generally worth trading
  • A strategy with Sharpe < 0.5 is probably not worth the stress

docs.title: The Three Stages: What They Mean
docs.text: Rainbow Trader evaluates models in three stages:

docs.title: Stage 1: Training Metrics
docs.text:
Data used: The data the model learned from (training set).

What it tells you: "Can the model learn patterns at all?"

⚠️ Warning: Good Stage 1 metrics don't guarantee good real performance. The model might be memorizing, not learning.

What to look for:

  • val_loss should be decreasing
  • Accuracy should be reasonable (not 33% which is random for 3 classes)

docs.title: Stage 2: Validation Metrics
docs.text:
Data used: Data the model has NEVER seen during training.

What it tells you: "Can the model generalize to new data?"

This is the most important ML metric stage!

What to look for:

  • val_loss similar to Stage 1 (if much higher = overfitting)
  • F1 score as high as possible
  • Accuracy meaningfully above random (>40% for 3 classes)

docs.title: Stage 3: Backtest Metrics (Mini-Backtest)
docs.text:
Data used: A small "tail" of the validation period, simulating real trading.

What it tells you: "Would this actually make money?"

This is what matters most for real trading!

What to look for:

  • Positive PnL (obviously)
  • Win Rate > 40% (consistency)
  • Sharpe Ratio > 0.5 (risk-adjusted performance)
  • Reasonable trade count (not just 2 lucky trades)

docs.title: Real Examples from Rainbow Trader
docs.text: Let's analyze actual trials from our training:

docs.title: Example 1: Trial 85 (THE BEST) ⭐
docs.text:
Stage 1 (Training):
  val_loss: 1.00
  accuracy: 0.52
  f1_macro: 0.40

Stage 2 (Validation):
  val_loss: 1.30
  accuracy: 0.37
  f1_macro: 0.34

Stage 3 (Backtest):
  PnL: +$1,528.67
  Return: +15.29%
  Win Rate: 55.2%
  Trades: 87
  Sharpe: 1.69

Analysis:

  • ML metrics are mediocre (accuracy 37%, F1 0.34)
  • BUT trading metrics are excellent!
  • Positive PnL ✓
  • Win Rate > 50% ✓
  • Sharpe > 1 ✓
  • Many trades (87) → statistically significant

Verdict: The model isn't "smart" by ML standards, but it makes money consistently. This is what we want!

docs.title: Example 2: Trial 88 (DECEPTIVE)
docs.text:
Stage 1 (Training):
  val_loss: 0.95
  accuracy: 0.54
  f1_macro: 0.45

Stage 2 (Validation):
  val_loss: 1.10
  accuracy: 0.40
  f1_macro: 0.37  ← Best F1!

Stage 3 (Backtest):
  PnL: -$759.89
  Return: -7.60%
  Win Rate: 44.4%
  Trades: 9
  Sharpe: -2.50

Analysis:

  • ML metrics look BETTER than Trial 85
  • But trading metrics are terrible
  • Negative PnL ✗
  • Negative Sharpe ✗
  • Only 9 trades → not statistically significant

Verdict: Good at "predicting" but bad at making money. The predictions might be correct but at wrong times or with wrong sizing.

docs.title: Example 3: Trial 35 (CONSERVATIVE)
docs.text:
Stage 3 (Backtest):
  PnL: +$315.21
  Return: +3.15%
  Win Rate: 61.8%
  Trades: 55
  Sharpe: 0.82

Analysis:

  • Lower return than Trial 85
  • But highest win rate (61.8%)
  • Good for someone who hates losing trades

Verdict: Consistent but not as profitable. Good as a secondary model.

docs.title: Decision Framework: How to Pick the Best Model
Step 1: Filter by Trading Metrics (Stage 3)
docs.text:
Eliminate any trial that:

  • ❌ Has negative PnL
  • ❌ Has less than 20 trades (not statistically significant)
  • ❌ Has Sharpe < 0

docs.title: Step 2: Rank by Risk-Adjusted Return
docs.text:
Among surviving trials, prioritize:

  1. Sharpe Ratio (>1 is ideal)
  2. Win Rate (>50% is ideal)
  3. PnL / Return %

docs.title: Step 3: Check for Overfitting
docs.text:
Compare Stage 1 and Stage 2 metrics:

  • If Stage 2 is MUCH worse → model is overfitting
  • If Stage 2 is similar → model generalizes well

docs.title: Step 4: Consider the Phase
docs.text:
Higher phases (4, 5) used more data for training:

  • Phase 1: 15% of data
  • Phase 2: 20%
  • Phase 3: 25%
  • Phase 4: 33%
  • Phase 5: 50%

Models from higher phases are generally more robust (but might take longer to train).

docs.title: Quick Decision Table
docs.text: If you want... Prioritize... Maximum profit Highest PnL / Return % Consistency Highest Win Rate Risk-adjusted Highest Sharpe Ratio Fewer false signals Highest Precision Catch all opportunities Highest Recall Balance Highest F1 Score

docs.title: 1. "High Accuracy = Good Model"
docs.text:
Wrong! Accuracy is misleading with imbalanced classes. A model predicting HOLD 90% of the time in a HOLD-dominated market has high accuracy but is useless.

Solution: Look at F1 score and trading metrics instead.

docs.title: 2. "Lowest Loss = Best Model"
docs.text:
Wrong! Loss is about prediction confidence, not trading profitability. A model can have low loss but still lose money.

Solution: Stage 3 PnL is the ultimate judge.

docs.title: 3. "More Trades = More Profit"
docs.text:
Wrong! Quality over quantity. 100 mediocre trades with 45% win rate will lose money to fees.

Solution: Look at Sharpe ratio and average trade quality.

docs.title: 4. "Stage 1 Metrics Matter"
docs.text:
Wrong! Stage 1 uses training data. The model has seen it before. It's like testing a student with the same questions they studied.

Solution: Focus on Stage 2 (validation) and Stage 3 (backtest) metrics.

docs.title: 5. "Ignore Sharpe if PnL is Positive"
docs.text:
Wrong! A positive PnL with negative Sharpe means you made money by luck, not skill. It won't repeat.

Solution: Require Sharpe > 0.5 minimum.

docs.title: Summary Cheat Sheet
docs.text:
METRICS TO PRIORITIZE (in order):

1. Stage 3 PnL         → Must be POSITIVE
2. Stage 3 Sharpe      → Want > 1.0 (minimum 0.5)
3. Stage 3 Win Rate    → Want > 50% (minimum 40%)
4. Stage 2 F1          → Higher = better generalization
5. Objective Value     → Composite score (useful for comparison)

METRICS TO BE CAREFUL WITH:

- Accuracy      → Misleading with imbalanced classes
- Stage 1 *     → Overfitting risk (training data)
- Raw PnL       → Without context of risk taken

RED FLAGS:

❌ Stage 2 loss >> Stage 1 loss (overfitting)
❌ Very few trades (< 20) - not statistically significant
❌ Negative Sharpe with positive PnL (lucky, not skilled)
❌ 100% win rate (definitely overfitted or too few trades)

docs.title: Conclusion
docs.text:
Metrics are tools, not answers. The "best" model depends on your goals:

  • Maximum returns: Prioritize PnL and Return %
  • Sleep at night: Prioritize Win Rate and Sharpe
  • Robustness: Prioritize Stage 2 F1 and use higher phases

For Rainbow Trader specifically, Trial 85 emerged as the best candidate because it excels where it matters most: making money consistently with acceptable risk.

Remember: ML metrics (accuracy, F1) tell you if the model is "smart", but trading metrics (PnL, Sharpe) tell you if it makes money. In the end, making money is what matters.

Last updated: January 2026

docs.title: Module Structure Decision
docs.text: Date: 2025-12-03 Status: ✅ APPROVED Related: KNOWLEDGE_SYSTEM_INTEGRATION_V2.md, INVOKE_FLOW_ARCHITECTURE.md

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
Decisión sobre dónde ubicar cada funcionalidad del Knowledge System y utilidades de LLM.

Resultado: Crear nuevo módulo graph-utils para servicios standalone que graph-orchestrator usa.

docs.title: Categorías
docs.text: Categoría Descripción Módulo Genérico Logging, config loading, paths common Standalone Servicios que se pueden usar a mano (CLI) graph-utils Motor Agentes, grafo, ejecución graph-orchestrator

docs.title: Estructura Final
docs.text:
modules/
│
├── common/                              # SOLO lo muy genérico
│   └── src/rainbow/common/
│       ├── __init__.py
│       ├── helpers/
│       │   ├── __init__.py
│       │   ├── config.py                # Config loading (genérico)
│       │   ├── logger.py                # Logging (genérico)
│       │   ├── basic_logger.py          # Basic logger
│       │   ├── paths.py                 # Path utilities (genérico)
│       │   └── validations.py           # Validaciones genéricas
│       ├── types/                       # Pydantic models compartidos
│       │   └── ...
│       └── entities/                    # Entidades compartidas
│           └── ...
│
├── graph-utils/                         # NUEVO: Servicios standalone
│   └── src/rainbow/graph_utils/
│       ├── __init__.py
│       │
│       ├── benchmark/                   # LLM Benchmark (MOVER de common)
│       │   ├── __init__.py
│       │   ├── runner.py                # Lógica de benchmark
│       │   ├── results.py               # Manejo de resultados YAML
│       │   └── cli.py                   # CLI: python -m rainbow.graph_utils.benchmark
│       │
│       ├── knowledge/                   # Knowledge System
│       │   ├── __init__.py

docs.title: Dependencias
docs.text:
┌─────────────────┐
                    │     common      │  (genérico: logging, config, paths)
                    └────────┬────────┘
                             │
                             ▼
                    ┌─────────────────┐
                    │   graph-utils   │  (standalone: benchmark, knowledge, cache)
                    └────────┬────────┘
                             │
                             ▼
                    ┌─────────────────┐
                    │graph-orchestrator│ (el motor: agents, graph, state)
                    └─────────────────┘
  • common → no depende de nadie
  • graph-utils → depende de common
  • graph-orchestrator → depende de graph-utils y common

docs.title: CLIs
docs.text: Comando Módulo Descripción python -m rainbow.graph_utils.benchmark run graph-utils Benchmarkea TODOS los modelos python -m rainbow.graph_utils.benchmark show graph-utils Muestra resultados guardados python -m rainbow.graph_utils.knowledge index --all graph-utils Indexa todo (code + docs) python -m rainbow.graph_utils.knowledge index --code graph-utils Indexa solo código python -m rainbow.graph_utils.knowledge index --docs graph-utils Indexa solo documentación python -m rainbow.graph_utils.knowledge index --module X graph-utils Indexa módulo específico python -m rainbow.graph_utils.knowledge search "query" graph-utils Busca en knowledge base python -m rainbow.graph_utils.knowledge status graph-utils Muestra estado del índice python -m rainbow.graph_utils.knowledge generate-summaries graph-utils Genera summaries para MCP python -m rainbow.graph_utils.mcp graph-utils Corre MCP server para Copilot/Windsurf python -m rainbow.graph_orchestrator graph-orchestrator Corre el agente principal

docs.title: Archivos a mover
docs.text: Archivo actual Nuevo destino Razón common/helpers/llm_benchmark.py graph-utils/benchmark/runner.py No es genérico, es específico de LLMs

docs.title: Archivos nuevos a crear
docs.text: Archivo Descripción graph-utils/benchmark/cli.py CLI para benchmark graph-utils/benchmark/results.py Manejo de resultados YAML graph-utils/knowledge/service.py KnowledgeService principal graph-utils/knowledge/indexer.py Indexación event-driven graph-utils/knowledge/embeddings.py Funciones de embeddings graph-utils/knowledge/vector_store.py Wrapper de Chroma graph-utils/knowledge/bm25.py BM25 via SQLite FTS5 graph-utils/knowledge/cli.py CLI para knowledge graph-utils/cache/decision_cache.py Cache de decisiones graph-utils/mcp/server.py MCP server para Copilot/Windsurf graph-utils/mcp/resources/__init__.py MCP resources (summaries, keywords) graph-utils/mcp/tools/search_knowledge.py Tool de búsqueda para MCP clients graph-utils/mcp/tools/get_context.py Tool de contexto para MCP clients graph-orchestrator/helpers/context.py ContextManager graph-orchestrator/helpers/tier0.py Tier 0 preprocessor graph-orchestrator/tools/knowledge.py Tool search_knowledge_base

docs.title: ¿Va en common?
docs.text:
• ✅ Si es usado por MUCHOS módulos (no solo graph-*)
  • ✅ Si es muy genérico (logging, config, paths)
  • ❌ Si es específico de LLMs/agents

docs.title: ¿Va en graph-utils?
docs.text:
• ✅ Si es un servicio standalone (puede correr sin el graph)
  • ✅ Si tiene CLI propio
  • ✅ Si graph-orchestrator lo importa como servicio
  • ❌ Si es parte del flow de ejecución de un agent

docs.title: ¿Va en graph-orchestrator?
docs.text:
• ✅ Si es parte del invoke flow (budget, context, tier0)
  • ✅ Si es un agent
  • ✅ Si es un tool de agent
  • ✅ Si es el estado del graph
  • ❌ Si puede usarse standalone

docs.title: Ejemplo de uso
docs.text:
# En graph-orchestrator/agents/base.py

from rainbow.graph_utils.knowledge import KnowledgeService
from rainbow.graph_utils.benchmark import get_benchmark_results
from rainbow.graph_utils.cache import DecisionCache

class BaseAgent:
    def __init__(self):
        self.knowledge = KnowledgeService()
        self.cache = DecisionCache()
        self.benchmark = get_benchmark_results()

    def invoke(self, state, messages):
        # 1. Context (interno)
        context = self._context_manager.prepare(messages)

        # 2. Budget check (interno)
        tier = self._budget_guard.ensure_within_budget(...)

        # 3. Get LLM (usa benchmark de graph-utils)
        estimated_time = len(prompt) / self.benchmark[model].tokens_per_second

        # 4. RAG search (usa knowledge de graph-utils)
        relevant = self.knowledge.hybrid_search(query)

        # 5. Invoke LLM
        response = llm.invoke(context)

        return response

docs.title: ~~rainbow-knowledge-mcp/~~ → graph-utils/mcp/
docs.text:
El plan original (Finding 18) mencionaba crear un módulo separado rainbow-knowledge-mcp/.

Decisión actualizada: El MCP va DENTRO de graph-utils/mcp/ porque: - Es un servicio standalone (puede correr sin el graph) ✅ - Tiene CLI propio ✅ - Reutiliza knowledge/ del mismo módulo ✅ - Mantiene todo organizado en un solo lugar ✅

docs.title: CLI paths actualizados
docs.text: ~~python -m rainbow.knowledge index --all~~ → python -m rainbow.graph_utils.knowledge index --all

docs.title: Archivos de config generados
docs.text:
Archivo Generado por Descripción config/graph-llm-benchmark.yaml graph-utils/benchmark Benchmarks de velocidad de modelos

Nota: Se removió "results" del nombre porque es un archivo de configuración (aunque se genere automáticamente). - La clase BaseConfig donde se va a leer va a ser GraphLlmBenchMarkConfig

docs.title: Pattern de BaseConfig para configs hijos
docs.text:
Para que graph_orchestrator_config tenga configs hijos (memory, knowledge, graph_models) que: 1. Tengan valores dentro del config padre (archivo único YAML) 2. Puedan tener su propio archivo separado en el futuro (ej: config/graph-models.yaml)

Los hijos deben ser instancias de BaseConfig:

class GraphOrchestratorConfig(BaseConfig):
    memory_config: MemoryConfig          # Para memory.summarization
    knowledge_config: KnowledgeConfig    # Para knowledge.vector_store
    graph_models_config: GraphModelsConfig  # Para graph_models.llm_tiers

docs.title: Ollama Local Testing Results
docs.text: Fecha: 2025-12-03 Sistema: Linux Modelos probados: nomic-embed-text, llama3.2, mistral Objetivo: Evaluar viabilidad de Ollama para Rainbow Trader

docs.title: 1.1 Queries Individuales
docs.text:
Query Tiempo (ms) Dimensiones Memoria (MB) cómo entrenar un modelo de trading 1253.0 768D +0.31 algorithmic trading with machine learning 133.7 768D +0.00 RSI indicator 14 period 274.2 768D +0.00 backtesting framework Python 54.6 768D +0.44 deep learning for time series prediction 89.3 768D +0.00

Análisis: - ✅ Promedio: 361.0 ms por embedding - ✅ Rango: 54.6 ms - 1253.0 ms - ✅ Memoria: Negligible (~0.3 MB) - ⚠️ Primera query lenta: Cold start (modelo cargado en memoria)

docs.title: 1.2 Escalabilidad (Batch Processing)
docs.text:
Batch Size Tiempo Total (ms) Tiempo/Embedding (ms) Memoria (MB) 1 72.0 72.0 +0.00 5 331.3 66.3 +0.00 10 628.0 62.8 +0.52 20 1537.9 76.9 +0.26

Análisis: - ✅ Escalable: ~65-77 ms por embedding en batches - ✅ Eficiente: Mejor rendimiento con batches (vs individual) - ✅ Memoria: Estable incluso con 20 embeddings

docs.title: 2.1 llama3.2 (3B - Summarization rápida)
docs.text:
Tipo Tiempo (ms) Tokens Tokens/sec Memoria (MB) SHORT (50 tokens max) 10890.1 17 1.6 +0.00 MEDIUM (100 tokens max) 10245.1 63 6.1 +0.00 LONG (200 tokens max) 18231.0 115 6.3 +0.00

Análisis: - ✅ Velocidad: 6.1 tokens/sec (promedio) - ✅ Consistente: ~10-18 segundos por respuesta - ⚠️ Lento para Tier 1: Mejor que OpenAI ($0), pero lento para real-time - ✅ Ideal para: Summarization, offline processing

docs.title: 2.2 mistral (7B - Mejor calidad)
docs.text:
Tipo Tiempo (ms) Tokens Tokens/sec Memoria (MB) SHORT (50 tokens max) 27782.2 20 0.7 +0.00 MEDIUM (100 tokens max) 21428.9 58 2.7 +0.00 LONG (200 tokens max) 32764.8 70 2.1 +0.00

Análisis: - ⚠️ Lento: 2.1 tokens/sec (promedio) - ⚠️ 27-32 segundos por respuesta: Demasiado para interacciones - ❌ NO recomendado para Tier 1: Mejor usar llama3.2 - ✅ Alternativa: Usar para offline analysis si necesitas mejor calidad

docs.title: 2.3 Comparativa LLM
docs.text: Métrica llama3.2 mistral Velocidad ⭐⭐⭐⭐ (6.1 tok/s) ⭐⭐ (2.1 tok/s) Calidad ⭐⭐⭐ ⭐⭐⭐⭐ Tiempo promedio 13.1 seg 27.3 seg Recomendación ✅ Tier 1 ❌ Demasiado lento

docs.title: 3. Workflow Combinado (Embeddings + LLM)
docs.text:
Tarea: "¿Cuál es el mejor indicador técnico para trading de corto plazo?"

Paso Tiempo (ms) Tokens % del Total 1. Embedding 113.7 — 0.4% 2. LLM Response 25741.0 181 99.6% TOTAL 25854.7 181 100%

Análisis: - ✅ Embeddings negligible: Solo 0.4% del tiempo total - ⚠️ LLM domina: 99.6% del tiempo es LLM - 💡 Implicación: Optimizar LLM es crítico, embeddings son "gratis"

docs.title: 🔴 CRÍTICO: LLM Responses
docs.text:
Escenario Tiempo Viabilidad Real-time chat 10-30 seg ❌ Inaceptable Batch processing OK ✅ Aceptable Offline summarization OK ✅ Aceptable

Problema: llama3.2 genera ~6 tokens/sec. Para una respuesta de 100 tokens = 16 segundos.

docs.title: 🟡 MODERADO: Cold Start
docs.text:
Evento Tiempo Primera query (cold start) 1253 ms Queries siguientes 54-274 ms

Solución: Mantener modelo en memoria (siempre corriendo ollama serve)

docs.title: ✅ Usar Ollama para:
docs.text:
1. Embeddings (nomic-embed-text)
  2. ✅ Rápido: 65-77 ms por embedding
  3. ✅ Gratis: $0
  4. ✅ Multilingüe

  5. Decisión: IMPLEMENTAR INMEDIATAMENTE

  6. Tier 1 Summarization (llama3.2)

  7. ✅ Gratis: $0
  8. ⚠️ Lento: 6 tok/sec
  9. ✅ Ideal para: Comprimir historial, resúmenes offline
  10. Decisión: IMPLEMENTAR CON CUIDADO (solo para batch)

docs.title: ❌ NO usar Ollama para:
docs.text:
1. Real-time chat responses
  2. ❌ 10-30 segundos por respuesta
  3. ❌ Experiencia de usuario pobre

  4. Alternativa: OpenAI/Anthropic (Tier 2-3)

  5. Mistral (7B)

  6. ❌ Demasiado lento: 2.1 tok/sec
  7. ❌ 27-32 segundos por respuesta
  8. Alternativa: Usar llama3.2 si necesitas local

docs.title: 6. Arquitectura Propuesta
docs.text:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    RAINBOW TRADER                           │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                             │
│  EMBEDDINGS (Ollama local)                                  │
│  ├── nomic-embed-text: 65-77 ms/embedding ✅               │
│  └── Uso: RAG retrieval, decision cache                    │
│                                                             │
│  TIER 1 LLM (Ollama local)                                  │
│  ├── llama3.2: 6 tok/sec ⚠️                                │
│  └── Uso: Offline summarization, batch processing          │
│                                                             │
│  TIER 2-3 LLM (OpenAI/Anthropic)                            │
│  ├── GPT-4, Claude: Real-time responses ✅                 │
│  └── Uso: Director decisions, analysis, coding             │
│                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

docs.text:
• [ ] Integrar langchain-ollama en requirements.txt
  • [ ] Crear OllamaEmbeddings provider en base.py
  • [ ] Setup Chroma con indexación de docs/código
  • Tiempo: 1-2 días
  • ROI: Elimina costo de OpenAI embeddings (~$50-100/mes)

docs.title: Fase 2: Implementar Tier 1 Summarization (SIGUIENTE)
docs.text:
• [ ] Crear ConversationSummaryMemory con llama3.2
  • [ ] Usar solo para batch processing (no real-time)
  • [ ] Agregar flag use_ollama: true/false en config
  • Tiempo: 2-3 días
  • ROI: Reduce contexto sin costo

docs.title: Fase 3: Monitoreo (CONTINUO)
docs.text:
• [ ] Medir tiempo real de embeddings en producción
  • [ ] Medir tiempo de summarization
  • [ ] Ajustar si es necesario

docs.title: 8. Conclusión
docs.text:
Aspecto Veredicto Embeddings ✅ EXCELENTE - Implementar ya Tier 1 LLM ⚠️ ACEPTABLE - Usar solo para batch Real-time responses ❌ NO - Mantener OpenAI/Anthropic Costo total ✅ BAJO - Embeddings gratis, LLM pago solo cuando necesario

Estimación de ahorro: - Embeddings: $50-100/mes → $0 ✅ - Tier 1 summarization: $20-50/mes → $0 ✅ - Total: $70-150/mes en ahorros

Generado: 2025-12-03 Duración total de pruebas: ~5 minutos Conclusión: Ollama es viable para embeddings y batch processing. Mantener OpenAI/Anthropic para real-time.

docs.title: Using VPN with Rainbow Trader
docs.text: This guide explains how to use a VPN or proxy with Rainbow Trader when accessing Binance from restricted regions like Indonesia.

docs.title: Why You Need a VPN/Proxy
docs.text: Binance restricts access from certain countries and regions due to regulatory requirements. If you're in a restricted region, API calls to Binance will fail with connection errors.

docs.title: Option 1: Use Built-in Proxy Support
docs.text:
The data-fetcher module has built-in proxy support. You can specify a proxy URL when fetching data:

# Using SOCKS5 proxy with data-fetcher
python -m rainbow.data_fetcher fetch-symbol \
    --symbol BTCUSDT \
    --timeframe 1d \
    --start 2024-01-01 \
    --end 2024-04-01 \
    --proxy socks5://127.0.0.1:1080

docs.title: Option 2: System-wide VPN
docs.text:
1. Install a VPN client (e.g., NordVPN, ExpressVPN, or OpenVPN)
  2. Connect to a server in a region where Binance is accessible
  3. Run your application normally

docs.title: Using SSH Tunnel as a SOCKS Proxy
docs.text:
If you have a server outside restricted regions, you can create an SSH tunnel:

# Create SSH tunnel as a SOCKS5 proxy
ssh -D 1080 -q -C -N user@your-server.com

# Now you can use socks5://127.0.0.1:1080 as your proxy

docs.title: Using a Commercial VPN with SOCKS5
docs.text:
Many commercial VPNs provide SOCKS5 proxy endpoints:

  1. Subscribe to a VPN service that offers SOCKS5 proxies
  2. Get the SOCKS5 server details from your provider
  3. Use those details with the --proxy parameter

docs.title: Testing Your Proxy Setup
docs.text:
A test script is provided to verify your proxy connection:

# Test direct connection
.venv/bin/python scripts/setup_proxy.py

# Test connection through proxy
.venv/bin/python scripts/setup_proxy.py --proxy socks5://127.0.0.1:1080

docs.title: Common Issues
docs.text:
1. Connection timeout: Verify your proxy/VPN is active and correctly configured
  2. Authentication failure: Check your proxy credentials if required
  3. SSL certificate errors: Some proxies may interfere with SSL; use verify=False with caution

docs.title: Getting More Information
docs.text:
For detailed errors, enable debug logging:

python -m rainbow.data_fetcher fetch-symbol --symbol BTCUSDT --timeframe 1d --start 2024-01-01 --end 2024-04-01 --proxy socks5://127.0.0.1:1080 --set log.level=DEBUG

docs.title: Installing Required Packages
docs.text:
The following packages are required for proxy support:

# Install from requirements.txt
.venv/bin/pip install -r modules/data-fetcher/requirements.txt

# Or install manually
.venv/bin/pip install PySocks requests[socks]

docs.title: Security Considerations
docs.text:
1. Use reputable VPN providers
  2. Consider that free proxies may monitor your traffic
  3. Be aware of the legal implications of using VPNs in your jurisdiction

docs.title: Problem: Sort maps are scattered and use different formats
docs.text:
AdminDashboardService (admin_dashboard_service.py:48-117) - Location: Module-level constants (GOOD) - Format: field_asc/field_desc as keys with pre-built SQLAlchemy clauses - Maps: _EXPERIMENT_SORT_MAP, _TRAINING_SORT_MAP, _TRIAL_SORT_MAP - Example:

_EXPERIMENT_SORT_MAP: dict[str, Any] = {
    "id_asc": Experiment.id.asc(),
    "id_desc": Experiment.id.desc(),
    "name_asc": Experiment.name.asc(),
    ...
}

BacktestDashboardService (backtest_dashboard_service.py:51-63) - Location: Module-level constant (GOOD) - Format: Field name as key with COLUMN ONLY (NOT pre-built clauses) ❌ - Map: _BACKTEST_SORT_MAP - Example:

_BACKTEST_SORT_MAP: dict[str, Any] = {
    "id": BacktestSession.id,              # ❌ NO .asc()/.desc()
    "status": BacktestSession.status,      # ❌ NO .asc()/.desc()
    "created": BacktestSession.created_at, # ❌ NO .asc()/.desc()
    ...
}

Issue: Backtest sort map is INCOMPLETE - missing direction information. Requires parsing in resolver.

docs.title: 2. TYPE DEFINITIONS - SCATTERED ACROSS MULTIPLE FILES
Problem: Types are defined in 3 different files with no clear separation
docs.text:
File Purpose Contains dashboard_types.py API contracts for admin dashboard ExperimentSummary, TrainingSummary, TrialSummary, ExperimentDetail, TrainingDetail, TrialDetail, *Filters, *Sort types.py Backtest-specific types BacktestSessionSummary, BacktestSessionDetail, BacktestSessionMetrics, BacktestTradeStats (DATACLASS!), CandleData, TradeData, EquityPoint, etc. dashboard_plan_types.py Plan-related types DashboardEnvelope, DashboardRange, DashboardBotConfig, DashboardExperimentPlan

Issue: No clear organization. Backtest types in types.py but admin types in dashboard_types.py. Why?

docs.title: Problem: Services use different naming patterns
docs.text:
Service File Pattern Purpose AdminDashboardService admin_dashboard_service.py *DashboardService Read-only dashboard queries for experiments/trainings/trials BacktestDashboardService backtest_dashboard_service.py *DashboardService Read-only dashboard queries for backtests ExperimentVisualizationService experiment_visualization_service.py *VisualizationService Read-only visualization endpoints ParquetVisualizationService parquet_visualization_service.py *VisualizationService Read-only visualization endpoints BacktestLaunchService backtest_launch_service.py *LaunchService Write operations (launch backtests) TrainingLaunchService training_launch_service.py *LaunchService Write operations (launch trainings)

Issue: - Why BacktestDashboardService but ExperimentVisualizationService? - Why not ExperimentDashboardService for consistency? - *DashboardService vs *VisualizationService - what's the difference? - No TrainingDashboardService - training queries are in AdminDashboardService

docs.title: 4. INTERNAL TYPE DEFINITIONS - DATACLASS vs PYDANTIC
Problem: Inconsistent use of dataclasses vs Pydantic models
docs.text:
BacktestTradeStats (backtest_dashboard_service.py:66-88)

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class BacktestTradeStats:
    """Typed container for trade aggregates used by the dashboard."""
    trades_total: int | None = None
    trades_closed: int | None = None
    ...

Issue: - This is a DATACLASS, not a Pydantic model - All other types use Pydantic BaseModel - Why the inconsistency? - Where should this live? In types.py? In a separate file?

docs.title: 5. SORT MAP RESOLVER LOGIC - INCONSISTENT IMPLEMENTATION
AdminDashboardService (Correct)
docs.text:
@staticmethod
def _resolve_order(sort: str, column_map: dict[str, Any], default: ColumnElement[Any]) -> ColumnElement[Any]:
    """Parse '<field>_asc' or '<field>_desc' and return SQLAlchemy order clause."""
    if sort.endswith("_asc"):
        field = sort[:-4]
        direction = "asc"
    elif sort.endswith("_desc"):
        field = sort[:-5]
        direction = "desc"
    else:
        return default

    col = column_map.get(field)
    if col is None:
        return default

    return col.asc() if direction == "asc" else col.desc()

docs.title: BacktestDashboardService (Incomplete)
docs.text:
# In list_sessions method around line 1785-1795
field = sort[:-4] if sort.endswith("_asc") else sort[:-5]
col = _BACKTEST_SORT_MAP.get(field)
if col is None:
    return BacktestSession.id.desc()
# ❌ MISSING: Apply direction (.asc()/.desc()) to col

Issue: Backtest resolver doesn't apply direction to the column!

docs.title: Problem: No dedicated training dashboard service
docs.text:
• Experiments: AdminDashboardService.list_experiments()
  • Trainings: AdminDashboardService.list_trainings() (mixed with experiments)
  • Trials: AdminDashboardService.list_trials() (mixed with experiments)
  • Backtests: BacktestDashboardService.list_sessions() (dedicated service)

Issue: Why is backtest special? Why not separate services for each entity?

docs.title: Current Structure
docs.text:
control_panel/
├── dashboard_types.py      # Admin dashboard types (Experiments, Trainings, Trials)
├── types.py                # Backtest types + visualization types
├── dashboard_plan_types.py # Plan-related types
└── services/
    ├── admin_dashboard_service.py
    ├── backtest_dashboard_service.py
    ├── experiment_visualization_service.py
    ├── parquet_visualization_service.py
    ├── backtest_launch_service.py
    └── training_launch_service.py

Issue: No clear separation. Should be:

control_panel/
├── types/
│   ├── admin_dashboard_types.py    # Experiments, Trainings, Trials
│   ├── backtest_types.py           # Backtest sessions
│   ├── visualization_types.py      # Experiment, Parquet visualization
│   ├── launch_types.py             # Training, Backtest launch
│   └── plan_types.py               # Plan-related
└── services/
    ├── admin_dashboard_service.py
    ├── backtest_dashboard_service.py
    ├── experiment_visualization_service.py
    ├── parquet_visualization_service.py
    ├── backtest_launch_service.py
    └── training_launch_service.py

docs.title: 📋 SUMMARY OF INCONSISTENCIES
docs.text: Issue Severity Location Impact Sort maps use different formats 🔴 HIGH admin_dashboard_service.py vs backtest_dashboard_service.py Inconsistent resolver logic, hard to maintain Types scattered across 3 files 🔴 HIGH dashboard_types.py, types.py, dashboard_plan_types.py Confusing organization, hard to find types Service naming inconsistent 🟡 MEDIUM Service files Unclear purpose of each service Dataclass mixed with Pydantic 🟡 MEDIUM BacktestTradeStats Inconsistent patterns Backtest sort resolver incomplete 🔴 HIGH backtest_dashboard_service.py Sort direction not applied correctly No dedicated training dashboard service 🟡 MEDIUM admin_dashboard_service.py Trainings mixed with experiments No clear type organization 🟡 MEDIUM control_panel/ root Hard to navigate

docs.title: Phase 1: Standardize Sort Maps
docs.text:
1. Update _BACKTEST_SORT_MAP to use field_asc/field_desc format with pre-built clauses
  2. Ensure all sort maps follow the same structure
  3. Move all sort maps to a dedicated sorts.py file

docs.title: Phase 2: Organize Types
docs.text:
1. Create types/ subdirectory
  2. Separate types by domain (admin, backtest, visualization, launch, plan)
  3. Keep consistent naming: *Summary, *Detail, *Metrics, *Filters, *Sort

docs.title: Phase 3: Standardize Services
docs.text:
1. Decide: Should trainings have dedicated service or stay in admin?
  2. Clarify naming: *DashboardService vs *VisualizationService
  3. Consider: Should each entity have its own service?

docs.title: Phase 4: Unify Internal Types
docs.text:
1. Move BacktestTradeStats to types/backtest_types.py
  2. Convert to Pydantic model for consistency
  3. Document all internal types

• admin_dashboard_service.py - Sort maps, resolver logic
  • backtest_dashboard_service.py - Sort maps, resolver logic, BacktestTradeStats
  • dashboard_types.py - Type organization

docs.title: Control Panel Component Unification Audit
docs.text: Status: In Progress Started: 2026-02-18 Priority: High

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Systematically audit and unify all list and detail components across the Control Panel to eliminate duplication and create a consistent, maintainable component architecture.

docs.title: Current Problems
docs.text:
1. Multiple trial list implementations - Same data shown differently across pages
  2. Multiple training list implementations - Duplicated table logic
  3. Multiple context/header components - Similar experiment/training summaries repeated
  4. Inconsistent table structures - Different columns, different actions for same entity
  5. No shared component library - Each page reimplements similar functionality

docs.title: A. TrialsTable.tsx (Full-featured list page)
docs.text:
Location: @/components/dashboard/trials/list/table/TrialsTable.tsx Used in: /experiments/{id}/trials/, /experiments/{id}/trainings/{id}/trials/, /trials/ Features:

  • Full pagination
  • Sortable columns (ID, Objective)
  • 9 columns: ID, Optuna #, Status, Objective, Stages (1/2/3), Eq/PnL/Trades, Phase/Study, Best Flags, Actions
  • Detailed stage metrics (s1, s2, s3)
  • Equity/PnL/Trades calculations
  • Phase/Study hierarchy info
  • Best flags (experiment + study)
  • Actions: Detail, Backtests, "Use in launch" button
  • Table density toggle (compact/comfortable)
  • Context-aware routing (experiment/study context)

docs.title: B. ExperimentTrialsTable.tsx (Simplified for experiment detail)
docs.text:
Location: @/components/dashboard/experiments/detail/shared/ExperimentTrialsTable.tsx Used in: /experiments/{id}/ detail page Features:

  • NO pagination (shows limited set)
  • NO sorting
  • 7 columns: ID, Optuna #, Status, Objective, Study, Best Flags, Actions
  • Simplified view - no stage details, no metrics
  • Best flags (experiment + study)
  • Actions: Detail, Backtests
  • Fixed to experiment context

docs.title: C. Experiments List Page (inline table)
docs.text:
Location: @/components/dashboard/experiments/list/ExperimentsListPage.tsx Features:

  • Inline table definition (NOT a separate component)
  • Shows experiment-level trial counts and best trial info
  • NOT a trials list, but shows trial summary data

DUPLICATION ISSUE: TrialsTable and ExperimentTrialsTable show the same entity (trials) with different columns and features. Should be ONE unified component with configuration options.

docs.title: A. TrainingsTable.tsx (Full-featured list page)
docs.text:
Location: @/components/dashboard/trainings/list/table/TrainingsTable.tsx Used in: /experiments/{id}/trainings/, /trainings/ Features:

  • Full pagination
  • Sortable columns (ID)
  • 10 columns: ID, Study, Status, Backend, Progress, Trials, Phase, Best Flag, Experiment, Actions
  • Progress percentage
  • Trial completion counts
  • Phase tracking
  • Best flag (only experiment-level)
  • Actions: Detail, Trials, Backtests

docs.title: B. ExperimentTrainingsTable.tsx (Simplified for experiment detail)
docs.text:
Location: @/components/dashboard/experiments/detail/shared/ExperimentTrainingsTable.tsx Used in: /experiments/{id}/ detail page Features:

  • NO pagination (shows limited set)
  • NO sorting
  • 7 columns: ID, Study, Status, Progress, Trials, Best Flag, Actions
  • Simplified view - no backend, no phase, no experiment name
  • Best flag (only experiment-level)
  • Actions: Detail, Trials
  • Fixed to experiment context

DUPLICATION ISSUE: TrainingsTable and ExperimentTrainingsTable show the same entity with different columns. Should be ONE unified component with configuration.

docs.title: ExperimentsListPage.tsx
docs.text:
Location: @/components/dashboard/experiments/list/ExperimentsListPage.tsx Features:

  • Inline table definition (NOT extracted to separate component)
  • Full pagination
  • Sortable columns (ID, Name)
  • 8 columns: ID, Name, Status, Market, Range, Trainings, Trials, Actions
  • Complex cell rendering (name + type, counts + best info)
  • Actions: Detail, Trainings, Trials, Backtests

ISSUE: Should be extracted to ExperimentsTable.tsx component for consistency.

docs.title: A. Experiment Context Components (3 copies)
docs.text:
1. TrialsExperimentContext.tsx
  2. Location: @/components/dashboard/trials/list/context/
  3. Shows experiment summary in trials list page
  4. Uses ContextSummaryCard with experiment metrics

  5. Links: Experiment, Trainings, Trials, Backtests

  6. TrainingExperimentContext.tsx

  7. Location: @/components/dashboard/trainings/list/context/
  8. Shows experiment summary in trainings list page
  9. Uses ContextSummaryCard with experiment metrics

  10. Links: Experiment, Trainings, Trials, Backtests

  11. ExperimentDetailHeader.tsx

  12. Location: @/components/dashboard/experiments/detail/shared/
  13. Shows experiment summary in experiment detail page
  14. Uses ContextSummaryCard with experiment metrics
  15. Links: Experiment, Trainings, Trials, Backtests, Requirements

DUPLICATION ISSUE: All three components show the SAME experiment data with nearly identical structure. Should be ONE unified ExperimentContextCard component.

docs.title: B. Training Context Components (2 copies)
docs.text:
1. TrialsTrainingContext.tsx
  2. Location: @/components/dashboard/trials/list/context/
  3. Shows training summary in trials list page
  4. Uses ContextSummaryCard with training metrics

  5. Links: Training, Backtests

  6. Training detail pages likely have similar headers (need to verify)

DUPLICATION ISSUE: Training context shown in multiple places with similar structure.

docs.title: C. Backtests Context Components
docs.text:
1. BacktestsExperimentContext.tsx
  2. Location: @/components/dashboard/backtests/list/context/
  3. Shows experiment summary in backtests list page
  4. Likely similar to other experiment contexts

docs.title: 5. SHARED COMPONENTS (Good - Already Unified)
docs.text:
These components are CORRECTLY unified and reused:

  • ✅ ContextSummaryCard - Generic card for showing context summaries
  • ✅ StatusBadge - Status display
  • ✅ BestFlags - Best trial/study flags
  • ✅ PaginationControls - Pagination UI
  • ✅ SortableTableHeader - Sortable column headers
  • ✅ TableDensityToggle - Density switcher
  • ✅ AdminShell - Page layout wrapper

docs.title: 1. Unified Table Components
docs.text: Create configurable table components that can be used in multiple contexts:

docs.title: UnifiedTrialsTable
docs.text:
Props:

interface UnifiedTrialsTableProps {
  trials: TrialSummary[];
  variant: "full" | "compact" | "minimal";
  pagination?: PaginationProps;
  sorting?: SortingProps;
  columns?: TrialColumn[];  // Allow column customization
  actions?: TrialAction[];  // Allow action customization
  context?: {
    experimentId?: number;
    studyId?: number;
    rangeStart?: string;
    rangeEnd?: string;
  };
  density?: "compact" | "comfortable";
  launchForm?: UseBacktestLaunchFormResult;
}

Variants:

  • full - All columns, all features (for list pages)
  • compact - Reduced columns, essential info (for detail pages)
  • minimal - Minimal columns, basic info (for summaries)

docs.title: UnifiedTrainingsTable
docs.text:
Props:

interface UnifiedTrainingsTableProps {
  trainings: TrainingSummary[];
  variant: "full" | "compact" | "minimal";
  pagination?: PaginationProps;
  sorting?: SortingProps;
  columns?: TrainingColumn[];
  actions?: TrainingAction[];
  context?: {
    experimentId?: number;
  };
}

docs.title: UnifiedExperimentsTable
docs.text: Extract from inline definition to reusable component.

docs.title: 2. Unified Context Components
docs.text: Create ONE context component per entity type:

docs.title: ExperimentContextCard
docs.text:
Props:

interface ExperimentContextCardProps {
  experiment: ExperimentDetail | ExperimentSummary;
  variant: "full" | "compact";
  actions?: "all" | "subset" | CustomAction[];
  showRequirements?: boolean;
}

Replaces:

  • TrialsExperimentContext
  • TrainingExperimentContext
  • ExperimentDetailHeader
  • BacktestsExperimentContext

docs.title: TrainingContextCard
docs.text:
Props:

interface TrainingContextCardProps {
  training: TrainingDetail | TrainingSummary;
  variant: "full" | "compact";
  experimentId?: number;
  actions?: "all" | "subset" | CustomAction[];
}

Replaces:

  • TrialsTrainingContext
  • Training detail headers

docs.title: 3. Column Configuration System
docs.text:
Create a column configuration system to allow flexible table customization:

// columns/trialColumns.ts
export const TRIAL_COLUMNS = {
  id: { label: "ID", sortable: true, className: "mono" },
  optunaNumber: { label: "Optuna #", sortable: false },
  status: { label: "Status", sortable: false },
  objective: { label: "Objective", sortable: true },
  stages: { label: "Stages (1/2/3)", sortable: false },
  metrics: { label: "Eq/PnL/Trades", sortable: false },
  phaseStudy: { label: "Phase/Study", sortable: false },
  bestFlags: { label: "Best Flags", sortable: false },
  actions: { label: "Actions", sortable: false },
} as const;

export const TRIAL_COLUMN_PRESETS = {
  full: ["id", "optunaNumber", "status", "objective", "stages", "metrics", "phaseStudy", "bestFlags", "actions"],
  compact: ["id", "optunaNumber", "status", "objective", "study", "bestFlags", "actions"],
  minimal: ["id", "status", "objective", "bestFlags"],
} as const;

docs.title: 1. How to control which columns show in each table?
docs.text:
Option A - Simple Variants (RECOMMENDED):

<UnifiedTrialsTable variant="full" />      // Shows ALL 9 columns (for /trials/ page)
<UnifiedTrialsTable variant="compact" />   // Shows 7 columns (for /experiments/{id}/ page)
<UnifiedTrialsTable variant="minimal" />   // Shows 4 columns (for summaries)
  • ✅ Simple to use
  • ✅ Consistent across pages
  • ❌ Less flexible

Option B - Granular Column Selection:

<UnifiedTrialsTable 
  columns={["id", "status", "objective", "bestFlags", "actions"]} 
/>
  • ✅ Maximum flexibility
  • ❌ Each page can show different columns (inconsistent)
  • ❌ More complex to maintain

Which do you prefer?

docs.title: 2. Table row spacing - should it be the same everywhere or different per table?
docs.text:
Current behavior: Each table remembers its own density setting separately. - Trials table can be "compact" - Trainings table can be "comfortable" - They don't sync

Option A - Keep per-table (current): - User sets density for trials table → only affects trials - User sets density for trainings table → only affects trainings

Option B - Make it global: - User sets density once → ALL tables change - One setting controls everything

Which makes more sense for your workflow?

docs.title: 3. Should I also audit and unify the Backtests tables?
docs.text:
I found duplicates in: - Trials (2 duplicates) - Trainings (2 duplicates) - Experiments (1 inline)

I haven't looked at Backtests yet. Should I include them in this unification work?

docs.title: 4. Should we create a reusable page wrapper to eliminate more duplication?
docs.text:
Current situation: Every list page repeats the same pattern:

// REPEATED in TrialsListPage, TrainingsListPage, ExperimentsListPage, etc.
<AdminShell title="..." subtitle="...">
  <ContextCard />           // Experiment/Training context
  <Filters />               // Search, status, etc.
  <Table />                 // The actual data
  <Pagination />            // Page controls
</AdminShell>

Proposed: Create EntityListPageLayout that handles this pattern:

<EntityListPageLayout
  entity="trials"
  context={experimentContext}
  filters={<TrialsFilters />}
  table={<UnifiedTrialsTable />}
/>

This would eliminate even MORE duplication. Should we do this?

docs.title: 5. Implementation order - all at once or step by step?
docs.text:
Option A - Phased (SAFER): 1. Week 1: Unify Trials components → test → deploy 2. Week 2: Unify Trainings components → test → deploy 3. Week 3: Unify Experiments components → test → deploy 4. Week 4: Unify Context cards → test → deploy

Option B - All at once: - Do everything in one big change - Test everything together - Deploy all changes at once

Which approach do you prefer?

docs.title: User Requirements (APPROVED):
docs.text:
1. ✅ Column Control: Option A - Simple Variants
  2. variant="full" / "compact" / "minimal"

  3. Consistent across pages, simple to use

  4. ✅ Table Density: Option A - Per-table

  5. Each table remembers its own density setting

  6. Trials can be compact, Trainings can be comfortable

  7. ✅ Backtests: Include in unification

  8. Use unified table component for ALL entities (trials, trainings, experiments, backtests)

  9. Include BacktestsExperimentContext in context card unification

  10. ✅ Page Layout Wrapper: YES

  11. Create EntityListPageLayout to eliminate page-level duplication

  12. Filters visually connected to tables for clarity

  13. ✅ Implementation: All at once

  14. Do everything in one big change
  15. No legacy code, no backward compatibility
  16. Clean slate approach

docs.title: Additional Critical Requirements:
docs.text:
1. ✅ ALL columns must be sortable
  2. Current: Only 1-2 columns sortable per table
  3. New: ALL columns sortable (except actions/complex cells)

  4. User decides sort field by clicking column header

  5. ✅ Visual clarity: Filters → Tables connection

  6. Current: Filters at top, tables far below, unclear connection
  7. New: Clear visual hierarchy, filters visually connected to tables

  8. Obvious what filters affect what tables

  9. ✅ Code quality principles:

  10. Elegance - Clean, beautiful code
  11. Modularity - Reusable components
  12. Simplicity - Easy to understand
  13. Robustness - Solid, reliable
  14. Efficiency - No redundancy
  15. NO legacy code - Fresh start
  16. NO backward compatibility - Modern approach only

docs.title: Control Panel Components Design
docs.text: Status: In Progress Started: 2026-02-18 Priority: High

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Design document for the 3 reusable components that will replace ~12-15 legacy components in the Control Panel.

Component Names: - Table - Generic table for all entities - ContextCard - Context card for all entity types - ListLayout - Page layout wrapper for all list pages

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: ONE table component that works for ALL entities: Trials, Trainings, Experiments, Backtests.

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• Variant system: full/compact/minimal
  • ALL columns sortable (except actions/complex cells)
  • Per-table density: compact/comfortable
  • Pagination built-in
  • Context-aware routing
  • Type-safe column configuration

docs.title: Props Interface
docs.text:
interface UnifiedEntityTableProps<TEntity, TSort extends string> {
  // Data
  entity: "trials" | "trainings" | "experiments" | "backtests";
  rows: TEntity[];

  // Pagination
  page: number;
  pageSize: number;
  totalPages: number;
  totalItems: number;
  onPrevious: () => void;
  onNext: () => void;

  // Sorting - ALL columns
  sort: TSort;
  onSort: (value: TSort) => void;

  // Display
  variant: "full" | "compact" | "minimal";

  // Context (for routing)
  context?: {
    experimentId?: number;
    studyId?: number;
    rangeStart?: string;
    rangeEnd?: string;
  };

  // Optional features
  launchForm?: UseBacktestLaunchFormResult; // For trials
  showDensityToggle?: boolean; // Default true for full variant
}

docs.title: Column Configuration System
docs.text:
Each entity has a column registry that defines ALL possible columns:

// columns/trialColumns.ts
export const TRIAL_COLUMNS = {
  id: {
    key: "id",
    label: "ID",
    sortKey: "id",
    sortable: true,
    render: (trial: TrialSummary) => <span className="mono">{trial.id}</span>,
  },
  optunaNumber: {
    key: "optunaNumber",
    label: "Optuna #",
    sortKey: "optuna_number",
    sortable: true,
    render: (trial: TrialSummary) => <span>#{trial.optuna_trial_number ?? "n/a"}</span>,
  },
  status: {
    key: "status",
    label: "Status",
    sortKey: "status",
    sortable: true,
    render: (trial: TrialSummary) => <StatusBadge value={trial.status} />,
  },
  objective: {
    key: "objective",
    label: "Objective",
    sortKey: "objective",
    sortable: true,
    render: (trial: TrialSummary) => formatFloatValue(trial.objective_value),
  },
  stages: {
    key: "stages",
    label: "Stages (1/2/3)",
    sortKey: "stage3_result", // Sort by stage 3
    sortable: true,
    render: (trial: TrialSummary) => <StagesCell trial={trial} />,
  },
  metrics: {
    key: "metrics",
    label: "Eq/PnL/Trades",
    sortKey: "stage3_total_pnl",
    sortable: true,
    render: (trial: TrialSummary) => <MetricsCell trial={trial} />,
  },
  phaseStudy: {
    key: "phaseStudy",
    label: "Phase/Study",
    sortKey: "phase",
    sortable: true,
    render: (trial: TrialSummary, context) => <PhaseStudyCell trial={trial} context={context} />,
  },
  bestFlags: {
    key: "bestFlags",
    label: "Best Flags",
    sortKey: null, // Not sortable - composite field
    sortable: false,
    render: (trial: TrialSummary) => (
      <BestFlags
        isBestForExperiment={trial.is_best_for_experiment}
        isBestForStudy={trial.is_best_for_study}
      />
    ),
  },
  actions: {
    key: "actions",
    label: "Actions",
    sortKey: null,
    sortable: false,
    render: (trial: TrialSummary, context, launchForm) => (
      <ActionsCell trial={trial} context={context} launchForm={launchForm} />
    ),
  },
} as const;

export type TrialColumnKey = keyof typeof TRIAL_COLUMNS;

docs.title: Variant Presets
docs.text:
// columns/trialColumns.ts
export const TRIAL_COLUMN_PRESETS: Record<TableVariant, TrialColumnKey[]> = {
  full: [
    "id",
    "optunaNumber",
    "status",
    "objective",
    "stages",
    "metrics",
    "phaseStudy",
    "bestFlags",
    "actions",
  ],
  compact: [
    "id",
    "optunaNumber",
    "status",
    "objective",
    "phaseStudy",
    "bestFlags",
    "actions",
  ],
  minimal: [
    "id",
    "status",
    "objective",
    "bestFlags",
  ],
};

docs.title: Usage Examples
docs.text:
// Full trials list page
<UnifiedEntityTable
  entity="trials"
  rows={trials}
  variant="full"
  page={page}
  pageSize={pageSize}
  totalPages={totalPages}
  totalItems={totalItems}
  sort={sort}
  onSort={setSort}
  onPrevious={() => goToPage(page - 1)}
  onNext={() => goToPage(page + 1)}
  context={{ experimentId, studyId }}
  launchForm={launchForm}
  showDensityToggle={true}
/>

// Compact trials in experiment detail
<UnifiedEntityTable
  entity="trials"
  rows={trials}
  variant="compact"
  page={1}
  pageSize={10}
  totalPages={1}
  totalItems={trials.length}
  sort={sort}
  onSort={setSort}
  onPrevious={() => {}}
  onNext={() => {}}
  context={{ experimentId }}
  showDensityToggle={false}
/>

// Trainings list
<UnifiedEntityTable
  entity="trainings"
  rows={trainings}
  variant="full"
  // ... same pagination/sorting props
/>

// Experiments list
<UnifiedEntityTable
  entity="experiments"
  rows={experiments}
  variant="full"
  // ... same pagination/sorting props
/>

// Backtests list
<UnifiedEntityTable
  entity="backtests"
  rows={backtestSessions}
  variant="full"
  // ... same pagination/sorting props
/>

│   └── ...
└── README.md                        # Usage documentation

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: ONE context card component that shows entity summaries (Experiment, Training, Trial) across all pages.

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• Multi-entity support: experiment/training/trial
  • Variant system: full/compact
  • Configurable actions: all/subset/custom
  • Metrics display: Flexible metric rows
  • Type-safe props

docs.title: Props Interface
docs.text:
interface UnifiedContextCardProps {
  // Entity type and data
  entity: "experiment" | "training" | "trial";
  data: ExperimentDetail | TrainingSummary | TrialSummary;

  // Display
  variant: "full" | "compact";

  // Actions
  actions?: "all" | "subset" | CustomAction[];
  showRequirements?: boolean; // For experiments only

  // Context for routing
  experimentId?: number;
  studyId?: number;
}

interface CustomAction {
  label: string;
  href: string;
}

docs.title: Usage Examples
docs.text:
// Experiment context in trials page
<UnifiedContextCard
  entity="experiment"
  data={experiment}
  variant="full"
  actions="all"
  showRequirements={false}
/>

// Experiment context in experiment detail
<UnifiedContextCard
  entity="experiment"
  data={experiment}
  variant="full"
  actions="all"
  showRequirements={true}
/>

// Training context in trials page
<UnifiedContextCard
  entity="training"
  data={training}
  variant="compact"
  actions="subset"
  experimentId={experimentId}
/>

docs.title: Replaces
docs.text:
• TrialsExperimentContext
  • TrainingExperimentContext
  • ExperimentDetailHeader
  • BacktestsExperimentContext
  • TrialsTrainingContext
  • All other context/header duplicates

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: ONE page wrapper that handles the common list page pattern: filters → context → table → pagination.

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• Clear visual hierarchy: Filters visually connected to tables
  • Flexible sections: Context, launch panel, table
  • Consistent spacing
  • Loading/error states

docs.title: Props Interface
docs.text:
interface EntityListPageLayoutProps {
  // Page metadata
  title: string;
  subtitle: string;

  // Sections (all optional)
  filters?: ReactNode;
  contextCard?: ReactNode;
  launchPanel?: ReactNode;
  table: ReactNode;

  // States
  loading?: boolean;
  error?: string | null;

  // Additional content
  children?: ReactNode;
}

docs.title: Usage Example
docs.text:
<EntityListPageLayout
  title="Trials"
  subtitle="Review trial outcomes and launch new backtests from selected models."
  filters={
    <TrialsFilters
      experimentFilter={experimentFilter}
      statusFilter={statusFilter}
      // ...
    />
  }
  contextCard={
    contextExperiment.data ? (
      <UnifiedContextCard
        entity="experiment"
        data={contextExperiment.data}
        variant="full"
        actions="all"
      />
    ) : null
  }
  launchPanel={
    <TrialBacktestLaunchPanel
      contextExperimentId={contextExperimentId}
      launchForm={launchForm}
    />
  }
  table={
    <UnifiedEntityTable
      entity="trials"
      rows={trials.data?.items ?? []}
      variant="full"
      // ...
    />
  }
  loading={trials.loading}
  error={trials.error}
/>

docs.title: Implementation Order
docs.text:
1. Phase 1: Create column definitions for all entities
  2. Phase 2: Implement UnifiedEntityTable
  3. Phase 3: Implement UnifiedContextCard
  4. Phase 4: Implement EntityListPageLayout
  5. Phase 5: Replace all legacy components (all entities at once)
  6. Phase 6: Test all pages
  7. Phase 7: Delete all legacy components

  • [ ] Backtests in experiment context (/experiments/{id}/backtests/)

docs.title: Sorting Verification
docs.text: For EACH page above, verify: - [ ] ALL columns are sortable (except actions/complex cells) - [ ] Clicking column header changes sort - [ ] Sort direction indicator shows correctly - [ ] Data actually sorts correctly

docs.title: Density Verification
docs.text:
• [ ] Density toggle appears on full variant tables
  • [ ] Density toggle does NOT appear on compact/minimal tables
  • [ ] Changing density persists in localStorage
  • [ ] Each table has independent density setting

docs.title: Visual Verification
docs.text:
• [ ] Filters are visually connected to tables
  • [ ] Clear hierarchy: filters → context → table
  • [ ] No confusion about what filters affect what
  • [ ] Consistent spacing and alignment

docs.title: Success Criteria
docs.text:
✅ Code Quality: - No duplicated table components - No duplicated context components - Clean, modular, reusable code - Type-safe throughout - No legacy code remaining

✅ Functionality: - ALL columns sortable (where appropriate) - Pagination works on all pages - Density toggle works and persists - Context routing works correctly - All actions work (Detail, Backtests, etc.)

✅ User Experience: - Clear visual hierarchy - Obvious filter → table connection - Consistent behavior across all pages - No visual regressions

✅ Maintainability: - Easy to add new columns - Easy to add new entities - Easy to modify variants - Well-documented code

docs.title: Modelos para ventanas pequeñas en trading con matriz tiempo×features
Qué problema estás resolviendo
docs.text:
Tu entrada es una ventana con forma [B, W, F], donde W suele estar entre 30 y 100 y F entre 30 y 300. Eso coincide con el encuadre clásico de “time series classification”: un modelo toma una secuencia (univariada o multivariante) y produce una distribución de probabilidad sobre clases. citeturn4search1

Tus salidas no son una serie futura de precios. Son tres predicciones sobre el “estado” del snapshot actual:

  • label (NONE / LONG / SHORT): una clase.
  • strength en [0,1]: un escalar.
  • quality en [0,1]: un escalar.

Strength y quality son regresiones, pero no son “forecast” de valores de la serie (no estás prediciendo close[t+1], close[t+2], etc.). Son medidas derivadas de un evento futuro que construye tu labeling (movimiento y trayectoria) y que vos querés estimar desde la ventana actual.

La confusión es normal porque ambos casos miran al futuro. La diferencia práctica es el tipo de target: forecasting suele significar “predicción de datos en tiempos futuros usando observaciones pasadas”, con targets que son valores futuros de la serie. citeturn5search4 En tu caso, el target es “clase + atributos” calculados a partir del futuro.

docs.title: Por qué no lo llamo forecasting, aunque sí “predice futuro”
docs.text:
En papers de forecasting, el output típico es una secuencia de valores futuros (uno o varios pasos) y el entrenamiento se centra en pérdidas estilo MSE/MAE sobre esos valores. citeturn5search4turn1search3

En tu pipeline, el output es una decisión (clasificación) y dos scores (regresión de atributos). Eso encaja en “clasificación/regresión supervisada sobre ventanas” más que en forecasting de señal. La propia literatura de time series classification define el problema como el aprendizaje de un mapeo desde series (uni o multivariantes) hacia etiquetas. citeturn4search1

Esto importa porque gran parte de los Transformers “de time series” se diseñaron y se comparan en benchmarks de forecasting, con objetivos y métricas que no coinciden con tu gating. Por ejemplo, PatchTST, iTransformer y Crossformer están formulados como forecasting multivariante. citeturn0search1turn0search2turn0search3 Aun así, muchas ideas se transfieren si vos las usás como extractor de representación y mantenés tus tres heads.

También existe una línea crítica que discute que, en forecasting, baselines lineales simples pueden superar a Transformers en varios datasets, lo cual es una señal de que el “qué tarea exacta” y el “qué inductive bias” pesan. citeturn1search3 Eso no invalida Transformers para tu caso, pero sí sugiere que conviene testear baselines no-attention en tu setup.

docs.title: La matriz como “imagen” del snapshot
docs.text:
Sí, entiendo el concepto: construís una matriz 2D consistente (tiempo en un eje, features en el otro) que representa un snapshot del mercado “ahora”. Luego querés clasificar ese snapshot (L/S/N) y estimar strength/quality. Eso es una formulación natural de clasificación de ventanas.

No es un concepto aislado: hay una línea de “time series to image” que justamente transforma series en imágenes para poder usar modelos de visión. Wang & Oates proponen codificar series como Gramian Angular Fields y Markov Transition Fields para habilitar técnicas de visión en clasificación e imputación. citeturn7search1turn7search5 También hay trabajos que tratan la clasificación como un problema de “textura” tras convertir a representaciones 2D (por ejemplo recurrence plots) y entrenan CNNs. citeturn7search2 Hay propuestas recientes de “time series as screenshot images” para clasificación multivariante, con comparativas en benchmarks. citeturn7search3turn7search19

image_group{"layout":"carousel","aspect_ratio":"16:9","query":["time series heatmap time x features snapshot","Gramian Angular Field time series image","recurrence plot time series image","Vision Transformer patch embedding diagram"],"num_per_query":1}

Dónde aparece el matiz: en visión, muchas arquitecturas (en especial CNNs) traen supuestos sobre vecindad y desplazamiento (un kernel se “desliza” y busca patrones repetibles). En una matriz tiempo×features, el eje “features” no tiene una geometría natural, salvo la que vos le impongas. Si permutás features, la “imagen” cambia en un sentido que no es análogo a mover un objeto dentro de una foto.

Con Transformers tipo ViT, el supuesto de desplazamiento es menor, pero igual existe un punto: para que el modelo use ubicación, se agregan embeddings de posición. En ViT, el input se parte en patches, se embebe cada patch, se suman position embeddings y se pasa por un Transformer encoder. citeturn6search0turn6search3 Eso aplica si tokenizás tu matriz como patches 2D (bloques tiempo×feature). Si no codificás posición en ambos ejes, el modelo pierde información de “dónde está” cada patch.

Tu modelo actual usa position embedding solo para el eje temporal (timestep). Las features entran en una proyección lineal Linear(F -> hidden_dim) por timestep. Eso funciona y, de hecho, es una forma estándar de procesar secuencias con muchos canales, pero deja el “modelado explícito de correlaciones entre features” en una zona indirecta: parte en la proyección y parte en el MLP interno del encoder.

Tu frase “no importa de dónde viene, importa el patrón” es válida como intención. El detalle es que el modelo necesita alguna forma de identidad: si dos features distintas tienen patrones con la misma forma, el modelo decide si son intercambiables o no según lo que pueda inferir del entrenamiento. En tu pipeline, la identidad hoy está en el orden fijo más la proyección aprendida.

Tu comentario sobre el orden también es correcto: entrenar con un orden y predecir con otro rompe el contrato de input. Esa es una condición de consistencia de cualquier arquitectura supervisada que no incluya un mecanismo explícito de “feature id”. Esto no es un tema de 1D vs 2D; es un tema de mapeo entrada→salida.

docs.title: Por qué el multi-timeframe pesa, incluso si “es solo un patrón”
docs.text:
No es un “problema” como error conceptual. Es una fuente de ambigüedad para el modelo.

En multi-timeframe, muchas features se actualizan a ritmos distintos. Si en tu ventana de 50 horas incluís una feature de 1 día, esa feature queda “plana” durante gran parte del eje temporal, con saltos cuando cambia el día. Eso genera patrones de repetición que el modelo puede aprender como atajos, o puede confundir con señales reales si no hay información adicional.

Además, tu índice de lag (t-0, t-1, …) no representa el mismo Δt real para todas las features cuando mezclás timeframes. Eso se parece a un caso de muestreo irregular/asíncrono por variable: cada variable tiene su propio patrón de observación. En esa familia de problemas, hay trabajos que incorporan explícitamente intervalos de tiempo como input, porque “cuánto tiempo pasó” cambia el significado. GRU-D, por ejemplo, integra masking y time interval para capturar patrones de observación y missingness. citeturn2search2turn2search10 Modelos tipo Neural CDE están formulados para series multivariantes con muestreo irregular y parcial, donde el tiempo entre observaciones importa. citeturn2search3turn2search11

Si no querés irte a continuous-time, hay un punto intermedio: agregar representaciones de tiempo que el modelo pueda usar como feature. Time2Vec propone un embedding de tiempo que se puede agregar a arquitecturas existentes. citeturn2search1 En Transformers para series, el tema “posición absoluta vs relativa vs combinación” también aparece como foco: ConvTran revisa estas opciones en clasificación multivariante y propone variantes (tAPE y eRPE) para mejorar generalización. citeturn2search0turn4search7

En tu caso, “MTF pesa” por dos razones operativas:

  • Semántica de “edad” de la señal: una señal de 1D con el mismo valor en 10 timesteps no está describiendo 10 mediciones nuevas; está describiendo 1 medición que se arrastra.
  • Mezcla de escalas: una misma forma visual sobre el eje temporal puede corresponder a escalas distintas dependiendo del timeframe del feature.

Ambas cosas se pueden ignorar y, a veces, igual funciona. El punto es que, cuando falla, suele fallar con comportamiento que parece “random” o “colapso” porque el modelo no tiene variables para desambiguar.

docs.title: Familias de modelos para tus tamaños W=30–100 y F=30–300
docs.text:
Transformers son una recomendación común porque self-attention conecta posiciones sin restricciones y permite interacción global dentro de la ventana. citeturn0search0 En tu rango de W, la complejidad cuadrática en W no es un freno. El debate tiende a moverse desde “si attention escala” hacia “qué tokenización y qué sesgo inductivo conviene”.

Transformers con foco en posición y clasificación Tu backbone actual es un Transformer encoder sobre tokens temporales, con embeddings de posición aprendidos. El punto frágil acá suele ser la codificación de orden: self-attention por sí sola no impone orden, por eso el Transformer original requiere positional encoding. citeturn0search0 En clasificación multivariante, ConvTran muestra que el tipo de position encoding cambia resultados y propone tAPE/eRPE más una etapa conv para embedding. citeturn2search0turn4search7 Esto es un candidato directo si querés “seguir en Transformer” y mover una pieza con impacto.

Tokenización por patches sobre el eje temporal PatchTST segmenta la serie en patches (subseries) que se usan como tokens. citeturn0search1turn0search5 Aunque el paper está en forecasting, la idea de patching sirve para ventanas chicas porque fuerza una representación local antes de que attention mezcle todo. Con W=30–100, patches de 5–10 te dejan 3–20 tokens.

Atención sobre variables (features como tokens) para capturar correlación multi-symbol iTransformer plantea que, cuando armás tokens por timestamp y fusionás muchas variables dentro de un token, podés perder representaciones por variable y terminar con mapas de atención sin interpretación. Propone invertir dimensiones: cada variable se vuelve un token y attention captura correlaciones multivariantes. citeturn0search2turn0search6 Para tu caso con F hasta 300 y correlaciones multi-symbol, esto calza con tu necesidad de “correlación entre instrumentos/indicadores” dentro del snapshot. El costo es O(F^2) en atención, que es otro régimen que el token temporal.

Modelos que diseñan explícitamente dependencia entre dimensiones Crossformer plantea “cross-dimension dependency” para series multivariantes. citeturn0search3turn0search11 Está formulado como forecasting, pero la tesis de “no tratar features como solo canales pasivos” es relevante cuando F es grande y hay mezcla de símbolos y signals.

Baselines no-attention que suelen rendir en clasificación de ventanas Un baseline que vale en tu rango es CNN/TCN. El trabajo de Bai et al. muestra que una arquitectura convolucional puede superar RNNs en tareas de secuencia y discute memoria efectiva. citeturn1search0turn1search4 Para clasificación, InceptionTime define el problema de clasificación de series y propone una familia CNN con resultados en benchmarks y foco en escalabilidad. citeturn1search1turn1search5 Si tu señal real está en patrones locales dentro de W=30–100, este tipo de inductive bias encaja.

ROCKET como baseline de “transformación + clasificador lineal” ROCKET usa kernels conv aleatorios para transformar la serie y entrena un clasificador lineal, con foco en velocidad y accuracy en clasificación. citeturn1search2turn1search6 Para vos sirve como control: si ROCKET se acerca a tu Transformer, tu cuello de botella puede estar fuera del backbone (label noise, gating, calibración).

MLP mixers y la línea de “menos arquitectura, más bias” En forecasting, LTSF-Linear cuestiona la efectividad de Transformers y muestra baselines lineales fuertes en varios datasets. citeturn1search3turn1search15 TSMixer propone una arquitectura all-MLP que mezcla sobre ejes tiempo y features. citeturn3search2turn3search6 Aunque el framing es forecasting, el bloque “mixing en dos ejes” es cercano a tu idea de matriz 2D.

Backbones alternativos a attention para secuencias S4 propone structured state space models como backbone para secuencias largas. citeturn3search0turn3search4 Mamba propone selective state space models y un backbone sin attention orientado a secuencias densas. citeturn3search1turn3search5 En W=30–100 no lo necesitás por longitud, pero sí puede ser un test de “dinámica temporal” distinta, con otro comportamiento de optimización.

Self-supervised pretraining para usar data sin labels TS2Vec propone aprendizaje contrastivo para representaciones universales de series y evalúa en clasificación y forecasting. citeturn3search3turn3search7 Si tenés mucho histórico sin labels o labels caros/rígidos, esto te da un camino: pretrain encoder, luego entrenar tus tres heads.

docs.title: Cómo rehacer el research como plan de decisión para tu pipeline
docs.text:
La forma de no “cambiar el problema” al cambiar el backbone es fijar el contrato:

Entrada Mantener [B, W, F] con orden de features estable. Tu condición de “mismo feature order en train e inference” es parte del contrato del modelo.

Salidas Mantener tres heads y las mismas activaciones/rangos (logits, strength en [0,1], quality en [0,1]). Esto hace comparables los resultados con tu gating.

Ablaciones que te contestan tus dudas conceptuales Una ablation te dice si “imagen” te sirve sin pagar costo de complejidad: entrenar un 2D-CNN o un ViT 2D (patches tiempo×feature) versus el Transformer temporal actual. La literatura de time-series-to-image muestra que convertir series a imágenes permite usar modelos de visión, pero también introduce una representación elegida (GAF/MTF/RP). citeturn7search1turn7search2turn7search19 En tu caso, ya tenés una “imagen” directa (heatmap tiempo×feature) sin pasar por GAF/MTF. El test te dice si tu representación ya alcanza.

El tema MTF se testea con una intervención mínima: agregar inputs de “timeframe id” o “edad” por feature. Si eso sube estabilidad o generalización, confirmás que había ambigüedad temporal. La idea de usar intervalos de tiempo como parte del input aparece en GRU-D y en modelos para muestreo irregular como Neural CDE. citeturn2search2turn2search3 Si preferís algo simple, Time2Vec es una opción para inyectar tiempo como embedding. citeturn2search1
