Criterio de elección de arquitectura con F hasta 300 Si tu señal depende de correlaciones multi-symbol, conviene un candidato “feature-centric”: iTransformer (features como tokens) o algo tipo cross-dimension (Crossformer). citeturn0search2turn0search3 Si tu señal depende de una forma temporal local por feature, conviene un candidato conv o patching: TCN/InceptionTime o patch tokens. citeturn1search0turn1search1turn0search1

Validación temporal y evaluación En finanzas, el split temporal y la prevención de leakage importan. La discusión de purging/embargo y validación en finanzas aparece en el material de López de Prado. citeturn5search3turn5search16 Eso encaja con tu Stage1/2/3 y con la necesidad de que la mejora sea backtestable, no solo “offline”.

Lo que te deja este encuadre es una regla: tu tarea no es forecasting de valores, pero sí es predicción supervisada sobre el futuro. Por eso podés tomar ideas de forecasting, pero tenés que filtrar por: tokenización, codificación de orden, y cómo el modelo usa correlación entre features, porque ahí está tu problema real con W chico, F grande, MTF y multi-symbol.

docs.title: Model options for small-window multi-timeframe trading sequences
Framing your current setup and the core modeling problem
docs.text:
Your current “TradingViT” is a Transformer encoder that consumes a 2D time-series window shaped as [batch, seq_len, features], projects features to an embedding dimension, adds a learned positional embedding, runs a stack of self-attention layers, then pools across time and produces multi-head outputs (class + two bounded regressions). This is structurally closer to a standard Transformer encoder from “Attention Is All You Need” than to a classical vision ViT that forms 2D patches from an image grid. citeturn11search0turn11search1

From the perspective of research literature, your input is a multivariate time series window (MTS). The central question is how to (a) encode temporal order, (b) model cross-feature interactions, and (c) stay stable under small-window supervision and non-stationary market regimes. The Transformer family is attractive because self-attention can connect any two positions in a sequence. citeturn11search0turn13search11

Two details in your setting matter for model choice:

First, you treat the window as “ticks” where a tick can mean 1h bars or 1d bars, and features can themselves come from multiple timeframes aligned by timestamp. This creates a representation where position index is a lag counter, but the real elapsed time per step can differ by feature/timeframe. In the broader time-series literature this resembles misaligned or irregular sampling across variables, a case where adding explicit time-interval information or adopting continuous-time models can change results. citeturn6search2turn6search0turn3search3

Second, your pipeline evaluates with temporal splits and embargo-like logic. This matches a known issue in finance: naive random CV can leak future-dependent information. A finance-specific discussion of purging/embargo and time-aware validation is covered in entity["book","Advances in Financial Machine Learning","lopez de prado 2018"] and related material. citeturn14search1turn14search14

docs.title: Transformer-family time-series backbones worth testing
docs.text: A useful way to organize Transformer alternatives is by what they tokenize and how they inject inductive bias for time.

docs.title: Patch tokens instead of timestamp tokens
docs.text:
Patch-based tokenization adapts the key ViT idea (“tokens are patches”) from vision to time series. In ViT, an image is split into fixed patches and fed to a Transformer. citeturn11search1

For time series, PatchTST applies segmentation along the time axis into subseries “patches” that become tokens, often with a “channel-independence” design. This is motivated by long lookbacks, but patching can also help short windows by forcing a local aggregation prior to attention, which can act as a bias toward local patterns. citeturn0search0turn13search7

How this maps to your use: keep your multi-head outputs, replace the encoder with “patch embed → transformer over patches → pool → heads”. When seq_len=50, you can test patch sizes like 5 or 10 (tokens 10 or 5), which changes attention’s effective granularity. The PatchTST paper and the broader Transformer-in-time-series survey both describe patching as a response to limitations of timestamp-level tokenization. citeturn0search0turn13search7turn13search11

docs.title: Variable tokens instead of time tokens
docs.text:
A dominant failure mode for multivariate time series is collapsing many variables at each timestep into a single token embedding, which can blur variable-specific dynamics. iTransformer flips the dimensions: it embeds time points of each variable into “variate tokens” and applies attention across variables, while feed-forward layers operate per variable token. citeturn0search9turn0search1

For your setting (many features and a modest seq_len), a “variables-as-tokens” backbone can be a direct fit: each feature becomes one token, and each token contains the lag vector of length seq_len (embedded by a small MLP/1D conv). This also aligns with your “2D matrix” intuition: you are now tokenizing along the feature axis instead of the time axis. The iTransformer paper provides the rationale and empirical results in forecasting contexts; the architectural idea transfers to classification/regression heads. citeturn0search9turn13search11

docs.title: Explicit cross-dimension modeling
docs.text:
Crossformer targets multivariate time series by embedding the input into a 2D array and modeling cross-dimension dependency explicitly, rather than treating cross-variable interaction as a side effect of time-token embeddings. citeturn4search0turn4search4

This design direction is relevant when your signal depends on interactions between indicators (feature-feature) as much as on temporal patterns (time-time). It is also aligned with your idea of the window as a 2D object rather than a 1D sequence. citeturn4search0turn13search11

docs.title: Time-series Transformers with built-in priors (decomposition, periodicity, multi-scale)
docs.text:
Several time-series Transformers add structural priors. Autoformer integrates decomposition (trend/seasonal) and an autocorrelation mechanism as a replacement for standard self-attention in long-term forecasting. citeturn1search1turn1search5 FEDformer uses seasonal-trend decomposition and frequency-domain mechanisms for long horizons. citeturn1search2turn1search6 TimesNet models temporal variation in a 2D space with an emphasis on multi-periodicity and is presented as “task-general” across time-series tasks. citeturn1search15turn1search3 Multi-resolution Transformer lines also formalize multi-scale processing and cite patching as a mechanism derived from ViT-style tokenization. citeturn9search9turn11search1

For your use, these families matter less for computational scaling (50 steps is not long) and more for inductive bias: decomposition and periodicity priors can stabilize training when labels are sparse/noisy or when regime drift is present. citeturn13search11turn14search16

docs.title: Time-aware position encoding variants
docs.text:
Transformers do not encode order by default; they rely on positional information additions or changes to attention. citeturn11search0turn11search7 For time series classification, ConvTran specifically focuses on absolute and relative position encoding variants (tAPE + eRPE) and couples them with a convolution-based input encoding for multivariate time series classification. citeturn10search13

In your case, this is one of the most “surgical” transformer-family changes: keep your encoder stack, replace absolute learned position_embedding with a relative or hybrid scheme, and compare outcomes under different window sizes and multiple tick granularities. citeturn10search13turn11search7

image_group{"layout":"carousel","aspect_ratio":"16:9","query":["PatchTST time series transformer diagram","iTransformer inverted transformer time series architecture","Crossformer multivariate time series transformer architecture","TimesNet time series foundation model architecture"],"num_per_query":1}

docs.title: Non-Transformer baselines that often match or beat attention on short windows
docs.text: A recurring pattern in time-series research is that model class matters less than bias/regularization and evaluation protocol, and that non-attention models can dominate on many tasks and datasets.

docs.title: Temporal convolutional networks and Inception-style CNNs
docs.text:
Temporal Convolutional Networks (TCNs) use dilated causal convolutions to build receptive fields across time without recurrence. A systematic evaluation by Bai et al. reports that a generic convolutional architecture can outperform LSTMs across multiple sequence modeling tasks and can have longer effective memory. citeturn2search0turn13search0

For time-series classification, InceptionTime is a CNN ensemble inspired by Inception modules and is evaluated on standard time-series classification benchmarks, with claims about scalability compared to prior methods. citeturn0search3turn0search7

Why these belong in your shortlist: for seq_len≈50, convolution kernels with dilation or multi-scale filters match the “small windows” regime, and they impose a local pattern bias that can be useful in noisy financial data contexts. citeturn2search0turn0search7turn14search16

docs.title: ROCKET and MiniROCKET as “fast strong baselines” for window classification
docs.text:
ROCKET transforms a time series with many random convolutional kernels and trains a linear classifier on summary features. MiniROCKET reformulates ROCKET to reduce compute and increase determinism while maintaining reported accuracy levels on UCR benchmarks. citeturn7search0turn7search1turn7search4

These methods are not neural sequence models in the Transformer sense, but they are relevant because your core directional label is a discrete classification problem on fixed-length windows. Using them as baselines can clarify whether the expensive part of modeling is temporal pattern extraction or downstream decision calibration. citeturn7search0turn7search1

docs.title: Linear / MLP mixers as control points
docs.text:
The paper “Are Transformers Effective for Time Series Forecasting?” argues that self-attention is permutation-invariant and can lose temporal information, and shows that simple linear baselines (LTSF-Linear) outperform multiple Transformer variants on long-term forecasting benchmarks. citeturn5search0turn5search12 Related follow-on work includes Mixer-style architectures like TSMixer that alternate mixing across time and feature dimensions using MLP blocks. citeturn4search1turn4search17

Even if you are not doing forecasting, this line of results supports including at least one “no-attention” baseline: flatten+MLP, TCN, or a time-feature mixer. citeturn5search0turn4search1

docs.title: Structured state space models and Mamba-family models
docs.text:
Structured State Space models (S4) are proposed as sequence models derived from continuous-time state space formulations, with attention to computational feasibility and performance on long-range dependency benchmarks. citeturn3search0turn3search4 Mamba proposes selective state space models as a linear-time alternative backbone to attention and reports scaling to long sequences and throughput differences versus Transformers. citeturn3search1turn3search5turn13search6

For your “small window” regime, SSMs are not about enabling long context, but about providing an alternate inductive bias for temporal dynamics with different optimization behavior than attention. If your current Transformer shows collapse, unstable training, or sensitivity to positional encoding choices, SSM backbones can be a controlled experiment. citeturn3search0turn3search1turn10search13

docs.title: Continuous-time models for irregular or misaligned sampling
docs.text:
When observations are not aligned on a single uniform grid, approaches that embed time intervals explicitly or treat hidden state dynamics in continuous time become relevant.

GRU-D incorporates missingness masks and time intervals into a recurrent model for multivariate time series with missing values. citeturn6search2 Latent ODEs and ODE-RNNs define continuous-time hidden dynamics and can operate across arbitrary time gaps. citeturn6search1turn6search5 Neural Controlled Differential Equations (Neural CDEs) target irregularly sampled multivariate time series and report results against ODE/RNN baselines. citeturn6search0turn6search4 Time2Vec proposes a model-agnostic vector representation of time that can be used in many architectures. citeturn3search3

In your pipeline, the multi-timeframe alignment “by last timestamp ≤ t” can create effective irregularity across features when interpreted in real elapsed time. If you choose to represent that explicitly (time deltas per feature per lag), these model classes become more directly applicable. citeturn6search2turn6search1turn3search3

docs.title: Multi-timeframe representation strategies that match your window construction
docs.text:
Your current window is a 2D object (lags × features), but features may come from different timeframes and “lag index” does not correspond to a single shared Δt across features. The literature suggests several ways to address similar structure.

One approach is explicit time encoding: append time delta features or learn time embeddings. Time2Vec supports this as a drop-in time representation that can be combined with attention, convolutions, or MLPs. citeturn3search3 If you stay within the Transformer family, ConvTran’s focus on absolute/relative position and its multivariate classification framing make it relevant to this axis. citeturn10search13

A second approach is architectural separation by resolution: encode each timeframe group with its own encoder, then fuse. The multi-resolution Transformer paper is an example of encoding and fusing multiple resolutions in a formal way, albeit in forecasting contexts. citeturn9search9 TimesNet’s multi-periodicity framing also fits multi-timeframe mixtures, because multiple periodicities show up as multiple dominant scales. citeturn1search15turn1search3

A third approach is to change tokenization: iTransformer’s variable tokens or Crossformer’s cross-dimension design both push modeling capacity toward feature-feature structure, which can be useful when the “time axis” is partly a bookkeeping device for multiple time bases. citeturn0search9turn4search0

docs.title: Updated research directions for pretraining and transfer to your supervised heads
docs.text:
Small labeled datasets and regime drift motivate pretraining and transfer.

A Transformer-based representation learning framework for multivariate time series is presented by Zerveas et al., with an explicit goal of pretraining for downstream tasks including classification and regression. citeturn10search0turn10search8 TS2Vec is an unsupervised contrastive framework that learns timestamp-level representations and reports improvements on time-series classification benchmarks. citeturn5search1turn5search9 A survey on self-supervised learning for time series proposes a taxonomy across generative, contrastive, and adversarial families, which helps in selecting pretraining objectives. citeturn10search6turn10search14

On the foundation model side, recent work focuses on large-scale pretraining for forecasting. TimesFM is released by entity["company","Google","tech company"] Research as a decoder-only foundation model for time-series forecasting, with claims about zero-shot transfer across domains and granularities. citeturn5search2turn5search10turn5search6 Chronos is released by entity["company","Amazon","e-commerce and cloud"] as a pretrained probabilistic forecasting framework that tokenizes time series values and trains language-model-like architectures. citeturn5search3turn5search15 TimeGPT is proposed by entity["company","Nixtla","time series company"] as a foundation model for time series with reported zero-shot forecasting results. citeturn8search3turn8search7 Timer proposes GPT-style generative pretraining for time series and frames it as “large time series models.” citeturn10search3turn8search10

These models are trained for forecasting, not your label scheme, but they point to a transfer pattern: use pretrained encoders/embeddings and train your classification/regression heads on top, or fine-tune end-to-end if data and compute allow. citeturn10search0turn5search6turn5search3turn10search3

docs.title: Model shortlist and experimental matrix aligned with your pipeline
docs.text:
A practical selection strategy is to test across three axes: tokenization, temporal-order mechanism, and cross-feature mechanism, while keeping your heads and your Stage1/2/3 evaluation stable.

A minimal shortlist that spans the space:

A “convolution-first” backbone: TCN or InceptionTime-style CNN (plus your three heads). citeturn2search0turn0search7 A “Transformer with position focus” backbone: ConvTran-style position encoding + conv embedding + attention stack. citeturn10search13 A “patch-token” Transformer: PatchTST-like patch embed + Transformer. citeturn0search0turn13search7 A “feature-token” Transformer: iTransformer-like inversion (features as tokens, lag vectors embedded per feature). citeturn0search9turn0search1 A non-attention control: ROCKET/MiniROCKET or an MLP mixer. citeturn7search0turn7search1turn4search1 A non-Transformer sequence backbone: S4 or Mamba-style SSM blocks with your heads. citeturn3search0turn3search1

For your multi-timeframe setting, add one explicit test per model: with and without time-interval/timeframe encoding. Time2Vec is one method for time representation; continuous-time models (Neural CDE / Latent ODE) are another path if you make the time axis explicit rather than implicit. citeturn3search3turn6search0turn6search1

For evaluation, keep two checks separate:

Offline predictive checks: calibration of tradeability probabilities and regression head error conditional on tradeable labels. This follows from your pipeline design and aligns with multi-head supervision. Backtest checks: Stage3 behavior under entry gating and thresholds. In finance literature, regime drift and non-stationarity can reverse offline improvements, so stage-by-stage stability is a required filter. citeturn14search16turn14search2

docs.title: Roadmap de Baselines y Modelos para Rainbow Trader
1. Objetivo de este documento
docs.text:
Este documento deja un checkpoint operativo para:

  • No perder contexto de los 3 documentos de research ya generados.
  • Traducir ese research a un plan ejecutable en tu hardware real.
  • Empezar por baselines sólidos antes de escalar a modelos complejos.
  • Tener un roadmap por fases con tiempos estimados, decisiones y criterios de salida.

Este checkpoint consolida:

  • docs/researchs/_ML/ML-deep-research-report.md
  • docs/researchs/_ML/ML-deep-research-report-2.md
  • docs/researchs/_ML/sugerencias.md
  • Hallazgos verificados en código del repo (model-trainer, backtesting-engine, window-preparer).

docs.title: 2. Estado actual del pipeline (verificado en código)
2.1 Contrato de entrada y salida
docs.text:
• Input del modelo: tensor [B, W, F].
  • W: ventana temporal (lags).
  • F: número de features seleccionadas para el trial.
  • Salidas del modelo:
  • logits para clase NONE/LONG/SHORT.
  • strength en [0, 1].
  • quality en [0, 1].

docs.title: 2.2 Modelo actual
docs.text:
• Modelo actual en producción de entrenamiento: TradingViT (Transformer temporal).
  • No es ViT 2D de imagen con patching espacial clásico.
  • Es encoder temporal sobre tokens por timestep.

docs.title: 2.3 Punto crítico de negocio
docs.text:
combined_confidence es fórmula propietaria del pipeline, no estándar de ViT:

  • c = probability^w_p
  • sq = strength^w_s * quality^w_q
  • combined = c + alpha * (1 - c) * sq

La decisión de entrada usa gate con:

  • entry_threshold
  • min_quality_threshold

docs.title: 3.1 "Random con misma frecuencia de trades" significa
docs.text:
Frecuencia = tasa de entradas efectivas sobre total de ticks evaluados.

Se obtiene de métricas existentes del trial:

  • training_metrics.stage2_metrics.prediction_health.rates.entry_rate_over_total_predictions

También se puede usar:

  • entry_count / total_predictions

docs.title: 3.2 De dónde sacar esa frecuencia
docs.text:
Fuentes válidas, en orden:

  1. Frecuencia de labels de entrenamiento (solo train).
  2. Frecuencia de un modelo referencia en train/val.
  3. Nunca calibrar con test final para evitar leakage de decisión.

docs.title: 3.3 Baselines van primero
docs.text:
Sí. Primero baselines. Luego arquitectura.

Si no superamos baselines de forma robusta, cambiar modelo no agrega claridad.

docs.title: 4. Protocolo experimental fijo (obligatorio)
docs.text:
Antes de correr barridos grandes, congelar:

  • Split temporal fijo.
  • Costos de ejecución fijos (slippage, fees, reglas).
  • Métrica objetivo principal fija.
  • Métricas secundarias obligatorias fijas.
  • Mismo proceso de reporte para todos los modelos.

docs.title: 4.1 Ventanas temporales recomendadas para empezar
docs.text:
Propuesta inicial (iteración rápida y robusta):

  • Train dev: 2021-01-01 a 2024-12-31
  • Validation: 2025-01-01 a 2025-12-31
  • Test final: 2026-01-01 a 2026-02-25
  • Stress holdout opcional: 2018-01-01 a 2020-12-31

No usar 2018-2026 full para TODO desde día 1, porque es muy costoso y mezclará preguntas.

docs.title: 4.2 Métricas mínimas para decisión
docs.text:
• Objetivo principal: score final de Stage 1/2/3 (o variante aprobada).
  • Secundarias obligatorias:
  • stage3_return_pct
  • stage3_sharpe_ratio
  • stage3_win_rate
  • stage3_trades_count
  • max drawdown (si está disponible en el flujo actual o agregado en baseline report).

docs.title: 5. Baselines obligatorios (fase inicial)
docs.text: Baseline Qué hace Costo Para qué sirve Always-flat Siempre NONE Muy bajo Piso absoluto del sistema Random-L Random calibrado por frecuencia de labels Muy bajo Control estocástico sin depender de modelo Random-B Random calibrado por entry rate de modelo referencia Muy bajo Control comparativo de política de entradas Regla simple EMA cross / RSI threshold Bajo Baseline técnico clásico Lineal Logistic/linear head Bajo Saber si lo no lineal realmente aporta MLP chico Flatten + MLP pequeño Bajo-medio Baseline fuerte en ventanas chicas

docs.title: 5.1 Especificación de Random-L y Random-B
docs.text:
Para cada tick:

  • entry ~ Bernoulli(p_trade)
  • Si entry = 1:
  • LONG ~ Bernoulli(p_long_cond)
  • Si no LONG, entonces SHORT
  • Si entry = 0: NONE

Parámetros:

  • p_trade: frecuencia objetivo.
  • p_long_cond: proporción LONG dentro de trades.

Correr 30 a 50 seeds por baseline random para estimar media y dispersión.

docs.title: 6. Restricciones reales de cómputo (tu hardware)
docs.text:
Hardware reportado:

  • NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4070 Laptop GPU 8GB CUDA.

Costo actual observado:

  • Modelo actual con W~80, F~200, 35/50 epochs: ~4 a 5 horas por trial.

Implicación:

  • No es viable hacer "full optuna de todo" en todas las familias desde el inicio.
  • Necesitamos búsqueda jerárquica por etapas de costo.

docs.title: 7.1 Perfiles de trial
docs.text:
• Perfil S (screening):
  • 8-10 epochs
  • data_fraction ~ 0.2-0.25
  • mini-backtest
  • Perfil M (refine):
  • 18-22 epochs
  • data_fraction ~ 0.5
  • mini-backtest
  • Perfil L (confirmación):
  • 35-50 epochs
  • data_fraction = 1.0
  • full backtest

docs.title: 7.2 Presupuesto ejemplo para 500 trials
docs.text:
Propuesta realista:

  • 300 trials S
  • 150 trials M
  • 50 trials L

Tiempo total estimado aproximado:

  • S: 35-45 min/trial
  • M: 90-120 min/trial
  • L: 4-5 h/trial

Total estimado: ~626 a ~775 horas GPU.

Interpretación:

  • 24/7: ~26 a ~32 días.
  • 12h/día: ~52 a ~64 días.

docs.title: 8. Espacio de búsqueda recomendado para W y F
docs.text:
No abrir extremos desde inicio.

Fase inicial:

  • W: {32, 48, 64, 80}
  • F: {64, 128, 192}

Regla:

  • Solo expandir a W > 80/100 o F > 192/224 si hay señal clara de mejora cerca del borde.

docs.title: 9. Roadmap por fases (de simple a complejo)
docs.text: Fase Objetivo Modelos/Acciones Salida esperada 0 Congelar protocolo Split, métricas, reporting, seeds Base comparable estable 1 Baselines Flat, Random-L, Random-B, reglas, lineal, MLP chico Piso y referencias sólidas 2 Mapa W/F barato Barrido rápido con MLP/TCN chico Rango útil de W y F 3 Refinar ViT actual Capacidad, regularización, pos encoding, threshold tuning Mejor versión de referencia 4 Primer nuevo backbone fuerte TCN/1D-CNN Comparación justa vs ViT 5 Transformers alternativos Patch temporal, iTransformer-style Medir tokenización alternativa 6 Híbridos y cross-dim CNN+Transformer, Crossformer-like Ver interacción local+global 7 Secuenciales alternativos S4/Mamba (piloto) Validar sesgo temporal alternativo 8 Pretraining/transfer TS2Vec y foundation-model transfer Potencial extra en data limitada

docs.title: 10. Catálogo completo de ideas (pros, cons, implementación)
docs.text: Familia Pros Contras Implementación Prioridad ViT temporal refinado Cambios mínimos sobre lo actual Puede sobreparametrizar en W chico Ajustes en vit.py Alta TCN / 1D-CNN Muy fuerte en ventanas pequeñas, estable Menos flexible global Nuevo tcn.py + factory + Optuna Alta MLP flatten Barato y rápido, baseline útil Overfit si crece mucho Nuevo mlp.py simple Alta (baseline) Patch Transformer Patching temporal reduce ruido local Más diseño de tokenización Nuevo vit_patch.py Media iTransformer-style Potencia correlaciones feature-feature Costo puede subir con F alto Nuevo itransformer.py Media Crossformer-like Cross-dim explícito Complejo de implementar Nuevo backbone dedicado Media-baja CNN + Transformer Buen balance local/global Más complejidad y tuning Nuevo cnn_transformer.py Media ROCKET/MiniROCKET Baseline de clasificación muy fuerte Integración distinta a pipeline NN Baseline externo/adaptador Media S4/Mamba Sesgo temporal distinto, potencial estabilidad Beneficio incierto para W corto Nuevo backbone complejo Baja Time2Vec Inyección temporal barata Mejora no garantizada Feature/embedding adicional Media GRU-D / Neural CDE / Latent ODE Bueno para irregularidad explícita Requiere rediseño de input Nueva línea de modelado Baja TS2Vec Buen pretraining SSL Flujo adicional de entrenamiento Etapa de pretraining + fine-tune Baja-media TimesFM/Chronos/TimeGPT/Timer (transfer) Potencial transferencia Integración costosa y no directa al target Adaptación avanzada Baja

docs.title: 11. Integración de un modelo nuevo en este repo
docs.text:
Checklist mínimo para cualquier nuevo backbone entrenable:

  1. Crear archivo en modules/model-trainer/src/rainbow/model_trainer/models/.
  2. Heredar contrato de BaseModel y devolver siempre:
  3. logits
  4. strength
  5. quality
  6. Registrar en models/factory.py.
  7. Incluir hiperparámetros en helpers/hyperparams_training.py.
  8. Agregar entrada en config/experiments/optuna.yaml:
  9. model_space.choices
  10. model_hyperparameters.<nuevo_modelo>
  11. Ajustar planes JSON de experimento para habilitar el modelo.
  12. Agregar tests de shape/forward y smoke de entrenamiento.

docs.title: 12. Política de Optuna recomendada
docs.text:
No usar full-search global desde el inicio.

Usar búsqueda por etapas:

  • Etapa A: seleccionar familia con hyper reducido.
  • Etapa B: expandir hyper dentro de top 2-3 familias.
  • Etapa C: confirmar en perfil L con full backtest.

Congelar hiperparámetros cuando corresponda:

  • Al explorar modelo, congelar parte de loss y thresholds.
  • Al explorar loss/thresholds, congelar arquitectura top.

docs.title: 13. Qué esperamos validar en cada etapa
Etapa de baselines
docs.text:
• Que cualquier modelo serio supere a random de forma robusta.
  • Que no estemos ganando por artifacts de evaluación.

docs.title: Etapa de familia de modelos
docs.text:
• Si el gap viene del backbone o del gating/política.
  • Qué sesgo inductivo funciona mejor para W chico y F alto.

docs.title: Etapa final
docs.text:
• Robustez temporal (validation + test + stress).
  • Estabilidad de trades y no solo mejora en un único score.

docs.title: 14. Primer sprint recomendado (acción inmediata)
docs.text:
Orden de ejecución para iniciar ya:

  1. Implementar baselines always-flat, random-L, random-B.
  2. Implementar baseline de regla simple (EMA/RSI).
  3. Correr torneo de baselines con seeds múltiples.
  4. Definir umbrales de "modelo aceptable" sobre esos resultados.
  5. Recién ahí iniciar fase 3 de refinamiento ViT.

docs.title: 15. Decisiones abiertas (para no bloquear)
docs.text:
• Definir objetivo primario exacto de ranking para esta campaña.
  • Confirmar ventana temporal oficial de train/val/test.
  • Confirmar presupuesto total de tiempo (modo 24/7 o diario).
  • Confirmar si 500 trials se mantiene como presupuesto total de campaña.

Este archivo es el checkpoint operativo para iniciar roadmap y baselines sin perder contexto.

docs.title: Baselines
docs.text:
Always-flat / Persistence (“siempre NONE”) Funciona como piso absoluto: nunca abre trades. En tu pipeline: signal=NONE, trade_probabilities=0, combined_confidence=0 siempre. Sirve para saber si el modelo realmente agrega valor o solo genera ruido/costos.

Random frequency-matched (Baseline A) Genera entradas aleatorias, pero con la misma tasa de entrada que tu sistema objetivo. En tu pipeline:

entry ~ Bernoulli(p_trade) Si entra: LONG/SHORT con proporción p_long y 1-p_long Si no entra: NONE p_trade lo sacás de train/val (ej: prediction_health.rates.entry_rate_over_total_predictions), nunca de test final. Reglas simples (EMA/RSI) Sistema deterministico con 1-2 indicadores fijos. En tu pipeline: produce LONG/SHORT/NONE y pasa por el mismo backtest y mismas reglas de ejecución. Sirve para comparar contra una estrategia interpretable y barata.

Lineal / Logístico Modelo sin deep net, con mismas features. En tu pipeline: puede ser multiclass (NONE/LONG/SHORT) o binario de entry + dirección separada. Es baseline muy importante para validar que la no linealidad sí aporta.

MLP chico (flatten) [W,F] -> flatten -> capas densas chicas -> heads. En tu pipeline: mantiene tus 3 salidas (logits, strength, quality). Suele sorprender en ventanas pequeñas; barato comparado con Transformer grande.

TCN / 1D-CNN (incluye línea InceptionTime) Baseline “deep” fuerte para secuencia corta. En tu pipeline: conv temporal sobre eje W, después heads actuales. Muy recomendado por relación costo/rendimiento para W chico y ruido financiero.

ROCKET / MiniROCKET Transformación con kernels aleatorios + clasificador lineal. En tu pipeline: útil como baseline externo de clasificación direccional (mínimo para label). Si esto se acerca a ViT, el cuello de botella no está en arquitectura compleja.

LTSF-Linear (lineal temporal fuerte) Baseline lineal puro de literatura de series temporales. En tu caso: se usa como control de “simple > complejo” en algunas condiciones. No reemplaza todo tu multi-head, pero es excelente referencia de dificultad real del problema.

TSMixer (MLP mixer tiempo/features) Baseline no-attention intermedio entre lineal y Transformer. En tu pipeline: mezclas por eje tiempo y eje feature, luego tus heads. Útil para probar si attention realmente es necesaria.

Ablation 2D: 2D-CNN / ViT 2D sobre matriz tiempo×features Baseline conceptual para testear tu hipótesis “snapshot tipo imagen”. En tu pipeline: compara representación 2D vs encoder temporal actual, manteniendo mismas salidas y scoring. Si no gana, evitás complejidad extra.

Aclaración importante En los docs también aparecen PatchTST, iTransformer, Crossformer, CNN+Transformer, S4, Mamba, etc. Esos son más “candidatos de arquitectura” que baseline inicial.

Orden recomendado solo de baselines (práctico)

Always-flat Random frequency-matched Reglas EMA/RSI Lineal/logístico MLP chico TCN/1D-CNN ROCKET/MiniROCKET TSMixer / LTSF-Linear Ablation 2D-CNN/ViT2D

docs.title: Rainbow trading thesis literature review and validation framework
docs.text: Markdown file: rainbow_thesis_deep_research.md

docs.title: What your codebase is already set up to prove
docs.text:
Your repositories already encode a falsification-style thesis test: separate “patterned,” “pattern-free,” and “real-market” data sources; run both real models and calibrated random baselines through the same pipeline; and optionally inject controlled leakage to verify the end-to-end flow and measure how much “cheating” changes results. fileciteturn32file0L1-L1

At the experiment level, your core config object explicitly treats “data provider” as a first-class lever (ExperimentConfig.data_provider) alongside the base symbol/timeframe, global range, feature window definition, Optuna settings, scoring settings, and a progressive “phases” system (PhasesConfig). fileciteturn32file0L1-L1

Your repo includes ready-to-run experiment templates that operationalize your “synthetic controls” idea:

  • A dummy experiment template (data_provider: "dummy", symbol DUMMYUSDT, 1h) that uses a small feature set (close + RSI) and a bounded Optuna search space to keep runs tractable. fileciteturn41file1L1-L1
  • A random experiment template (data_provider: "random", symbol RANDOMUSDT, 1h) that mirrors the dummy setup but is intended to be patternless under the null, again with a small Optuna search space. fileciteturn43file0L1-L1

At the configuration layer, you also define DummyProviderConfig as a synthetic sine-wave generator (amplitude / phase / period in candles) and RandomProviderConfig as a seeded random-walk style generator with configurable “jump” distribution. fileciteturn8file0L1-L1

A dedicated “dummy pipeline” script in rainbow-trader shows the intended full pipeline order (sync data → process features → create experiment → generate labels → build windows → train → backtest) and is designed to let you swap providers via config overrides. fileciteturn41file0L1-L1

Crucially, you already implemented two “controlled leakage / controlled shortcut” features that map directly to your thesis validation narrative:

  • A LEAK indicator that explicitly introduces temporal leakage by shifting an input series backward (bringing future values into the present), for sanity-checks and leak-sensitivity experiments. fileciteturn29file0L1-L1
  • A label-copy signal that recomputes LONG/SHORT labels using the same labeling algorithm as your labeling service, then exposes them as binary feature columns—effectively a “cheat code” feature to prove models can recover an extremely direct signal when it exists. fileciteturn29file19L1-L1

On the modeling side, you built a family of random baselines intended to be run through the exact same training/backtesting machinery as the real networks. The model-type enum includes both real models (e.g., ViT variants) and random baselines (always-flat, label-prior matched, entry-policy matched, Markov). fileciteturn23file6L1-L1 That “random-but-calibrated” design is very important for thesis-grade inference: it makes the null hypothesis stronger than naïve coin-flips, reducing the chance you “win” because a model merely exploits class imbalance or trivial persistence rather than learning market structure.

Finally, your stack already centralizes “what counts as better” into the Optuna scoring layer: you translate backtest metrics (e.g., profitability, drawdown, Sharpe, win rate) into normalized stage scores and a weighted composite objective for trial comparison. fileciteturn16file4L1-L1

docs.title: Market efficiency and why your thesis is not automatically anti-EMH
docs.text:
The Efficient Market Hypothesis (EMH) is often summarized as “markets are unpredictable,” but the stronger claim is about whether abnormal risk-adjusted profits are possible once information is public and trading costs/competition are considered. The canonical survey formalized the “weak,” “semi-strong,” and “strong” forms and framed the empirical problem as joint tests of market efficiency and an asset-pricing model. citeturn0search2

A key nuance for your thesis write-up is that “evidence against a pure random walk” is not identical to “guaranteed exploitable edge at scale.” In fact, classic tests reject the random-walk hypothesis in equity data, for example through variance-ratio style tests, without necessarily implying that a specific trading rule will survive costs, market impact, and competition. citeturn2search0turn2search4

Your thesis framing fits well with the Adaptive Markets Hypothesis (AMH): markets can look efficient in some regimes and less efficient in others because the ecology of participants, constraints, and available technology changes over time. AMH explicitly reframes “efficiency” as evolving rather than fixed, which matches your intuition about regime dependency and why a system should be evaluated across multiple market environments. citeturn0search1

Empirical finance also documents persistent return patterns (often treated as “anomalies”) such as momentum—strategies that buy recent winners and sell recent losers generating positive average returns in certain horizons—again with caveats around implementation realism and robustness over time. citeturn2search5

docs.title: Machine learning and deep learning in finance, with emphasis on transformers and ViT
docs.text:
A large, credible stream of ML-in-finance evidence focuses on the cross-section of expected returns (forecasting relative returns across many assets rather than directionally trading a single series). A flagship result is the “Empirical Asset Pricing via Machine Learning” work by entity["people","Shihao Gu","finance researcher"], entity["people","Bryan Kelly","finance professor"], and entity["people","Dacheng Xiu","finance professor"], which benchmarks many ML methods and reports meaningful economic gains in portfolio implementations relative to traditional linear models, attributing gains to nonlinear interactions among predictors. citeturn4search4

A second stream is market microstructure and ultra-short-horizon predictability, where the “signal” (if any) is often tied to order flow and limit order book dynamics rather than daily OHLCV. DeepLOB is a widely cited deep learning approach for mid-price movement prediction from limit order book data, and is frequently discussed as evidence that deep networks can extract stable patterns from certain microstructure-rich datasets. citeturn4search2

docs.title: Why transformers are attractive for your codebase’s representation
docs.text:
Transformers were introduced as a sequence model that replaces recurrence with self-attention, enabling direct modeling of long-range dependencies. citeturn9search1 For multivariate time series, transformer variants are commonly motivated by the need to learn (1) which features matter and (2) which time points matter, often under nonstationarity.

Temporal Fusion Transformer (TFT) by entity["people","Bryan Lim","machine learning researcher"] and coauthors is particularly relevant as a “bridge citation” for your thesis narrative because it is an attention-based architecture explicitly designed for multi-horizon forecasting with mixed inputs and includes mechanisms for variable selection and interpretability. citeturn9search8

Finance-specific transformer applications are growing and increasingly appear in venues beyond arbitrary preprints. For example: - A 2024 entity["company","IBM","technology company"] research publication presents a multimodal transformer for financial time series forecasting that explicitly targets combining text and numeric time series sources. citeturn6search7 - An open-access entity["organization","Expert Systems with Applications","journal, elsevier"] paper applies transformer architectures with time embeddings to FX-spot prediction and includes benchmarking against alternatives, reflecting ongoing empirical exploration of transformer suitability in financial forecasting problems. citeturn6search9

docs.title: Vision Transformer and the “charts contain information” angle
docs.text:
Vision Transformer (ViT) formalizes the idea that an image can be represented as a sequence of patches (tokens) and processed with transformer blocks, achieving strong results when pre-trained at scale. citeturn8search3

This matters for your implementation because your “FAT window” representation is structurally similar to a 2D grid (feature × time) and your model space explicitly includes ViT-style architectures, indicating you are treating the window as a visually structured object rather than only as a 1D sequence. fileciteturn23file6L1-L1

More importantly for thesis defense, there is now peer-reviewed evidence (not just blog posts) that visual information in stock charts can contain economically meaningful signals. A 2026 entity["organization","Finance Research Letters","journal, elsevier"] paper (“From vision to value…”) reports that factors derived from deep learning on stock chart images—including ViT-derived signals—can add incremental pricing power beyond a large set of established factors, which is very closely aligned with your high-level claim “there are patterns in the numbers and models can extract them.” citeturn7search8

In addition, entity["organization","IEEE Access","journal, ieee"] published work (2025) explicitly combining ViT-like processing of candlestick image representations with transformer-style temporal models for movement classification, including statistical comparisons to baselines. citeturn7search1turn7search5

docs.title: Statistical significance and multiple-testing controls are the core thesis risk
docs.text:
If your system runs “thousands of combinations” of features, windows, and hyperparameters, the main scientific challenge becomes data snooping / multiple testing: discovering a configuration that looks excellent by chance in a finite historical sample. The classic formalization is White’s Reality Check, which proposes a bootstrap-based test of whether the best model in a search has predictive superiority over a benchmark once you account for the specification search itself. citeturn0search0

Hansen’s Superior Predictive Ability (SPA) test addresses a related problem: testing whether a benchmark is outperformed by at least one alternative while reducing sensitivity to poor alternatives and data snooping effects. citeturn1search0

entity["people","Joseph P. Romano","econometrician"] and entity["people","Michael Wolf","econometrician"] extend this theme with stepwise multiple testing procedures aimed at controlling family-wise error rates in the context of comparing many strategies to a common benchmark (exactly the “many trials” situation your Optuna + feature-selection search creates). citeturn2search1

In mainstream asset-pricing research, the “factor zoo” critique provides a widely cited argument that the usual t>2 significance rule becomes too weak after decades of mining. entity["people","Campbell R. Harvey","finance economist"], entity["people","Yan Liu","finance researcher"], and entity["people","Heqing Zhu","finance researcher"] propose a multiple-testing framework and argue that new discoveries should clear a substantially higher t-statistic hurdle (often summarized as “around 3.0” rather than 2.0) to reduce false discoveries. citeturn3search2

For performance metrics, you should be especially careful with Sharpe ratios. The “Deflated Sharpe Ratio” work explicitly targets selection bias and non-normality, providing a correction framework for “best-of-many backtests.” citeturn1search2 This connects directly to your “compare against random models repeatedly” plan: you are effectively constructing an empirical distribution of baseline outcomes, and deflation-style corrections are designed for this setting.

Relatedly, the “Probability of Backtest Overfitting” line of work formalizes how quickly apparent backtest skill can be manufactured when many configurations are tried, and proposes cross-validation approaches to estimate overfitting risk. citeturn1search9turn1search7

docs.title: Random baselines and “null experiments” as a principled scientific method
docs.text:
Your setup (dummy vs random provider, real models vs random baselines, with optional leakage) is best understood as a three-layer inference strategy:

  1. Positive control: On deterministic synthetic data (sine-wave-like dynamics), a competent pipeline should learn and profit (or at least achieve high predictive scores). Your dummy provider and dummy template are built for that. fileciteturn8file0L1-L1
  2. Negative control: On random data, performance should collapse to baseline unless leakage is introduced. Your random provider template is built for this. fileciteturn43file0L1-L1
  3. Mechanism check: Introduce known leakage and confirm performance increases, which demonstrates the pipeline is sensitive to information flow and that your evaluation can detect “cheating.” Your LEAK indicator and label-copy signal are explicit mechanisms for this. fileciteturn29file0L1-L1 fileciteturn29file19L1-L1

Statistically, what you are doing aligns with the null-hypothesis framing behind Reality Check / SPA: you define a benchmark (random baseline or no-skill model) and test whether a searched-over family of strategies beats it after adjusting for search. citeturn0search0turn1search0

Your random-model family is a particularly strong benchmark because some variants are calibrated from the observed label distribution and transition behavior (e.g., Markov structure), which means the baseline can mimic “class prior exploitation” and simple persistence patterns. That is exactly what a skeptic would demand if you claim you found true predictive structure. fileciteturn23file2L1-L1 fileciteturn23file7L1-L1

In addition to formal tests (Reality Check / SPA), the broader inference toolkit for time-series strategy evaluation often uses resampling. White’s Reality Check is itself bootstrap-based, and the literature commonly emphasizes that resampling should respect serial dependence (e.g., block/bootstrap variants rather than iid shuffles) when dealing with time series. citeturn0search0

docs.title: Data leakage, temporal alignment, and why your “leak toggles” are valuable
docs.text:
In financial ML, leakage often enters through subtle time alignment mistakes (feature computed using future prices, target not shifted correctly, normalization using full-sample statistics, etc.). The consequence is exactly what your prompt described: a system appears to “find patterns” in truly random data. White’s discussion of data snooping makes the general point that repeated reuse of a single history can create convincing but spurious results without any real underlying merit. citeturn0search0

Your code includes two extremely useful, explicit leakage probes:

  • The LEAK indicator is declared as intentionally leaking (“shifting backward”), providing a “known-bad” feature to confirm end-to-end leak sensitivity and to measure how much such a shift inflates scores. fileciteturn29file0L1-L1
  • The label-copy signal literally recomputes the label logic and exposes the resulting LONG/SHORT as features, creating an almost direct path from future-labeled outcomes into the model input (a controlled “shortcut learning” test). fileciteturn29file19L1-L1

These are also conceptually aligned with modern leakage discussions: a controlled “break the pipeline on purpose” test is one of the fastest ways to verify that a training/backtest stack reacts strongly to future information and therefore must be guarded carefully in the real experiments. citeturn10search4

Note on the “Kaufman (2020)” reference from your prompt: I did not find a clearly identifiable, primary academic source matching that exact citation that is specifically about temporal leakage in ML backtesting. The name is ambiguous (multiple authors named Kaufman publish on data/analytics). If you have the paper title or venue, it can be pinned down and added as a precise citation in the bibliography.

On the prevention side, a big part of leakage avoidance is ensuring that window construction uses only data available up to the decision timestamp. Your window builder explicitly locates the last index at or before the sampling timestamp and slices a historical window ending at that time, rather than slicing symmetric windows around it. fileciteturn36file4L1-L1

Similarly, your labeling strategy is explicitly future-dependent (labels are computed from the next horizon of price moves) which is correct for supervised learning, but also means that training/test splits must avoid overlapping label horizons. The label generation logic is clearly based on forward windows of future increments (max future move up/down and constraints before reaching the extrema). fileciteturn26file0L1-L1

The backtest-overfitting literature (PBO / related work) frames this as a structural problem: in finance your labels and events have temporal extent, and naive data splitting can accidentally let information bleed across train/test boundaries. citeturn1search9turn1search7

docs.title: Curated bibliography mapped to your thesis questions
docs.text:
This section lists the most thesis-relevant papers/sources from the research scan and explains why each is load-bearing for your defense.

Efficient markets and “where predictability can exist”: - Fama (1970): defines EMH forms and frames the empirical difficulty (joint tests). Use this to define what claim you are and are not making. citeturn0search2 - Lo (2004): Adaptive Markets Hypothesis; lets you argue that predictability may be regime-dependent and time-varying rather than a permanent inefficiency. citeturn0search1 - Lo & MacKinlay (1987/1988): strong random-walk rejections via variance-ratio logic; use to motivate why “prices are not pure iid noise” is a defensible starting point. citeturn2search0turn2search4 - Jegadeesh & Titman (1993): canonical momentum evidence, often interpreted as persistent return predictability at certain horizons. citeturn2search5

Machine learning / deep learning with credible empirical grounding: - Gu, Kelly & Xiu (entity["organization","NBER","economic research org, us"] 2018; RFS 2020): shows that ML methods can produce economically meaningful improvements in a rigorous asset-pricing setup; useful both as precedent and as a cautionary tale (they emphasize careful benchmarking). citeturn4search4 - DeepLOB (2018 arXiv; later IEEE TSP): deep learning can extract stable patterns from microstructure data and generalize across instruments, supporting the “patterns exist in some market data” narrative. citeturn4search2 - entity["people","Ashish Vaswani","computer scientist"] et al. (2017): transformer architecture foundation; cite to justify why attention-based models are reasonable sequence learners. citeturn9search1 - entity["people","Alexey Dosovitskiy","computer scientist"] et al. (ViT, 2021): patch-token modeling foundation; cite to justify why a 2D “window grid” can plausibly be fed to ViT-like architectures. citeturn8search3 - Finance Research Letters (2026), “From vision to value…”: direct evidence that ViT on stock chart images can produce signals with incremental pricing power; this is uniquely aligned with your “patterns detectable by models” thesis. citeturn7search8 - Temporal Fusion Transformer (2019/2021): widely used attention-based forecasting design with variable selection and interpretability; useful to justify systematic feature selection and multi-horizon design decisions. citeturn9search8 - IBM (ICAIF 2024) and ESWA (2024 FX transformer): modern finance-specific transformer deployments and benchmarking culture. citeturn6search7turn6search9

Statistical significance, data snooping, and “best-of-many” backtests: - White (2000) Reality Check: the key citation when you search over many configurations and report the best one; directly addresses your Optuna situation. citeturn0search0 - Hansen (2005) SPA: related superiority test designed for many-model comparisons against a benchmark. citeturn1search0 - Romano & Wolf (2005): stepwise multiple testing controlling family-wise error rates in benchmarking many strategies. citeturn2search1 - Harvey, Liu & Zhu (2016): “factor zoo” multiple testing framework; use this for the meta-argument “the default statistical bar is too low once you do large searches.” citeturn3search2 - Bailey & López de Prado (2014) Deflated Sharpe Ratio: performance metric correction for selection bias, overfitting, and non-normality; directly relevant if Sharpe is a key headline metric. citeturn1search2 - Bailey et al. (2014/2017) Backtest overfitting / PBO: formalizes why trying many variants makes excellent backtests unsurprising and proposes tools to quantify overfitting risk. citeturn1search1turn1search9

docs.title: Random Baseline Guide for Thesis Validation
1. Title and Goal
docs.text:
The goal of this document is to define a rigorous, decision-complete method to validate that model performance is statistically better than calibrated random baselines.

In this project, "random" does not mean naive uniform guessing. It means controlled stochastic policies calibrated from allowed data (training, or locked dev if explicitly defined before final testing).

docs.title: 2. Why Random Baselines Are Mandatory
docs.text:
A single random run is weak evidence because random outcomes have variance. One seed can look very bad or unexpectedly good by chance.

Multiple calibrated random baselines are required because they let us estimate a distribution of outcomes under a null hypothesis:

  • "No real predictive edge."

Fairness principle for all comparisons:

  • Same temporal split.
  • Same execution costs and slippage settings.
  • Same gate behavior (entry_threshold, min_quality_threshold).
  • Same backtest engine and rules.

If any of these differs, the model vs random comparison is not methodologically valid.

Implementation rule for this project:

  • Random baseline probabilities are calibrated from observed training labels.
  • They are not Optuna-suggested hyperparameters.

docs.title: 3. Freeze Temporal Splits and Reporting Protocol
What freeze temporal splits means
docs.text:
Define and lock train/validation/test periods once, before running the campaign. Do not adjust boundaries after inspecting results.

Example:

  • Train: 2021-01-01 to 2024-12-31
  • Validation: 2025-01-01 to 2025-12-31
  • Test: 2026-01-01 to 2026-02-25

The exact dates can differ, but they must be fixed before analysis.

docs.title: What freeze report protocol means
docs.text:
Define the full evaluation procedure before running experiments, then do not change it after observing outcomes:

  • Metrics to report.
  • Number of random seeds.
  • Statistical tests and p-value formula.
  • Acceptance thresholds for thesis support.
  • Ranking and tie-break rules.

This prevents:

  • Cherry-picking windows or metrics.
  • Moving-goalpost bias ("change criteria until model wins").

docs.title: Freeze checklist
docs.text:
• Split boundaries fixed.
  • Metrics fixed.
  • Seed count fixed.
  • Statistical tests fixed.
  • Pass/fail thesis criteria fixed.

docs.title: 4. Always-Flat Baseline
docs.text: Always-flat is the minimum baseline and always outputs NONE.

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
At each tick:

  • signal = NONE.
  • prob_none = 1.0, prob_long = 0.0, prob_short = 0.0.

docs.title: Why keep it
docs.text:
• It verifies that your strategy beats "never trade."
  • It gives a strict floor for activity-based baselines.
  • It is useful for sanity checks and pipeline regression tests.

docs.title: Limitation
docs.text:
• It does not test directional skill under active trading.
  • It is usually too weak to be your only random baseline.

docs.title: Random-L Full Detail (Label-Prior Matched)
docs.text: Random-L is a label-prior matched random baseline. It samples directly from empirical class shares and does not explicitly model entry policy mechanics.

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
At each tick, sample one class from calibrated class priors:

  • P(NONE) = p_none
  • P(LONG) = p_long
  • P(SHORT) = p_short

with:

  • p_none + p_long + p_short = 1.

docs.title: Calibration source rules
docs.text:
• Recommended: estimate priors from training data only.
  • Optional: estimate from training + validation only if the protocol is frozen before touching final test.
  • Never estimate priors from final test.

docs.title: Random-L algorithm pseudocode
docs.text:
def sample_random_l(rng, p_none: float, p_long: float, p_short: float) -> str:
    u = rng.random()
    if u < p_none:
        return "NONE"
    if u < p_none + p_long:
        return "LONG"
    return "SHORT"

docs.title: Mapping to pipeline prediction fields
docs.text:
Use calibrated priors as emitted probabilities:

  • prob_none = p_none
  • prob_long = p_long
  • prob_short = p_short
  • trade_probabilities = p_long + p_short
  • signal: sampled class
  • confidence: probability of sampled class
  • strength, quality, combined_confidence: same policy family used for Random-B to keep fair comparability.

docs.title: Strengths
docs.text:
• Very simple and transparent.
  • Good first stochastic baseline above always-flat.
  • Captures class imbalance in NONE/LONG/SHORT.

docs.title: Weaknesses
docs.text:
• Ignores explicit entry-policy structure.
  • Memoryless and non-temporal.
  • Usually weaker null than Random-B for policy-level comparison.

docs.title: Random-B Full Detail
docs.text: Random-B is a frequency-matched random baseline. It matches entry activity and directional mix, but remains stochastic and memoryless.

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
At each tick:

  • Sample entry/no-entry with p_trade.
  • If entry occurs, sample LONG vs SHORT with p_long_cond.
  • If no entry, output NONE.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• p_trade: target entry frequency.
  • p_long_cond: probability of LONG conditional on entry.
  • p_short_cond = 1 - p_long_cond.

docs.title: Calibration source rules
docs.text:
• Recommended: estimate parameters from training data only.
  • Optional: use training + validation only if this choice is frozen before touching final test.
  • Never estimate from final test.

docs.title: Random-B algorithm pseudocode
docs.text:
def sample_random_b(rng, p_trade: float, p_long_cond: float) -> str:
    enter = rng.random() < p_trade
    if not enter:
        return "NONE"
    is_long = rng.random() < p_long_cond
    return "LONG" if is_long else "SHORT"

docs.title: Mapping to pipeline prediction fields
docs.text:
To integrate fairly with your current pipeline, the random baseline should emit the same output schema as model predictions:

  • signal: sampled label (NONE, LONG, SHORT).
  • prob_none, prob_long, prob_short: calibrated class probabilities used by the baseline.
  • trade_probabilities = prob_long + prob_short.
  • confidence: probability of the sampled class.
  • strength, quality: defined by baseline policy (see recommended options below).
  • combined_confidence: computed with the same formula used in model pipeline.

Recommended policy for strength and quality:

  • Option A (direction-only null, simpler): fixed constants (for example 0.5, 0.5).
  • Option B (closer behavioral null, preferred): sample from empirical training distributions (optionally conditioned by sampled signal).

Important:

  • Keep this choice fixed before test evaluation.
  • Apply the same gate and downstream logic regardless of baseline type.

docs.title: Gate interaction
docs.text:
Random-B predictions must pass through:

  • entry_threshold on combined_confidence.
  • min_quality_threshold on quality.

This is mandatory for fair comparison versus model results that also pass the same gate.

docs.title: Strengths
docs.text:
• Stronger than always-flat.
  • Matches realistic activity level of the target policy.
  • Easy to implement and explain.

docs.title: Weaknesses
docs.text:
• Memoryless (IID over time).
  • Does not model persistence/regime behavior by itself.

docs.title: 6. Random-Markov Full Detail (Optional Advanced)
docs.text: Random-Markov extends random baselines with temporal persistence.

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
Instead of sampling each tick independently, the next signal depends on previous signal:

  • P(s_t | s_{t-1})

with states in {NONE, LONG, SHORT}.

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text: Financial predictions often exhibit short-run persistence in action sequences. IID random may be too weak as null. Markov random creates a harder and more realistic null baseline.

docs.title: Transition matrix
docs.text:
Use a 3x3 transition matrix:

          next NONE   next LONG   next SHORT
prev NONE     a           b           c
prev LONG     d           e           f
prev SHORT    g           h           i

Each row sums to 1.

docs.title: Calibration
docs.text:
Estimate transitions from training sequence counts:

  • Count transitions count(prev -> next).
  • Apply smoothing (for example Laplace) to avoid zero-probability transitions.
  • Normalize each row.

docs.title: Sampling algorithm pseudocode
docs.text:
def sample_random_markov(rng, prev_state: str, transition_probs: dict[str, dict[str, float]]) -> str:
    probs = transition_probs[prev_state]
    u = rng.random()
    if u < probs["NONE"]:
        return "NONE"
    if u < probs["NONE"] + probs["LONG"]:
        return "LONG"
    return "SHORT"

Initialization:

  • Sample first state from empirical class prior (training), or set fixed start state and keep it fixed across seeds.

docs.title: Optional extension
docs.text:
Two-level Markov:

  • Level 1: Markov over entry/no-entry.
  • Level 2: Conditional direction LONG/SHORT when entry occurs.

This can improve interpretability and control.

docs.title: Trade-offs
docs.text:
• Better realism than IID random.
  • More parameters and calibration choices.
  • More risk of methodological mistakes if protocol is not frozen.

docs.title: Positioning
docs.text: Use Random-Markov as optional advanced baseline after Random-B.

docs.title: 7. Statistics to Prove Better Than Random
docs.text:
Evaluate at least:

  • Stage 3 return.
  • Stage 3 Sharpe.
  • Stage 3 win rate.
  • Final objective score.

docs.title: Multi-seed requirement
docs.text: Run each random baseline with many seeds (recommended 30 to 50) to estimate its outcome distribution.

docs.title: Compute random distribution summary
docs.text:
For each metric and baseline:

  • Mean.
  • Standard deviation.
  • Confidence interval (for example 95% CI for the mean).

docs.title: Model percentile vs random distribution
docs.text:
Compute where the model sits relative to random outcomes:

  • Higher percentile is better for maximize metrics.
  • This gives intuitive effect interpretation.

docs.title: Empirical p-value
docs.text:
For maximize metrics:

p = (1 + #random >= model) / (N + 1)

For minimize metrics:

p = (1 + #random <= model) / (N + 1)

Where:

  • N is number of random seeds.
  • #random >= model counts random runs that are at least as good as model.

Lower p means stronger evidence against the random-null behavior.

docs.title: Effect size requirement
docs.text:
Do not rely on p-value alone. Require practical effect size:

  • Absolute delta vs random mean/median.
  • Optional standardized effect (for example Cohen-style ratio):
effect_z = (model - mean_random) / std_random

docs.title: Consistency requirement
docs.text: A thesis claim should hold across defined temporal periods/splits, not only one lucky window.

docs.title: 8. Clarification on Train vs Validation vs Test Usage
docs.text:
Question clarified:

  • "Does this mean not use validation data, only training?"

Answer:

  • Calibration must never use final test data.
  • Preferred default is training-only calibration.
  • Validation can be used for development calibration only if protocol is frozen before final test is touched.

Reason:

  • Final test must remain untouched evidence of generalization.

docs.title: What leakage means here
docs.text:
Leakage happens when test information influences baseline calibration.

Example of leakage:

  • Estimate p_trade or transition matrix using final test distribution.

docs.title: Why harmful
docs.text:
• Baseline becomes tuned to evaluation target.
  • Random baseline may look artificially stronger or weaker.
  • Statistical significance becomes biased and unreliable.

docs.title: Wrong vs correct
docs.text:
Wrong:

  • "We computed Random-B probabilities from final test labels/predictions."

Correct:

  • "We computed Random-B probabilities from training data (or locked dev set), then evaluated once on test."

docs.title: 10. Fair Comparison: Model With Gate vs Random With Same Gate
Pitfall
docs.text: Comparing model outputs after gate to random outputs before gate is invalid.

docs.title: Correct comparison
docs.text:
Both model and random baseline must run through identical decision path:

  • Same entry_threshold.
  • Same min_quality_threshold.
  • Same execution costs/slippage settings.
  • Same backtest horizon and tick set.

docs.title: Fairness checklist
docs.text:
• Gate parameters identical.
  • Backtest config identical.
  • Data split identical.
  • Metrics and objective formula identical.

docs.title: 11. Thesis Acceptance Criteria
docs.text:
Claim support should require all of the following:

  • Positive effect size versus random distribution.
  • Statistically significant empirical p-value at the chosen threshold.
  • Consistency across defined periods/splits.

Fail or reject support when:

  • Improvement appears in only one split.
  • P-values are not significant.
  • Gains vanish under nearby period changes or stress splits.

docs.title: 12. Recommended Reporting Template
docs.text:
Use one compact table per baseline family:

Metric Random mean Random std Random 95% CI Model value Model percentile Empirical p-value Pass Stage3 return Sharpe Win rate Objective

Conclusion template:

Thesis verdict: supported / partially supported / not supported.
Reason summary:
1) Effect size vs random: ...
2) Empirical p-values: ...
3) Consistency across periods: ...

docs.title: 13. Common Mistakes and Anti-Patterns
docs.text:
• Calibrating probabilities from final test data.
  • Running only one random seed.
  • Changing metrics/rules after seeing outcomes.
  • Comparing gated model against ungated random baseline.
  • Treating distribution divergence alone as proof of predictive edge.

docs.title: Practical Defaults for This Project
docs.text:
• File scope: documentation only.
  • Statistical direction default: maximize metrics unless explicitly marked minimize.
  • Seed recommendation: 30 to 50 random seeds per random baseline variant.
  • Baseline order recommendation:
  • Always-flat
  • Random-L
  • Random-B
  • Random-Markov (optional advanced)

docs.title: Script 2 Baseline Decisions — Progressive Model Complexity
Context
docs.text: This document summarizes the research and decisions made for Script 2 (Thesis Proof) regarding the progressive model complexity approach for baselines. The original plan only used ViT models vs Random baselines. This revision adds intermediate model tiers to strengthen the thesis and provide more granular evidence about the nature of exploitable patterns.
