  • docs/researchs/_ML/random.md — Random baseline specification
  • docs/researchs/_ML/ML-roadmap-checkpoint.md — ML development roadmap
  • docs/researchs/_ML/sugerencias.md — Prioritized suggestions for ML pipeline

docs.title: 1. Problem Statement
docs.text:
The original Script 2 design compares ViT directly against Random baselines. This creates a complexity gap: if ViT wins, we don't know if a simple linear model would have also won. If ViT loses, we don't know if the model is too complex (overfitting) or if there truly are no patterns.

The progressive approach fills this gap by testing models of increasing complexity:

Random → Rule-Based → Logistic → MLP → TCN → ViT → Axial ViT

Each step answers a specific question about the nature of exploitable patterns.

docs.title: Level 0: Random Baselines (Already Implemented ✅)
docs.text:
• Models: random_always_flat, random_l, random_b, random_markov
  • Trainable: No
  • What they test: Null hypothesis — "No exploitable patterns exist"
  • Code: modules/model-trainer/src/rainbow/model_trainer/models/random.py
  • Status: Fully implemented and tested in Script 1

docs.title: Level 1: Rule-Based Baseline (NEW — To Implement)
docs.text:
• Model type: rule_baseline
  • Trainable: No (non-trainable, like random baselines)
  • What it tests: "Do classical TA rules (e.g., RSI > 70 → SHORT) have predictive power?"
  • Input: Same [B, W, F] tensor as all models
  • Key requirement: Model must know feature names to map rules to tensor positions

Configuration system: uses the SAME flow as all other models:

The rule-based model uses the exact same configuration pipeline as every other model:

  1. YAML defaults: config/experiments/models/rule-baseline-model-hyperparameters.yaml
  2. Pydantic config: RuleBaselineModelHyperparametersConfig in optuna_config.py
  3. Resolution: Same resolution flow as all models. Since it's non-trainable, values are fixed (not OptunaParam). Registered in MODEL_HYPERPARAMETERS_TYPE_MAP["rule_baseline"].

No new configuration system is needed — this is the standard model config flow.

Expression-based rules (inspired by CONDITION signal):

Rules use expression strings following the same philosophy as the CONDITION signal (modules/data-processing/src/rainbow/data_processing/signals/condition.py), which parses boolean expressions via Python AST:

  • CONDITION supports: <, <=, >, >=, ==, !=, and, or, not, arithmetic, parentheses for grouping, and dot notation for column references.
  • The rule-based model reuses the same AST parsing approach, but instead of generating Polars expressions (for DataFrames), it generates tensor operations (for [B, W, F] input).

Timestep notation: {-T} with curly braces

Since the model input is [B, W, F] (a window of W timesteps), expressions need to specify WHICH timestep to evaluate. The notation uses curly braces {T} to avoid collision with parentheses used for mathematical grouping:

  • rsi_14{-1} → RSI value at the last timestep (most recent)
  • rsi_14{0} → RSI value at the first timestep (oldest in window)
  • rsi_14{-1} > rsi_14{-5} → Compare RSI at last tick vs 5 ticks back

Curly braces are chosen specifically because parentheses () may be needed for expression grouping (e.g., (rsi_14{-1} > 70) and (volume{-1} > 1000)).

How it works:

  1. Constructor receives feature_names: list[str] (sorted alphabetically, matching tensor dim 2)
  2. Builds name → index mapping: self._feature_index = {name: i for i, name in enumerate(feature_names)}
  3. Rules are a list of (expression: str, signal: str) pairs from config
  4. Expressions are parsed via AST in __init__ (once, not every forward call)
  5. Feature names in expressions are mapped to tensor indices; timestep {T} is mapped to the window dimension index
  6. forward(x): for each sample in the batch, rules are evaluated in priority order. First matching rule wins. If no rule matches → default_signal (typically "NONE").

Critical design decisions:

  • Feature names must be passed to the model. Today create_model() receives input_dim (count) and feature_weights (list of floats) but NOT the names. The names ARE available in training.py line 278: feature_columns_names_ordered = sorted(feature_columns_with_weights.keys()). This list must be forwarded to create_model().

  • Features arrive as-is from the experiment config. If the experiment includes raw RSI (0-100) without normalization, then raw RSI values arrive at the model. The user controls this via experiment configuration — whether to apply normalization pipelines or not.

  • Non-trainable pattern: Same as TradingRandomBaseline — uses a _anchor parameter to maintain a differentiable graph for the training loop. Weights don't update.

Model output fields explained:

  • sampled_logit_margin: The logit value assigned to the predicted class. Since this model is non-trainable and doesn't produce real logits, it creates them synthetically: logits = [0.0, 0.0, 0.0] then logits[predicted_class] = sampled_logit_margin. After softmax, higher margin = more "confident" prediction. This is the same mechanism used by all random baselines (see random.py lines 131-132).
  • strength_value: Fixed output for the strength head (0.0-1.0). Since this model has no learned strength estimation, it outputs this constant for every prediction.
  • quality_value: Fixed output for the quality head (0.0-1.0). Same rationale as strength.

These three fields are float | OptunaParam | None to follow the standard config pattern, but in practice for rule-based they should be fixed values — there is no reason for Optuna to tune them in a non-trainable baseline.

Example YAML default config:

# config/experiments/models/rule-baseline-model-hyperparameters.yaml
rule_baseline_model_hyperparameters:
  rules:
    - expression: "rsi_14{-1} > 70"
      signal: "SHORT"
    - expression: "rsi_14{-1} < 30"
      signal: "LONG"
    - expression: "ema_9{-1} > ema_21{-1}"
      signal: "LONG"
    - expression: "ema_9{-1} < ema_21{-1}"
      signal: "SHORT"
  default_signal: "NONE"
  strength_value: 0.5
  quality_value: 0.5
  sampled_logit_margin: 5.0

More complex expression examples (leveraging AST flexibility):

# Combined conditions with parentheses for math grouping
- expression: "(rsi_14{-1} > 70) and (volume{-1} > 1000)"
  signal: "SHORT"

# Cross-timestep comparison (momentum)
- expression: "rsi_14{-1} > rsi_14{-5}"
  signal: "LONG"

# Arithmetic in expression
- expression: "ema_9{-1} - ema_21{-1} > 0"
  signal: "LONG"

docs.title: Level 2: Logistic Regression (NEW — To Implement)
docs.text:
• Model type: logistic
  • Trainable: Yes
  • What it tests: "Are there exploitable LINEAR patterns in the data?"
  • Architecture: Flatten [B, W, F] → [B, W*F], then nn.Linear(W*F, num_classes)
  • Heads: Same 3-head output as all models (logits + strength + quality)
  • Hyperparameters: Almost none — only window_size, learning_rate, batch_size
  • Why it matters: If logistic beats random, there IS linear signal. A reviewer cannot object "it's just overfitting" because logistic regression has minimal capacity.

Architecture detail:

Input: [B, W, F]
  → Flatten: [B, W*F]
    → Linear(W*F, num_classes) → logits: [B, 3]
    → Linear(W*F, 1) + Sigmoid → strength: [B, 1]
    → Linear(W*F, 1) + Sigmoid → quality: [B, 1]

docs.title: Level 3: Small MLP (NEW — To Implement)
docs.text:
• Model type: mlp
  • Trainable: Yes
  • What it tests: "Are there exploitable NON-LINEAR patterns?"
  • Architecture: Flatten [B, W, F] → hidden layers → heads
  • Hyperparameters: hidden_dim (64-256), num_hidden_layers (1-2), dropout (0.0-0.3)
  • Why it matters: Captures feature interactions that logistic regression misses

Architecture detail:

Input: [B, W, F]
  → Flatten: [B, W*F]
    → Linear(W*F, hidden_dim) + ReLU + Dropout
    → [Optional: Linear(hidden_dim, hidden_dim) + ReLU + Dropout] × (num_layers-1)
      → Linear(hidden_dim, num_classes) → logits: [B, 3]
      → Linear(hidden_dim, 1) + Sigmoid → strength: [B, 1]
      → Linear(hidden_dim, 1) + Sigmoid → quality: [B, 1]

docs.title: Level 4: TCN / 1D-CNN (NEW — To Implement)
docs.text:
• Model type: tcn
  • Trainable: Yes
  • What it tests: "Does temporal structure matter? Are there sequential patterns?"
  • Architecture: 1D convolutions over time axis, then global pooling → heads
  • Hyperparameters: channels (32-128), kernel_size (3-7), num_layers (2-4), dropout
  • Why it matters: Respects temporal ordering (unlike flatten-based models) without the complexity of attention mechanisms. If TCN beats MLP, temporal patterns exist.

Architecture detail:

Input: [B, W, F]
  → Transpose to [B, F, W] (Conv1D expects channels-first)
    → Conv1D(F, channels, kernel_size) + ReLU + Dropout
    → Conv1D(channels, channels, kernel_size) + ReLU + Dropout
    → ... (num_layers)
      → Global Average Pooling over time: [B, channels]
        → Linear(channels, num_classes) → logits: [B, 3]
        → Linear(channels, 1) + Sigmoid → strength: [B, 1]
        → Linear(channels, 1) + Sigmoid → quality: [B, 1]

docs.title: Level 5: ViT (Already Implemented ✅)
docs.text:
• Model type: vit, vit_compact
  • Trainable: Yes
  • What it tests: "Do attention-based temporal patterns provide edge?"
  • Code: modules/model-trainer/src/rainbow/model_trainer/models/vit.py

docs.title: Level 6: Axial ViT (Already Implemented ✅)
docs.text:
• Model type: axial_vit, axial_vit_compact
  • Trainable: Yes
  • What it tests: "Does 2D attention (time × features) help?"
  • Code: modules/model-trainer/src/rainbow/model_trainer/models/axial_vit.py

docs.title: 3. What Each Comparison Result Means
docs.text:
Comparison If Winner Beats Random (p < 0.05) Interpretation Rule-Based > Random Classical TA rules have some predictive power Validates that raw TA features carry information Logistic > Random Linear exploitable patterns exist Strongest possible evidence — minimal model, minimal overfitting risk Logistic > Rule-Based Learned linear combinations beat hand-crafted rules ML adds value over manual rules MLP > Logistic Non-linear patterns exist that linear models miss Feature interactions matter TCN > MLP Temporal structure contains exploitable information Sequential patterns beyond flat feature vectors ViT > TCN Attention mechanisms capture patterns convolutions miss Long-range temporal dependencies exist ViT > Random but Logistic ≈ Random Patterns exist but require complex models to detect Higher risk of overfitting, weaker evidence None > Random No exploitable patterns detected Honest negative result

Key insight for the thesis: The EARLIER in the progression a model beats random, the STRONGER the evidence. If logistic regression beats random, it's nearly irrefutable proof of exploitable patterns (minimal model, minimal overfitting capacity).

docs.title: 4. Progressive Phases as Discard Mechanism
docs.text:
The existing progressive training phases functionality can serve as an efficient discard mechanism for all trainable model tiers, not just ViT:

Phase 1: data_fraction=0.50 → Explore broadly on half the data
Phase 2: data_fraction=0.75 → Refine top N performers
Phase 3: data_fraction=1.00 → Final validation on full data

Application to new model tiers:

  • Logistic/MLP (Tier A/B): Phases DISABLED — these are fast models with few hyperparameters. Single-phase optimization is sufficient.
  • TCN (Tier B/C): Phases can be ENABLED if trial count is high enough (≥60 trials). TCN has moderate hyperparameter space.
  • ViT/Axial ViT (Tier C/D): Phases ENABLED as in original plan. Complex models benefit most from progressive refinement.

docs.title: What already works for all new models
docs.text: Component Compatible? Notes BaseModel interface ✅ All models implement forward(x) → {logits, strength, quality} Training loop ✅ Same loop for all, including non-trainable (random/rules) LazyParquetDataset ✅ Produces [B, W, F] tensor for any model PyTorchModelWrapper ✅ Wraps any nn.Module for backtest inference BacktestingEngine ✅ Uses ModelProtocol.predict(), model-agnostic Optuna integration ✅ Each model type gets its own hyperparameter suggester Scoring (3-stage) ✅ Model-agnostic scoring pipeline Progressive phases ✅ Works for any trainable model

docs.title: What needs to change
docs.text: Change Scope Effort Pass feature_names to create_model() 1 param added to factory Small TradingRuleBaseline model class New file ~150-200 lines Medium TradingLogistic model class New file ~80-100 lines Small TradingMLP model class New file ~120-150 lines Small TradingTCN model class New file ~150-200 lines Medium Resolved hyperparameters for each model type New Pydantic models Small each Optuna hyperparameter suggesters for each type New files ~100-150 lines each Small each Factory branches for each model type ~20 lines per model in factory.py Small model_flow_capabilities for each model type Config update Small Experiment JSON templates for Script 2 New JSON files per tier per model Medium run_script2.sh bash script New script Medium analyze_script2_results.py analysis script New script Medium

docs.title: What does NOT change
docs.text:
• BaseModel abstract class — no modification needed
  • Training loop (training.py) — model-agnostic
  • Data preparation (data_preparation.py, data_lazy_parquet_dataset.py) — unchanged
  • Backtest infrastructure — unchanged
  • Scoring system — unchanged
  • Database schema — unchanged
  • CLI commands — unchanged

docs.title: 6. Feature Name Availability — Code Evidence
docs.text:
The feature names are already computed in the training flow. Here is the exact code path:

In training.py (line 225-228):

feature_columns_with_weights = {
    selected_feature.column_name: float(selected_feature.weight)
    for selected_feature in resolved_hyperparameters.feature_selection.feature_columns_with_weights
}

In training.py (line 278):

feature_columns_names_ordered = sorted(feature_columns_with_weights.keys())

This feature_columns_names_ordered is a list[str] with the feature names in the exact same order as the tensor's dim 2 (input_dim). It is computed BEFORE create_model() is called. Forwarding it to the factory requires adding one parameter.

docs.title: 7. Walk-Forward Validation — ENABLED
docs.text:
Walk-forward validation is ENABLED in Script 2. After Script 1 experience (4 days of iterations, fragile single-split results), walk-forward was deemed essential for robust evidence.

Design: 4 sequential folds within a 13-month range (2025-01 to 2026-02). Each fold is a standalone 4-month experiment with training_split=0.80 (~3.2mo train, ~0.8mo test).

Fold 1: range 2025-01-01 → 2025-05-01, training_split=0.80
Fold 2: range 2025-04-01 → 2025-08-01, training_split=0.80
Fold 3: range 2025-07-01 → 2025-11-01, training_split=0.80
Fold 4: range 2025-10-01 → 2026-02-01, training_split=0.80

Why it's valuable: Tests if patterns persist across different market regimes. A model that only works in one time period may be overfitting to market conditions.

Compute impact: Does NOT multiply compute by 4x — each fold trains on less data so converges faster. Net overhead is ~1.2-1.5x.

Total: 40 base experiments × 4 folds = 160 experiment runs.

Consequence for phases: Progressive training phases are DISABLED for all tiers. With ~3.2 months of training data per fold, Phase 1 at 50% data fraction would train on only ~1.6 months — too little data for meaningful progressive refinement.

docs.title: 8. Multiple Testing Correction
docs.text:
With the expanded model progression, the number of comparison pairs increases. The Holm-Sidak correction adjusts p-values to account for this.

How Holm-Sidak works:

  1. Collect all p-values from all comparison pairs
  2. Sort them from smallest to largest
  3. For the smallest p-value, required threshold = 1 - (1 - α)^(1/N)
  4. For the next, threshold = 1 - (1 - α)^(1/(N-1))
  5. Continue until a p-value fails its threshold; all remaining also fail

With 96 pairs (6 real × 4 random × 4 tiers):

  • Strictest threshold: p < 0.000527 (for the most significant pair)
  • Least strict: p < 0.05 (for the least significant pair)
  • This is acceptable — if patterns truly exist, the best models should have very small p-values that survive correction

Mitigation: The progressive approach actually HELPS with multiple testing: if Logistic already beats Random with p < 0.001, adding more complex models that also beat Random reinforces the finding rather than diluting it.

docs.title: 9. Decisions Summary
docs.text:
Decision Rationale Add Rule-Based baseline Represents "human expert TA knowledge"; bridges random to trainable Add Logistic baseline Minimal trainable model; if it wins, strongest possible thesis evidence Add MLP baseline Tests non-linear patterns with minimal architecture Add TCN baseline Tests temporal patterns without attention complexity Keep ViT + Axial ViT Already implemented; test full attention-based models Feature names passed to factory Required for rule-based model; useful for interpretability Rules configured via experiment JSON No hardcoded values; user controls rules per experiment Raw (non-normalized) features for rule-based User decides normalization per experiment; thesis allows any price-derived features Progressive phases DISABLED for all tiers Incompatible with 4-month fold windows (~1.6mo at 50% data fraction) Walk-forward ENABLED (4 folds) Tests robustness across temporal regimes; 40 base × 4 folds = 160 runs Holm-Sidak for multiple testing correction Handles expanded comparison pairs correctly

Document created: 2026-03-08 Updated: 2026-03-09 — Coherence audit: walk-forward ENABLED, phases DISABLED, 96 comparison pairs. Status: DECISIONS FINALIZED — walk-forward + evolution log require implementation before execution.

docs.title: Sugerencias
docs.text:
n concreto, que probarioas vos, en orden

De facil a compelo de implementar, en base a la estructura actual y de impacto mayor a menor, esperado

y la diea de los baselines, todo, incluime

yo puedo luego poner en optuna que pruebe diferentes modelos

Lock the evaluation protocol (highest impact)

Fix one validation window and one test window, time-based, no overlap.

Define one Optuna objective: your real target (PnL/Sharpe) computed on the same backtest rules for every trial.

Add 2 secondary metrics you always log: trade count, max drawdown (or downside deviation).

Add baselines (fast, anchors Optuna)

Baseline A (random): random entry signal with same trade frequency constraint as your bot.

Baseline B (always-flat): never trade.

Baseline C (simple rules): 1–2 indicators only (EMA cross, RSI threshold) with fixed params.

Baseline D (linear/logistic): same features as your model, no deep net.

Baseline E (persistence): predict “no action” always, then measure how your classifier compares on precision/recall for “entry”.

Make the training logs useful (fast)

Write a single CSV per run with: epoch, train_loss, val_loss, objective_metric, secondary metrics.

Save best checkpoint by objective, not by val_loss.

Store each trial’s config + metrics in one JSON next to the checkpoint.

Data leakage checks (fast, high impact if present)

Assert no future data in features/labels (timestamp checks).

Assert normalization is fit only on train (and applied to val/test).

If you build windows, assert label horizon never crosses into validation/test.

Stabilize class imbalance (easy)

Use weighted sampler OR class weights, not both at once (test both as separate trials).

Track per-class precision/recall so Optuna does not “win” by predicting only the majority class.

Fix decision threshold as a tuned parameter (easy, high impact)

Do not assume 0.5.

Tune threshold on validation to maximize your objective proxy (or F1 for entry), then freeze and backtest.

Align loss with what matters (medium)

If your output is “enter/no-enter”: try BCEWithLogits with pos_weight, then focal loss.

If you output multi-class (long/short/none): try class-weighted CE.

Keep the Optuna objective on backtest metric, not the loss.

Learning rate schedule + warmup (medium, usually strong)

Try: cosine decay with warmup, and one-cycle.

Add gradient clipping (global norm) as a trial parameter on/off.

Batch size strategy (medium)

Keep effective batch size constant via gradient accumulation.

Let Optuna vary: batch_size, accumulation_steps, lr together (linked params).

Model family sweep (medium)

Start from your current model as “reference”.

Then try, in this order:

MLP over flattened features (cheap baseline that often surprises).

1D CNN/TCN over time axis (fast, strong for sequences).

Transformer encoder (your ViT-style) with smaller depth/width first.

Let Optuna choose among model families via a categorical param.

Feature grouping experiments (medium)

Group features by timeframe or by indicator family and test:

Concatenate all (current)

Separate towers per group + late fusion

Shared trunk + group embeddings

Keep the same evaluation windows.

Label and horizon as Optuna params (harder, can be big)

Tune only 2–3 label knobs:

horizon

threshold for “significant move”

optional neutral zone

Cache labels per setting to avoid recomputing everything.

Calibration + trade policy (harder)

Calibrate probabilities (Platt/Isotonic) on validation.

Convert probability to position sizing rule (mapping) and optimize that mapping separately from the model.

Robustness checks (hard, high value)

Walk-forward validation (multiple folds in time).

Stress test spreads/slippage regimes as part of objective.

Ensemble (hard, sometimes best final gain)

Top-K trial checkpoints ensemble:

average probs

rank-average signals

Only after your single-model pipeline is stable.

Optuna setup suggestion (so you can “probe different models” cleanly)

One study, categorical params:

model_family = {mlp, tcn, transformer}

loss_type = {bce, focal, ce}

sampler_strategy = {class_weight, weighted_sampler, none}

threshold = float

Linked params per family:

mlp: layers, hidden, dropout

tcn: channels, kernel, dilation_depth

transformer: d_model, n_layers, n_heads, dropout, patch/window config

Prune on the backtest metric computed on a small validation slice first, then confirm on full validation.

en castellamo porfa

Perfecto. Te lo ordeno claro, en castellano, y en el orden que yo probaría:

Primero: lo más fácil de implementar y con mayor impacto esperado. Después: más complejo o más experimental.

1) Fijar bien el protocolo de evaluación (impacto altísimo, fácil)

Antes de cambiar modelos:

Split temporal fijo (train / validation / test).

Siempre misma ventana de validación.

Objetivo de Optuna = tu métrica real (Sharpe, PnL o combinación Stage 1/2/3).

Guardar:

Sharpe

PnL

número de trades

max drawdown

Sin esto, todo lo demás es ruido.

2) Baselines obligatorios (impacto conceptual enorme, muy fácil)

Esto es clave. Te da piso y techo.

Baseline 1 — Siempre NONE

Nunca entra. Te dice cuánto “ruido” tiene tu métrica.

Baseline 2 — Random con frecuencia controlada

Genera LONG/SHORT con misma frecuencia promedio que tu bot. Si tu modelo no supera eso con claridad → problema.

Baseline 3 — Regla simple

Por ejemplo:

EMA cross

RSI threshold

Sin red neuronal.

Baseline 4 — Modelo lineal

Logistic regression o MLP muy chico sobre:

Solo features t-0

O flatten completo

Muchos modelos complejos no superan esto.

3) Ajustar el threshold como hiperparámetro (impacto alto, fácil)

No fijes 0.5.

Haz que Optuna pruebe:

entry_threshold

min_quality_threshold

Muchas veces el modelo está bien pero la regla de entrada lo mata.

4) Alinear la loss con lo que realmente importa (impacto alto)

Probar:

BCE con pos_weight

Cross entropy con class weights

Focal loss

Pero siempre optimizar en Optuna por métrica de backtest, no por loss.

5) Scheduler + warmup + gradient clipping (impacto medio-alto)

Muy subestimado.

Probar:

Cosine decay con warmup

One-cycle

Gradient clipping on/off

A veces mejora estabilidad mucho más que cambiar arquitectura.

6) Explorar familias de modelo (ordenado de simple a complejo)

Ahora sí, arquitectura.

6.1 MLP sobre flatten (muy simple)

Input: [W * F] Capas densas. Dropout.

Es un baseline fuerte en ventanas pequeñas.

6.2 TCN / 1D CNN temporal (impacto alto, implementación media)

Conv1D sobre eje tiempo. Dilataciones. Muy buena inductive bias temporal. Menos overfitting que Transformer en W pequeño.

Para W = 30–100 suele ser muy sólido.

6.3 Tu Transformer actual (refinado)

Antes de cambiar arquitectura:

probar menos capas

menos heads

mayor regularización

patching temporal (agrupar 2–4 ticks como un patch)

Con W pequeño, el Transformer puede estar sobreparametrizado.

6.4 Tokenización alternativa (intermedio)

Hoy usás:

tokens = timesteps

Podrías probar:

tokens = features (iTransformer style)

o mixto (Cross attention tiempo-feature)

Cuando F = 300 y W = 50:

W² = 2500 (barato)

F² = 90000 (mucho más pesado)

Entonces atención temporal es barata. Atención entre features puede explotar memoria.

6.5 CNN + Transformer híbrido (más complejo)

CNN primero → reduce ruido local. Transformer después → modela dependencias largas.

Muy buena combinación para trading.

6.6 State Space Models (S4 / Mamba) (más complejo)

Ventajas:

Escalan linealmente en W.

Muy estables en secuencias.

Pero:

W es pequeño en tu caso.

Beneficio computacional no es crítico.

Implementación más compleja.

No lo probaría antes que TCN.

6.7 Ensemble de mejores trials (final)

Después de estabilizar:

Top 3–5 modelos

Promedio de probabilidades

En trading suele mejorar estabilidad.

Sobre tu concepto de “imagen 2D snapshot”

Tu idea NO es errónea.

Es válida.

Pero hay una diferencia importante:

Una imagen:

Es aproximadamente invariante a traslaciones locales.

No tiene dirección temporal obligatoria.

Tu matriz:

El eje vertical (tiempo) tiene dirección obligatoria.

No es intercambiable.

t-0 ≠ t-49.

Entonces:

✔ Pensarla como imagen sirve conceptualmente. ✘ Tratarla como imagen pura (convoluciones 2D simétricas) puede romper la causalidad.

Por eso:

Conv1D temporal tiene más sentido que Conv2D clásica.

Transformer temporal tiene sentido.

¿Es MTF un problema real?

En tu enfoque snapshot:

No es un problema grave.

Porque:

Todo está alineado por timestamp.

El modelo no “sabe” si algo viene de 1H o 1D.

Solo ve patrones en columnas.

El problema aparece si:

Mezclás features mal alineadas temporalmente.

O el horizonte del label depende de timeframe.

Pero conceptualmente, tu enfoque lo resuelve bien.

Dimensiones reales: W=30–100, F=30–300

Esto cambia todo.

Complejidad atención temporal:

O(W²) Con W=50 → 2500. Barato.

Complejidad atención por features:

O(F²) Con F=300 → 90k. Mucho más costoso.

Conclusión: Atención temporal tiene más sentido que atención sobre features.

Riesgo de overfitting

F puede ser 300. W puede ser 50.

Flatten = 15000 inputs.

Si tu dataset no es enorme:

MLP grande → overfit.

Transformer profundo → overfit.

Modelos con inductive bias fuerte (TCN) pueden generalizar mejor.

Si yo tuviera que probar en orden

Orden práctico real:

Baselines simples (lineal, MLP chico).

TCN / 1D CNN.

Ajustar y simplificar tu Transformer.

CNN + Transformer híbrido.

Ensemble.

Recién después modelos más exóticos.

Mi recomendación concreta para tu caso

Con ventanas pequeñas y muchas features:

El candidato más prometedor antes de ir a cosas raras:

TCN bien regularizado.

Tiene:

Bias temporal fuerte.

Menos parámetros efectivos.

Mejor comportamiento con datasets medianos.

No necesita atención compleja.

Tu Transformer puede seguir siendo competitivo, pero lo probaría contra TCN seriamente.

docs.title: Scope
docs.text:
This audit compares three regime views built from the same BTCUSDT history:

  • 5m -> daily dominant from 20260324_154928_BTCUSDT_5m_regime_history
  • 1d native from 20260324_162703_BTCUSDT_1d_native_regime_history
  • 1w native from 20260324_162703_BTCUSDT_1w_native_regime_history

The goal is not to describe artifacts again. The goal is to decide how to replace arbitrary 3-month Script2 folds with training logic that is more regime-aware and more operationally meaningful.

docs.title: 1. 5m -> daily dominant is useful for diagnosis, not for primary fold construction
docs.text:
Evidence:

  • taxonomy_27_full top state TRENDING | HIGH_VOL | BEARISH covers 20.45% of days, but its average period length is only 1.4308 days and its maximum is 8.0 days.
  • taxonomy_9_trend_volatility top state RANGING | LOW_VOL covers 30.35% of days, but average period length is only 1.6047 days and maximum is 8.0 days.
  • taxonomy_9_trend_direction top state TRENDING | BEARISH covers 39.53% of days, but average period length is only 1.6567 days and maximum is 7.0 days.
  • Every daily threshold summary for 30d, 60d, and 90d returns zero.

Interpretation:

  • This view is still too twitchy to define contiguous training folds.
  • Even after collapsing intraday states into daily winners, the state still flips too often.
  • This does not mean the view is useless. It is very valuable for label diagnostics and directional skew.

Useful evidence from labels:

  • taxonomy_9_trend_direction shows strong directional separation:
  • TRENDING | BULLISH: 15.25% tradable share, 43.89 tradable labels/day, 34.52% directional imbalance.
  • TRENDING | BEARISH: 15.08% tradable share, 43.40 tradable labels/day, -35.41% directional imbalance.
  • taxonomy_9_trend_volatility shows volatility matters for opportunity density:
  • TRENDING | HIGH_VOL: 17.16% tradable share, 49.43 tradable labels/day.
  • RANGING | LOW_VOL: 12.71% tradable share, 36.60 tradable labels/day.

Conclusion:

  • Keep this view as a diagnostic overlay.
  • Do not use it as the primary fold engine.

docs.title: 2. 1d native is the best current backbone for regime-oriented training
docs.text:
Evidence:

  • taxonomy_27_full:
  • TRENDING | NORMAL_VOL | BULLISH: 23.58% of bars, 21 periods, average 19.95 days, max 62.0 days.
  • TRENDING | NORMAL_VOL | BEARISH: 24.42%, 35 periods, average 12.4 days, max 60.0 days.
  • taxonomy_9_trend_volatility:
  • TRENDING | NORMAL_VOL: 53.57%, 46 periods, average 20.70 days, max 64.0 days.
  • RANGING | NORMAL_VOL: 22.73%, average 12.63 days, max 39.0 days.
  • taxonomy_9_trend_direction:
  • TRENDING | BULLISH: max 69.0 days.
  • TRENDING | BEARISH: max 69.0 days.
  • Threshold summary is finally operational:
  • taxonomy_27_full: 30 periods with at least 12 bars.
  • taxonomy_9_trend_volatility: 46 periods with at least 12 bars.
  • taxonomy_9_trend_direction: 27 periods with at least 12 bars.

Interpretation:

  • This is the first view that produces blocks long enough to become real train/validation units.
  • It is much more stable than 5m -> daily dominant, but still detailed enough to separate bullish versus bearish trend environments.
  • It looks like the best current compromise between stability and information content.

Useful evidence from labels:

  • Label density is much flatter than in 5m -> daily dominant.
  • That is not a weakness. It means the macro state is smoother and less biased by intraday noise.
  • taxonomy_9_trend_volatility:
  • TRENDING | NORMAL_VOL: 14.49% tradable share.
  • RANGING | NORMAL_VOL: 14.08%.
  • NEUTRAL | NORMAL_VOL: 14.81%.
  • taxonomy_9_trend_direction:
  • TRENDING | BULLISH: 14.92% tradable share.
  • TRENDING | BEARISH: 13.77%.

Conclusion:

  • This is the best candidate for replacing arbitrary 3-month folds.
  • If we redesign training now, this should be the main regime axis.

docs.title: 3. 1w native is excellent as a macro filter, but too coarse to be the only training axis
docs.text:
Evidence:

  • taxonomy_27_full:
  • TRENDING | NORMAL_VOL | BULLISH: average 75.83 days, max 224.0 days.
  • TRENDING | NORMAL_VOL | BEARISH: average 51.8 days, max 147.0 days.
  • taxonomy_9_trend_volatility:
  • TRENDING | NORMAL_VOL: 57.08%, average 65.15 days, max 259.0 days.
  • taxonomy_9_trend_direction:
  • TRENDING | BULLISH: max 224.0 days.
  • TRENDING | BEARISH: average 100.33 days, max 210.0 days.
  • taxonomy_3_volatility is almost collapsed:
  • NORMAL_VOL dominates 93.40% of bars with max 553.0 days.

Interpretation:

  • This view gives very strong persistence.
  • It is almost too stable.
  • Several taxonomies compress market history into a few giant states, which is good for macro context but weak for fine specialization.

Useful evidence from labels:

  • Many label distributions become very flat:
  • taxonomy_3_trend: TRENDING 14.72%, NEUTRAL 14.78%, RANGING 14.98%.
  • taxonomy_9_trend_volatility: most large groups cluster around 14.76% to 14.84%.
  • That suggests this view is better for regime routing or top-level gating than for per-trade specialization.

Conclusion:

  • Use 1w native as a macro regime filter or router.
  • Do not make it the only fold definition.

docs.title: What The Results Suggest About Training
What should stop
docs.text:
• Stop treating arbitrary 3-month windows as the default scientific unit.
  • Stop assuming a temporal fold is meaningful just because it is contiguous in time.
  • Stop trying to force 27 exact daily-dominant states from 5m into contiguous folds. The evidence says they are too unstable.

docs.title: What is already justified now
docs.text:
• It is already justified to redesign experiments around 1d native regimes.
  • It is not necessary to fully finish the persistence state machine before starting the first regime-oriented training redesign.
  • The current detector is imperfect, but it is already good enough to support a first generation of regime-based training experiments at the 1d level.

docs.title: What is not justified yet
docs.text:
• It is not justified to jump directly to 27 regime-specialized models.
  • It is not justified to use only 1w native regimes as the full training axis.
  • It is not justified to assume transitions are harmless. We still need explicit transition handling before any live-routing logic.

docs.title: Option A. 1d native pooled same-regime training
docs.text:
How it works:

  • Pick a 1d native taxonomy, preferably taxonomy_9_trend_direction or taxonomy_9_trend_volatility.
  • Collect all segments from the same regime family across multiple years.
  • Train on pooled non-contiguous history from that regime family.
  • Validate on later held-out segments from the same regime family.

Why it is attractive:

  • It directly answers your thesis: train on same market type, not arbitrary calendar windows.
  • It gives more data than one contiguous block.
  • It matches the evidence that 1d native states are stable enough to define meaningful segments.

Recommended first version:

  • Start with taxonomy_9_trend_direction.
  • Families:
  • TRENDING | BULLISH
  • TRENDING | BEARISH
  • RANGING | BULLISH
  • RANGING | BEARISH
  • Skip tiny families until we have persistence logic.

docs.title: Option B. 1d native contiguous-block training
docs.text:
How it works:

  • Use the longest contiguous 1d native periods as train/validation units.
  • Example: train on one or more long TRENDING | NORMAL_VOL | BULLISH blocks, validate on a later bullish block of the same family.

Why it is attractive:

  • Cleaner scientifically.
  • Easier to explain than pooled non-contiguous training.
  • Good first benchmark against current Script2 folds.

Why it is weaker than Option A:

  • Less data.
  • More variance from one block to another.
  • Risk of over-reading one specific bull or bear episode.

docs.title: Option C. Two-stage hierarchy: 1w native router, 1d native specialist
docs.text:
How it works:

  • First detect the macro regime with 1w native.
  • Inside that macro regime, select a 1d native specialist model or specialist hyperparameter family.

Example:

  • If 1w native says TRENDING | NORMAL_VOL | BULLISH, route to the 1d native bullish-trend family.
  • If 1w native says TRENDING | NORMAL_VOL | BEARISH, route to bearish specialists.

Why it is attractive:

  • Uses the best property of each detector:
  • 1w native: strong persistence
  • 1d native: useful specialization
  • This looks like the most realistic path toward a live model router.

Why not first:

  • It is more complex operationally.
  • Better as phase 2 after proving that 1d native specialization actually helps.

docs.title: Option D. Global model plus regime features
docs.text:
How it works:

  • Keep one global training set.
  • Add regime context as features:
  • 1d native taxonomy
  • 1w native taxonomy
  • maybe later confirmed versus candidate regime

Why it is attractive:

  • Lowest operational complexity.
  • Easy baseline.
  • Lets us test whether regime information helps before committing to multiple models.

Why it may underperform:

  • The model still has to learn very different behaviors in one parameter space.
  • Strong regime-specific edges can get blurred.

docs.title: Option E. Regime-aware do-not-trade policy
docs.text:
How it works:

  • Use regime only to block trades in low-information or noisy environments.
  • Candidate sources:
  • 5m -> daily dominant low dominance days
  • future MIXED / UNSTABLE states
  • low tradable-density groups

Why it matters:

  • One likely outcome of this research is not only “which model to use”, but also “when not to trade”.
  • Some environments may be better handled by no position than by a weaker specialist.

docs.title: What I Would Actually Do Next
Phase 1: first real redesign
docs.text:
I would start now with:

  1. 1d native as the main regime backbone.
  2. taxonomy_9_trend_direction as the first family to test.
  3. Pooled same-regime training across non-contiguous segments.
  4. Held-out later same-regime validation.
  5. A plain baseline with one global model for comparison.

This is the cleanest first experiment that actually attacks the thesis.

docs.title: Phase 2: macro conditioning
docs.text:
If phase 1 shows signal:

  1. add 1w native as a macro router or gating feature
  2. compare:
  3. global model
  4. 1d native specialist models
  5. 1w -> 1d hierarchical routing

docs.title: Phase 3: transition safety
docs.text:
After that:

  1. implement persistence
  2. implement candidate versus confirmed
  3. add MIXED / UNSTABLE
  4. test whether transition periods are where models break

docs.title: My Recommendation On The Detector Plan
docs.text:
I would not pause everything to perfect the detector first.

I would do this:

  • Proceed now with regime-oriented training design using current 1d native output.
  • Continue the follow-up plan selectively, not sequentially.

Priority order:

  1. Build the first 1d native regime-based training experiments.
  2. Map Script2 folds and model outcomes onto 1d native and 1w native regimes.
  3. Then add persistence and transition logic.
  4. Only after that consider HMM, Markov switching, or change-point benchmarks.

Why:

  • The current evidence is already strong enough to reject arbitrary 3-month folds as the main research design.
  • The current evidence is already strong enough to justify 1d native regime-oriented experiments.
  • The next detector improvements are important, but they should refine the regime framework, not block it.

docs.title: Bottom Line
docs.text:
• 5m -> daily dominant: keep as diagnostic overlay.
  • 1d native: use as the main training regime axis now.
  • 1w native: use as macro context or routing layer, not as the only fold definition.
  • Best first experiment: pooled same-regime training on 1d native, with held-out later same-regime validation and a global-model baseline beside it.

docs.title: Regime Follow-Up Tasks
docs.text:
1. Native 1d regime pipeline Build a second path inside analyze_btc_regime_history.py that fetches or reuses BTCUSDT 1d, computes indicators and regimes directly on daily candles, and exports the same family of outputs as the current 5m -> daily dominant path. Keep the two paths clearly separated in filenames and summaries so we can compare them side by side.

  2. 5m -> daily dominant versus native 1d comparison Add a direct comparison layer that answers where both methods agree, where they disagree, and how often. Export daily agreement tables, confusion matrices, and persistence comparisons for 3, 9, and 27 taxonomies.

  3. Persistence state machine Extend the regime logic with three explicit states: observed_regime, candidate_regime, and confirmed_regime. Add a boolean confirmed flag plus counters such as days_in_candidate, days_in_confirmed, and days_since_last_switch.

  4. Confirmation rules Test regime confirmation rules that can run live without future leakage. At minimum support:

  5. minimum duration in days
  6. consecutive-day confirmation
  7. majority vote over a rolling daily window

  8. configurable confidence threshold based on daily dominance percentage

  9. Hysteresis and transition friction Add asymmetric entry and exit thresholds so the regime does not flip on tiny changes. The implementation must make it explicit when a regime is only a weak candidate versus a confirmed switch. Export the transition points so we can inspect whipsaw behavior.

  10. MIXED and low-confidence days Introduce a MIXED or UNSTABLE bucket for days where the daily winner is too weak to trust. Make the purity threshold configurable and export counts, percentages, and contiguous periods for these mixed days.

  11. Regime outputs focused on fold design Produce fold-oriented artifacts that are easy to read:

  12. longest contiguous periods by taxonomy
  13. longest confirmed periods by taxonomy
  14. threshold summaries for 30d, 60d, and 90d

  15. candidate fold tables with start, end, duration, dominance, and return

  16. Label analysis extensions Keep using Script2 labeling_config, then expand the outputs so we can inspect:

  17. tradable density by regime
  18. LONG versus SHORT skew by regime
  19. NONE density by regime

  20. daily label density during transition periods versus confirmed periods

  21. Script2 fold alignment Map the four Script2 folds onto the regime timelines and measure:

  22. dominant regimes in train and validation
  23. regime drift between train and validation

  24. whether profitable folds align with stable or unstable regimes

  25. Predictions and model behavior Once Script2 prediction artifacts are wired in, join predictions with regime state so we can measure which models and hyperparameter families behave best in:

    • confirmed bullish environments
    • confirmed bearish environments
    • high-volatility environments
    • transition or mixed environments

  26. Tier B follow-up Reuse the same framework on Tier B as soon as enough runs exist. Keep Tier A and Tier B outputs comparable so we can see whether the richer feature set improves regime sensitivity, robustness, or model specialization.

  27. Research-grade alternatives Add optional benchmarks for more advanced causal regime methods that can still run live:

    • Hidden Markov Models
    • Markov-switching models
    • online change-point detection These should be comparison tracks, not replacements, until they prove operational value.

docs.title: What This File Is
docs.text:
What this section is: a short entry point for the run, with pointers to the more focused summaries.

  • Daily regime file: summary_daily_regimes.md
  • Label distribution file: summary_label_distributions.md
  • Intraday fragmentation file: summary_intraday_fragmentation.md

docs.title: All Generated Artifacts
docs.text:
What this section is: a full inventory of what this script writes into the run folder, explained in simple language so each file has a clear purpose.

  • run_context.json: the machine-readable receipt for the run. It records the exact input range, parquet ids, row counts, and Script2 labeling settings used.
  • summary.md: the front door. Read this first to understand what the run is, what files exist, and where to go next.
  • summary_daily_regimes.md: the daily regime notebook. It shows which basket owned each day and how long those daily stretches lasted.
  • summary_label_distributions.md: the labels notebook. It shows how LONG, SHORT, and NONE labels distribute inside each daily regime basket.
  • summary_intraday_fragmentation.md: the fragmentation notebook. It explains how noisy the exact 5m regime states are before we collapse them by day.
  • daily_regimes.csv: one row per day. This is the daily market map with the winning basket for each 3/9/27 taxonomy and the dominance percentage of that winner.
  • daily_regimes_with_labels.csv: one row per day plus daily label totals. This is the easiest place to spot days with many tradable labels or days that are mostly NONE.
  • labels_with_daily_regimes.parquet: one row per label timestamp joined with its day's dominant regime baskets. Use this when you want row-level analysis instead of daily aggregates.
  • daily_taxonomy_*_coverage.csv: one file per taxonomy, grouped by daily dominant basket. These files answer 'how many days did each basket own, and how persistent was it across daily periods?'.
  • daily_taxonomy_*_periods.csv: one file per taxonomy, one row per contiguous daily block. These files answer 'when did this basket start, when did it end, and how long did it last?'.
  • intraday_taxonomy_*_coverage.csv: one file per taxonomy, grouped from exact 5m states. These files answer 'which exact states consume the most bars even if they do not stay stable for long?'.
  • intraday_taxonomy_*_periods.csv: one file per taxonomy, one row per uninterrupted 5m exact-state run. These files are for diagnosing twitchiness, not for proposing folds directly.
  • label_distribution_taxonomy_*.csv: one file per taxonomy showing label behavior by daily dominant basket. These files answer 'where are the LONGs, where are the SHORTs, and where is trading mostly absent?'.

docs.title: Interpretation Hints
docs.text:
What this section is: reading guidance so the daily, label, and intraday files are not confused with each other.

  • Intraday exact-state periods remain useful diagnostically, but they are expected to fragment heavily.
  • Daily dominant baskets are the fold-oriented view: they tell us what regime owned each day.
  • Label distributions over daily dominant baskets help reveal long/short dominance and sparse-trade days.
  • If a daily regime has many days but very few tradable labels, it may be a natural do not trade candidate.

  2. summary_label_distributions.md to understand LONG/SHORT/NONE density inside those daily baskets.
  3. summary_intraday_fragmentation.md to understand why the current detector still feels twitchy.

docs.title: Daily Coverage Top 12

docs.title: What This File Is
docs.text: What this file is: how Script2 LONG/SHORT/NONE labels distribute inside the daily dominant regime baskets, including density-per-day metrics.

docs.title: taxonomy_27_full
docs.text:
What this section is: How Script2 labels behave when the full 27-state basket dominates the day.

  • Label distribution CSV: label_distribution_taxonomy_27_full.csv

docs.title: Quick Highlights
docs.text:
What this section is: fast takeaways: which groups have the most tradable density, the biggest LONG skew, the biggest SHORT skew, and the most NONE labels.

  • Highest tradable density: NEUTRAL | HIGH_VOL | BULLISH with 18.06% tradable labels and 52.00 tradable labels/day.
  • Strongest LONG skew: TRENDING | NORMAL_VOL | BULLISH with 37.93% directional imbalance.
  • Strongest SHORT skew: TRENDING | HIGH_VOL | BEARISH with -40.44% directional imbalance.
  • Highest NONE share: NEUTRAL | LOW_VOL | BULLISH with 90.28% NONE labels and 260.00 NONE/day.

  • short_count: number of SHORT labels

  • short_share_pct: SHORT labels as a share of all labels
  • none_share_pct: NONE labels as a share of all labels
  • directional_imbalance_pct: LONG versus SHORT skew inside tradable labels; positive means LONG-heavy, negative means SHORT-heavy
  • avg_daily_dominance_pct: average dominance of the winning basket across all days in the group

docs.title: All Generated Artifacts
docs.text:
What this section is: a complete list of what this native-timeframe run writes to disk.

  • run_context.json: exact inputs, parquet ids, row counts, and Script2 label settings used.
  • summary.md: the entry point for this native timeframe run.
  • summary_regimes.md: the main regime notebook for native contiguous periods and coverage.
  • summary_label_distributions.md: the label notebook for LONG, SHORT, and NONE behavior inside native regimes.
  • regime_bars.csv: one row per native candle with all regime labels attached.
  • labels_with_regimes.parquet: one row per 5m label joined to the latest known native regime.
  • native_taxonomy_*_coverage.csv: one file per taxonomy summarizing which native regime groups own the most bars.
  • native_taxonomy_*_periods.csv: one file per taxonomy with contiguous native regime periods.
  • label_distribution_taxonomy_*.csv: one file per taxonomy showing label behavior inside native regime groups.

docs.title: All Generated Artifacts
docs.text:
What this section is: a complete list of what this native-timeframe run writes to disk.

  • run_context.json: exact inputs, parquet ids, row counts, and Script2 label settings used.
  • summary.md: the entry point for this native timeframe run.
  • summary_regimes.md: the main regime notebook for native contiguous periods and coverage.
  • summary_label_distributions.md: the label notebook for LONG, SHORT, and NONE behavior inside native regimes.
  • regime_bars.csv: one row per native candle with all regime labels attached.
  • labels_with_regimes.parquet: one row per 5m label joined to the latest known native regime.
  • native_taxonomy_*_coverage.csv: one file per taxonomy summarizing which native regime groups own the most bars.
  • native_taxonomy_*_periods.csv: one file per taxonomy with contiguous native regime periods.
  • label_distribution_taxonomy_*.csv: one file per taxonomy showing label behavior inside native regime groups.

docs.title: Quick Highlights
docs.text:
What this section is: fast takeaways: highest tradable density, strongest LONG skew, strongest SHORT skew, and largest NONE share.

  • Highest tradable density: RANGING | HIGH_VOL | NEUTRAL with 17.06% tradable labels and 49.14 tradable labels/day.
  • Strongest LONG skew: TRENDING | LOW_VOL | NEUTRAL with 10.38% directional imbalance.
  • Strongest SHORT skew: TRENDING | LOW_VOL | BEARISH with -29.78% directional imbalance.
  • Highest NONE share: TRENDING | LOW_VOL | BEARISH with 88.84% NONE labels and 255.86 NONE/day.
