docs.title: Script 2 Ensemble Phase 1 Block A Audit
docs.text:
• Date: 2026-04-03
  • Scope: Preliminary audit of the first systematic ensemble discovery batch
  • Batch: 20260403_021500_phase1_block_a
  • Status: Preliminary but clearly encouraging

docs.title: 1. Purpose
docs.text:
This note records what the first systematic ensemble batch already tells us about the Script 2 ensemble hypothesis.

The goal here is not to claim final thesis conclusions.

The goal is to answer one narrower question:

Does a structured ensemble workflow already show credible signs that it can improve monetization through better selectivity, not just through random noise or infrastructure artifacts?

The short answer is:

  • yes, preliminarily
  • and the signal is strong enough to justify continuing to the next ensemble blocks

docs.title: 2. What Was Executed
docs.text:
Block A is the single-family monetization block defined in 20260403_SCRIPT2_ENSEMBLE_DISCOVERY_CAMPAIGN.md.

It used:

  • Tier B source pool only
  • 20260325_tierB_mlp_ex
  • 20260328_tierB_corrected
  • folds:
  • F1
  • F3
  • families:
  • mlp
  • tcn
  • vit
  • ranking modes:
  • promise
  • friction
  • top_per_model:
  • 2
  • 3
  • diversity modes:
  • off
  • mild
  • tight
  • merge rules:
  • the current 8-rule battery

Execution summary:

  • 72 completed plan executions
  • 576 rule-level ensemble runs
  • 33 / 72 executions produced a positive best ensemble

The campaign-level annex is here:

  • 20260403_SCRIPT2_ENSEMBLE_PHASE1_BLOCK_A_CAMPAIGN_REPORT.md

docs.title: 3. Why These Results Matter
docs.text:
The pre-ensemble thesis evidence had already established the main economic problem:

  • models often learn something
  • but the signal rarely survives as net profit
  • trade count and friction dominate the outcome

That is why Block A matters.

This batch is the first deliberate ensemble test that asks:

  • can we combine trials in a way that reduces churn
  • while keeping or improving the quality of the remaining trades

The important lens here is therefore not only raw PnL.

The important lens is:

  • trade reduction
  • net PnL change
  • gross-proxy change (PnL + friction)
  • gross-per-trade change
  • whether improvements are better than proportional relative to the trade cut

That is the strongest way to distinguish:

  • “this only traded less” from
  • “this traded less and selected better”

docs.title: 4.1 Positive executions are no longer rare inside the batch
docs.text:
At the execution level, 33 / 72 executions had a positive best ensemble.

That does not mean the full campaign is solved.

But it is already a much healthier picture than the original single-trial landscape, where positive outcomes were extremely scarce.

docs.title: 4.2 Several top executions are strongly profitable
docs.text:
Examples from the batch:

  • block_a_mlp_f1_promise_top2_tight: +$445.93
  • block_a_mlp_f1_promise_top3_tight: +$412.42
  • block_a_tcn_f1_promise_top2_tight: +$256.99
  • block_a_vit_f3_friction_top2_off: +$161.83

Those are not tiny fluctuations.

They are large enough to justify serious follow-up rather than treating the ensemble path as a dead end.

docs.title: 4.3 Delta vs best source is often meaningfully positive
docs.text:
Several of the strongest executions are not only positive in absolute terms.

They also improve materially versus the best available source baseline within the same execution context.

Examples:

  • block_a_mlp_f1_promise_top2_tight: +$373.31 vs best source
  • block_a_tcn_f1_promise_top2_tight: +$445.36 vs best source
  • block_a_vit_f3_friction_top2_off: +$515.00 vs best source

That is a much stronger signal than “the ensemble happened to be positive.”

It says the merge process can sometimes outperform the individual source trials it came from.

docs.title: 5. Selectivity vs Friction Findings
docs.text: This is the most important part of the current audit.

docs.title: 5.1 Better-than-proportional cases exist clearly
docs.text:
The batch now tracks:

  • Trade Red%
  • PnL Imp%
  • Gross Chg%
  • Gross/trade Chg%
  • Selectivity Surplus
  • Interpretation

Several of the strongest executions are classified as Better than proportional.

That matters because it means:

  • the ensemble did not improve merely by cutting trades
  • the net improvement was larger than the trade cut alone would suggest

Examples:

  • block_a_mlp_f1_promise_top2_tight
  • Trade Red% = 33.33%
  • PnL Imp% = 514.05%
  • Surplus = 480.72%
  • Gross/trade Chg% = 94.07%
  • block_a_tcn_f1_promise_top2_tight
  • Trade Red% = 12.99%
  • PnL Imp% = 236.43%
  • Surplus = 223.44%
  • Gross/trade Chg% = 99.96%
  • block_a_vit_f3_friction_top2_off
  • Trade Red% = 41.57%
  • PnL Imp% = 145.82%
  • Surplus = 104.25%
  • Gross/trade Chg% = 172.07%

These are exactly the kinds of cases that support the ensemble thesis most strongly.

docs.title: 5.2 Gross-per-trade improvement is a second confirmation
docs.text:
Net PnL can improve simply because friction falls.

Gross-per-trade is therefore an important confirmation metric.

When gross-per-trade improves, the remaining trades appear economically better on average.

The batch summary already shows that this happens often enough to matter:

  • mlp: 66.67% gross-per-trade improvement rate
  • tcn: 70.83%
  • vit: 50.00%

That is not proof of final robustness.

But it is good evidence that the ensembles are sometimes choosing better opportunities, not only fewer opportunities.

docs.title: 5.3 Tight diversity looks encouraging
docs.text:
At the current coarse ladder:

  • tight was the best diversity mode by average best-ensemble PnL
  • it also showed the strongest average gross-per-trade change in the trade-count section

This does not prove the chosen thresholds are optimal.

But it does support the current idea that prediction diversity is worth treating as a first-class research axis, not as a cosmetic option.

docs.title: 6. Family-Level Reading
docs.text:
The current family summary should be read as preliminary directional evidence, not as final ranking truth.

At this stage:

  • vit has the best average best-ensemble PnL among the three families
  • mlp shows some of the strongest better-than-proportional cases
  • tcn also produces strong selectivity-surplus outcomes in the best executions

The important conclusion is not “one family already won.”

The important conclusion is:

  • all three families show enough life to justify continuing
  • and none of them looks dead enough to exclude yet

docs.title: 7.1 Merge-rule conclusions are still weak
docs.text:
This batch does not justify any strong claim such as:

  • all_must_agree is the best rule
  • majority_vote is worse
  • weighted voting is definitively superior or inferior

Why not?

Because Block A is still working with small selected pools.

In practice, these executions are often merging only 2 or 3 members.

In that regime:

  • several merge rules collapse to very similar behavior
  • voting rules can become nearly equivalent
  • the true expressive difference between rules is still under-tested

This is visible directly in the campaign report, where some rules produce identical or near-identical aggregate behavior.

So the correct reading is:

  • merge-rule differences are interesting
  • but they are not yet a strong finding
  • the stronger test of merge logic will come with larger multi-member pools, especially in cross-model blocks

docs.title: 7.2 Block A is only the first block
docs.text:
This batch covers only:

  • single-family ensembles
  • F1 and F3
  • a narrow Tier B source frontier

It does not yet answer:

  • whether cross-model pools are stronger
  • whether larger ensembles improve or collapse
  • whether diversity helps equally across broader member sets
  • whether the best Block A behavior survives in later blocks

docs.title: 7.3 Average campaign PnL is still mostly negative
docs.text:
Even with these encouraging signs, the campaign averages are still not broadly positive.

That means:

  • the ensemble path is promising
  • but not solved
  • and still needs systematic follow-up before any thesis-level victory statement

docs.title: 8. What This Audit Supports
docs.text:
At this point, the evidence supports the following claims:

  1. The ensemble workflow is not only functional; it is economically interesting.
  2. Several ensemble executions materially outperform their best source baselines.
  3. Some of the strongest gains are better-than-proportional, which argues against a pure “less friction only” explanation.
  4. Gross-per-trade improvements appear often enough to support the idea of improved trade selection.
  5. Diversity-aware selection deserves to remain central in the next campaign stage.
  6. The ensemble direction is now strong enough to justify deeper and broader testing.

docs.title: 9. What This Audit Does Not Support Yet
docs.text:
The current evidence does not yet support:

  1. a final ranking of merge rules
  2. a final ranking of model families
  3. a final claim that ensembles are robustly profitable overall
  4. a final claim that the selected diversity thresholds are the right ones
  5. a final thesis statement about ensemble superiority across the full Script 2 space

Those require more blocks and broader coverage.

docs.title: 10. Next Recommended Step
docs.text:
The correct next move is to continue the systematic campaign, not to stop here.

Recommended next step:

  1. run the next ensemble block, especially the cross-model monetization block
  2. keep the selectivity/proportionality lens active
  3. continue treating diversity as a primary axis
  4. delay strong merge-rule conclusions until larger pools are tested

In short:

  • the current signal is clearly positive
  • the ensemble hypothesis is now materially more credible
  • but the right response is disciplined expansion, not premature closure

docs.title: Script 2 Ensemble Phase 1 Block B Audit
docs.text:
• Date: 2026-04-04
  • Scope: Preliminary audit of the cross-model ensemble monetization block
  • Batch: 20260403_phase1_block_b
  • Status: Preliminary and materially informative

docs.title: 1. Purpose
docs.text:
This note records what the cross-model ensemble block now tells us about the main Script 2 ensemble question:

Do cross-model combinations look stronger than single-family ensembles when the real objective is net monetization under friction?

This is the most important Phase 1 question, because the thesis-side value of ensemble work is not merely “can one family vote with itself,” but whether different families can be combined into a better economic decision process.

The short answer from Block B is:

  • yes, preliminarily
  • and the evidence is stronger than it was after Block A alone

docs.title: 2. What Was Executed
docs.text:
Block B is the cross-model monetization block defined in 20260403_SCRIPT2_ENSEMBLE_DISCOVERY_CAMPAIGN.md.

It used:

  • Tier B source pool only
  • 20260325_tierB_mlp_ex
  • 20260328_tierB_corrected
  • folds:
  • F1
  • F3
  • cross-model pools:
  • tcn,vit
  • mlp,tcn
  • mlp,vit
  • mlp,tcn,vit
  • logistic,mlp,tcn,vit
  • ranking modes:
  • promise
  • friction
  • top_per_model = 1
  • diversity modes:
  • off
  • mild
  • tight
  • merge rules:
  • the current 8-rule battery

Execution summary:

  • 60 completed plan executions
  • 480 rule-level ensemble runs
  • 28 / 60 executions produced a positive best ensemble

The campaign-level annex is here:

  • 20260404_SCRIPT2_ENSEMBLE_PHASE1_BLOCK_B_CAMPAIGN_REPORT.md

docs.title: 3. Why Block B Matters More Than Block A
docs.text:
Block A already showed that ensembles could be economically interesting.

But Block A still stayed inside one family at a time.

Block B is more important because it asks whether the ensemble idea improves when we let different model families interact.

That is much closer to the real thesis-side hypothesis:

  • different families may capture partially different edges
  • consensus or partial agreement between them may create a more selective and more economically useful signal

Block B therefore deserves more weight than Block A when we choose what to do next.

docs.title: 4.1 The strongest cross-model pool is now closer to breakeven than the best Block A family
docs.text:
From Block A:

  • best family by average best-ensemble PnL was vit at -$17.32

From Block B:

  • best cross-model pool by average best-ensemble PnL is mlp,tcn,vit at -$3.85

That is still not a final profitability claim.

But it is an important directional signal:

  • the best cross-model pool is now economically closer to neutral than the best single-family pool

docs.title: 4.2 Fold F1 is now clearly positive on average
docs.text:
Block B fold summary:

  • F1 average best-ensemble PnL: +$46.90
  • F3 average best-ensemble PnL: -$239.14

This is stronger than the Block A picture, where:

  • F1 was only -$9.17 on average

So the current evidence supports a more specific statement:

  • the cross-model thesis looks especially alive in F1

docs.title: 4.3 Tight diversity again comes out best
docs.text:
Just as in Block A, Block B again favors tight as the best diversity mode by average best-ensemble PnL:

  • tight: -$79.13
  • mild: -$104.48
  • off: -$104.76

This is still not proof that the chosen thresholds are optimal.

But it is now a repeated pattern across both Phase 1 blocks, which gives the diversity idea substantially more credibility.

docs.title: 4.4 There are very strong better-than-proportional cases
docs.text:
The best Block B execution:

  • block_b_mlp_tcn_vit_f1_friction_top1_tight
  • best net PnL: +$407.52
  • delta vs best source: +$499.05
  • Trade Red% = 64.62%
  • PnL Imp% = 545.22%
  • Selectivity Surplus = 480.59%
  • Gross/trade Chg% = 257.29%

That is exactly the kind of result that argues against a simple “less trading, less friction” story.

It says:

  • the ensemble traded much less
  • net improved dramatically more than the trade cut alone would suggest
  • and the economic quality of the remaining trades improved sharply

docs.title: 5.1 mlp,tcn,vit now looks like the strongest core candidate
docs.text:
Among the tested pools, mlp,tcn,vit is the best by average best-ensemble PnL:

  • average best PnL: -$3.85
  • positive-execution rate: 58.33%

That makes it the strongest current candidate for the “core money pool.”

docs.title: 5.2 logistic,mlp,tcn,vit is interesting but not yet clean
docs.text:
The broader pool with logistic is second by average best-ensemble PnL:

  • average best PnL: -$44.70
  • positive-execution rate: 58.33%

So logistic cannot be dismissed.

But the block also shows that this broader pool can produce unstable turnover patterns in some executions, especially on the gross side.

That means:

  • logistic may add useful diversity
  • but it is not yet proven to be a universally stabilizing member

docs.title: 5.3 mlp,tcn looks high-upside but unstable
docs.text:
The mlp,tcn pair has:

  • the highest positive-execution rate: 66.67%

But its average best-ensemble PnL is still:

  • -$173.47

That usually means:

  • some very strong wins
  • but too much instability or too many weak executions around them

So this pair should not be discarded, but it also should not yet be treated as the clean winner.

docs.title: 6. Selectivity vs Friction Findings
docs.text: This remains the key interpretive lens.

docs.title: 6.1 Better-than-proportional evidence remains strong
docs.text:
Block B continues the same pattern already seen in Block A:

  • several top executions are clearly Better than proportional
  • the best cases improve much more than a simple trade cut would imply

That matters because it supports the stronger interpretation:

  • at least some ensembles are selecting better opportunities
  • not merely suppressing turnover

docs.title: 6.2 Gross-per-trade still matters as confirmation
docs.text:
Block B family summary shows:

  • mlp,tcn,vit: gross-per-trade improvement rate 58.33%
  • logistic,mlp,tcn,vit: 58.33%
  • tcn,vit: 58.33%

This is not overwhelming proof on its own.

But together with the better-than-proportional cases, it strengthens the case that the cross-model ensembles are sometimes preserving or improving gross edge per decision.

docs.title: 7. Merge-Rule Caveat Still Applies
docs.text:
Block B is more informative than Block A for merge rules, but the same core warning still applies:

  • do not overread merge-rule rankings yet

Why:

  • the pools are larger than Block A, but they are still small enough that some voting rules collapse toward similar behavior
  • all_must_agree and majority_vote:0.85 still look nearly identical in the aggregate tables
  • several strong outcomes are driven at least as much by the selected source pool as by the merge rule itself

So the correct reading is:

  • merge-rule behavior is becoming more interesting
  • but the current evidence is still not a final verdict on merge design

docs.title: 8. What Block B Changes In The Overall Picture
docs.text:
After Block A alone, the ensemble direction looked promising.

After Block B, the picture is stronger:

  1. the main cross-model thesis now looks more credible than it did after single-family testing alone
  2. the best cross-model pool is now stronger than the best single-family pool on average
  3. F1 now looks like a serious monetization zone for cross-model ensembles
  4. repeated support for tight diversity means the diversity-aware planner is no longer just an implementation feature; it is part of the economic story

That is enough to justify continued expansion.

docs.title: 9. What This Still Does Not Prove
docs.text:
Even after Block B, the current evidence still does not prove:

  1. that ensembles are broadly profitable overall
  2. that one merge rule is the final winner
  3. that logistic should always be included
  4. that the current diversity thresholds are optimal
  5. that the same story will hold in later folds or under source-pool ablations

So the signal is stronger now, but still preliminary.

docs.title: 10. Recommended Next Block
docs.text:
The next block should not be F2/F4 controls yet.

The next block should be a source provenance ablation.

Why this is the best next step:

Block B already suggests that cross-model pools can work.

The next practical question is:

Are the good ensembles mainly coming from 20260325_tierB_mlp_ex, from 20260328_tierB_corrected, or only from mixing both?

That is more actionable right now than jumping straight to harder control folds, because it tells us where the winning members are actually coming from.

Recommended Phase 2 ablation:

  • source pools:
  • 20260325 only
  • 20260328 only
  • combined
  • model pools:
  • tcn,vit
  • mlp,tcn
  • mlp,tcn,vit
  • logistic,mlp,tcn,vit
  • folds:
  • F1
  • F3
  • ranking:
  • promise
  • friction
  • diversity:
  • off
  • tight

That block should answer whether the current cross-model signal is:

  • coming mainly from one run
  • strengthened by mixing runs
  • or diluted by mixing incompatible frontier styles

docs.title: 11. Bottom Line
docs.text:
Block B is the strongest ensemble evidence so far.

The correct takeaway is:

  • the ensemble direction is now more credible than it was after Block A
  • cross-model combinations appear more promising than single-family ensembles
  • the next intelligent step is to understand source provenance before expanding blindly

docs.title: Script 2 Ensemble Phase 2 Source Provenance Ablation Audit
docs.text:
• Date: 2026-04-05
  • Scope: Preliminary audit of the source-provenance ablation block
  • Batch: 20260404_phase2_source_provenance_ablation
  • Status: Preliminary, materially informative, and decision-shaping

docs.title: 1. Purpose
docs.text:
This note records what the source-provenance ablation block now tells us about the current Script 2 ensemble thesis:

Are the strongest ensemble results coming mainly from one source frontier, or does the corrected Tier B pool contribute enough to justify mixing it in as a primary source?

This question matters because Block B already suggested that cross-model ensembles can be economically interesting. The next uncertainty was whether the source frontier behind those ensembles should be:

  • 20260325_tierB_mlp_ex only
  • 20260328_tierB_corrected only
  • or both combined

The short answer from this block is:

  • 20260325_tierB_mlp_ex is currently the strongest primary source pool
  • the combined pool is still interesting, but secondary
  • 20260328_tierB_corrected alone should be deprioritized for now

docs.title: 2. What Was Executed
docs.text:
This block used:

  • source provenance modes:
  • 20260325_tierB_mlp_ex
  • 20260328_tierB_corrected
  • both combined
  • folds:
  • F1
  • F3
  • model pools:
  • tcn,vit
  • mlp,tcn
  • mlp,tcn,vit
  • logistic,mlp,tcn,vit
  • ranking modes:
  • promise
  • friction
  • top_per_model = 1
  • diversity modes:
  • off
  • tight
  • merge rules:
  • the current 8-rule battery

Execution summary:

  • 96 completed plan executions
  • 768 rule-level ensemble runs
  • 41 / 96 executions produced a positive best ensemble

The campaign-level annex is here:

  • 20260405_SCRIPT2_ENSEMBLE_PHASE2_SOURCE_PROVENANCE_ABLATION_CAMPAIGN_REPORT.md

docs.title: 3.1 20260325_tierB_mlp_ex is the strongest primary source frontier
docs.text:
Average best-ensemble net PnL by provenance:

  • 20260325_tierB_mlp_ex: -$24.08
  • 20260325_tierB_mlp_ex + 20260328_tierB_corrected: -$72.18
  • 20260328_tierB_corrected: -$290.02

Positive best-ensemble rate by provenance:

  • 20260325_tierB_mlp_ex: 65.62%
  • combined: 53.12%
  • 20260328_tierB_corrected: 9.38%

Selectivity surplus by provenance:

  • 20260325_tierB_mlp_ex: +84.34%
  • combined: +62.85%
  • 20260328_tierB_corrected: -31.95%

This is the most important result of the block.

It means the current ensemble edge is not being sourced evenly across the two Tier B frontiers. Right now, the auto-selected frontier is doing most of the economic heavy lifting.

docs.title: 3.2 The combined pool is still viable, but not the leader
docs.text:
The combined provenance is not a failure.

It remains second on:

  • average best-ensemble PnL
  • positive-execution rate
  • average PnL delta vs best source

It also still produces some of the strongest individual executions, especially in F1.

So the combined pool should not be discarded.

But this batch does not support treating the combined frontier as the new default primary source. It still trails the simpler 20260325-only setup on the metrics that matter most.

docs.title: 3.3 20260328_tierB_corrected alone is weak enough to deprioritize
docs.text:
20260328_tierB_corrected alone is materially worse than the other two source modes:

  • average best-ensemble PnL: -$290.02
  • positive best-ensemble rate: 9.38%
  • average selectivity surplus: -31.95%

This does not prove that the corrected frontier has zero value.

But it does say something important:

  • it is currently not competitive as a standalone primary ensemble source pool
  • if it contributes later, it is more likely as a selective diversity donor than as the main frontier

docs.title: 4.1 F1 remains the main money fold
docs.text:
Average best-ensemble PnL by provenance inside F1:

  • 20260325_tierB_mlp_ex: +$114.86
  • combined: +$74.16
  • 20260328_tierB_corrected: -$83.78

So F1 still carries the strongest evidence that the ensemble thesis can produce real monetization.

docs.title: 4.2 F3 is still materially weaker
docs.text:
Average best-ensemble PnL by provenance inside F3:

  • 20260325_tierB_mlp_ex: -$163.02
  • combined: -$218.52
  • 20260328_tierB_corrected: -$496.26

That does not erase the positive F3 cases.

But it does reinforce the current asymmetry:

  • F1 is the fold where the thesis looks alive
  • F3 still shows partial support, but not with the same strength

docs.title: 5.1 The best cross-fold recipes are mostly promise-ranked
docs.text:
When grouping by full recipe across F1 and F3 (source pool + model pool + rank mode + diversity mode), the strongest average net PnL results are:

  • combined logistic,mlp,tcn,vit, promise, tight: +$185.58
  • 20260325 only logistic,mlp,tcn,vit, promise, off: +$153.70
  • 20260325 only logistic,mlp,tcn,vit, promise, tight: +$147.96
  • 20260325 only tcn,vit, promise, tight: +$140.86
  • 20260325 only mlp,tcn,vit, promise, tight: +$140.25
  • 20260325 only mlp,tcn, promise, off: +$138.03

Important caveat:

  • each of these recipe averages comes from only 2 executions (F1 and F3)

So they are highly informative, but still preliminary.

docs.title: 5.2 The biggest absolute winners are still concentrated in F1
docs.text:
Top net-PnL execution:

  • phase2_prov_tierb_auto_only_mlp_tcn_vit_f1_friction_top1_off
  • best net PnL: +$426.42
  • delta vs best source: +$517.95

This is again a strong better-than-proportional case and reinforces the idea that the ensemble edge is currently concentrated in F1, especially when the source frontier is 20260325-only.

docs.title: 5.3 Merge-rule readings remain secondary
docs.text:
This report still shows all_must_agree frequently landing on top.

That is useful to note, but it should not be over-read yet.

The current member counts are still modest, and several rules can behave similarly when the pool is small. The provenance result is much more important than the merge-rule ranking at this stage.

docs.title: 6. What This Changes
docs.text:
This block narrows the next search space significantly.

Before this ablation, it was still reasonable to treat the two Tier B source frontiers as roughly interchangeable inputs.

After this block, that is no longer the best reading.

The current evidence says:

  • use 20260325_tierB_mlp_ex as the primary source frontier
  • keep the combined pool as a secondary candidate
  • stop spending major search budget on 20260328_tierB_corrected alone

That is a meaningful simplification.

It reduces experimental branching while staying aligned with the evidence.

docs.title: 7. Recommended Next Step
docs.text:
The next useful question is no longer source provenance.

It is cross-fold transfer.

More specifically:

  • take the strongest recipe families discovered across F1 and F3
  • and test those same recipes on F2 and F4

That is the most direct way to answer the next thesis-side question:

Can the best current ensemble recipes survive outside the discovery folds, or is the current edge too fold-local?

Under the current planner workflow, this means:

  • replaying the same recipe specification on F2 and F4
  • using the same source pool, model pool, ranking mode, diversity mode, and top_per_model
  • while allowing the planner to re-select the actual fold-compatible trials

So this is recipe transfer, not exact fixed-trial transfer.

That is still useful and still worth doing now.

If later we want exact fixed-trial transfer, the runner can already execute manual --source-trial-id sets, but the current batch workflow does not yet orchestrate that pattern.

docs.title: 8. Three-Run Synthesis
docs.text: Taken together, the three completed ensemble blocks now give a much clearer picture than any single block alone.

docs.title: 8.1 What looks materially supported now
docs.text:
• the ensemble thesis is no longer just speculative
  • there are repeated better-than-proportional cases
  • diversity-aware selection, especially tight, keeps recurring as useful
  • F1 is the clearest money fold
  • cross-model pools are more important than single-family pools
  • the strongest current primary source frontier is 20260325_tierB_mlp_ex

That is already a meaningful body of evidence.

docs.title: 8.2 What pattern is emerging
docs.text:
The current pattern is not random.

It looks like this:

  1. the source frontier matters a lot
  2. the strongest frontier today is 20260325_tierB_mlp_ex
  3. cross-model combinations matter more than staying inside one family
  4. tighter diversity constraints often help rather than hurt
  5. the best outcomes often come from reducing trade count while improving economic quality per remaining trade

That is a coherent methodological direction, not just a few lucky runs.

docs.title: 8.3 What confidence we should claim
docs.text:
I would describe the current confidence as:

  • high confidence that the ensemble path is worth pursuing
  • medium confidence that the best current direction is 20260325-primary, cross-model, diversity-aware
  • low confidence on universal merge-rule conclusions
  • low-to-medium confidence on fold generalization beyond F1

So the big green light is on the ensemble direction itself, but not yet on a single final production recipe.

docs.title: 9. Cross-Fold Methodology
docs.text:
The next question is whether we can find one model or ensemble recipe that is positive across all four folds.

That is a very good next target, but it has to be split into two different methodologies.

docs.title: 9.1 Recipe transfer
docs.text:
This is what the new Phase 3 batch does.

It keeps the same recipe:

  • same source pool
  • same model pool
  • same ranking mode
  • same diversity mode
  • same top_per_model

and re-runs the planner on F2 and F4.

That means:

  • the recipe is transferred
  • the exact selected trials are not fixed

This is the easiest next test and still very informative.

docs.title: 9.2 Fixed-trial transfer
docs.text:
This is the harder and stricter test.

It would mean:

  • choose one exact set of source_trial_ids
  • run those exact same trained models on the other fold ranges

This is not the same as planner-based transfer.

It answers a different question:

Do the exact trained members that looked good in one discovery fold still behave well on the other fold windows?

docs.title: 9.3 Does the current backtest stack allow it?
docs.text:
Yes, partially.

The current runner already supports:

  • repeated --source-trial-id
  • manual --range-start
  • manual --range-end

So the core execution path for fixed-trial transfer already exists.

What the current batch workflow does not do yet is orchestrate that mode as a thesis block automatically. The current batch runner is planner-first and fold-first.

docs.title: 9.4 Why this becomes four experiments, not one
docs.text:
A true all-fold validation cannot be one single backtest.

Each fold has its own execution range, so one fixed-trial cross-fold study would naturally become:

  • one run on F1
  • one run on F2
  • one run on F3
  • one run on F4

Then their results should be aggregated afterward into one fold-level summary.

That part is conceptually clean.

It just needs a small orchestration layer above the existing runner.

docs.title: 9.5 Do we need prediction regeneration?
docs.text:
Most likely, yes.

If the fixed trials were never executed on the target fold range, the needed prediction artifacts probably do not exist yet for that window.

In that case, the current runner logic would:

  • try to reuse a compatible predictions parquet
  • and regenerate it when missing

So fixed-trial cross-fold is not blocked by missing predictions.

It is mostly a workflow/orchestration problem, not a core-engine problem.

docs.title: 10. Conclusion
docs.text:
This block is a strong narrowing step.

It does not just say that ensembles can work.

It says something more actionable:

  • the current edge is mostly coming from 20260325_tierB_mlp_ex
  • the combined pool is still relevant but not the leader
  • 20260328_tierB_corrected alone is currently too weak to prioritize
  • F1 remains the clearest monetization fold
  • the next smart step is to test whether the best recipes transfer into the harder folds

That gives Phase 3 a clear direction without exploding the search space again.

And after that, if the signal still holds, the next serious escalation is fixed-trial all-fold validation to see whether we can identify one ensemble recipe family that remains economically positive across all four folds.

docs.title: Script 2 Ensemble Phase 3 Cross-Fold Recipe Transfer Audit
docs.text:
• Date: 2026-04-06
  • Scope: Preliminary audit of the cross-fold recipe-transfer block
  • Batch: 20260405_phase3_cross_fold_recipe_transfer
  • Status: Negative on strict profitability, but highly informative

docs.title: 1. Purpose
docs.text:
This block asked a harder question than the previous discovery blocks.

The goal was no longer:

  • can ensembles work in the folds where they were discovered?

The goal was:

  • can the strongest discovered recipes transfer into the harder folds F2 and F4?

This is an important escalation step because it starts probing whether the ensemble story is:

  • only fold-local
  • or at least partially portable beyond F1 and F3

The short answer from this block is:

  • not yet, at least not at the level of net profitability

That is not the answer we wanted, but it is a very useful answer.

docs.title: 2. What Was Executed
docs.text:
This block used the pooled Tier B candidate frontier:

  • 20260325_tierB_mlp_ex
  • 20260328_tierB_corrected

It transferred selected recipe families into:

  • F2
  • F4

using these recipe dimensions:

  • model pools:
  • logistic,mlp,tcn,vit
  • mlp,tcn,vit
  • mlp,tcn
  • tcn,vit
  • ranking modes:
  • promise
  • friction
  • diversity modes:
  • off
  • tight
  • top_per_model = 1
  • the usual 8-rule merge battery

Execution summary:

  • 14 completed executions
  • 112 rule-level ensemble runs
  • 0 / 14 positive best-ensemble executions

The campaign-level annex is here:

  • 20260406_SCRIPT2_ENSEMBLE_PHASE3_CROSS_FOLD_RECIPE_TRANSFER_CAMPAIGN_REPORT.md

docs.title: 3.1 No recipe transferred cleanly into net-positive F2/F4 performance
docs.text:
This is the headline.

Even after narrowing the search space toward the strongest recipe families:

  • no execution crossed into positive best-ensemble net PnL

That means the current evidence does not support the claim that the recipe patterns found in F1/F3 are already portable into F2/F4 as profitable recipes.

This is a strong negative result.

And it matters.

docs.title: 4. Why This Is Still Useful
docs.text:
This block did not fail in an informational sense.

It told us something important:

  • the current ensemble edge looks significantly more fold-specific than the earlier discovery blocks might have suggested

That is exactly the kind of thing this transfer block was supposed to test.

So although the result is economically disappointing, it is methodologically good and necessary.

docs.title: 5.1 The ensembles often improved relative to the best source baseline
docs.text:
Even though all 14 / 14 best ensembles remained net negative, the average delta versus the best source baseline was still positive in both transfer folds:

  • F2: +$215.76
  • F4: +$86.68

That means the ensemble recipes were often less bad than the best individual selected source trial.

This does not rescue the profitability story.

But it does mean the transfer recipes were not completely useless.

docs.title: 5.2 tight again helped
docs.text:
Average best-ensemble PnL by diversity mode:

  • tight: -$153.99
  • off: -$381.58

That is a large gap.

So the same pattern seen earlier still survives here:

  • tighter diversity control is generally helping the selection process

It is not enough to make these folds profitable.

But it is still an important repeated pattern.

docs.title: 5.3 tcn,vit was the least bad family in this transfer block
docs.text:
By average best-ensemble PnL:

  • tcn,vit: -$121.98
  • logistic,mlp,tcn,vit: -$206.27
  • mlp,tcn,vit: -$235.62
  • mlp,tcn: -$519.31

This is only a weak relative win, not a strong positive result.

But it does suggest that the simplest pair may transfer less badly than the broader pools in the harder folds.

docs.title: 6. Selectivity Reading
docs.text:
This block adds an important nuance to the selectivity story.

In earlier blocks, many strong cases were:

  • better than proportional
  • and also clearly net positive

Here, we still see some better-than-proportional cases, for example:

  • phase3_xfold_tcn_vit_f2_promise_tight
  • phase3_xfold_log_mlp_tcn_vit_f2_promise_tight
  • phase3_xfold_mlp_tcn_vit_f4_friction_tight

But that improvement was not enough to cross into positive net territory.

So the current reading is:

  • the ensemble logic can still improve selectivity in harder folds
  • but the folds themselves remain too economically weak for that improvement to become profitability

That is a very different message than:

  • "the recipe did nothing"

The recipe did something.

It just did not do enough.

docs.title: 7. What This Says After All Completed Blocks
docs.text: At this point, the cumulative picture is fairly coherent.

docs.title: 7.1 What now looks well supported
docs.text:
• the ensemble direction itself is real and worth continuing
  • F1 remains the strongest monetization fold
  • cross-model combinations matter
  • diversity-aware selection keeps helping
  • recipe transfer from F1/F3 into F2/F4 is not enough by itself

docs.title: 7.2 What now looks unsupported
docs.text:
• the hope that the current best recipes would simply generalize into net profitability on F2/F4

This block does not support that.

docs.title: 7.3 What the real open question becomes now
docs.text:
The next real question is no longer:

  • "do the recipes transfer?"

because we now have a decent answer:

  • not enough

The next real question is:

  • do the exact trained members transfer better than the recipe alone?

That is a different and stricter hypothesis.

docs.title: 8. Recommended Next Step
docs.text:
I would not spend another broad batch on more planner-based recipe transfer variations right now.

The next best step is:

docs.title: Fixed-trial all-fold validation
docs.text:
Take a curated but materially sized block of the strongest already-discovered ensembles from the earlier good folds and run the same exact trial ids on all fold ranges.

That would answer the stronger question:

  • can one concrete ensemble survive across all four fold windows?

This is the test that now matters most.

Under the current stack, that means:

  • not another planner-first batch
  • but a runner-first orchestration using repeated --source-trial-id
  • one execution per fold/range
  • then an aggregate audit across the four fold runs

docs.title: Why this is the right next step
docs.text:
Recipe transfer already failed to achieve net positivity.

So if we still care about the "all folds" goal, the next serious thing to test is the exact-members version, not more recipe permutations.

That is a much better use of the next experiment budget.

docs.title: 9. Completed Fixed-Trial Candidate Selection
docs.text:
The candidate selection for the next block is now complete.

This selection was performed across the already completed positive-signal blocks:

  • Phase 1 / Block A
  • Phase 1 / Block B
  • Phase 2 / source-provenance ablation

The important methodological point is this:

  • provenance was treated only as descriptive metadata
  • not as a hard selection axis
  • candidates were kept or discarded by merit across the pooled evidence

docs.title: 9.1 Selection logic
docs.text:
The shortlist was built from a multi-signal read of the completed campaign artifacts, prioritizing:

  • top net PnL
  • top delta vs best source baseline
  • top selectivity surplus
  • top gross-per-trade improvement
  • repeated positive or near-positive strength across more than one block
  • repeated support for tight where that pattern looked stable

The basket was also forced to preserve recipe diversity across:

  • single-family and cross-model pools
  • promise and friction
  • off and tight
  • compact pairs, triples, and broader four-member pools

docs.title: 9.2 Final selected candidate class
docs.text:
The selected fixed-trial candidate class contains 16 ensembles:

  1. 1954,2436,2662
  2. mlp,tcn,vit
  3. strongest F1 friction off cross-model case from the completed evidence
  4. 1954,3641,2662
  5. mlp,tcn,vit
  6. strong tight cross-model variant
  7. 1943,1925
  8. mlp
  9. strongest compact single-family promise tight case
  10. 1943,1925,1910
  11. mlp
  12. broader single-family variant with repeated strong selectivity signal
  13. 1954,3641
  14. mlp,tcn
  15. strong compact cross-model friction off pair
  16. 3641,2397
  17. tcn
  18. strong single-family tcn case with high delta vs source
  19. 2049,2530,2795
  20. mlp,tcn,vit
  21. best positive F3 promise off triple
  22. 2795,2796
  23. vit
  24. strongest compact vit case from the positive F3 frontier
  25. 2894,1943,3641,2662
  26. logistic,mlp,tcn,vit
  27. strongest broad four-model tight configuration
  28. 1701,1925,2426,2662
    • logistic,mlp,tcn,vit
    • second broad four-model tight configuration with high delta vs source
  29. 1954,3129,3140
    • mlp
    • strong friction off single-family alternative
  30. 2795,2796,2800
    • vit
    • broader vit family variant to test whether the family still survives with three members
  31. 2530,3752
    • tcn
    • compact tcn pair from the positive F3 promise off frontier
  32. 3050,2049,2530,2795
    • logistic,mlp,tcn,vit
    • broad four-model F3 promise off case
  33. 1954,3874
    • mlp,vit
    • compact two-family alternative kept for recipe diversity
  34. 2530,2795
    • tcn,vit
    • minimal cross-model pair kept as the simplest transfer candidate

docs.title: 9.3 What was deliberately preserved in the selection
docs.text:
The candidate class was intentionally not collapsed to only the absolute top few cases.

It preserves:

  • mlp, tcn, and vit single-family leaders
  • mlp,tcn, mlp,vit, tcn,vit, and mlp,tcn,vit cross-model leaders
  • broad logistic,mlp,tcn,vit cases
  • both off and tight
  • both promise and friction
  • both F1- and F3-discovered elites

That gives the next block enough width to be meaningful without turning into an unstructured brute-force dump.

docs.title: 9.4 Resulting batch
docs.text:
The next batch is now materialized as:

  • 20260406_phase4_fixed_trial_all_fold_validation.json

It contains:

  • 16 fixed ensemble candidates
  • 4 fold windows each
  • 64 fold-level executions total

This batch no longer replans candidates per fold.

Instead, it runs the same exact trial ids on:

  • F1
  • F2
  • F3
  • F4

so that the next audit can answer the harder question:

  • does any concrete ensemble survive across all four folds?

docs.title: 10. Conclusion
docs.text:
This block is a clean negative result, but it is not a bad result.

It tells us that:

  • the current recipe patterns do not automatically generalize into profitable F2/F4 behavior
  • diversity-aware selection still helps
  • relative improvement over weak baselines still appears
  • but the transfer folds remain too economically hostile

So the right next step is not another planner sweep.

It is a fixed-trial all-fold validation block built around a curated but meaningfully sized candidate class, and that candidate class has now been selected and materialized into the next batch JSON.

docs.title: Script 2 Ensemble Provenance Clarification Audit
docs.text:
• Date: 2026-04-06
  • Scope: Clarification note to avoid over-reading the provenance ablation block
  • Status: Methodological correction and framing guardrail

docs.title: 1. Why This Note Exists
docs.text:
The previous provenance ablation block can be misread if the word source is taken too literally.

That would be a mistake.

This note exists to make the intended interpretation explicit before that misreading propagates into later thesis text, later planning notes, or later ensemble decisions.

docs.title: 2. What source Means Here
docs.text:
In this ensemble work, source does not mean:

  • a different market
  • a different labeling universe
  • a different target definition
  • a different economic regime definition
  • a truly different prediction problem

In this context, the so-called source groups are all still part of the same Script 2 problem.

They differ mainly by search frontier details such as:

  • feature subset construction
  • forced vs auto-selected feature count
  • hyperparameter exploration path
  • trial budget coverage
  • architectural settings explored in practice

So the correct reading is:

  • these are different experimental frontiers of the same problem
  • not different information sources in any strong thesis sense

docs.title: 3. What the Provenance Block Does and Does Not Prove
docs.text:
The provenance block does not prove:

  • that one frontier is intrinsically superior forever
  • that one frontier should become the only acceptable origin of ensemble members
  • that models from another frontier should be ignored
  • that the weaker frontier contains no useful trials

What it does show is much narrower:

  • in the specific tested budget and current tested recipes, some frontiers produced stronger candidate members than others

That is a useful observation.

But it is not the same as saying:

  • "the source is what matters"

That stronger statement would be unjustified.

docs.title: 4. The Right Selection Principle
docs.text:
The right practical principle remains:

the best models are the best models, regardless of where they come from

That means the correct ensemble construction philosophy is still:

  1. gather eligible candidates
  2. rank them by a meaningful criterion
  3. promise
  4. friction
  5. pnl
  6. stage3
  7. or whatever ranking is being studied
  8. combine them under the current ensemble selection logic
  9. keep provenance only as descriptive metadata

So provenance is allowed to be:

  • metadata
  • an audit lens
  • a stress-test dimension

But it should not become the main decision principle.

docs.title: 5. Why the Weak Standalone Frontier Should Not Be Over-Interpreted
docs.text:
The weaker standalone results from the corrected frontier can have many possible explanations that do not imply that the frontier is fundamentally bad.

Examples:

  • insufficient trial count
  • insufficient hyperparameter diversity
  • not enough dropout variation
  • not enough regularization variation
  • not enough architecture depth/width exploration
  • a forced 45-feature space that simply needed a broader search budget

So a poor standalone outcome from that frontier can mean:

  • "this search frontier was under-explored"

not necessarily:

  • "this frontier contains nothing useful"

That distinction matters.

docs.title: 6. What the Provenance Block Is Still Good For
docs.text:
The provenance ablation is still useful, but only when framed correctly.

It is useful for:

  • checking where the current strong candidates happened to come from
  • seeing whether one frontier is currently dominating the discovered top ranks
  • understanding whether mixing frontiers helps or hurts under the present ranking and diversity setup
  • deciding where to spend additional search budget

It is not useful as a final philosophical answer to:

  • "which source should we trust"

because that question is already framed too strongly.

docs.title: 7. Methodological Correction
docs.text:
The better thesis-side formulation is:

  • we are not comparing fundamentally different sources
  • we are comparing candidate pools produced by different search frontiers
  • the goal is still to discover the best-performing ensemble members and recipes globally

So if a future run with the corrected frontier produces stronger candidates, those candidates should be taken seriously immediately.

No one should read the provenance block as a reason to exclude them.

docs.title: 8. Critique of the Previous Framing
docs.text:
Yes, the previous framing risked sounding more rigid than it should have.

The risky implication was:

  • "maybe we should prefer one source over another as a rule"

That is not the right operational conclusion.

The better conclusion is:

  • "right now, under this tested budget, stronger members were found more often in one frontier than in another"

That is descriptive.

It is not a selection law.

docs.title: 9. Operational Consequence
docs.text:
Going forward, the safest practical interpretation is:

  • keep aggregating candidate pools broadly
  • rank globally
  • let merit decide
  • keep provenance visible for auditability only

If provenance is used again, it should be used as:

  • a diagnostic dimension
  • not a hard gate

docs.title: 10. Final Takeaway
docs.text:
The provenance block should be read as a temporary empirical snapshot of where good members were found, not as a statement that ensemble quality is determined by provenance itself.

The core principle remains unchanged:

  • good members can come from anywhere
  • good ensemble recipes should be built from what performs best
  • provenance is descriptive, not sacred

That is the framing future readers should keep in mind when they read the earlier provenance notes.

docs.title: Correlation Analysis
docs.text:
Date: 2026-03-31 Source: Extracted from Tier B Rescue Audit, Section 11 Scope: Pearson correlations across all 4 Script 2 runs (trainable models only) Purpose: Inform Tier B Rescue design with data-driven metric relationships Auto-generated by: script2_audit_correlations.py + manual rescue analysis blockquotes

Note: Cross-references to "Section N" refer to the main audit document unless noted.

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
• 1. Global Correlation Matrix
  • 2. The Friction Paradox — Why S2/F1 Don't Correlate with PnL
  • 3. Overfitting Analysis — S1-S2 Gap vs Trading Performance
  • 4. Window Size vs Performance
  • 5. Feature Count vs Performance
  • 6. Regularization vs Generalization
  • 7. Selectivity Analysis — Trade Count vs Quality
  • 8. Per-Model Correlation Profiles
  • 9. Per-Fold Correlation Profiles
  • 10. Epoch Convergence vs Quality
  • 11. Loss Weight Impact
  • 12. Key Takeaways for Tier B Rescue Design

docs.title: Insights
docs.text:
The matrix separates a model-quality cluster (S2, F1 Mac, Entry F1) from an execution-cost cluster (Trades, Est.Fric, PnL). The strongest structural signal is not S2 -> PnL, but Trades -> Est.Fric -> PnL, which is why profitable realized trading remains rare even when validation metrics improve.

🔍 Rescue Analysis: The two-cluster structure directly validates the 5-metric seed selection (Section 6.4): S2 and Entry F1 from the signal cluster, PnL and Sharpe from the execution cluster, and S3 bridging both (it requires good S2 AND low overfitting to score well). The strongest path to profitability is not S2 → PnL but Fewer Trades → Lower Friction → Higher PnL, which means the rescue search space must prioritize hyperparameters that control selectivity (thresholds, gate weights) alongside the traditional objective targets.

docs.title: 2. The Friction Paradox — Why S2/F1 Don't Correlate with PnL
docs.text: Results over 1183 trials with ≥5 trades:

docs.title: 2.1 Global Correlations
docs.text: Correlation r Interpretation S2 vs PnL -0.01 ZERO F1 Macro vs PnL -0.13 Weak Entry F1 vs PnL -0.19 Weak Trades vs PnL -0.81 EXTREME S2 vs F1 Macro +0.85 EXTREME S2 vs Sharpe +0.01 ZERO

docs.title: Insights
docs.text: Globally, S2, F1 Macro, and Entry F1 do not translate into realized PnL. What does stand out is the extreme negative Trades vs PnL relationship, which means the monetization problem is dominated by turnover and friction rather than by the absence of predictive signal.

docs.title: 2.2 Per-Fold Correlations Adjusted for Trade Count (PnL/trade)
docs.text:
When we normalize PnL by number of trades, the raw trade-count effect is partially controlled:

Correlation F1 F2 F3 F4 S2 vs PnL/trade +0.10 +0.22 +0.08 +0.11 F1 Macro vs PnL/trade +0.10 +0.12 -0.03 +0.08 Entry F1 vs PnL/trade -0.19 +0.04 -0.01 +0.30 S2 vs PnL (raw) +0.08 -0.13 -0.11 -0.12 Trades vs PnL -0.70 -0.90 -0.85 -0.72

docs.title: Insights
docs.text: Normalizing by trade count softens the raw S2 -> PnL disconnect, but the effect remains modest and fold-dependent. This supports a narrower claim than 'friction disappears': controlling for turnover reveals some per-trade edge, but not enough to rescue high-activity models automatically.

docs.title: 2.3 The Friction Mechanism (Data-Driven)
docs.text:
The mechanism is:

  1. A model with better S2/F1 detects more patterns → generates more trade signals
  2. More signals → more trades (the gate lets more through)

  3. Each trade costs friction (slippage + commission round-trip)

  4. Profitable trials average 106 trades

  5. Losing trials average 239 trades
  6. Trade difference: 133 extra trades
  7. Average friction per trade: $2.80
  8. Estimated friction drag from extra trades: $372

When PnL is normalized per trade (removing the trade-count effect), positive correlations emerge — confirming that better models DO capture slightly more edge per trade, but not enough to offset the friction from trading more often.

Friction Paradox Insight: The global S2-vs-PnL correlation is r=-0.01 (effectively zero), while Trades-vs-PnL is r=-0.81 (EXTREME negative). This is the core friction paradox: signal quality alone does not predict profitability because more signal → more trades → more friction. Implication for rescue: models must be MORE SELECTIVE (higher threshold, stricter gate) to reduce trade count and minimize friction drag.

🔍 Rescue Analysis: The $2.80/trade friction × 133 extra trades gap = $372 explains why the 1.2% profitability rate is structural, not accidental. Rescue targeting: keeping trade count ≤150 (profitable average = 106 trades) would save ~$250 in friction vs the losing average. Per-trade edge normalization shows models DO learn slightly better with higher S2, but the marginal gain (~$0.50/trade) cannot offset $2.80/trade friction — breakeven requires 5.6× improvement in per-trade edge, which no current model achieves. This sets the rescue design constraint: optimize for fewer, higher-quality trades rather than more accurate signal detection.

docs.title: 3. Overfitting Analysis — S1-S2 Gap vs Trading Performance
docs.text: The S1-S2 gap measures overfitting: high S1 (train) with low S2 (validation) = memorization.

docs.title: 3.1 Global Overfitting Correlations
docs.text: Correlation r n Interpretation S1-S2 Gap vs S3 -0.10 1183 Weak S1-S2 Gap vs PnL +0.11 1183 Weak S1-S2 Gap vs Sharpe +0.01 1183 ZERO S1-S2 Gap vs S2 -0.01 1183 ZERO S1-S2 Gap vs Objective +0.26 1183 Moderate

docs.title: Insights
docs.text: At the global level, overfitting is not behaving like a single clean driver of failure. The gap has only weak relationships with PnL, Sharpe, and S3, so the main conclusion here should be 'mixed and model-dependent' rather than a universal monotonic penalty.

docs.title: Insights
docs.text:
Globally, the S1-S2 gap has r=+0.11 with PnL and r=-0.01 with S2. The worst average overfitter is MEX, but the sign and strength of the gap relationship vary by architecture. The safe read is to regularize where the model-specific rows support it, not to assume one global overfitting rule for all families.

🔍 Rescue Analysis: AXV stands out: lowest gap (0.045) but strongest r(Gap,PnL) = –0.63, meaning even small overfitting is very costly for AXV — the rescue should enforce strict regularization (higher dropout, lower LR) for AXV experiments specifically. MEX is the worst overfitter (gap = 0.468) but tolerates it moderately (r = –0.17). TCN shows counterintuitive r(Gap,S2) = +0.35: more overfitting → better S2, likely because TCN memorizes fold-specific patterns that boost validation but don't generalize (confirmed by zero PnL-positive TCN-F2 trials in Section 2.1). Per-model regularization priors should be encoded in the rescue search space bounds.

docs.title: 4. Window Size vs Performance
docs.text: Does the observation window length correlate with model quality?

docs.title: 4.1 Global Window Correlations
docs.text: Correlation r Interpretation Window vs Objective +0.07 Weak Window vs S2 +0.17 Weak Window vs PnL +0.05 Weak Window vs Trades -0.02 ZERO Window vs Sharpe +0.02 ZERO

docs.title: Insights
docs.text: Globally, window size has only a weak relationship with Objective (r=+0.07). The interesting signal is in the per-model rows: TCN benefits much more on S2 than MLP or MEX, so window size should stay architecture-specific in the rescue search space.

docs.title: 5. Feature Count vs Performance
docs.text:
Tier A uses 2 features, Tier B uses 10-45. Does more features help?

Correlation r Interpretation Features vs S2 +0.28 Moderate Features vs PnL -0.09 Weak Features vs S1-S2 Gap +0.38 Moderate Features vs Objective +0.05 ZERO Features vs Sharpe -0.05 ZERO

docs.title: 5.1 Head-to-Head: 2 Features (Tier A) vs 45 Features (Tier B)
Insights
docs.text: The head-to-head table shows the trade-off clearly: 45 features buy only a modest average S2 gain, but they also increase the S1-S2 gap, worsen average PnL, and increase turnover. This is evidence for a harder optimization regime, not evidence that more features are automatically better.

docs.title: Insights
docs.text:
More features correlate r=+0.38 with the S1-S2 gap and r=+0.28 with S2. The net takeaway is not 'feature-rich is bad' or 'feature-rich is good', but that richer inputs raise both representational capacity and overfitting pressure, so the rescue design has to manage both at the same time.

🔍 Rescue Analysis: The 45-feature regime buys +0.019 S2 at the cost of +0.228 gap — a 12:1 overfitting-to-signal ratio. The rescue should avoid forcing 45 features and instead search the 10–44 auto-selection range (as tierB_mlp_ex did), where 9/15 of the top Objective trials originated (Section 2) vs only 4/15 for 45-forced tierB_corrected. The +46 extra trades from 45 features × $2.80/trade = ~$129 additional friction further erodes any S2 gain. Feature count should be a searchable hyperparameter in the rescue, not a fixed constant.

docs.title: 6. Regularization vs Generalization
docs.text: Do dropout and weight_decay (from resolved hyperparameters) predict better generalization?

docs.title: 6.1 Dropout Correlations
docs.text:
875 trials with dropout data (logistic models have no dropout parameter)

Correlation r Interpretation Dropout vs S2 -0.01 ZERO Dropout vs S1-S2 Gap -0.05 ZERO Dropout vs PnL -0.01 ZERO Dropout vs Trades +0.00 ZERO

docs.title: Insights
docs.text: At the aggregate level, dropout is not emerging as a universal control knob. The near-zero global rows mean dropout should remain in the search space, but the rescue should not hard-code a strong global prior from this table alone.

docs.title: 6.2 Weight Decay Correlations
docs.text:
1183 trials with weight_decay data

Correlation r (raw) r (log₁₀) Interpretation (log) WD vs S2 +0.03 +0.03 ZERO WD vs S1-S2 Gap +0.25 +0.21 Moderate WD vs PnL +0.05 +0.07 Weak WD vs Objective +0.10 +0.11 Weak

docs.title: Insights
docs.text: Weight decay is also mixed in the aggregate view. The surprising positive correlation with the S1-S2 gap means higher weight_decay is not automatically reducing overfitting across the full population, so any stronger regularization recommendation has to be justified per model family.

docs.title: Insights
docs.text:
The per-model rows confirm that regularization response is architecture-specific: MEX looks more supportive for higher dropout than TCN or VIT, while the global aggregates remain weak. The rescue should therefore search regularization broadly, but avoid turning 'more regularization' into a single blanket rule.

🔍 Rescue Analysis: Actionable per-model guidance for rescue search bounds: AXV strongly benefits from dropout (r vs PnL = +0.37) — bias AXV search toward dropout 0.15–0.30. TCN is hurt by dropout on S2 (r = –0.28) — keep TCN dropout moderate (0.05–0.15). MEX shows dropout reduces gap (r = –0.24) and slightly helps PnL — allow broader range 0.10–0.30. MLP and VIT show near-zero effects globally — keep default range, don't waste search budget on extreme values. These priors should inform the neighbor perturbation radii in the rescue's 3-block design.

docs.title: 7. Selectivity Analysis — Trade Count vs Quality
docs.text: Testing how trade count changes realized profitability, per-trade economics, and risk-adjusted quality.

docs.title: 7.1 Trade Count Correlations
docs.text: Correlation r Interpretation Trades vs PnL/trade +0.45 Strong Trades vs Win% +0.42 Strong Trades vs Sharpe +0.45 Strong Trades vs PnL -0.81 EXTREME

docs.title: Insights
docs.text: This table shows a real tension: more trades are associated with better PnL/trade, Win%, and Sharpe, but with worse total PnL. The conclusion is not 'fewer trades are better at everything'; it is that aggressive strategies can look cleaner on efficiency metrics while still losing money once friction accumulates.

docs.title: 7.2 Performance by Trade Count Bucket
docs.text:
Buckets: Low (≤127), Medium (127-297), High (>297)

Bucket n Avg Trades Avg PnL Avg PnL/trade Avg Sharpe Avg Win% Avg Friction Low 393 100 $-419 $-4.14 -5.92 22.2% $286 Medium 388 226 $-551 $-2.49 -2.78 29.2% $633 High 402 383 $-1012 $-2.63 -3.34 28.1% $1048

docs.title: Insights
docs.text:
The bucket table makes the trade-off concrete. The low-trade bucket has the least negative average PnL, but the medium bucket has better average PnL/trade, Sharpe, and Win%. For the rescue, that means we should target controlled turnover rather than blindly minimizing trade count.

🔍 Rescue Analysis: The rescue target zone is the Low-Medium boundary (100–150 trades): this captures the Low bucket's PnL advantage (least negative at –$419 avg) while approaching the Medium bucket's better per-trade efficiency. The High bucket (>297 trades, –$1,012 avg PnL, $1,048 avg friction) is structurally unprofitable and should be avoided. For neighbor generation: when perturbing seed hyperparameters, evaluate whether the perturbation pushes estimated trade count toward 100–150 or away from it. The $762 avg friction gap between Low ($286) and High ($1,048) buckets = $762 of pure execution waste.

docs.title: Insights
docs.text:
The friction paradox holds across all families, but no model family shows a meaningfully positive S2 -> PnL link. The strongest row is VIT at r=+0.01, which is still essentially flat. Rescue selection therefore needs multi-metric diversity rather than faith in a single family-specific validation shortcut.

🔍 Rescue Analysis: No model has positive r(S2,PnL), confirming multi-metric seed selection is mandatory — optimizing S2 alone helps NO architecture. LOG has the most extreme r(Trades,PnL) = –0.88, making it the most friction-sensitive model — LOG rescue MUST prioritize trade-count reduction above all else. AXV has r(EntryF1,PnL) = –0.61 (strongest negative in any cell) — AXV models with high timing skill are the most friction-trapped. The extraction script should weight PnL/Sharpe seeds more heavily for LOG and AXV, while S2/Entry F1 seeds may be more useful for TCN/VIT where the execution gap is less extreme.

docs.title: 9. Per-Fold Correlation Profiles
docs.text:
Same key correlations computed independently for each fold:

Fold n r(S2,PnL) r(Trades,PnL) r(S1-S2,PnL) r(S2,Sharpe) Avg PnL Avg Trades Profitable% F1 296 +0.08 -0.70 +0.46 +0.09 $-690 259 2.7% F2 301 -0.13 -0.90 -0.08 +0.23 $-778 301 0.0% F3 275 -0.11 -0.85 +0.04 +0.08 $-716 238 2.2% F4 311 -0.12 -0.72 +0.01 +0.14 $-482 156 0.0%

docs.title: Insights
docs.text:
F1 is the only fold with a clearly positive profitability rate advantage, while F2 remains the hardest. What is stable across folds is Trades -> PnL staying strongly negative; what changes by fold are the weaker relationships such as S2 -> PnL and S1-S2 gap -> PnL, so the fold regime still matters.

🔍 Rescue Analysis: F2 (0.0% profitable, highest avg trades 301) and F4 (0.0% profitable, lowest avg trades 156) are the rescue priority folds. F4's lower trade count suggests it is closer to the profitability barrier and may respond to targeted adjustment. F2's r(Trades,PnL) = –0.90 (strongest of any fold) means F2 rescue MUST achieve dramatic trade reduction to have any chance. F1's unique r(S1-S2,PnL) = +0.46 suggests mild overfitting helps PnL specifically in F1 — the rescue should tolerate slightly higher gap for F1 experiments. Cross-fold seeding (using F1/F3 profitable seeds for F2/F4 experiments) is the main mechanism to inject profitable-region knowledge into the zero-profit folds.

docs.title: 10. Epoch Convergence vs Quality
docs.text: Analysis of 1185 trials with epoch convergence data.

docs.title: 10.1 Epoch Correlations
docs.text: Correlation r Interpretation Best Epoch vs Objective +0.24 Moderate Best Epoch vs S2 +0.18 Weak Best Epoch vs PnL +0.19 Weak Epoch Ratio vs Objective +0.27 Moderate Epoch Ratio vs S2 +0.23 Moderate

docs.title: 10.2 Early vs Late Convergers (threshold: 80% of epochs)
docs.text: Group n Avg Obj Avg S2 Avg PnL Avg Trades Early (<80%) 1154 0.2502 0.255 $-663 237 Late (≥80%) 31 0.2624 0.251 $-630 232

docs.title: Insights
docs.text: Best epoch vs Objective is positive but only moderate (r=+0.24), and late convergers are rare in the full population (2.6%). This supports a targeted epoch-budget increase for the architectures and runs that truly reach the limit, not a blanket assumption that all trials need more epochs.

docs.title: 11. Loss Weight Impact
docs.text: Correlations between loss component weights and outcomes (1183 trials):

docs.title: 11.1 Loss Weight vs Outcome Correlations
docs.text: Loss Weight r(vs Obj) r(vs S2) r(vs PnL) r(vs Trades) r(vs Sharpe) Classification +0.01 +0.23 -0.03 +0.04 +0.02 Strength +0.04 -0.03 +0.11 -0.09 -0.00 Quality -0.06 -0.07 +0.04 -0.06 -0.01 Entry Gate -0.03 -0.29 +0.00 -0.01 -0.10

docs.title: Insights
docs.text: Across all models, loss weights show weak-to-moderate links at best. The standout row here is not Entry Gate -> Trades, but rather that Classification aligns positively with S2 while Entry Gate aligns negatively with S2, which argues for caution when treating gate weight as an automatic profitability lever.

docs.title: 11.2 Entry Gate Weight — The Selectivity Lever
docs.text:
Entry gate weight vs Trades: r=-0.01 (ZERO)

Entry gate weight vs PnL: r=+0.00 (ZERO)

The entry_gate loss weight is intended to penalize wrong entry timing more strongly. In principle, higher values could make the model more selective, but these aggregate rows do not demonstrate a strong population-wide effect on either trade count or realized profitability.

docs.title: Insights
docs.text: Entry Gate remains a candidate selectivity lever, but the aggregate evidence is weak: Gate vs Trades is r=-0.01 and Gate vs PnL is r=+0.00. The per-model rows are mixed, so the rescue should test gate-weight neighborhoods explicitly rather than baking in a strong directional prior.

docs.title: 12. Key Takeaways for Tier B Rescue Design
docs.text: Data-driven synthesis from 1183 trials (≥5 trades) out of 1185 total completed:

docs.title: The Friction Paradox (CRITICAL)
docs.text:
1. S2 vs PnL: r=-0.01 — Signal quality does NOT predict profitability
  2. Trades vs PnL: r=-0.81 — More trades STRONGLY predicts losses
  3. Profitable trials average 106 trades vs 239 for losing (Δ=133 trades)
  4. Only 1.2% of trials are profitable — selectivity is the key differentiator

docs.title: Overfitting Dynamics
docs.text:
1. Feature count vs S1-S2 gap: r=+0.38 — More features = more overfitting risk
  2. 45 forced features require aggressive regularization to prevent memorization
  3. Weight_decay shows surprising positive correlation with gap (r=+0.21)

docs.title: Rescue Configuration Recommendations
docs.text:
1. Test selectivity levers explicitly: higher prediction thresholds and entry-gate neighborhoods
  2. Entry gate weight vs Trades: r=-0.01, vs PnL: r=+0.00
  3. Keep regularization model-specific: search broader dropout/weight_decay, but avoid one blanket bias
  4. Increase epoch budget selectively: favor families or runs that actually hit the budget ceiling
  5. Seed diversity (CONFIRMED): 5 metrics × 4 top = 20 seeds per model before dedup. Metrics: S3, S2, PnL, Sharpe, Entry F1 (see Section 6.4 for redundancy analysis). Cross-fold seeding enabled, cross-model seeding disabled

docs.title: Model-Specific Notes
docs.text:
• AXV (n=32): avg gap=0.045, avg PnL=$-562, 0% profitable, avg 177 trades
  • LOG (n=308): avg gap=0.031, avg PnL=$-898, 0% profitable, avg 300 trades
  • MEX (n=216): avg gap=0.468, avg PnL=$-626, 0% profitable, avg 230 trades
  • MLP (n=289): avg gap=0.281, avg PnL=$-602, 1% profitable, avg 221 trades
  • TCN (n=103): avg gap=0.127, avg PnL=$-544, 2% profitable, avg 189 trades
  • VIT (n=235): avg gap=0.204, avg PnL=$-535, 3% profitable, avg 211 trades
