docs.title: Insights
docs.text: The matrix separates a model-quality cluster (S2, F1 Mac, Entry F1) from an execution-cost cluster (Trades, Est.Fric, PnL). The strongest structural signal is not S2 -> PnL, but Trades -> Est.Fric -> PnL, which is why profitable realized trading remains rare even when validation metrics improve.

docs.title: 2. The Friction Paradox — Why S2/F1 Don't Correlate with PnL
docs.text: Results over 1669 trials with ≥5 trades:

docs.title: 2.1 Global Correlations
docs.text: Correlation r Interpretation S2 vs PnL +0.188 Weak F1 Macro vs PnL +0.089 Weak Entry F1 vs PnL +0.038 ZERO Trades vs PnL -0.856 EXTREME S2 vs F1 Macro +0.899 EXTREME S2 vs Sharpe +0.060 Weak

docs.title: Insights
docs.text: Globally, S2, F1 Macro, and Entry F1 do not translate into realized PnL. What does stand out is the extreme negative Trades vs PnL relationship, which means the monetization problem is dominated by turnover and friction rather than by the absence of predictive signal.

docs.title: 2.2 Per-Fold Correlations Adjusted for Trade Count (PnL/trade)
docs.text:
When we normalize PnL by number of trades, the raw trade-count effect is partially controlled:

Correlation F1 F2 F3 F4 S2 vs PnL/trade +0.198 +0.414 +0.102 +0.253 F1 Macro vs PnL/trade +0.184 +0.323 +0.011 +0.211 Entry F1 vs PnL/trade -0.017 +0.299 +0.009 +0.350 S2 vs PnL (raw) +0.362 -0.003 +0.201 +0.044 Trades vs PnL -0.769 -0.912 -0.881 -0.775

docs.title: Insights
docs.text: Normalizing by trade count softens the raw S2 -> PnL disconnect, but the effect remains modest and fold-dependent. This supports a narrower claim than 'friction disappears': controlling for turnover reveals some per-trade edge, but not enough to rescue high-activity models automatically.

docs.title: 2.3 The Friction Mechanism (Data-Driven)
docs.text:
The mechanism is:

  1. A model with better S2/F1 detects more patterns → generates more trade signals
  2. More signals → more trades (the gate lets more through)

  3. Each trade costs friction (slippage + commission round-trip)

  4. Profitable trials average 106 trades

  5. Losing trials average 250 trades
  6. Trade difference: 144 extra trades
  7. Average friction per trade: $3.03
  8. Estimated friction drag from extra trades: $437

When PnL is normalized per trade (removing the trade-count effect), positive correlations emerge — confirming that better models DO capture slightly more edge per trade, but not enough to offset the friction from trading more often.

Friction Paradox Insight: The global S2-vs-PnL correlation is r=+0.188 (effectively zero), while Trades-vs-PnL is r=-0.856 (EXTREME negative). This is the core friction paradox: signal quality alone does not predict profitability because more signal → more trades → more friction. Implication for rescue: models must be MORE SELECTIVE (higher threshold, stricter gate) to reduce trade count and minimize friction drag.

docs.title: 3. Overfitting Analysis — S1-S2 Gap vs Trading Performance
docs.text: The S1-S2 gap measures overfitting: high S1 (train) with low S2 (validation) = memorization.

docs.title: 3.1 Global Overfitting Correlations
docs.text: Correlation r n Interpretation S1-S2 Gap vs S3 -0.015 1669 ZERO S1-S2 Gap vs PnL +0.216 1669 Moderate S1-S2 Gap vs Sharpe +0.050 1669 Weak S1-S2 Gap vs S2 +0.269 1669 Moderate S1-S2 Gap vs Objective +0.357 1669 Moderate

docs.title: Insights
docs.text: At the global level, overfitting is not behaving like a single clean driver of failure. The gap has only weak relationships with PnL, Sharpe, and S3, so the main conclusion here should be 'mixed and model-dependent' rather than a universal monotonic penalty.

docs.title: Insights
docs.text: Globally, the S1-S2 gap has r=+0.216 with PnL and r=+0.269 with S2. The worst average overfitter is MEX, but the sign and strength of the gap relationship vary by architecture. The safe read is to regularize where the model-specific rows support it, not to assume one global overfitting rule for all families.

docs.title: 4. Window Size vs Performance
docs.text: Does the observation window length correlate with model quality?

docs.title: 4.1 Global Window Correlations
docs.text: Correlation r Interpretation Window vs Objective +0.145 Weak Window vs S2 +0.272 Moderate Window vs PnL +0.117 Weak Window vs Trades -0.053 Weak Window vs Sharpe +0.034 ZERO

docs.title: Insights
docs.text: Globally, window size has only a weak relationship with Objective (r=+0.145). The interesting signal is in the per-model rows: TCN benefits much more on S2 than MLP or MEX, so window size should stay architecture-specific in the rescue search space.

docs.title: 5. Feature Count vs Performance
docs.text:
Tier A uses 2 features, Tier B uses 10-45. Does more features help?

Correlation r Interpretation Features vs S2 +0.575 Strong Features vs PnL +0.171 Weak Features vs S1-S2 Gap +0.554 Strong Features vs Objective +0.280 Moderate Features vs Sharpe +0.028 ZERO

docs.title: 5.1 Head-to-Head: 2 Features (Tier A) vs 45 Features (Tier B)
Insights
docs.text: The head-to-head table shows the trade-off clearly: 45 features buy only a modest average S2 gain, but they also increase the S1-S2 gap, worsen average PnL, and increase turnover. This is evidence for a harder optimization regime, not evidence that more features are automatically better.

docs.title: Insights
docs.text: More features correlate r=+0.554 with the S1-S2 gap and r=+0.575 with S2. The net takeaway is not 'feature-rich is bad' or 'feature-rich is good', but that richer inputs raise both representational capacity and overfitting pressure, so the rescue design has to manage both at the same time.

docs.title: 6. Regularization vs Generalization
docs.text: Do dropout and weight_decay (from resolved hyperparameters) predict better generalization?

docs.title: 6.1 Dropout Correlations
docs.text:
873 trials with dropout data (logistic models have no dropout parameter)

Correlation r Interpretation Dropout vs S2 -0.014 ZERO Dropout vs S1-S2 Gap -0.049 ZERO Dropout vs PnL -0.014 ZERO Dropout vs Trades +0.001 ZERO

docs.title: Insights
docs.text: At the aggregate level, dropout is not emerging as a universal control knob. The near-zero global rows mean dropout should remain in the search space, but the rescue should not hard-code a strong global prior from this table alone.

docs.title: 6.2 Weight Decay Correlations
docs.text:
1669 trials with weight_decay data

Correlation r (raw) r (log₁₀) Interpretation (log) WD vs S2 +0.179 +0.149 Weak WD vs S1-S2 Gap +0.334 +0.271 Moderate WD vs PnL +0.115 +0.111 Weak WD vs Objective +0.175 +0.163 Weak

docs.title: Insights
docs.text: Weight decay is also mixed in the aggregate view. The surprising positive correlation with the S1-S2 gap means higher weight_decay is not automatically reducing overfitting across the full population, so any stronger regularization recommendation has to be justified per model family.

docs.title: Insights
docs.text: The per-model rows confirm that regularization response is architecture-specific: MEX looks more supportive for higher dropout than TCN or VIT, while the global aggregates remain weak. The rescue should therefore search regularization broadly, but avoid turning 'more regularization' into a single blanket rule.

docs.title: 7. Selectivity Analysis — Trade Count vs Quality
docs.text: Testing how trade count changes realized profitability, per-trade economics, and risk-adjusted quality.

docs.title: 7.1 Trade Count Correlations
docs.text: Correlation r Interpretation Trades vs PnL/trade +0.445 Strong Trades vs Win% +0.418 Strong Trades vs Sharpe +0.479 Strong Trades vs PnL -0.856 EXTREME

docs.title: Insights
docs.text: This table shows a real tension: more trades are associated with better PnL/trade, Win%, and Sharpe, but with worse total PnL. The conclusion is not 'fewer trades are better at everything'; it is that aggressive strategies can look cleaner on efficiency metrics while still losing money once friction accumulates.

docs.title: 7.2 Performance by Trade Count Bucket
docs.text:
Buckets: Low (≤126), Medium (126-324), High (>324)

Bucket n Avg Trades Avg PnL Avg PnL/trade Avg Sharpe Avg Win% Avg Friction Low 551 100 $-425 $-4.19 -5.92 22.4% $297 Medium 553 238 $-632 $-2.70 -3.04 28.7% $765 High 565 405 $-1160 $-2.86 -3.42 27.6% $1193

docs.title: Insights
docs.text: The bucket table makes the trade-off concrete. The low-trade bucket has the least negative average PnL, but the medium bucket has better average PnL/trade, Sharpe, and Win%. For the rescue, that means we should target controlled turnover rather than blindly minimizing trade count.

docs.title: Insights
docs.text: The friction paradox holds across all families, but no model family shows a meaningfully positive S2 -> PnL link. The strongest row is RBO at r=+0.109, which is still essentially flat. Rescue selection therefore needs multi-metric diversity rather than faith in a single family-specific validation shortcut.

docs.title: Insights
docs.text: F1 is the only fold with a clearly positive profitability rate advantage, while F2 remains the hardest. What is stable across folds is Trades -> PnL staying strongly negative; what changes by fold are the weaker relationships such as S2 -> PnL and S1-S2 gap -> PnL, so the fold regime still matters.

docs.title: 10. Epoch Convergence vs Quality
docs.text: Analysis of 1183 trials with epoch convergence data.

docs.title: 10.1 Epoch Correlations
docs.text: Correlation r Interpretation Best Epoch vs Objective +0.238 Moderate Best Epoch vs S2 +0.180 Weak Best Epoch vs PnL +0.185 Weak Epoch Ratio vs Objective +0.276 Moderate Epoch Ratio vs S2 +0.230 Moderate

docs.title: 10.2 Early vs Late Convergers (threshold: 80% of epochs)
docs.text: Group n Avg Obj Avg S2 Avg PnL Avg Trades Early (<80%) 1152 0.2503 0.255 $-664 237 Late (≥80%) 31 0.2624 0.251 $-630 232

docs.title: Insights
docs.text: Best epoch vs Objective is positive but only moderate (r=+0.238), and late convergers are rare in the full population (2.6%). This supports a targeted epoch-budget increase for the architectures and runs that truly reach the limit, not a blanket assumption that all trials need more epochs.

docs.title: 11. Loss Weight Impact
docs.text: Correlations between loss component weights and outcomes (1669 trials):

docs.title: Insights
docs.text: Across all models, loss weights show weak-to-moderate links at best. The standout row here is not Entry Gate -> Trades, but rather that Classification aligns positively with S2 while Entry Gate aligns negatively with S2, which argues for caution when treating gate weight as an automatic profitability lever.

docs.title: 11.2 Entry Gate Weight — The Selectivity Lever
docs.text:
Entry gate weight vs Trades: r=+0.055 (Weak)

Entry gate weight vs PnL: r=-0.148 (Weak)

The entry_gate loss weight is intended to penalize wrong entry timing more strongly. In principle, higher values could make the model more selective, but these aggregate rows do not demonstrate a strong population-wide effect on either trade count or realized profitability.

docs.title: Insights
docs.text: Entry Gate remains a candidate selectivity lever, but the aggregate evidence is weak: Gate vs Trades is r=+0.055 and Gate vs PnL is r=-0.148. The per-model rows are mixed, so the rescue should test gate-weight neighborhoods explicitly rather than baking in a strong directional prior.

docs.title: 12. Key Takeaways for Tier B Rescue Design
docs.text: Data-driven synthesis from 1669 trials (≥5 trades) out of 1675 total completed:

docs.title: The Friction Paradox (CRITICAL)
docs.text:
1. S2 vs PnL: r=+0.188 — Signal quality does NOT predict profitability
  2. Trades vs PnL: r=-0.856 — More trades STRONGLY predicts losses
  3. Profitable trials average 106 trades vs 250 for losing (Δ=144 trades)
  4. Only 0.8% of trials are profitable — selectivity is the key differentiator

docs.title: Overfitting Dynamics
docs.text:
1. Feature count vs S1-S2 gap: r=+0.554 — More features = more overfitting risk
  2. 45 forced features require aggressive regularization to prevent memorization
  3. Weight_decay shows surprising positive correlation with gap (r=+0.271)

docs.title: Rescue Configuration Recommendations
docs.text:
1. Test selectivity levers explicitly: higher prediction thresholds and entry-gate neighborhoods
  2. Entry gate weight vs Trades: r=+0.055, vs PnL: r=-0.148
  3. Keep regularization model-specific: search broader dropout/weight_decay, but avoid one blanket bias
  4. Increase epoch budget selectively: favor families or runs that actually hit the budget ceiling
  5. Seed diversity: Use top-N from multiple metrics (Obj, S2, S3, PnL, Sharpe) to avoid converging on one metric that doesn't predict real performance

Note: Cross-references to "Section N" refer to the main audit document unless noted.

docs.text:
• 1. Top Models by Objective — Global Ranking

  • 1.2 Top Models by PnL (Actual Profit)
  • 1.2b Top Models by PnL+Fric (Gross Profit Proxy)
  • 1.3 Top Models by Sharpe Ratio
  • 1.4 Top Models by Win Rate (min 10 trades)
  • 1.5 Top Models by Entry F1 (S2 stage)

docs.title: 1. Top Models by Objective — Global Ranking
Insights
docs.text:
Objective Insight: VIT (8/15), MLP (4/15) lead the objective ranking. 6 of the top 15 objective trials have positive net PnL. The strongest composite result is T2530 (TCN-F3) with Obj=0.5432, S2=0.257, and PnL=+$48.

🔍 Rescue Analysis: F3 dominates the profitable cluster (5 of 6 PnL-positive rows). The tierB_mlp_ex run (auto-features, 10–44) sources 9/15 vs tierB_corrected's 4/15 (45 forced), suggesting auto-feature selection improves the quality frontier. T2530 (TCN-F3) appears in 4/9 rankings (#1 Obj, #1 Sharpe, #8 PnL, #2 Win%) — the single strongest trial across the entire audit and a priority seed candidate for the extraction script. No LOG or AXV appear in this top-15, confirming both need dedicated rescue exploration. VIT's 8/15 presence is skewed toward F3/F4, indicating fold-regime dependence rather than universal dominance.

docs.title: Insights
docs.text:
S2 Insight: TCN (12/15), VIT (3/15) dominate the validation ranking. Only 0 of the top 15 S2 trials also have positive net PnL, which reinforces that strong validation signal quality and live trading profitability are not the same thing.

🔍 Rescue Analysis: TCN-F2 monopoly (11/15) reveals TCN learns the strongest patterns in F2 validation, but with ZERO PnL overlap — the Friction Paradox (Section 11.2) in its purest form. The average PnL of this top-15 is approximately –$700. For rescue: S2 seeds capture pattern-learning ability that MAY transfer to other folds via cross-fold seeding — the hypothesis is that TCN-F2 hyperparameters, when applied to F1/F3, might produce profitable trades at lower turnover. The 0/15 PnL overlap confirms S2 alone is insufficient for seed selection, validating the 5-metric approach (Section 6.4).

docs.title: Insights
docs.text:
PnL Insight: 14 completed trials across all runs have positive net PnL. The profit leaders cluster in folds F1 (8), F3 (6). The top realized winner is T2391 (TCN-F1) at +$186.

🔍 Rescue Analysis: F2 and F4 produce ZERO profitable trials out of 612 combined — a regime effect, not model quality. Low trade count is the common thread: 12 of 14 profitable trials have ≤200 trades. Tier A rescue (20 trials/experiment) contributes 4/14 profitable, a 4× higher hit rate than Tier B — suggesting that the smaller search space was more focused. Feature count varies widely among winners (2–45), confirming profitability is driven by selectivity (trades) rather than input richness. T2685 (VIT-F2, #15 with 0 trades/$0 PnL) should be excluded from seed candidates as a degenerate trial.

docs.title: 1.2b Top Models by PnL+Fric (Gross Profit Proxy)
Insights
docs.text:
PnL+Fric Insight: This ranking uses PnL + Est.Fric, so it behaves as a gross-edge proxy rather than a realized-profit metric. 13 of the top 15 rows have at least 200 trades, and only 5 still show positive net PnL. The largest gross signal comes from T2662 (VIT-F1) at +$1681.

🔍 Rescue Analysis: F1 near-monopoly (14/15) reveals gross trading edge concentrates in F1 even though net PnL is split between F1 and F3. The gap between best gross (+$1,681) and best net PnL (+$186) quantifies friction's damage: ~$1,500 destroyed. LOG appears for the first time (T2894 at #4, +$677 gross) — logistic models CAN generate raw edge but lose it to friction (329 trades × $2.80 ≈ $920 friction). This ranking identifies the best "edge buried under friction" candidates, useful for rescue configs that also search friction-reduction parameters (threshold, gate weight).

docs.title: Insights
docs.text:
Sharpe Insight: 17 completed trials across all runs achieve positive Sharpe. VIT (9/15), MLP (3/15) dominate the top risk-adjusted results. The highest-Sharpe trials also tend to be more selective than the high-turnover signal leaders.

🔍 Rescue Analysis: Strong inverse correlation between Sharpe rank and trade count: top 5 average 37 trades vs 181 for bottom 5 — all 15 positive-Sharpe trials have ≤234 trades. Sharpe overlaps heavily with the PnL top-15 (10 shared rows), making it the most PnL-aligned of the 5 chosen seed metrics. Tier A contributes 4 of the top 7 (2-feature models), suggesting simpler features produce better risk-adjusted returns when selectivity is high. For rescue: Sharpe seeds should receive high extraction priority and are the best "money-making" axis seeds alongside PnL.

docs.title: 1.4 Top Models by Win Rate (min 10 trades)
Insights
docs.text:
Win Rate Insight: T1525 (VIT-F1) leads at 57.5%. Even inside this filtered ranking, 4 of the top 15 rows still have Sharpe <= 0.10, so hit rate alone is not enough for rescue selection.

🔍 Rescue Analysis: Win rate >43% requires <100 trades in every case — extreme selectivity is the only path to high hit rates. Tier A models dominate the top 5 (3/5 from 2-feature configs), further supporting that simpler feature sets enable more confident predictions. The 4/15 with Sharpe ≤ 0.10 despite high Win% reveal a "win often, lose big" pattern — they win more trades but their losing trades are larger. Win% was excluded from the 5 seed metrics (Section 6.4) because r=+0.85 with Sharpe makes it redundant; Sharpe seeds already capture this signal.

docs.title: 1.5 Top Models by Entry F1 (S2 stage)
Insights
docs.text:
Entry F1 Insight: VIT (7/15), TCN (6/15) dominate the trade-detection ranking. The average trade count across this top 15 is 315, which explains why strong entry timing often still collapses under friction.

🔍 Rescue Analysis: ZERO overlap with the PnL top-15 — the best trade-timing models are the worst money-makers. This is the most direct evidence that signal quality (detecting when to enter) and monetization (PnL net of friction) are structurally misaligned. High Entry F1 triggers excessive trading → friction death trap. For rescue: Entry F1 seeds capture timing skill on a different axis from PnL/Sharpe seeds; including both ensures the rescue search space covers both "good at detecting" and "good at making money" hyperparameter regions. This metric's value is in diversity, not direct profitability.

docs.title: 1.6 Top Models by F1 Macro (S2 stage)
Insights
docs.text:
F1 Insight: TCN (10/15), VIT (3/15) dominate directional quality. Only 0 of the top 15 F1 Macro rows also have positive net PnL, which shows that direction skill still needs execution efficiency to matter in the backtest.

🔍 Rescue Analysis: Highly redundant with S2 (r=+0.85, Section 11.1), explaining why TCN-F2 again dominates both rankings (10/15 here vs 12/15 in S2). Excluded from the 5 seed metrics in Section 6.4 precisely to avoid this double-counting. LOG appears at #15 (T3054), showing logistic models achieve directional quality competitive with deep architectures — relevant for assessing whether LOG deserves comparable rescue compute budget. F1 Macro seeds would be near-duplicates of S2 seeds, confirming the exclusion was correct.

docs.title: 1.7 Top Models by JS Divergence (lower = better, min 10 trades)
Insights
docs.text:
JS Insight: MLP (7/15), MEX (6/15) produce the most calibrated prediction distributions in this cut. Only 0 of these top 15 low-JS rows show positive net PnL, so JS remains a useful sanity check, not a primary selection metric.

🔍 Rescue Analysis: F4 dominance (10/15 from F4) reveals perfect calibration correlates with the least profitable fold — low JS means predictions match label distribution but says nothing about trading edge. MLP and MEX dominate because feedforward architectures produce smoother output distributions. Excluded from the 5 seed metrics (Section 6.4); useful only as a degeneracy filter (JS > 0.2 suggests degenerate prediction collapse) rather than a seed selection criterion. No TCN appears here, suggesting attention-based models produce less calibrated but more decisive outputs.

docs.title: 1.8 Cross-Metric Analysis — Who Appears in Multiple Rankings?
Insights
docs.text:
Cross-Metric Insight: The most recurrent names are T2530 (TCN-F3), T2798 (VIT-F3), T1418 (MLP-F3), T927 (MLP-F3), T3955 (VIT-F3), T1943 (MLP-F1), T2171 (MEX-F1), T2391 (TCN-F1). This section now counts over 9 rankings, so PnL+Fric participation is included in the overall consistency score instead of being ignored. The main caveat is that PnL+Fric favors higher-turnover rows, so recurrence should be read together with realized PnL, Sharpe, and trade count rather than as a standalone quality score.

🔍 Rescue Analysis: Clear signal/execution split: T2738 and T2462 (both F2) appear via the signal cluster (S2, Entry F1, F1 Macro) but have deeply negative PnL (–$1,225 and –$648). Conversely, F3 trials (T2530, T2798, T1418, T927, T3955) appear via execution-aligned metrics (PnL, Sharpe, Objective). No F2 or F4 trial achieves count ≥ 4 in execution metrics — these folds need the most rescue exploration. The 8 trials with count = 4 map closely to the seed extraction candidates from Section 15: their multi-metric presence indicates robust hyperparameter regions rather than metric-specific optima, making them the highest-confidence starting points for neighbor generation.

docs.title: Proportionality Lens
docs.text: This section asks whether lower net losses come only from trading less, or whether the rerun also looks more selective on a per-trade basis.

docs.title: Interpretation Table Legend
docs.text:
What this section is: plain-language definitions for the columns in the interpretation table below.

  • Rank: Candidate priority carried from the original plan.
  • Trial: Original Script 2 trial ID.
  • Variant: Threshold ladder choice used in the rerun (mild, recommended, or strict).
  • Trade Red%: Percentage reduction in trades versus the original stage-3 backtest. Higher means the rerun traded less.
  • PnL Imp%: Net PnL improvement relative to the magnitude of the original net PnL. Positive is better. Example: +50% means the rerun recovered half of the original net loss magnitude.
  • Gross Chg%: Change in PnL+Fric relative to the magnitude of the original PnL+Fric. Positive is better. This is the closest column to asking whether gross edge survived after removing friction effects.
  • Orig PnL/trade: Original net PnL divided by original trade count.
  • New PnL/trade: Rerun net PnL divided by rerun trade count.
  • Orig Gross/trade: Original PnL+Fric divided by original trade count.
  • New Gross/trade: Rerun PnL+Fric divided by rerun trade count.
  • Interpretation: Automatic label comparing PnL Imp% against Trade Red%.

docs.title: What To Look At
docs.text:
What this section is: a fast reading guide for deciding whether a rerun looks genuinely promising.

  • First check Interpretation: Better than proportional is the strongest signal. It means net PnL improved more than a simple reduction in turnover would suggest.
  • Then check Gross Chg%: positive values are especially important because they suggest the rerun kept or improved gross edge, not just reduced friction.
  • Then compare Orig Gross/trade versus New Gross/trade: if the new value is higher, the bot appears to be selecting better opportunities per trade.
  • PnL Imp% is useful, but by itself it can be inflated by merely trading less. It matters most when it is paired with positive or less-negative Gross Chg%.
  • A very high Trade Red% with poor Gross Chg% usually means the threshold became too strict: the bot traded much less, but also discarded too much gross edge.

docs.title: Best Delta
docs.text:
The strongest gross-proxy delta came from T3710 (TCN-F2, recommended) at +134 versus baseline.

Interpretation guide: Better than proportional means net PnL improved by more than the trade-count reduction alone would suggest. Roughly proportional means the gain is consistent with simply trading less. Worse than proportional means turnover fell, but net PnL did not improve enough to support a strong selectivity claim.

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
1. Methodology
  2. Grouped Analysis — By Model

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text: This report evaluates whether trained models produce statistically superior objective_value distributions compared to random baselines.

docs.title: What is being compared?
docs.text: Each trained model (e.g. mlp, tcn, vit) runs multiple trials during training. Each trial produces an objective_value score. We pool the objective_value scores from completed trials and compare them against the scores from random baseline models using a statistical test.

docs.title: Two levels of analysis
docs.text:
1. Grouped analysis (main sections below) — experiments are pooled by combinations of {Run, Tier, Model, Fold} dimensions. For example, "By Model" pools ALL trials from every mlp experiment into one group, then tests that group against each random type. This answers: "Does this model type beat randoms overall?"
  2. Raw pairwise analysis (Appendix A) — each individual experiment is compared 1-to-1 against each random baseline of the same fold. This answers: "Does this specific experiment beat this specific random?"

docs.title: Random baselines
docs.text:
Three random baseline types exist, only in Tier A: RLA (random_l), RBO (random_b), and RMK (random_markov). Every real model is always compared against all three separately — randoms are never pooled together.

When fold is part of the grouping dimensions, randoms are filtered to the matching fold. When fold is NOT in the grouping, all random trials across folds are pooled together.

docs.title: Statistical test
docs.text:
• Mann-Whitney U two-sided test (non-parametric, compares rank distributions)
  • Holm-Sidak correction for multiple testing (applied independently within each section)
  • Bootstrap 95% CI for median difference (1000 iterations, seed 2026)

docs.title: PASS criteria (all three must hold)
docs.text:
1. median(real) > median(random) — the trained model must outperform
  2. Holm-Sidak corrected p < 0.05 — statistically significant
  3. rank-biserial > 0.3 — medium or larger effect size

docs.title: How to read the tables
docs.text: Column Meaning Group The combination of dimension values that defines this group vs Random Which random baseline type is being compared against n_exp Number of real experiments contributing ≥1 completed trial n_real / n_rand Number of pooled trial scores on each side Med Real / Med Rand Median objective_value for each side Diff Median difference (Med Real − Med Rand); positive = real is better p Raw Mann-Whitney p-value p_corr Holm-Sidak corrected p-value (accounts for multiple comparisons) Effect Rank-biserial effect size (−1 to +1; >0.3 = medium effect) CI 95% Bootstrap 95% confidence interval for the median difference Status PASS if all 3 criteria met, FAIL otherwise, SKIP if no data

docs.text:

docs.title: Overall Assessment
docs.text:
• 5 of 7 trainable model types statistically outperform all 3 random baselines (grouped By Model, p < 0.05 corrected, effect > 0.3)
  • Both tiers PASS all 6 tests — superiority holds across feature configurations
  • Strongest evidence: mlp_ex (avg effect 0.66) and vit (avg effect 0.58)
  • No model achieves 100% pairwise pass rate, but this is expected: fold-specific anomalies and small sample sizes create pairwise failures that disappear when trials are pooled in the grouped analysis

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text: This report evaluates whether trained models produce statistically superior objective_value distributions compared to random baselines.

docs.title: What is being compared?
docs.text: Each trained model (e.g. mlp, tcn, vit) runs multiple trials during training. Each trial produces an objective_value score. We pool the objective_value scores from completed trials and compare them against the scores from random baseline models using a statistical test.

docs.title: Two levels of analysis
docs.text:
1. Grouped analysis (main sections below) — experiments are pooled by combinations of {Run, Tier, Model, Fold} dimensions. For example, "By Model" pools ALL trials from every mlp experiment into one group, then tests that group against each random type. This answers: "Does this model type beat randoms overall?"
  2. Raw pairwise analysis (Appendix A) — each individual experiment is compared 1-to-1 against each random baseline of the same fold. This answers: "Does this specific experiment beat this specific random?"

docs.title: Random baselines
docs.text:
Three random baseline types exist, only in Tier A: RLA (random_l), RBO (random_b), and RMK (random_markov). Every real model is always compared against all three separately — randoms are never pooled together.

When fold is part of the grouping dimensions, randoms are filtered to the matching fold. When fold is NOT in the grouping, all random trials across folds are pooled together.

docs.title: Statistical test
docs.text:
• Mann-Whitney U two-sided test (non-parametric, compares rank distributions)
  • Holm-Sidak correction for multiple testing (applied independently within each section)
  • Bootstrap 95% CI for median difference (1000 iterations, seed 2026)

docs.title: PASS criteria (all three must hold)
docs.text:
1. median(real) > median(random) — the trained model must outperform
  2. Holm-Sidak corrected p < 0.05 — statistically significant
  3. rank-biserial > 0.3 — medium or larger effect size

docs.title: Scope
docs.text:
• Baseline run under analysis: results/20260319_035820
  • Rescue run under analysis: results/20260323_tierA_rescue_v2
  • Audit log: 20260323_SCRIPT2_AUDIT_LOG.md
  • Tier A rescue audit: 20260323_SCRIPT2_TIER_A_RESCUE_AUDIT.md
  • Thesis plan reference: EXPERIMENT_PLAN_THESIS_VALIDATION.md
  • Status note: AXV-F1 from the rescue run is still executing, so axial conclusions remain provisional.

docs.title: Executive Summary
docs.text:
• Tier A does not validate the trading thesis economically yet. There is weak learnability, but absolute edge is still tiny.
  • After unifying baseline Tier A with Tier A rescue, only two trainable best trials are positive:
  • ViT-F1: PnL=+81.52, Return=+0.008%
  • MLP-F3: PnL=+16.01, Return=+0.002%
  • MLP is the family that benefited the most from rescue. The rescue improved search quality materially, but it did not convert Tier A into a robust money-making tier.
  • TCN shows repeated one-sided collapse behavior. In Tier A it looks more like a degeneration pattern than a trustworthy edge source.
  • ViT can survive when it becomes extremely selective. The best surviving case is not broad trading skill. It is disciplined abstention.
  • The current 3 x 3 x 3 = 27 combined regime label is too sparse for first operational use. In validation, the dominant combined state only explains about 8% to 12% of bars, so "same top combined regime" is not enough by itself.
  • Tier B remains the correct next priority, but the next tiers should be analyzed and likely redesigned with regime-aware logic.

docs.title: Method Notes
docs.text:
• Fold windows come from the Script 2 generator and thesis plan.
  • Effective validation windows were not estimated manually. They were read from prediction parquet metadata in the database.
  • Regime classification in this audit uses the current legacy regime logic:
  • trend by ADX
  • volatility by ATR ratio versus rolling average
  • direction by DI +/- and EMA fast/slow
  • "Unified Tier A + rescue" means:
  • for each model/fold pair, compare baseline best trial and rescue best trial
  • keep the stronger result as the current best known state

docs.title: F1
docs.text:
• Train dominant dimensions:
  • trend: TRENDING (40.0%)
  • volatility: LOW_VOL (43.9%)
  • direction: BULLISH (39.0%)
  • Validation dominant dimensions:
  • trend: RANGING (41.6%)
  • volatility: LOW_VOL (49.2%)
  • direction: BULLISH (44.3%)
  • Top combined state:
  • train: TRENDING | HIGH_VOL | BEARISH (7.9%)
  • validation: TRENDING | LOW_VOL | BULLISH (9.1%)
  • Reading:
  • trend leadership changes between train and validation
  • the winning unified model is ViT
  • this is the clearest case where selectivity mattered more than raw activity

docs.title: F2
docs.text:
• Train dominant dimensions:
  • trend: RANGING (39.9%)
  • volatility: LOW_VOL (44.6%)
  • direction: BULLISH (40.0%)
  • Validation dominant dimensions:
  • trend: RANGING (44.8%)
  • volatility: LOW_VOL (36.8%)
  • direction: BULLISH (40.5%)
  • Top combined state:
  • train: RANGING | LOW_VOL | NEUTRAL (7.7%)
  • validation: TRENDING | HIGH_VOL | BEARISH (7.9%)
  • Reading:
  • coarse trend/vol/direction leaders look similar enough
  • the top combined state still changes
  • nobody wins economically here
  • this is an important warning: "roughly same regime family" is not enough

docs.title: F3
docs.text:
• Train dominant dimensions:
  • trend: RANGING (42.5%)
  • volatility: LOW_VOL (38.3%)
  • direction: BULLISH (39.0%)
  • Validation dominant dimensions:
  • trend: TRENDING (47.0%)
  • volatility: LOW_VOL (38.8%)
  • direction: BULLISH (39.7%)
  • Top combined state:
  • train: TRENDING | HIGH_VOL | BEARISH (7.6%)
  • validation: TRENDING | HIGH_VOL | BEARISH (11.1%)
  • Reading:
  • same top combined state
  • different dominant trend dimension
  • only MLP wins economically
  • this suggests architecture matters even when regime overlap improves

docs.title: F4
docs.text:
• Train dominant dimensions:
  • trend: TRENDING (42.3%)
  • volatility: LOW_VOL (42.1%)
  • direction: BULLISH (39.9%)
  • Validation dominant dimensions:
  • trend: TRENDING (39.5%)
  • volatility: LOW_VOL (39.3%)
  • direction: BEARISH (45.1%)
  • Top combined state:
  • train: TRENDING | HIGH_VOL | BEARISH (8.8%)
  • validation: TRENDING | HIGH_VOL | BEARISH (12.4%)
  • Reading:
  • the top combined state matches
  • the dominant direction flips from bullish to bearish
  • everyone still loses
  • this is the strongest proof that the combined label alone is too coarse and too sparse

docs.title: Why The Current Combined Regime Is Not Enough
docs.text:
• The top combined regime in validation only carries:
  • F1: 9.1%
  • F2: 7.9%
  • F3: 11.1%
  • F4: 12.4%
  • That means almost 88% to 92% of validation bars live outside the dominant combined state.
  • Consequence:
  • even when train and validation share the same top combined state, most of the fold is still governed by other states
  • the practical regime distribution can drift while the top label appears unchanged

docs.title: Hard Reading
docs.text:
• logistic is stable and never convincing. It reaches decent objectives in some folds, but all best trials remain economically negative.
  • MLP is the most promising family in Tier A after rescue.
  • TCN still looks structurally fragile.
  • ViT remains the best candidate for selective trading under weak features.
  • axial_vit is still unresolved.

docs.title: Economic Reality Check
docs.text:
• The strongest criticism of Tier A is simple:
  • many best-objective trials are still money losers
  • This means the current objective can still reward predictive quality that does not translate into enough economic edge.
  • Tier A is therefore useful as a scientific probe, but not yet as a deployment-grade signal source.

docs.title: Label Interpretation
docs.text:
• F4 is the clearest short-heavy fold.
  • The label shift is not enormous, but it is directionally meaningful.
  • This helps explain why several models drifted hard toward short in F4.

  • RANGING | LOW_VOL | BULLISH
  • TRENDING | LOW_VOL | BULLISH
  • F2:
  • TRENDING | LOW_VOL | BULLISH
  • RANGING | LOW_VOL | BULLISH
  • F3:
  • TRENDING | LOW_VOL | BULLISH
  • TRENDING | NORMAL_VOL | BULLISH
  • F4:
  • TRENDING | LOW_VOL | BULLISH
  • RANGING | LOW_VOL | BULLISH

• F1:
  • RANGING | LOW_VOL | NEUTRAL
  • RANGING | LOW_VOL | BEARISH
  • F2:
  • RANGING | LOW_VOL | BEARISH
  • TRENDING | NORMAL_VOL | BULLISH
  • F3:
  • TRENDING | HIGH_VOL | BEARISH
  • TRENDING | NORMAL_VOL | BEARISH
  • F4:

  • RANGING | LOW_VOL | BEARISH

docs.title: Winning Cases
docs.text:
• ViT-F1:
  • LONG=1.6%
  • SHORT=3.2%
  • NONE=95.3%
  • PnL=+81.52

  • Reading:

    • it wins by refusing most trades
    • this is disciplined filtering, not broad market mastery

  • MLP-F3:

  • LONG=22.4%
  • SHORT=1.6%
  • NONE=76.0%
  • PnL=+16.01
  • Reading:
    • it also survives through selectivity
    • it expresses a directional bullish bias without flooding the market

docs.title: Degeneration Patterns
docs.text:
• TCN-F1:
  • SHORT=100.0%
  • TCN-F4:
  • SHORT=100.0%
  • TCN-F2:
  • LONG=83.7%, SHORT=0.0%
  • Reading:
  • these are not healthy prediction profiles
  • they look like collapse into a single directional reflex

docs.title: Overtrading or Directional Imbalance
docs.text:
• logistic-F3:
  • LONG=81.7%
  • SHORT=16.5%
  • still loses
  • mlp-F2:
  • LONG=52.0%
  • SHORT=18.7%
  • rescue improved it a lot, but it still loses too much money
  • vit-F4:
  • LONG=20.6%
  • SHORT=78.7%
  • still loses

docs.title: 1. Tier A is too poor in information
docs.text:
• close + RSI is enough to reveal learnability differences between families
  • it is not enough to produce robust, portable edge across these folds
  • this is the strongest argument for continuing with Tier B

docs.title: 2. Selectivity beats activity in weak-feature space
docs.text:
• the only positive unified cases are the most selective ones
  • that suggests the best Tier A use may be filtering or abstention logic, not a full standalone signal engine

docs.title: 3. Rescue improved search more than it improved the thesis
docs.text:
• rescue found better pockets, especially for MLP
  • but most improved pockets still remain economically negative
  • this means the rescue was useful operationally, but it does not rescue the business conclusion by itself

docs.title: 4. Same regime name is not enough
docs.text:
• F3 and F4 prove this from opposite angles:
  • F3: same top combined state, one family wins
  • F4: same top combined state, nobody wins
  • the regime state has to be treated as a distribution, not as a single winner label

docs.title: 5. The objective may still be too tolerant of unprofitable behavior
docs.text:
• best-objective does not consistently imply best-money
  • for future tiers, this should be monitored aggressively
  • this is not yet a call to rewrite the objective, but it is a clear warning sign

docs.title: Strategic Implications for Tier B
docs.text:
• Continue with Tier B.
  • Treat Tier A as a low-information scout, not as the final battlefield.
  • Prioritize MLP and ViT as the most interesting families to carry forward.
  • Treat TCN with skepticism until it proves it can stop collapsing into one-sided behavior.
  • Keep AXV in observation mode until the current rescue finishes and the family has enough completed evidence.

docs.title: Strong Recommendation
docs.text: • Do not keep relying only on arbitrary rolling windows once Tier B is finished.

docs.title: Recommended Progression
docs.text:
1. Start with a small regime taxonomy:
  2. first try 3 families or 9 families
  3. do not jump directly to 27 for production decisions
  4. Evaluate regime similarity between train and validation explicitly:
  5. trend distribution similarity
  6. volatility distribution similarity
  7. direction distribution similarity
  8. combined distribution similarity
  9. Build "same-regime validation" experiments:
  10. train on one regime family
  11. validate on the same regime family
  12. Only after that, evaluate a mixture-of-experts idea:
  13. detect current regime
  14. choose model family by regime

docs.title: Immediate Work Plan
docs.text:
1. Refactor the old regime scripts into a shared scripts/analysis/regime/ core plus thin CLIs.
  2. Add one Script 2 specific regime analyzer that reads DB + parquets directly.
  3. Re-run this audit automatically once AXV-F1 rescue completes.
  4. Apply the same framework to Tier B as soon as enough runs exist.
  5. Compare 3, 9, and 27 regime taxonomies on coverage, stability, and practical usefulness.
  6. Design candidate regime-based folds after Tier B conclusions are in.

docs.title: Current Thesis Reading
docs.text:
• The system is not dead.
  • The system is not validated yet.
  • There is evidence of learnability.
  • There is not yet evidence of robust autonomous profitability from Tier A.
  • The most honest reading is:
  • keep going
  • move to richer features
  • make regime analysis first-class
  • stop pretending that a single temporal fold design is enough

docs.title: Auditoría de Features: Tiers B a E (Script 2)
Resumen por Tier
docs.text:
[!NOTE] Feature definitions = definiciones de indicadores en el tier config (cada EMA, RSI, etc. cuenta como 1 definición, sin importar cuántas columnas genera). Columnas reales = columnas de salida en los templates generados (MACD = 3 cols, BB = 5 cols, etc., incluye OHLCV raw + derivados).

Tier Feature defs Indicadores Signals OHLCV sets Multi-symbol Columnas reales B 18 17 0 1 (BTC 5m) ❌ 44 C 64 32 32 1 (BTC 5m) ❌ 100 D 133 66 33 5 (BTC 5m/15m/1h/4h/1d) ❌ 211 E 189 89 33 8 (BTC+ETH+BNB multi-tf) ✅ 355

docs.title: Auditoría: Relativización y Normalización por Indicador
docs.text: La normalización rolling por columna destruye los cruces entre features (ej. EMA_fast vs EMA_slow), porque cada columna tiene su propio rolling mean/std. Esto es por diseño — no es un bug.

docs.title: ¿Por qué normalizar es necesario?
docs.text:
Sin normalización, features con rangos muy distintos causan problemas al modelo:

  • EMA(8)/close ≈ 0.997 (varianza ~0.001) vs RSI/100 ≈ 0.50 (varianza ~0.20)
  • El gradiente prioriza features con mayor varianza → EMA queda ignorada
  • El learning rate no puede ser correcto para todas las features a la vez

La normalización (z-score rolling) pone todas las features en media≈0, std≈1.

docs.title: ✅ Features bien configuradas (sin normalización — ya acotadas)
docs.text:
Indicador Rel. Method Rango Norm Tiers RSI (7/14/21) /100 [0, 1] NONE B-E ADX, +DI, -DI /100 [0, 1] NONE B-E Stochastic K%, D% /100 [0, 1] NONE B-E BB upper/middle/lower BAND_POSITION (÷close) ~[0.95, 1.05] NONE B-E BB percent_b NONE [0, 1] NONE B-E Drawdown NONE [-1, 0] NONE D-E Time (sin/cos) NONE [-1, 1] NONE C-E Correlation NONE [-1, 1] NONE (si se usa)

[!TIP] Estos indicadores conservan su semántica completa. El modelo puede interpretar directamente RSI=0.7 como "sobrecomprado", ADX=0.4 como "tendencia fuerte", etc.

docs.title: ⚡ Features normalizadas (ROBUST z-score — pierden semántica directa, ganan comparabilidad)
docs.text: Indicador Rel. Method Norm Tiers Nota EMA (9/12/14/21/26/50/100/200) /close ROBUST B-E Cruces perdidos → compensados por signals MACD line /close ROBUST B-E Cruce MACD-Signal perdido en norm MACD signal /close ROBUST B-E — MACD histogram /close ROBUST B-E Mantiene signo del cruce post-norm ATR (14) /close ROBUST B-E Solo valor propio, no relación OBV DIFF/rolling_mean(vol) ROBUST B-E Solo valor propio VWAP /close ROBUST C-E Similar a EMA Volatility (10/20/50) NONE ROBUST C-E Escala varía con régimen BB bandwidth NONE ROBUST B-E Escala varía con régimen PPO (Price Oscillator) /input2 ROBUST C-E Ya es ratio de EMAs Ratio (cross-tf, DI) NONE ROBUST C-E Spread (DI, RSI disagree, net flow) DIVIDE_BY_SUM ROBUST C-E Net flow EMAs (5/10/20) /close ROBUST C-E

docs.title: Tier B — Solo Indicadores Base (18 feature defs → 44 columnas)
docs.text:
Contenido: OHLCV + 8 EMAs + 3 RSIs + MACD + BB + ATR + ADX + Stochastic + OBV

Hallazgo: Sin signals. El modelo recibe EMAs normalizadas pero no tiene señales de cruce. Depende 100% de que el modelo aprenda patrones de los z-scores individuales. El MACD histogram es la única pista de cruce de EMAs.

[!WARNING] Tier B no tiene signals binarios. Las relaciones de cruce entre EMAs no están representadas explícitamente. El modelo solo puede aprenderlas indirectamente via MACD histogram o combinaciones de z-scores.

docs.title: Tier C — Indicadores + Signals + Extras (64 feature defs → 100 columnas)
docs.text:
Agrega vs B: VWAP, Volatility (3), Time features, Drawdown, Spread (ADX DI, net flow), Ratio (ADX DI), Net flow EMAs, PPO, y 32 signals.

Signals incluidos: - Regime: ADX trending/ranging, bull/bear, high/low vol - Trading: RSI OB/OS, MACD bull/bear - Crossovers: EMA 9/21, 21/50, 50/100, 100/200 (up + down) - MACD line/signal crossover - BB breakout up/down - Stochastic OB/OS - ADX strong/very strong/weak - Volume z-score (>2, normal) - Price above/below EMA200, golden/death cross

[!TIP] Tier C es donde se "reparan" las relaciones perdidas. Los 32 signals binarios capturan las reglas de trading clásicas que la normalización destruye en los features continuos.

docs.title: Tier D — Multi-Timeframe BTC (133 feature defs → 211 columnas)
docs.text:
Agrega vs C: MTF (15m, 1h, 4h, 1d) para EMAs, RSI, MACD, ATR, ADX, Drawdown. Cross-TF ratios (close vs EMA50 en cada TF). Cross-TF RSI disagree. Regime signals para cada TF.

Observación: Los signals de crossover siguen siendo solo BTC 5m. No hay crossover signals para timeframes superiores.

docs.title: Tier E — Multi-Symbol (189 feature defs → 355 columnas)
docs.text:
Agrega vs D: ETH (5m/15m/1h) y BNB (5m/15m/1h) con OHLCV + EMAs + RSI + MACD + BB + ATR + ADX + Stochastic + OBV.

Observación importante: Los signals (crossovers, regimes, thresholds) siguen siendo solo de BTC. ETH y BNB no tienen signals propios. El modelo recibe features continuos normalizados de ETH/BNB pero sin señales binarias de cruce para esos activos.

[!WARNING] ETH y BNB en Tier E no tienen signals de crossover ni regime. Sus features están normalizadas independientemente, así que el modelo no tiene forma explícita de saber si el RSI de ETH cruzó 70 o si las EMAs de BNB cruzaron.

docs.title: Veredicto Global
docs.text: Aspecto Estado Features ya acotados (RSI, ADX, Stoch, BB, Drawdown, Time, Correlation) sin normalizar ✅ Correcto Features con escala variable (EMA, MACD, ATR, OBV, VWAP, Volatility) normalizados ✅ Correcto Cruces de EMA compensados con signals ✅ Solo en BTC 5m (Tiers C+) Cruces de MACD compensados con signals ✅ Solo en BTC (Tiers C+) MACD hist preserva magnitud de cruce EMA ✅ En todos los tiers Signals de regime multi-TF ✅ Solo BTC (Tier D+) Signals de crossover multi-TF ❌ Solo BTC 5m Signals para ETH/BNB ❌ No existen en Tier E

docs.title: Sobre la pregunta de descubrimiento del modelo
docs.text:
La normalización per-column sí limita la capacidad del modelo de descubrir relaciones cross-feature nuevas. Pero hay que ser preciso en qué pierde:

Lo que el modelo SÍ puede descubrir (post-normalización): - Patrones temporales: "cuando EMA_fast está 2σ arriba de su media Y RSI > 0.7 → probable reversión" - Combinaciones no lineales de z-scores individuales - Correlaciones entre z-scores que aparecen antes de movimientos

Lo que NO puede descubrir: - "EMA_fast está X% más arriba que EMA_slow" (la magnitud del spread entre dos features normalizados independientemente no es interpretable) - Relaciones basadas en la posición relativa de dos features que no tienen un signal o feature derivado que lo capture

Mitigación: Cada relación cross-feature que importa debe tener su propio feature derivado (MACD, Spread, Ratio, Signal). Esto es trabajo manual de feature engineering, sí. Pero es el trade-off de normalizar: estabilidad de entrenamiento a cambio de necesitar features derivados explícitos para capturar relaciones.

Las relaciones no-lineales complejas entre features que un humano no anticipó NO pueden ser descubiertas si dependen del valor relativo directo entre columnas. Esto es inherente a la normalización per-column, no un bug del sistema.

docs.title: Cómo se compensan los cruces perdidos
docs.text:
Los cruces entre features se capturan con:

  1. Signals binarios (0/1): Cruces EMA, RSI>70, MACD>0, Stoch OB/OS, ADX strong/weak, BB breakout, golden/death cross
  2. MACD histogram: Es literalmente EMA_fast - EMA_slow, captura dirección Y magnitud del cruce
  3. Spread/Ratio indicators: spread_adx_di captura +DI vs -DI, ratio_adx_di igual

Limitación: Los signals binarios solo dicen sí/no, no magnitud. El MACD histogram es el único que captura la amplitud del cruce de EMAs, y luego se normaliza (pero mantiene el signo = dirección del cruce).

docs.title: OHLCV: Pipeline Relative Price
docs.text: El pipeline RELATIVE_PRICE crea columnas nuevas a partir de los OHLCV raw. No modifica las columnas originales.

docs.title: Columnas que crea
docs.text: Columna creada Fórmula Rango típico Qué captura return_close (close - close_prev) / close_prev [-0.1, 0.1] Retorno porcentual de cierre hl_pct (high - low) / close [0, 0.05] Rango intra-vela normalizado (volatilidad) oc_pct (close - open) / open [-0.05, 0.05] Cuerpo de la vela (dirección) volume_ratio volume / rolling_mean(volume) [0, 5+] Volumen relativo a su media móvil quote_volume_return (qv - qv_prev) / qv_prev [-1, 10+] Cambio porcentual en volumen USD aggressive_buy_ratio taker_buy_vol / total_vol [0, 1] Proporción compradora agresiva aggressive_sell_ratio taker_sell_vol / total_vol [0, 1] Proporción vendedora agresiva

docs.title: Columnas raw que quedan intactas
docs.text: Las columnas OHLCV originales se preservan en el output del pipeline: timestamp, open, high, low, close, volume_base, quote_volume, number_of_trades, taker_buy_base_volume, taker_buy_quote_volume, taker_sell_base_volume, taker_sell_quote_volume.

docs.title: ¿Se normalizan las columnas OHLCV?
docs.text:
Sí, todas. FeatureOHLCV.get_normalization_meta() retorna NormalizationMethod.ROBUST para TODAS las columnas numéricas (tanto OHLCV raw como las RELATIVE_PRICE_COLS derivadas).

Esto incluye raw open, high, low, close, volume_base, etc. — todas reciben ROBUST z-score rolling.

docs.title: ¿Se normalizan los derivados (return_close, etc.)?
docs.text:
Sí. Están en la misma meta: ROBUST para todo. Y tiene sentido:

Columna derivada ¿Necesita norm? Por qué return_close Sí (ROBUST) Su magnitud varía con la volatilidad del mercado hl_pct Sí (ROBUST) Rango muy dependiente del régimen oc_pct Sí (ROBUST) Igual que return_close volume_ratio Sí (ROBUST) Puede tener outliers enormes (>10x) quote_volume_return Sí (ROBUST) Outliers aún más extremos aggressive_buy_ratio Sí (ROBUST) Ya acotado [0,1], pero norm lo centra en 0 aggressive_sell_ratio Sí (ROBUST) Ídem

[!NOTE] aggressive_buy_ratio y aggressive_sell_ratio están naturalmente en [0,1] y suman ~1 cuando hay volumen. En principio podrían ir sin normalización (como RSI), pero el sistema los normaliza con ROBUST. El efecto es que se centran en ~0 con std~1, lo cual no es incorrecto pero pierde la semántica de "ratio" (0.5 = equilibrado).

docs.title: Columnas OHLCV en los experimentos: ¿cuáles se seleccionan realmente?
docs.text:
Los experimentos NO incluyen todas las columnas raw. Solo seleccionan un subconjunto:

Grupo Columnas seleccionadas Mandatory Tiers ohlcv_price open, high, low, close ❌ No B-E ohlcv_volume volume_base, quote_volume, number_of_trades, taker_buy_base_volume, taker_buy_quote_volume, taker_sell_base_volume, taker_sell_quote_volume ❌ No B-E ohlcv_returns return_close, oc_pct ✅ Sí B-E ohlcv_volatility hl_pct ✅ Sí B-E ohlcv_relationship volume_ratio, quote_volume_return, aggressive_buy_ratio, aggressive_sell_ratio ✅ Sí B-E

[!IMPORTANT] Las columnas raw de precio (open, high, low, close) y volumen crudo son no-mandatory y sin weight_range. Las columnas derivadas (return, hl_pct, oc_pct, ratios) son mandatory. Esto es correcto: los precios raw son redundantes con los indicadores relativizados, pero los retornos/ratios son información fundamental.

docs.title: Auditoría: Mandatory, Weight Range y Feature Selection
docs.text: [!IMPORTANT] Fuente de verdad: templates generados en experiments/v20260325_tierB_mlp_ex/. Código verificado: hyperparams_feature_selection.py líneas 196-258.

docs.title: ¿Qué significa weight_range = None?
docs.text:
weight_range: None NO significa que el modelo aprende el peso libremente. Significa:

  1. Optuna no optimiza el peso de esa columna (no registra hyperparámetro de peso).
  2. La columna recibe peso fijo 1.0 (neutral).
  3. Ese 1.0 se normaliza junto con todos los demás pesos para que sumen 1.0.
  4. Resultado: todas las columnas con weight_range=None arrancan con peso igual entre sí.
  5. Luego el modelo tiene un feature_scale learnable que parte de ese peso normalizado.

En la práctica, como todas las columnas de todos los tiers tienen weight_range=None, esto significa que todas arrancan con peso 1/N donde N = número de columnas seleccionadas por Optuna.

docs.title: ¿Qué es mandatory?
docs.text:
mandatory: True → la columna siempre se incluye en el modelo. Optuna no puede excluirla durante feature selection. Pero su peso arranca igual que el resto (1/N).

mandatory: False → Optuna puede excluirla durante feature selection. Si queda fuera, no participa en absoluto.

docs.title: ¿Qué es feature_selection_config?
docs.text:
Mecanismo completamente independiente de weight_range. Controla cuántas columnas selecciona Optuna, no cómo se ponderan.

  • min_features: mínimo de columnas totales que Optuna debe seleccionar (mandatory + optional).
  • max_features: máximo de columnas totales. null = sin tope (puede usar todas las disponibles).
  • "auto": valor especial — se resuelve a total_regular (el total exacto de columnas disponibles del tier). Ver sección abajo.
  • Optuna sugiere un feature_columns_count entre [max(min_features, mandatory_count), max_features].
  • Luego selecciona las optional columns por scoring + grouping strategy.

docs.title: Resumen por Tier (trainable/mlp)
docs.text: Tier Total cols Mandatory Optional min_features max_features A 2 0 2 2 2 B 44 7 37 10 null (=44) C 100 40 60 45 null (=100) D 211 74 137 80 null (=211) E 355 88 267 95 null (=355)

docs.title: Resumen por Tier (rule_based — templates generados)
docs.text:
Tier Total cols Mandatory Optional min_features max_features B-E 12 0 12 12 12

[!NOTE] Rule-based usa exactamente 12 features fijas (min=max=12), todas non-mandatory. min==max fuerza inclusión de todas sin necesidad de usar el flag mandatory. Estas 12 son las rule_raw columns para las expresiones del rule engine.

docs.title: ¿Qué es mandatory y qué no? (trainable)
docs.text: Mandatory ✅ Non-mandatory ❌ OHLCV derivados BTC 5m: return, hl_pct, oc_pct, vol_ratio, quote_vol_return, agg_buy, agg_sell (7 cols) OHLCV raw price: open, high, low, close (4 cols por symbol) Signals BTC 5m: crossovers, thresholds (27 cols, Tier C+) OHLCV raw volume: volume, quote_vol, trades, taker_buy/sell (7 cols por symbol) Regime BTC 5m: trending/ranging, bull/bear, high/low vol (6 cols, Tier C+) OHLCV MTF: price, volume, return, hl_pct (Tier D+) Signals MTF BTC: RSI OB/OS, MACD bull/bear por TF (16 cols, Tier D+) Todos los indicadores: EMA, RSI, MACD, BB, ATR, ADX, Stoch, OBV, VWAP Regime MTF BTC: por cada TF (18 cols, Tier D+) Todos los derivados: Spread, Ratio, PPO, net flow, volatility OHLCV derivados ETH/BNB 5m (14 cols, Tier E) Time features (sin/cos hour, day, etc.) Cross-TF ratios y spreads Todos los indicadores/OHLCV de ETH y BNB

docs.title: Mandatory por grupo (detalle)
docs.text: Grupo Tier B Tier C Tier D Tier E ohlcv_returns 2 2 2 6 ohlcv_volatility 1 1 1 3 ohlcv_relationship 4 4 4 12 regime — 6 6 6 regime_mtf — — 18 18 signals — 27 27 27 signals_mtf — — 16 16 TOTAL 7 40 74 88
