docs.text: Grupo Tier B Tier C Tier D Tier E ohlcv_price 4 4 4 12 ohlcv_volume 7 7 7 21 ohlcv_price_mtf — — 16 32 ohlcv_volume_mtf — — 4 8 ohlcv_returns_mtf — — 4 8 ohlcv_volatility_mtf — — 4 8 trend (EMA) 11 13 13 35 trend_mtf — — 16 26 momentum (RSI, MACD, Stoch) 8 9 9 25 momentum_mtf — — 16 32 volatility (BB, ATR, vol) 6 11 11 23 volatility_mtf — — 11 13 volume (OBV) 1 2 2 4 time — 10 10 10 cross_tf — — 6 6 flow (net flow) — 4 4 4 TOTAL 37 60 137 267

docs.title: ¿Qué pasa con las columnas OHLCV raw (open, high, low, close)?
docs.text:
Las columnas OHLCV raw de precio están presentes en todos los tiers como optional (non-mandatory):

  • ohlcv_price_btc_5m_open, _high, _low, _close → presentes en B-E, non-mandatory
  • En Tier E se agregan ETH y BNB (ohlcv_price_eth_5m_*, ohlcv_price_bnb_5m_*)
  • Las columnas MTF (ohlcv_price_mtf_btc_15m_*, etc.) aparecen en D-E

¿Llegan al modelo? Depende de Optuna: - Son optional → Optuna puede excluirlas si su selection score es bajo. - Pero min_features es alto (80 en D, 95 en E) relativo al total de optional columns, así que la probabilidad de que sean excluidas es baja en tiers grandes. - En Tier B con min_features=10 y 37 optional, sí pueden quedar fácilmente fuera.

¿Cómo llegan? Si Optuna las selecciona: 1. Pasan por RELATIVE_PRICE pipeline → se crean las columnas derivadas (return, hl_pct, etc.) y se preservan las raw. 2. Luego FeatureOHLCV.get_normalization_meta() aplica ROBUST z-score rolling a TODAS (raw + derivadas). 3. El modelo recibe close como un z-score rolling (ej: "el close actual está 1.5σ arriba de su media rolling").

docs.title: ¿Qué pasa con las columnas relative price (return_close, hl_pct, etc.)?
docs.text:
Las columnas derivadas por RELATIVE_PRICE pipeline están presentes y son mandatory en BTC 5m para todos los tiers:

  • ohlcv_returns_btc_5m_return (return_close) → mandatory ✅
  • ohlcv_volatility_btc_5m_hl_pct → mandatory ✅
  • ohlcv_returns_btc_5m_oc_pct → mandatory ✅
  • ohlcv_relationship_btc_5m_vol_ratio → mandatory ✅
  • ohlcv_relationship_btc_5m_quote_vol_return → mandatory ✅
  • ohlcv_relationship_btc_5m_agg_buy → mandatory ✅
  • ohlcv_relationship_btc_5m_agg_sell → mandatory ✅

En Tier E, las derivadas de ETH y BNB también son mandatory (14 cols adicionales).

Las derivadas MTF (ej: ohlcv_returns_mtf_btc_15m_return) son non-mandatory y aparecen solo en D-E.

¿Cómo llegan al modelo? 1. RELATIVE_PRICE pipeline calcula el valor derivado. 2. ROBUST z-score rolling se aplica sobre la columna derivada. 3. El modelo recibe el z-score (ej: "el return actual es 2σ más alto que su media rolling" → movimiento inusualmente grande).

docs.title: Implicación de min_features vs total
docs.text:
Tier Mandatory min_features max_features Optuna elige entre A 0 2 2 Exactamente 2 de 2 (sin elección) B 7 10 44 De 3 a 37 optional (mínimo 10 total, 7 fijos) C 40 45 100 De 5 a 60 optional (mínimo 45 total, 40 fijos) D 74 80 211 De 6 a 137 optional (mínimo 80 total, 74 fijos) E 88 95 355 De 7 a 267 optional (mínimo 95 total, 88 fijos)

[!WARNING] En todos los tiers, min_features es solo ligeramente mayor que mandatory_count. Esto significa que Optuna debe seleccionar muy pocas optional columns como mínimo: - Tier B: al menos 3 optional de 37 disponibles - Tier C: al menos 5 optional de 60 - Tier D: al menos 6 optional de 137 - Tier E: al menos 7 optional de 267

Como max_features=null (sin tope), Optuna puede usar TODAS. Pero el mínimo es bajo — un trial podría seleccionar solo 10 features en Tier B, excluyendo 34 columnas incluyendo todos los EMAs.

docs.title: Confirmación: weight_range
docs.text: Tu intuición de dejar weight_range con min == max para hacer columnas implícitamente mandatory no se implementó. El weight_range es null en todas las columnas de todos los tiers. No hay ningún weight_range definido a nivel de columna en ningún tier.

docs.title: Valores de min_features / max_features por categoría
docs.text:
Estos valores vienen del config JSON, no tienen relación con weight_range:

Categoría min_features max_features Resultado trainable Tier A 2 2 Exactamente 2 features fijas trainable Tier B 10 null Optuna elige entre 10 y 44 trainable Tier C 45 null Optuna elige entre 45 y 100 trainable Tier D 80 null Optuna elige entre 80 y 211 trainable Tier E 95 null Optuna elige entre 95 y 355 rule_based (B-E) "auto" "auto" Exactamente todas las cols disponibles random_models (B-E) "auto" "auto" Exactamente todas las cols disponibles always_none (B-E) "auto" "auto" Exactamente todas las cols disponibles

[!NOTE] null y "auto" son distintos: - max_features=null → sin tope superior, Optuna puede llegar hasta el total disponible pero no está forzado a hacerlo. - "auto" → se resuelve exactamente a total_regular en tiempo de generación, forzando min == max == total. Ver sección siguiente.

docs.title: ¿Qué significa "auto"?
docs.text:
Cuando min_features o max_features es "auto", el generador corre un preflight antes de crear los templates. El preflight ejecuta MEGA para contar cuántas columnas regulares (total_regular) produce el tier. Después:

min_features = "auto"  →  se resuelve a  total_regular
max_features = "auto"  →  se resuelve a  total_regular

Si ambos son "auto" → min == max == total_regular → se usan TODAS las columnas disponibles, sin feature selection. Optuna no puede excluir ninguna.

Esto es intencional para las categorías baseline: - rule_based: necesita las columnas exactas que sus reglas referencian. - random_models: son baselines de referencia, no optimizan feature selection. - always_none: predice siempre NONE, el feature set no importa.

docs.title: El problema fundamental
docs.text:
La normalización per-column rolling (z-score ROBUST) destruye las relaciones cross-feature que dependen de la magnitud relativa directa entre columnas. Ejemplo: después de normalizar independientemente EMA_9 y EMA_21, la comparación z_ema9 > z_ema21 no equivale a ema9 > ema21 porque cada columna tiene su propio rolling mean/std.

El sistema actual compensa esto con signals binarios y features derivados (MACD, Spread, Ratio). Pero la pregunta es: ¿hay alternativas de normalización que preserven estas relaciones sin sacrificar estabilidad de entrenamiento?

docs.title: Alternativa 1: Group Normalization (normalización por grupo)
docs.text:
Concepto: En vez de normalizar cada columna independientemente, agrupar features relacionadas y calcular estadísticas compartidas (mean/std) para todo el grupo.

Ejemplo: Todas las EMAs (9, 12, 14, 21, 26, 50, 100, 200) comparten el mismo rolling mean y std. Si antes de normalizar EMA_9 > EMA_21, después de normalizar con stats compartidas EMA_9_norm > EMA_21_norm sigue siendo cierto.

Pros: - Preserva el orden relativo dentro del grupo (cruces de EMAs sobreviven) - Cada grupo queda en escala ~N(0,1) para el modelo - Implementación relativamente simple

Contras: - Relaciones entre grupos diferentes siguen destruidas (EMA vs RSI) - Los features del grupo deben tener escalas similares pre-normalización (funciona para EMAs relativizadas, no para mezclar EMA con ATR) - Si un feature del grupo tiene outliers extremos, afecta a todos

Aplicabilidad a rainbow-trader: Alta. Los EMAs ya están relativizados (/close), así que comparten escala. Se podría aplicar group norm al grupo EMA sin cambiar el resto del pipeline. El MACD (line, signal, histogram) también podría compartir stats.

docs.title: Alternativa 2: Cross-Sectional Rank Normalization
docs.text:
Concepto: En cada timestep, rankear todos los N features y convertir a percentiles [0, 1] o [-1, 1]. No usa estadísticas históricas — solo la posición relativa de cada feature respecto a los demás en ese instante.

Ejemplo: Si en t=100 tienes 18 features, el feature con mayor valor recibe rank 1.0, el menor 0.0, y los demás se interpolan.

Pros: - Completamente no-paramétrica (sin rolling window, sin mean/std) - Robusto a outliers extremos - Preserva el ordenamiento relativo entre features en cada timestep - Usado en competencias serias: Numerai (el hedge fund crowd-sourced más grande) usa rank normalization como estándar

Contras: - Pierde información de magnitud completamente (solo queda el ranking) - No distingue entre "EMA_9 está 5% arriba de EMA_21" vs "EMA_9 está 0.01% arriba" - Sensible al número de features (rankear 18 features vs 189 da granularidad muy diferente) - No es estacionaria en el sentido temporal (la distribución de ranks puede cambiar)

Aplicabilidad a rainbow-trader: Media-baja. Pierde demasiada información de magnitud. Las EMAs relativizadas tienen valores cercanos (~0.99-1.01), y la magnitud de la diferencia importa (un cruce de 0.01% vs 2% es muy diferente para trading). Podría funcionar como feature adicional (rank como columna extra), no como reemplazo.

docs.title: Alternativa 3: Adaptive Input Normalization (DAIN)
docs.text:
Concepto: Una capa neuronal adicional al inicio del modelo que aprende los parámetros de normalización de forma adaptativa. Propuesto en el paper "Deep Adaptive Input Normalization for Financial Time Series" (Passalis et al., 2020).

Arquitectura: Un mini-network que recibe el input raw y produce: (1) un shift adaptativo, (2) un scale adaptativo, (3) opcionalmente un power transform. Los parámetros se aprenden durante el entrenamiento.

Pros: - La normalización se adapta al régimen de mercado - Puede aprender a preservar relaciones cross-feature si son útiles para el loss - No requiere feature engineering manual para compensar - Referenciado en literatura financiera específica (Springer JSPS 2020)

Contras: - Añade complejidad al modelo (más parámetros, más riesgo de overfitting) - Necesita más datos para aprender la normalización además de la tarea principal - En la práctica, el mini-network puede converger a algo similar a z-score - No hay garantía de que preserve relaciones útiles — depende del loss

Aplicabilidad a rainbow-trader: Media. Interesante como experimento futuro. Requeriría modificar la arquitectura del modelo (agregar una capa DAIN antes del encoder). Podría ser un Tier experimental.

docs.title: Alternativa 4: Solo Features Bounded (todo como ratios/retornos)
docs.text:
Concepto: Diseñar TODOS los features como cantidades naturalmente acotadas (ratios, retornos, indicadores 0-1), eliminando la necesidad de normalización.

Ejemplo: En vez de EMA raw + normalización, usar solamente: - ema_9 / ema_21 (ratio directo, cruce = ratio cruzando 1.0) - (ema_9 - ema_21) / close (spread normalizado por precio) - RSI ya es [0,1], no necesita nada

Pros: - No hay normalización → no hay destrucción de relaciones - Features directamente interpretables - Simple y robusto

Contras: - Requiere feature engineering muy cuidadoso - No todos los features se expresan fácilmente como ratios (volumen, OBV) - Escala de retornos varía con volatilidad (un return de 1% en BTC cripto vs stock) - Algunos ratios tienen outliers extremos (volume_ratio puede ser >10x)

Aplicabilidad a rainbow-trader: Parcialmente implementada. El sistema YA usa RELATIVE_INDICATOR (divide por close/input/100). El problema es que los retornos y ratios resultantes aún tienen escalas variables (por eso se normaliza después). Para eliminar norm completamente, habría que acotar artificialmente o usar clipping.

docs.title: Alternativa 5: Instance Normalization / Layer Normalization interna
docs.text:
Concepto: Delegar la normalización al modelo. En cada forward pass, el modelo normaliza internamente: - LayerNorm: Normaliza across features per sample (un timestep, todas las features) - Instance Norm: Similar pero por canal/feature across tiempo

Pros: - El modelo aprende qué normalización es útil - LayerNorm across features preserva relaciones relativas entre features en cada timestep - Estándar en Transformers (el sistema ya usa ViT/Axial ViT)

Contras: - No resuelve la no-estacionariedad del input (features con drift temporal) - LayerNorm per-sample no tiene memory across time - Si el input tiene outliers extremos, pueden dominar el gradiente antes de que LayerNorm actúe - BatchNorm tiene problemas con batches pequeños

Aplicabilidad a rainbow-trader: Ya parcialmente presente. Los modelos ViT/Axial ViT internamente usan LayerNorm. Pero el input al modelo ya está normalizado externamente. La pregunta sería: ¿qué pasa si se alimenta el input sin normalización externa y se confía solo en LayerNorm? Probablemente inestable por los outliers de volumen y la escala raw de precios (~70,000 para BTC).

docs.title: Alternativa 6: Quantile Normalization
docs.text:
Concepto: Transformar cada feature a una distribución uniforme [0,1] o gaussiana usando su CDF empírica rolling. Cada valor se mapea a su percentil histórico.

Pros: - Robusto a outliers (todo queda en [0,1] o N(0,1)) - No asume distribución específica - Preserva el ordenamiento temporal de cada feature

Contras: - Per-column → mismo problema de destrucción de relaciones cross-feature - Computacionalmente más costoso que z-score (requiere mantener CDF empírica rolling) - Non-trivial con datos no estacionarios (la CDF cambia con el régimen)

Aplicabilidad a rainbow-trader: Baja como reemplazo, útil como complemento. Es per-column, así que no resuelve el problema fundamental. Pero podría ser más robusto que z-score para features con distribuciones muy asimétricas (volumen, retornos en crashes).

docs.title: ¿Qué hacen otros sistemas de ML trading?
docs.text: Sistema / Referencia Normalización Cross-feature Numerai (hedge fund crowd-sourced) Cross-sectional rank per era Solo ranking relativo, sin magnitud QuantConnect / Quantopian Per-feature z-score (lookback window) Feature engineering manual (mismo enfoque rainbow-trader) Marcos López de Prado (Advances in Financial ML) Fractional differentiation + cumsum returns Preserva memoria de la serie, no normaliza cross-features Temporal Fusion Transformer (Google, 2021) Instance-wise normalization + Variable Selection Network VSN aprende qué features importan por contexto Deep RL for Trading (papers varios) Per-feature rolling z-score Signals explícitos o reward shaping Kaggle competition winners (crypto/stock) Standard per-feature norm + heavy feature engineering Mismo enfoque: normalizar + features derivados manuales DAIN (Passalis et al., 2020) Learned adaptive normalization layer Red neuronal aprende la normalización

docs.title: Veredicto: ¿Qué debería explorar rainbow-trader?
docs.text:
Alternativa Prioridad Esfuerzo Ganancia esperada Group Norm para EMAs 🟢 Alta Bajo Preserva cruces EMA sin cambiar arquitectura Ratio features directos (ema_9/ema_21) como features extras 🟢 Alta Bajo Complementa signals con magnitud de cruce DAIN (Adaptive Norm) como Tier experimental 🟡 Media Medio Potencialmente mejor pero requiere cambio de modelo Cross-sectional rank como feature adicional 🟡 Media Bajo Agrega info de posición relativa Eliminar norm y confiar en LayerNorm 🔴 Baja Bajo Probablemente inestable con datos financieros raw Quantile Norm reemplazando ROBUST 🔴 Baja Medio No resuelve el problema fundamental

[!TIP] La recomendación principal es Group Normalization para la familia EMA: usar rolling mean/std compartidos para todas las EMAs de un mismo symbol/timeframe. Esto preservaría los cruces de EMAs sin necesitar signals binarios como compensación, y es compatible con el pipeline actual (solo cambia cómo se calculan las stats en NormalizationPipeline).

Como complemento inmediato de bajo esfuerzo: agregar features explícitos de ratio EMA (ema_fast/ema_slow) que capturan la magnitud del cruce, no solo la dirección.

docs.title: What This Document Is
docs.text:
A design plan for a new MARKET_STATE indicator type and market_state feature block that replaces the current approach of 6 binary regime signals with a richer set of continuous columns covering regime strength, persistence, transitions, and meta-stability.

This plan does not touch Tier A or the current Tier B. It defines a new extended Tier B (B_ext) and upgrades Tier C/D/E.

docs.title: The current market_regime block is too lossy
docs.text:
The existing market_regime block (available in Tier C+) produces 6 binary SIGNAL features:

Signal Logic Output trending ADX >= 25 0 or 1 ranging ADX <= 20 0 or 1 bullish plus_di > minus_di AND ema_14 > ema_50 0 or 1 bearish minus_di > plus_di AND ema_14 < ema_50 0 or 1 high_vol atr_ratio > 1.2x rolling_avg 0 or 1 low_vol atr_ratio < 0.8x rolling_avg 0 or 1

Three problems:

  1. Information destruction. Continuous values are discretized into 0/1. The model cannot distinguish ADX=25.1 (barely trending) from ADX=50 (extremely strong trend). The model cannot learn that its optimal threshold might be ADX=22 instead of 25. We impose our hardcoded thresholds and throw away the gradient.

  2. Volatility ratio is computed and discarded. The market_regime block computes atr / close relative to its 2016-bar rolling average to produce the high_vol and low_vol signals. This continuous ratio is explicitly marked as an internal dependency (generate_mega_experiment.py line 3189: "We intentionally do NOT add it as a top-level feature, so it does not become a model input column"). The model receives the binary result but never sees the underlying continuous value. This is the only regime dimension whose raw input is not available to the model from any other block.

  3. Missing dimensions entirely. The 6 signals answer what the current regime is, but nothing about:

  4. How long it has been active (persistence)
  5. How confidently it is classified (stability)
  6. Whether it just changed (transition awareness)
  7. Whether the regime itself is stable or volatile (meta-stability)
  8. Whether higher timeframes agree (cross-timeframe coherence)

docs.title: Evidence from regime research
docs.text:
The Regime Training Options Audit found that:

  • 5m -> daily dominant regimes are too twitchy for fold construction (max contiguous period: 8 days for 27-state taxonomy).
  • 1d native regimes are the best current backbone (max contiguous: 69 days for trend+direction).
  • Models break most during transitions between regimes.
  • The audit explicitly recommends Phase 3: implement persistence, candidate vs confirmed, and MIXED/UNSTABLE states.

The Regime Folds Follow-Up Plan items #3, #4, #5 request:

A new indicator type that outputs persistence and transition columns directly addresses these requirements at the feature level.

docs.title: 3: Persistence state machine (observed, candidate, confirmed states with counters)
4: Confirmation rules (minimum duration, consecutive-day confirmation, majority vote, confidence threshold)
5: Hysteresis and transition friction (asymmetric thresholds, weak candidate vs confirmed switch)
What
docs.text:
A new MARKET_STATE indicator type that produces multiple continuous columns from a single coherent computation. It reuses existing indicator outputs (ADX, ATR, EMA, Bollinger Bands) as inputs and adds stateful regime tracking on top.

The columns are organized in four groups. Each group can be independently enabled or disabled in the block configuration.

docs.title: Group 1: Continuous regime components
docs.text:
These values are already computed by existing blocks or internally by the market_regime block. The goal is to expose them as continuous model features instead of discretizing them into binary signals.

Column What it is Range Source trend_strength Raw ADX value 0-100 Already available from adx block. Only needs to be ensured present even when adx block is not in the group. direction_score (plus_di - minus_di) / (plus_di + minus_di) [-1, +1] Normalized directional dominance. Already computed by derived.di_dominance in Tier C+ as a SPREAD, but not available in lower tiers. ema_trend (ema_fast - ema_slow) / ema_slow continuous Continuous trend alignment. EMA periods configurable (default: 14 fast, 50 slow). volatility_ratio (atr / close) / rolling_avg(atr / close) ~1.0 center Currently computed internally by market_regime and discarded. Centered around 1.0. Values > 1.2 mean high vol, < 0.8 mean low vol. This is the highest-value column to expose because it is the only regime dimension whose continuous form is not available from any existing block. bb_squeeze bb_bandwidth / rolling_avg(bb_bandwidth) ~1.0 center Bollinger Band squeeze indicator. Centered around 1.0. Low values signal volatility compression (often precedes breakouts).

Implementation complexity: Low. These can be built with existing IndicatorType values (RATIO, SPREAD) or by ensuring existing block outputs are included. No new pipeline types needed.

docs.title: Group 2: Persistence and transitions
docs.text:
These are completely new computations that require stateful regime tracking. They answer questions that no existing feature can answer: how long has this regime been active? and how stable is it?

Column What it is Range Notes bars_since_trend_change Number of bars since the trend component (TRENDING/NEUTRAL/RANGING) last changed 0 to N Log-scaled or normalized by rolling window to keep values in a learnable range. bars_since_direction_change Same for the direction component (BULLISH/NEUTRAL/BEARISH) 0 to N Same scaling. bars_since_vol_change Same for the volatility component (HIGH_VOL/NORMAL_VOL/LOW_VOL) 0 to N Same scaling. regime_stability Fraction of the last N bars (configurable, e.g. 288 = 1 day of 5m) that agree on the current combined regime [0, 1] High values mean the regime is well-established. Low values mean the market is in transition or mixed. Directly addresses FOLLOW_UP_PLAN #6 (MIXED/low-confidence detection). transition_recency Exponential decay from the last regime change. exp(-lambda * bars_since_last_change) [0, 1] High = just changed (transition zone). Low = stable for a long time. Lambda is configurable. Directly addresses FOLLOW_UP_PLAN #5 (hysteresis).

Implementation complexity: Medium. Requires a new IndicatorType (e.g., MARKET_STATE) because these computations are stateful: they depend on the sequence of regime classifications, not just the current bar's indicator values. The closest existing precedent is the DRAWDOWN indicator, which also tracks state across bars (peak-to-trough).

These columns, combined with the existing 6 binary signals, provide all the information that the FOLLOW_UP_PLAN items #3, #4, #5, #6 describe:

FOLLOW_UP_PLAN item Covered by #3 Persistence state machine (observed/candidate/confirmed, counters) bars_since_*_change columns give the counters. The model learns its own observed/candidate/confirmed thresholds from data instead of us hardcoding them. #4 Confirmation rules (min duration, consecutive-day, majority vote, confidence) bars_since_*_change gives duration. regime_stability gives the majority-vote / confidence measure. The model learns the confirmation rule that works best. #5 Hysteresis and transition friction transition_recency explicitly encodes how recent the last change was. Combined with regime_stability, the model can learn to distrust recent transitions with low stability. #6 MIXED / low-confidence days regime_stability directly measures this. Low stability = mixed/unstable. The model can learn to reduce position sizing or avoid trading when stability is low.

The key insight: instead of hardcoding a persistence state machine with arbitrary thresholds (e.g., "confirmed after 3 consecutive days"), we give the model the raw persistence and stability signals and let it learn the optimal thresholds from training data.

docs.title: Group 3: Meta-regime
docs.text:
Standard quantitative finance practice for characterizing regime dynamics.

Column What it is Why it matters trend_momentum Rate of change of ADX over N bars Is the trend strengthening or weakening? A high ADX that is falling means the trend is dying. A low ADX that is rising means a new trend may be forming. vol_of_vol Rolling standard deviation of volatility_ratio changes Measures the stability of the volatility regime itself. High vol-of-vol means the market is alternating rapidly between calm and volatile states. Low vol-of-vol means the current volatility state is persistent and trustworthy.

Implementation complexity: Low. Rate of change can be a SPREAD or simple arithmetic on existing values. Rolling std is a VOLATILITY-type computation.

docs.title: Group 4: Cross-timeframe regime agreement (deferred)
docs.text:
Column What it is regime_agreement_htf Do higher-timeframe regimes agree with the base timeframe regime?

Status: Deferred. This requires multi-timeframe data to be available, which only applies when the experiment uses multiple timeframes (Tier D/E in the thesis). The market_state block configuration should support this group, but implementation is postponed until after Groups 1-3 are validated.

Design note: When implemented, this should be configurable by specifying which higher timeframes to compare against, not hardcoded to specific tiers.

docs.title: Why a single new indicator type instead of many small ones
docs.text:
Groups 1, 3 could technically be built from existing indicator types (RATIO, SPREAD, VOLATILITY). But there are strong reasons to unify everything into one indicator type:

  1. Coherent computation. The regime classification (TRENDING/RANGING, BULLISH/BEARISH, HIGH_VOL/LOW_VOL) must be computed once before the Group 2 persistence columns can count bars since last change. If we split the computation, the regime classification happens in multiple places or needs to be passed between features. A single indicator type computes the regime classification once and derives all columns from it.

  2. Single configuration surface. One block config with clear group enable/disable flags. No need to wire up 12 separate features with cross-references.

  3. Consistency with regime detector. The thresholds and logic (ADX >= 25, DI crossover, ATR ratio baseline) live in one place. If we change a threshold, all derived columns update consistently.

  4. Expandable. Adding Group 4 (cross-timeframe) or future groups (e.g., HMM-based regime probabilities) means adding columns to one indicator, not designing new feature types.

docs.title: Block configuration schema
docs.text:
"market_state": {
    "enabled": true,
    "mandatory": false,
    "groups": {
        "continuous": true,      // Group 1: trend_strength, direction_score, etc.
        "persistence": true,     // Group 2: bars_since_*, stability, transition
        "meta": true,            // Group 3: trend_momentum, vol_of_vol
        "cross_tf": false        // Group 4: regime_agreement_htf (deferred)
    },
    "per_tf": [
        {
            "tfs": ["5m"],
            // Regime classification parameters (same as current market_regime)
            "adx_period": 14,
            "adx_strong_trend": 25.0,
            "adx_weak_trend": 20.0,
            "ema_fast_period": 14,
            "ema_slow_period": 50,
            "atr_period": 14,
            "vol_baseline_days": 7,
            "vol_multipliers": {"high": 1.2, "low": 0.8},
            // Persistence parameters (Group 2)
            "stability_window": 288,  // bars for regime_stability rolling window (~1 day of 5m)
            "transition_decay_lambda": 0.01,  // exp decay rate for transition_recency
            // Meta parameters (Group 3)
            "trend_momentum_window": 20,  // bars for ADX rate of change
            "vol_of_vol_window": 50  // bars for rolling std of volatility_ratio
        }
    ]
}

This configuration is generic and tier-agnostic. Tiers are a thesis concept. The block itself is a reusable indicator that can be configured for any experiment.

docs.title: Relationship to existing market_regime block
docs.text:
The existing market_regime block and its 6 binary signals remain unchanged and available. The new market_state block is a separate, complementary block.

In the MEGA generator block_groups, tiers that want both would list both blocks:

"block_groups": {
    // Current Tier B: no regime features
    "standard": ["ohlcv", "ema", "rsi", "macd", "bollinger_bands", "atr", "adx", "stochastic", "obv"],

    // New extended Tier B: adds market_state (groups 1+3 only)
    "standard_ext": ["ohlcv", "ema", "rsi", "macd", "bollinger_bands", "atr", "adx", "stochastic", "obv", "market_state"],

    // Tier C: full single-tf (existing blocks + market_state with all single-tf groups)
    "full_single_tf": [
        "ohlcv", "ema", "rsi", "macd", "bollinger_bands", "atr", "adx", "stochastic",
        "obv", "vwap", "volatility", "time_features", "drawdown", "derived", "signals",
        "market_regime", "market_state"
    ],

    // Tier D/E: same + multi-tf (market_state cross_tf group enabled)
    "full_multi_tf": [
        "ohlcv", "ema", "rsi", "macd", "bollinger_bands", "atr", "adx", "stochastic",
        "obv", "vwap", "volatility", "time_features", "drawdown", "derived", "signals",
        "market_regime", "market_state"
    ]
}

Group enable/disable per tier would be controlled via block_overrides in the tier config:

Tier market_state groups enabled Total new columns A (not included) 0 B_ext continuous + meta (Groups 1+3) 7 C continuous + persistence + meta (Groups 1+2+3) 12 D/E All groups including cross_tf (Groups 1+2+3+4, when implemented) 12+

docs.title: For Tier B_ext
docs.text:
The model receives 7 continuous regime features where before it had 0. This gives it the raw ingredients to learn:

  • Trend strength and whether the market is trending, ranging, or in between (without hardcoded 25/20 thresholds)
  • Directional dominance as a continuous score, not a binary flag
  • Volatility relative to its recent baseline (the value that was previously computed and discarded)
  • Bollinger squeeze as a breakout anticipation signal
  • Whether the current trend is strengthening or weakening
  • Whether the volatility regime is stable or itself volatile

docs.title: For Tier C
docs.text:
Everything from Tier B_ext plus 5 persistence columns. This means the model can learn:

  • To trust a regime that has been active for many bars
  • To distrust a regime that just changed
  • To reduce confidence when regime stability is low (MIXED/transition zones)
  • To behave differently in the first bars of a new regime vs an established regime
  • The optimal confirmation thresholds from data instead of hardcoded rules

docs.title: For the thesis
docs.text:
The market_state block directly enables the training options proposed in the Regime Training Options Audit:

  • Option D (global model plus regime features): Add market_state columns to the global training set and test whether regime information helps the model specialize.
  • Option E (regime-aware do-not-trade): Use regime_stability and transition_recency to learn when NOT to trade.
  • Phase 3 (transition safety): The persistence columns implement the candidate/confirmed concept without hardcoding the state machine.

docs.title: Phase 1: Group 1 + Group 3 (continuous + meta)
docs.text:
• 7 columns, all implementable with existing indicator types or simple arithmetic
  • No new IndicatorType required yet
  • Can be built as individual RATIO/SPREAD indicators composed together in the MEGA generator
  • Deliverable: new market_state block in blocks_catalog with continuous and meta groups
  • Risk: low

docs.title: Phase 2: Group 2 (persistence)
docs.text:
• 5 columns requiring stateful computation
  • New IndicatorType.MARKET_STATE added to the pipeline
  • The indicator receives regime classification parameters, classifies each bar, and tracks state
  • Deliverable: persistence columns working in the pipeline, enabled for Tier C+
  • Risk: medium (new indicator type in the data pipeline)

docs.title: Phase 3: Group 4 (cross-timeframe)
docs.text:
• Deferred until Groups 1-3 are validated
  • Requires multi-timeframe experiments to be running
  • Design note: configurable by listing which higher timeframes to compare against

docs.title: Open Questions
docs.text:
1. Should Group 1 columns go through RELATIVE_INDICATOR normalization? Some of them are already relative (volatility_ratio, bb_squeeze are centered around 1.0). Others are bounded (trend_strength 0-100, direction_score -1 to +1). The normalization pipeline may need to be configured per-column.

  2. Scaling for bars_since_* columns. Raw bar counts can be very large (thousands). Options: log-scale, divide by a configurable window, or let the normalization pipeline handle it. Log-scale is most common in quant literature for duration features.

  3. Should the 6 existing binary signals be kept when market_state is active? Current recommendation: yes, keep both. The binaries are cheap and serve as easy shortcuts. The continuous columns provide the gradient. They complement each other.

  4. Interaction with derived.di_dominance. The direction_score column overlaps with the existing di_dominance derived feature. When both market_state and derived blocks are active, we should either skip the duplicate or let feature selection handle it.

docs.title: How This Gets Built: The Indicator In Detail
docs.text: Everything above describes what we want. Everything below describes how we build it, based on how indicators actually work in this codebase.

docs.title: Anatomy of an indicator in this system
docs.text: Every indicator in rainbow-trader follows the same pattern. Before designing MARKET_STATE, here is the pattern that ADX, DRAWDOWN, Bollinger Bands, and all 17 existing indicators follow.

docs.title: Files per indicator
docs.text:
# Module File What it contains 1 rainbow-common types/configs/indicators/{name}_config.py Pydantic config class extending BaseIndicatorGenerated. Declares column_outputs_generated: ClassVar[list[str]] (fixed list of output column names), instance parameters (periods, windows, thresholds), test config, maybe-config for optional overrides. 2 rainbow-common types/configs/__init__.py Import config classes. Add to IndicatorConfigUnion type alias. Add to __all__. 3 rainbow-common types/enums.py Add value to IndicatorType enum. Add entry to INDICATOR_GROUPS dict (maps indicator to FeatureGroup for feature selection taxonomy). 4 rainbow-core types/composed/indicators.py Pydantic composed type extending BaseIndicator. Declares relative_meta (how each column is relativized), normalization_meta (how each column is normalized), get_warm_up_periods(), get_config(). Also: add {name}_config field to IndicatorConfig wrapper. Update BaseIndicator.indicator_type Literal union. 5 rainbow-data-processing indicator/{name}.py NEW FILE. The actual computation: process_{name}(inputs, config, ...) -> ProcessResult. Pure Polars expressions. Validates inputs, filters by range, computes columns, trims warm-up, cleans NaN/Inf, validates output. 6 rainbow-data-processing helpers/indicator_dispatch.py Add one isinstance(config, XxxConfig): return process_xxx(...) branch to the dispatch chain. 7 MEGA generator generate_mega_experiment.py Add block handler (reads config from JSON, calls builder.ensure_* for dependencies, calls builder.add_indicator with input refs). 8 MEGA generator generate_mega_experiment.json Add entry to blocks_catalog with default config. Add to relevant block_groups.

Total per indicator: 6 existing files to modify + 1 new file + 1 JSON config.

docs.title: Concrete example: DRAWDOWN indicator
docs.text:
Config (drawdown_config.py):

class DrawdownConfig(BaseIndicatorGenerated):
    """Drawdown. Columns: drawdown, max_drawdown"""
    segement_path: ClassVar[Path | str | None] = Path("indicators")
    column_outputs_generated: ClassVar[list[str]] = ["drawdown", "max_drawdown"]
    period: int
    test: DrawdownTestConfig

Composed type (indicators.py):

class DrawdownIndicator(BaseIndicatorOneInput):
    indicator_type: Literal[IndicatorType.DRAWDOWN] = IndicatorType.DRAWDOWN
    feature_group: ClassVar[FeatureGroup] = FeatureGroup.VOLATILITY
    config_type: ClassVar[type[BaseConfig]] = DrawdownConfig
    relative_meta: ClassVar = {
        "drawdown": RelativeIndicatorMeta(method=RelativeMethod.NONE),
        "max_drawdown": RelativeIndicatorMeta(method=RelativeMethod.NONE),
    }
    normalization_meta: ClassVar = {
        "drawdown_rel": NormalizationMethod.NONE,
        "max_drawdown_rel": NormalizationMethod.NONE,
    }
    def get_warm_up_periods(self) -> int:
        return self.get_config().period * 2

Process function (drawdown.py): validates inputs → filters time range → computes rolling_max → drawdown = (price - rolling_max) / rolling_max → max_drawdown = rolling_min(drawdown) → trims warm-up → cleans NaN/Inf → validates → returns ProcessResult.

Dispatch (indicator_dispatch.py): one branch in the isinstance chain:

if isinstance(indicator_config, DrawdownConfig):
    return process_drawdown(indicator_inputs, indicator_config, ...)

MARKET_STATE follows this exact pattern. The only difference is: more output columns (12 vs 2), more inputs (6 vs 1), and the computation includes regime classification + run-length counting.

docs.title: MarketStateConfig design
docs.text:
The Pydantic config for the indicator itself. This is what lives in rainbow-common/types/configs/indicators/market_state_config.py.

class MarketStateConfig(BaseIndicatorGenerated):
    """Market State indicator. Produces 12 continuous columns characterizing
    regime strength, persistence, transitions, and meta-stability.

    Columns:
        Group 1 (continuous): trend_strength, direction_score, ema_trend,
            volatility_ratio, bb_squeeze
        Group 2 (persistence): bars_since_trend_change, bars_since_direction_change,
            bars_since_vol_change, regime_stability, transition_recency
        Group 3 (meta): trend_momentum, vol_of_vol
    """

    segement_path: ClassVar[Path | str | None] = Path("indicators")

    # All 12 columns, always computed. Which ones become experiment features
    # is decided by the MEGA generator, not the indicator.
    column_outputs_generated: ClassVar[list[str]] = [
        # Group 1: continuous regime components
        "trend_strength",
        "direction_score",
        "ema_trend",
        "volatility_ratio",
        "bb_squeeze",
        # Group 2: persistence and transitions
        "bars_since_trend_change",
        "bars_since_direction_change",
        "bars_since_vol_change",
        "regime_stability",
        "transition_recency",
        # Group 3: meta-regime
        "trend_momentum",
        "vol_of_vol",
    ]

    # --- Regime classification thresholds ---
    adx_strong_trend: float = 25.0    # ADX >= this → TRENDING
    adx_weak_trend: float = 20.0      # ADX <= this → RANGING (between = NEUTRAL)
    ema_fast_period: int = 14         # For ema_trend and direction classification
    ema_slow_period: int = 50         # For ema_trend and direction classification

    # --- Volatility baseline ---
    vol_baseline_window: int = 2016   # Rolling mean window for ATR/Close ratio baseline.
                                      # Default 2016 = 7 days of 5m bars.
                                      # For 1h: 168. For 1d: 30.
    vol_high_multiplier: float = 1.2  # volatility_ratio > this → HIGH_VOL regime
    vol_low_multiplier: float = 0.8   # volatility_ratio < this → LOW_VOL regime

    # --- Persistence parameters (Group 2) ---
    stability_window: int = 288       # Rolling window for regime_stability.
                                      # Default 288 = 1 day of 5m bars.
                                      # For 1h: 24. For 1d: 7.
    transition_decay_lambda: float = 0.01  # Exponential decay rate for transition_recency.
                                           # 0.01 → half-life ~69 bars (~5.75h at 5m).

    # --- Meta parameters (Group 3) ---
    trend_momentum_window: int = 20   # Window for ADX rate of change.
    vol_of_vol_window: int = 50       # Window for rolling std of volatility_ratio changes.

    test: MarketStateTestConfig


class MarketStateTestConfig(BaseConfig, BaseIndicatorValidation):
    """Market State validation configuration."""


class MaybeMarketStateConfig(IndicatorColumnMeta):
    """Optional per-feature Market State overrides."""
    adx_strong_trend: float | None = None
    adx_weak_trend: float | None = None
    ema_fast_period: int | None = None
    ema_slow_period: int | None = None
    vol_baseline_window: int | None = None
    vol_high_multiplier: float | None = None
    vol_low_multiplier: float | None = None
    stability_window: int | None = None
    transition_decay_lambda: float | None = None
    trend_momentum_window: int | None = None
    vol_of_vol_window: int | None = None

docs.title: Why all 12 columns are always computed (not configurable at indicator level)
docs.text:
Every indicator in this system has column_outputs_generated as a ClassVar — a class-level constant. ADX always produces 3 columns (adx, plus_di, minus_di). Bollinger always produces 5 (upper_band, middle_band, lower_band, bandwidth, percent_b). Not all may be used by every experiment.

MARKET_STATE follows the same rule: always 12 columns. Reasons:

  1. Pipeline consistency. The pipeline assumes column_outputs_generated is fixed. The relative_indicator and normalization stages iterate over this list. Dynamic columns would break this assumption.
  2. One code path. process_market_state has one implementation. No conditional logic for "which groups are enabled."
  3. Cheap. The computation is vectorized Polars executing in Rust. 12 columns vs 7 is negligible. The expensive part is the parent indicators (ADX, EMA, ATR, BB), already computed and cached as parquets.
  4. The MEGA generator controls what becomes a feature. The generator reads feature_groups flags from the block config and only adds the selected columns as experiment features. Unused columns exist in the indicator parquet but are never loaded into the FAT window.

docs.title: Configuration examples for different timeframes
docs.text:
The indicator is timeframe-agnostic. Window sizes are in bars:

// 5-minute bars (thesis default)
{
    "vol_baseline_window": 2016,    // 7 days × 288 bars/day
    "stability_window": 288,        // 1 day
    "trend_momentum_window": 20,    // ~1.7 hours
    "vol_of_vol_window": 50         // ~4.2 hours
}

// 1-hour bars
{
    "vol_baseline_window": 168,     // 7 days × 24 bars/day
    "stability_window": 24,         // 1 day
    "trend_momentum_window": 12,    // 12 hours
    "vol_of_vol_window": 24         // 1 day
}

// 1-day bars
{
    "vol_baseline_window": 30,      // 30 days
    "stability_window": 7,          // 1 week
    "trend_momentum_window": 5,     // 1 week
    "vol_of_vol_window": 14         // 2 weeks
}

docs.title: Why these defaults
docs.text: Parameter Default Rationale adx_strong_trend=25.0 / adx_weak_trend=20.0 Matches regime_detector.py constants (ADX_STRONG=25, ADX_WEAK=20) ema_fast_period=14 / ema_slow_period=50 Matches regime_detector.py (EMA_FAST=14, EMA_SLOW=50) vol_baseline_window=2016 Matches regime_detector.py (VOL_ROLLING_WINDOW=2016) vol_high=1.2 / vol_low=0.8 Matches existing market_regime block thresholds stability_window=288 1 day of 5m. Regime persistence measured in days, not hours. transition_decay_lambda=0.01 Half-life ~69 bars (~5.75h at 5m). Transition is "recent" for about half a day. trend_momentum_window=20 ~1.7h at 5m. ADX changes slowly; short window captures recent acceleration. vol_of_vol_window=50 ~4.2h at 5m. Enough samples to be meaningful, not so many it smooths everything.

docs.title: MarketStateIndicator design (composed type)
docs.text:
This is what lives in rainbow-core/types/composed/indicators.py:

class MarketStateIndicator(BaseIndicator):
    """Market State. 12 continuous columns for regime characterization."""

    indicator_type: Literal[IndicatorType.MARKET_STATE] = IndicatorType.MARKET_STATE
    feature_group: ClassVar[FeatureGroup] = FeatureGroup.TREND
    config_type: ClassVar[type[BaseConfig]] = MarketStateConfig

    # Per-column relativization. Most columns are already bounded or relative.
    relative_meta: ClassVar[dict[str, RelativeIndicatorMeta]] = {
        "trend_strength": RelativeIndicatorMeta(method=RelativeMethod.DIVIDE_BY_100),
            # ADX 0-100 → 0-1
        "direction_score": RelativeIndicatorMeta(method=RelativeMethod.NONE),
            # Already [-1, +1]
        "ema_trend": RelativeIndicatorMeta(method=RelativeMethod.NONE),
            # Already relative: (fast-slow)/slow
        "volatility_ratio": RelativeIndicatorMeta(method=RelativeMethod.NONE),
            # Already centered ~1.0
        "bb_squeeze": RelativeIndicatorMeta(method=RelativeMethod.NONE),
            # Already centered ~1.0
        "bars_since_trend_change": RelativeIndicatorMeta(method=RelativeMethod.NONE),
            # Log-scaled in computation
        "bars_since_direction_change": RelativeIndicatorMeta(method=RelativeMethod.NONE),
        "bars_since_vol_change": RelativeIndicatorMeta(method=RelativeMethod.NONE),
        "regime_stability": RelativeIndicatorMeta(method=RelativeMethod.NONE),
            # Already [0, 1]
        "transition_recency": RelativeIndicatorMeta(method=RelativeMethod.NONE),
            # Already [0, 1]
        "trend_momentum": RelativeIndicatorMeta(method=RelativeMethod.NONE),
            # Small continuous, normalization handles it
        "vol_of_vol": RelativeIndicatorMeta(method=RelativeMethod.NONE),
            # Non-negative, normalization handles it
    }

    # Per-column normalization (after relative_indicator pipeline).
    # Suffix _rel because normalization runs after the relative_indicator step.
    normalization_meta: ClassVar[dict[str, NormalizationMethod]] = {
        "trend_strength_rel": NormalizationMethod.NONE,      # 0-1 after DIVIDE_BY_100
        "direction_score_rel": NormalizationMethod.NONE,      # [-1, +1]
        "ema_trend_rel": NormalizationMethod.ROBUST,          # Unbounded, needs scaling
        "volatility_ratio_rel": NormalizationMethod.NONE,     # ~1.0 center, bounded in practice
        "bb_squeeze_rel": NormalizationMethod.NONE,           # ~1.0 center, bounded in practice
        "bars_since_trend_change_rel": NormalizationMethod.NONE,   # Log-scaled [0, ~1]
        "bars_since_direction_change_rel": NormalizationMethod.NONE,
        "bars_since_vol_change_rel": NormalizationMethod.NONE,
        "regime_stability_rel": NormalizationMethod.NONE,     # [0, 1]
        "transition_recency_rel": NormalizationMethod.NONE,   # [0, 1]
        "trend_momentum_rel": NormalizationMethod.ROBUST,     # Unbounded
        "vol_of_vol_rel": NormalizationMethod.ROBUST,         # Unbounded
    }

    market_state_config: MaybeMarketStateConfig | None = None

    def get_warm_up_periods(self) -> int:
        config = self.get_config()
        return self.get_warm_up_periods_static(config)

    @classmethod
    def get_warm_up_periods_static(cls, config: MarketStateConfig) -> int:
        """Warm-up dominated by vol_baseline_window. Uses 2x conservative rule."""
        _ = cls
        max_window = max(
            config.vol_baseline_window,
            config.stability_window,
            config.trend_momentum_window,
            config.vol_of_vol_window,
        )
        return max_window * 2

docs.title: Warm-up analysis
docs.text:
Window Default (5m) Bars Warm-up (2x) vol_baseline_window 2016 7 days 4032 bars (~14 days) stability_window 288 1 day 576 bars (~2 days) trend_momentum_window 20 ~1.7h 40 bars vol_of_vol_window 50 ~4.2h 100 bars

Dominant: vol_baseline_window → 4032 bars warm-up at 5m. This is the same cost as the existing market_regime block (which already uses a 2016-bar rolling mean for the ATR/Close baseline). No new warm-up penalty compared to existing regime features.

The parent indicators (ADX, EMA, ATR, BB) have their own warm-up handled by the pipeline before MARKET_STATE runs. ADX warm-up is ~42 bars, EMA slow is ~150 bars — much smaller than MARKET_STATE's own 4032.

docs.title: What parent features MARKET_STATE needs
docs.text:
Input # Input name Source indicator Columns used 1 input_adx ADX indicator adx, plus_di, minus_di 2 input_ema_fast EMA indicator (period=fast) ema 3 input_ema_slow EMA indicator (period=slow) ema 4 input_atr ATR indicator atr 5 input_close OHLCV (clean price) close 6 input_bb Bollinger Bands indicator bandwidth

6 inputs. This is more than RATIO (2) or SPREAD (2), but the mechanism is the same: validate_and_get_indicator_inputs(inputs, expected_count=6, input_names=[...]). CONDITION signals already handle N inputs (4 inputs in the bullish/bearish direction signals).

docs.text:
OHLCV (close) ─────────────────────────────┐
                                            │
ADX (adx, plus_di, minus_di) ──────────────┤
                                            │
EMA fast (ema) ─────────────────────────────┼──→ MARKET_STATE → 12 columns
                                            │
EMA slow (ema) ─────────────────────────────┤

Bollinger Bands (bandwidth) ────────────────┘

All parent indicators are computed and cached as parquets before MARKET_STATE runs. The pipeline resolves dependencies automatically. The MEGA generator's block handler calls builder.ensure_adx(...), builder.ensure_ema(...), etc. — exactly like the existing market_regime block does at line 3072–3075.

docs.title: process_market_state computation (pseudocode)
docs.text:
This is the core function in rainbow-data-processing/indicator/market_state.py. All computations are vectorized Polars — no Python loops.

def process_market_state(
    inputs: list[IndicatorInput],
    config: MarketStateConfig,
    timeframe: str, range_start: datetime, range_end: datetime, ...
) -> ProcessResult:

    # ── 1. VALIDATE AND JOIN INPUTS ──
    validated = validate_and_get_indicator_inputs(
        inputs, expected_count=6,
        input_names=["input_adx", "input_ema_fast", "input_ema_slow",
                     "input_atr", "input_close", "input_bb"]
    )
    frame = join_all_inputs_on_timestamp(validated)
    # Columns available: adx, plus_di, minus_di, ema (x2), atr, close, bandwidth

    # ── 2. GROUP 1: CONTINUOUS REGIME COMPONENTS ──

    frame = frame.with_columns(
        # trend_strength: raw ADX passthrough
        pl.col("adx").alias("trend_strength"),

        # direction_score: normalized DI dominance, bounded [-1, +1]
        pl.when(pl.col("plus_di") + pl.col("minus_di") > 0)
        .then((pl.col("plus_di") - pl.col("minus_di"))
              / (pl.col("plus_di") + pl.col("minus_di")))
        .otherwise(0.0)
        .alias("direction_score"),

        # ema_trend: relative EMA alignment
        pl.when(pl.col("input_ema_slow_ema") != 0)
        .then((pl.col("input_ema_fast_ema") - pl.col("input_ema_slow_ema"))
              / pl.col("input_ema_slow_ema"))
        .otherwise(0.0)
        .alias("ema_trend"),
    )

    # volatility_ratio: ATR/Close relative to its rolling mean
    frame = frame.with_columns(
        (pl.col("atr") / pl.col("close")).alias("_atr_close_ratio")
    ).with_columns(
        pl.col("_atr_close_ratio")
        .rolling_mean(window_size=config.vol_baseline_window,
                      min_periods=config.vol_baseline_window)
        .alias("_atr_close_baseline")
    ).with_columns(
        pl.when(pl.col("_atr_close_baseline") > 0)
        .then(pl.col("_atr_close_ratio") / pl.col("_atr_close_baseline"))
        .otherwise(1.0)
        .alias("volatility_ratio")
    )

    # bb_squeeze: bandwidth relative to its rolling mean
    frame = frame.with_columns(
        pl.col("bandwidth")
        .rolling_mean(window_size=config.vol_baseline_window,
                      min_periods=config.vol_baseline_window)
        .alias("_bb_baseline")
    ).with_columns(
        pl.when(pl.col("_bb_baseline") > 0)
        .then(pl.col("bandwidth") / pl.col("_bb_baseline"))
        .otherwise(1.0)
        .alias("bb_squeeze")
    )

    # ── 3. INTERNAL REGIME CLASSIFICATION ──
    # These intermediate columns are NOT output. They are used to compute Group 2.
    # Classifies each bar into discrete regime states.

    frame = frame.with_columns(
        # Trend: 0=RANGING, 1=NEUTRAL, 2=TRENDING
        pl.when(pl.col("adx") >= config.adx_strong_trend).then(pl.lit(2))
        .when(pl.col("adx") <= config.adx_weak_trend).then(pl.lit(0))
        .otherwise(pl.lit(1))
        .cast(pl.Int8).alias("_trend_regime"),

        # Direction: 0=BEARISH, 1=NEUTRAL, 2=BULLISH
        pl.when(
            (pl.col("plus_di") > pl.col("minus_di"))
            & (pl.col("input_ema_fast_ema") > pl.col("input_ema_slow_ema"))
        ).then(pl.lit(2))
        .when(
            (pl.col("minus_di") > pl.col("plus_di"))
            & (pl.col("input_ema_fast_ema") < pl.col("input_ema_slow_ema"))
        ).then(pl.lit(0))
        .otherwise(pl.lit(1))
        .cast(pl.Int8).alias("_direction_regime"),

        # Volatility: 0=LOW_VOL, 1=NORMAL_VOL, 2=HIGH_VOL
        pl.when(pl.col("volatility_ratio") > config.vol_high_multiplier).then(pl.lit(2))
        .when(pl.col("volatility_ratio") < config.vol_low_multiplier).then(pl.lit(0))
        .otherwise(pl.lit(1))
        .cast(pl.Int8).alias("_vol_regime"),
    )

    # Combined regime: unique integer 0-26 (3×3×3 = 27 possible states)
    frame = frame.with_columns(
        (pl.col("_trend_regime") * 9
         + pl.col("_direction_regime") * 3
         + pl.col("_vol_regime"))
        .cast(pl.Int16).alias("_combined_regime")
    )

    # ── 4. GROUP 2: PERSISTENCE AND TRANSITIONS ──
    #
    # The key technique: detect changes → create run groups via cum_sum
    # → count bars within each group. Fully vectorized, no Python loops.

    # Detect changes (0 = same as previous bar, 1 = changed)
    frame = frame.with_columns(
        (pl.col("_trend_regime") != pl.col("_trend_regime").shift(1))
            .cast(pl.Int32).alias("_trend_changed"),
        (pl.col("_direction_regime") != pl.col("_direction_regime").shift(1))
            .cast(pl.Int32).alias("_dir_changed"),
        (pl.col("_vol_regime") != pl.col("_vol_regime").shift(1))
            .cast(pl.Int32).alias("_vol_changed"),
        (pl.col("_combined_regime") != pl.col("_combined_regime").shift(1))
            .cast(pl.Int32).alias("_combined_changed"),
    )

    # bars_since_*_change:
    #   Step 1: cum_sum(change_flag) → group ID that increments at each change
    #   Step 2: cum_count() within each group → bars since last change
    #   Step 3: Log-scale: log1p(counter) / log1p(stability_window)
    #           This maps [0, ∞) to [0, ~1+] where 1.0 = stable for stability_window bars
    scale = config.stability_window

    for prefix, change_col, output_col in [
        ("trend", "_trend_changed", "bars_since_trend_change"),
        ("dir", "_dir_changed", "bars_since_direction_change"),
        ("vol", "_vol_changed", "bars_since_vol_change"),
    ]:
        run_group = f"_{prefix}_run_group"
        raw_counter = f"_{prefix}_raw_counter"
        frame = frame.with_columns(
            pl.col(change_col).cum_sum().alias(run_group)
        ).with_columns(
            pl.lit(1).cum_sum().over(run_group).alias(raw_counter)
        ).with_columns(
            (pl.col(raw_counter).cast(pl.Float64).log1p()
             / math.log1p(scale))
            .alias(output_col)
        )

    # regime_stability: fraction of last N bars without combined regime changes
    # = 1.0 - (number_of_changes_in_window / window_size)
    # Approximation of "fraction of window with same regime". Fully vectorized.
    frame = frame.with_columns(
        (1.0 - pl.col("_combined_changed")
         .rolling_sum(window_size=config.stability_window, min_periods=1)
         .cast(pl.Float64) / config.stability_window)
        .alias("regime_stability")
    )

    # transition_recency: exp(-lambda * bars_since_combined_change)
    frame = frame.with_columns(
        pl.col("_combined_changed").cum_sum().alias("_combined_run_group")
    ).with_columns(
        pl.lit(1).cum_sum().over("_combined_run_group").alias("_combined_raw_counter")
    ).with_columns(
        (-config.transition_decay_lambda
         * pl.col("_combined_raw_counter").cast(pl.Float64))
        .exp()
        .alias("transition_recency")
    )

    # ── 5. GROUP 3: META-REGIME ──

    # trend_momentum: rate of change of ADX
    frame = frame.with_columns(
        ((pl.col("adx") - pl.col("adx").shift(config.trend_momentum_window))
         / config.trend_momentum_window)
        .alias("trend_momentum")
    )

    # vol_of_vol: rolling std of volatility_ratio changes
    frame = frame.with_columns(
        pl.col("volatility_ratio").diff()
        .rolling_std(window_size=config.vol_of_vol_window,
                     min_periods=config.vol_of_vol_window)
        .alias("vol_of_vol")
    )

    # ── 6. CLEANUP ──
    # Drop internal columns (prefix _), keep 12 output columns + timestamp + inputs
    # Standard: trim_warm_up, clean_indicator_output, validate
    output_columns = ["timestamp", ...inputs...,
                      *MarketStateConfig.column_outputs_generated, SYNTHETIC_COL]
    # ... trim, clean, validate, return ProcessResult ...

docs.title: Key implementation notes
docs.text:
1. No Python loops. The bars_since_*_change counters use cum_sum to create run-length groups and cum_sum().over(group) to count within groups. These are pure Polars expressions executing in Rust.

  2. regime_stability approximation. The exact definition ("fraction of last N bars with same regime as current bar") would require rolling_map with a Python callback (~100x slower). We use the approximation 1 - change_frequency: 1.0 - rolling_sum(change_flag, window) / window. This measures "what fraction of the window had no regime change" — highly correlated with the exact definition, and fully vectorized.

  3. Log-scaling for counters. Raw bars_since_* can be thousands (a regime lasting 1 day at 5m = 288 bars, lasting 1 week = 2016). Log-scaling: log1p(counter) / log1p(stability_window) compresses to ~[0, 1+]. Value of 1.0 means "stable for at least stability_window bars." Values >1.0 for very long regimes — fine, the model can learn what these mean.

  4. Division safety. All divisions guard against zero denominators with pl.when(...).then(...).otherwise(...).

  5. First-bar handling. shift(1) produces null for the first row. != null evaluates to null, which .cast(pl.Int32) maps to 0. So the first bar counts as "no change" — correct behavior.
