docs.title: Crypto ML Trading Research for Thesis Validation
Context from your thesis framework and current Script 2 status
docs.text:
Your thesis framework tests whether models can extract signal from real crypto market data by comparing trainable models against structured random baselines, while holding the execution engine and trading constraints constant. This design matches what the literature treats as the core problem in empirical trading research: separating “forecast skill” from “backtest luck,” and separating “model signal” from “strategy/execution tuning.” citeturn4search0turn4search2turn4search16

Within your plan, Script 2 uses (a) ATR-based directional labels with a fixed horizon and thresholds to produce a three-class problem (NONE/LONG/SHORT), (b) a progressive “model complexity ladder” (rule-based → logistic → MLP → TCN → transformer), and (c) a three-stage score that mixes train, validation, and backtest outputs into a single Optuna objective. That scaffold aligns with a recurring theme in crypto ML papers: predictive metrics alone (accuracy, MAE/RMSE) do not establish trading edge unless the work also reports a trading simulation that includes costs and a benchmark comparison. citeturn1search0turn1search20turn0search11

Your Tier A “regime audit” notes two points that map directly to the external literature: 1) edge is regime-conditional and can vanish when train/test conditions shift, and 2) regime labels that are too sparse are hard to use for either training control or evaluation slicing.

Those match how regime-switching crypto papers model market states (often via HMMs and Markov-switching volatility models) and how they report forecast differences by regime. citeturn1search8turn1search1turn1search18

In your Tier A audit, the best unified results show small positive PnL only in isolated fold/model cases, alongside repeated collapse patterns for some sequence models (one-sided prediction). That pattern is consistent with two properties that recur in crypto datasets: (a) class imbalance in sparse-entry labeling schemes and (b) “near-efficiency” in the weak-form sense, which leaves limited signal at many horizons unless the trade trigger is selective and the feature set is richer than price-only indicators. citeturn0search12turn1search20turn2search2

docs.title: What “exploitable patterns exist” means in crypto papers, and when it holds
docs.text:
A substantial slice of the literature frames crypto as weak-form close to efficient at many horizons, while still reporting pockets of predictability. One example is a 2023 Scientific Reports study that concludes the Bitcoin market is “near-efficient” under its modeling and tests, which implies limited stable forecast structure in raw returns. citeturn0search12

At the same time, multiple strands report strategy-level profits under specific conditions, often tied to trend regimes, volatility states, market stress, or microstructure effects:

Technical rule profitability appears most often in trend-dominant environments. A Finance Research Letters paper (2019) reports that “trading range breakout” has forecasting power and outperforms buy-and-hold by Sharpe ratio, with sub-period results pointing to better outcomes in trending markets. citeturn2search0 A broader crypto technical-trading study (Annals of Operations Research, published 2019; issue date 2021) tests a large universe of technical rules and reports predictability/profitability in several crypto markets, while also stating that Bitcoin predictability does not persist in its out-of-sample segment. citeturn2search5

Momentum is one of the most cited cross-asset anomalies in crypto, but its tradability depends on trading costs, liquidation rules, and portfolio construction. A 2023 SSRN paper on cross-sectional momentum reports a concrete configuration (30-day formation, 7-day holding) and finds excess returns relative to a Bitcoin benchmark for long-only momentum variants in its sample. citeturn2search1 A 2024 SSRN study argues that many crypto momentum portfolios that look statistically profitable become economically weak once one accounts for trading frictions and portfolio “realism,” and it distinguishes time-series momentum as more durable than cross-sectional momentum. citeturn2search2 Related evidence on time-series momentum in crypto also exists in journal form. citeturn2search7turn2search9

On-chain and network-derived indicators appear in multiple “predict + trade” pipelines. A 2023 Chaos, Solitons & Fractals paper proposes blockchain-network indicators inspired by mining activity and reports that variables such as hash rate, difficulty, and cost per transaction can support trading or price-direction prediction in its experiments. citeturn1search16 A 2025 Engineering Applications of Artificial Intelligence paper combines price, on-chain metrics, and technical indicators and evaluates models for both direction and magnitude, which maps to the “classification labels vs numeric returns” split you called out. citeturn1search3

Crypto derivatives introduce “carry-like” and basis effects that can be traded rule-wise and can also be used as ML features. A 2022 arXiv preprint on perpetual futures derives no-arbitrage prices/bounds, documents deviations, and reports that a simple trading strategy can yield high Sharpe ratios even under high costs on a major exchange in its empirical work. citeturn3search1 A 2026 journal article focused on funding rates builds a large high-frequency panel across exchanges and concludes that large apparent arbitrage spreads do not translate into consistent post-cost profits unless spreads persist long enough, with forced exits occurring in a large share of opportunities. citeturn3search0 A 2025 open-access journal pre-proof analyzes risk/return of funding-rate arbitrage across centralized and decentralized venues. citeturn3search3

This mix supports a working thesis refinement: crypto alpha in academic and practitioner studies is more often conditional (regime, frictions, venue structure, feature availability) than unconditional. citeturn1search8turn2search2turn3search0

docs.title: Model classes used in crypto trading research, with concrete design choices
Predict-then-trade supervised systems
docs.text:
A common pattern in “ML crypto trading” papers is to predict either (a) price direction as a classifier or (b) price/return as a regressor, then convert predictions into trades by thresholding or ranking.

A 2025 open-access study in Discover Artificial Intelligence compares Gradient Boosting, XGBoost, SVR, and LSTM on 30 cryptocurrencies, evaluating forecast accuracy using MAE/RMSE/R² and generating trading signals via moving-average logic and model outputs. This is representative of a large group of papers that optimize forecast metrics but provide limited evidence that forecast metrics translate into net trading PnL under costs. citeturn1search0

In contrast, papers that explicitly focus on “forecasting and trading” often incorporate regime shifts as part of the evaluation design. A 2021 Financial Innovation paper on crypto predictability uses distinct validation/test periods aligned to different market conditions (including bear conditions) to test whether models remain useful when the market direction changes between validation and test. This directly addresses your “train on one regime, validate on another” question, even if the paper’s regime definition is period-based rather than latent-state-based. citeturn1search20

docs.title: Deep learning systems that mix direction and magnitude outputs
docs.text:
Several papers explicitly separate the “direction vs magnitude” channels. A 2025 Engineering Applications of Artificial Intelligence paper reports experiments that predict both Bitcoin direction and magnitude using price, on-chain, and technical indicator features, evaluating multiple ML/DL model families (SVM, Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, LSTM, CNN-LSTM, GRU, TCN, and other time-series deep models). This is directly relevant to your design choice (label prediction) versus regression-based outcomes. citeturn1search3

A 2025 paper on Bitcoin direction prediction with on-chain data reports a feature pipeline that evaluates a large number of on-chain metrics, applies feature selection/dimensionality reduction, and then compares Random Forest, TCN, and CNN-LSTM in the direction task. citeturn1search21

docs.title: Reinforcement learning and portfolio-weight policies
docs.text:
RL papers in crypto often shift the target from “predict a label” to “output an action or portfolio weight,” with reward functions defined on portfolio growth, transaction costs, and drawdown/risk. This corresponds to your “who predicts labels vs who predicts results/numbers” split.

A widely cited crypto portfolio RL framework (arXiv 2017) reports backtests on a crypto market with a 30-minute trading period, includes commission in backtesting, and reports multi-fold returns over a short horizon in its experiments; it also instantiates the framework with CNN/RNN/LSTM variants. citeturn0search3

More recent portfolio RL work includes deep Q-learning frameworks for cryptocurrency portfolios. citeturn0search1 Another line uses RL for markets with a changing number of tradable assets and reports outperformance versus baselines on a crypto market dataset, while also modeling transaction costs within the portfolio rebalancing logic. citeturn0search0

A DRL trading survey highlights recurring issues: reward shaping, market frictions, non-stationarity, and the gap between reported backtests and deployable systems. citeturn0search11

docs.title: Rule-based strategies used as baselines and as standalone research objects
docs.text:
Rule-based strategies remain in the academic record as both baselines and as standalone profit claims.

A Bitcoin technical-rule paper (Finance Research Letters, 2019) finds that a trading range breakout rule is the key contributor among the set of tested trend-following indicators, and that results concentrate in trending segments. citeturn2search0

A 2024 International Review of Economics & Finance paper tests a large universe of “simple technical trading rules” across daily and intraday variants, applies multiple-hypothesis logic to select rules after transaction costs, and reports that rule portfolios can beat buy-and-hold out-of-sample on a risk/return basis. citeturn2search10

A 2020 Finance Research Letters paper examines moving-average strategies across privacy-focused coins and reports that excess returns over buy-and-hold appear for Dash in its sample, while aggregate performance across the set does not deliver excess returns beyond buy-and-hold. That result matters for thesis framing: “a rule exists that worked on one asset” is not equivalent to “a class of rules works cross-sectionally.” citeturn2search3

docs.title: Features used in crypto ML trading, and how they map to your tiers
docs.text:
Across the papers retrieved, feature sets cluster into five families that can be mapped onto a Tier A→E progression:

Price and OHLCV transforms. Many comparative studies rely on OHLCV-derived features plus returns/volatility transforms, and they evaluate models on price forecasting metrics. citeturn1search0 This tends to align with your minimal tier only when the label mechanism is selective; otherwise, the model learns the sample mean behavior and produces “no-trade” or directional collapse patterns under imbalance.

Technical indicators. Many crypto prediction and trading papers incorporate indicator families similar to your Tier B set (EMA/MACD/Bollinger/ADX/ATR/Stochastic/volume indicators), often with feature selection or dimensionality reduction. citeturn1search3turn1search5 In the literature, these features often serve as the primary “structured signal extraction” layer in the absence of microstructure or on-chain data.

On-chain/network metrics. The on-chain feature family includes mining/network variables (hash rate, difficulty), transaction-based variables, and value/realized/unrealized metric families. Studies that incorporate these features treat them as an orthogonal signal source relative to technical indicators. citeturn1search16turn1search3turn1search21

Order book and microstructure features. Order book depth, spread, and imbalance are treated as primary variables for market quality and short-horizon price pressure. citeturn1search2turn1search9 In ML terms, this feature family tends to support models that operate at shorter horizons than your 1-hour label horizon, unless one aggregates order book snapshots into lower-frequency summaries.

Derivatives features. Funding rates, futures-spot basis, and related derivatives variables are both (a) standalone tradable signals in “carry/basis” strategies and (b) candidate predictive features for ML models that aim to forecast risk-on/risk-off shifts, liquidations, or crowding. citeturn3search1turn3search0turn3search3

Your Tier design (A minimal → B indicators → later tiers with more blocks, multi-timeframe, multi-symbol) matches how papers’ feature sets scale from “price only” to “macrostructure + microstructure + cross-asset context.” At the same time, your Tier A audit conclusion (“close + RSI reveals learnability differences but does not deliver consistent net profits”) matches the empirical theme that price-only indicators often do not carry sufficient stable edge once costs and regime shifts are accounted for. citeturn1search20turn2search2turn0search12

docs.title: How models are compared in the literature and what “validation” means for trading
Predictive validation versus trading validation
docs.text:
Many crypto ML papers report training and validation on forecast metrics (MAE/RMSE/R² for regression; accuracy/F1 for classification). citeturn1search0turn1search3 This answers “does the model fit targets,” but it does not answer “does the model generate net returns under a trading rule,” because the mapping from predictions to trades introduces thresholds, position sizing, and cost sensitivity.

Your three-stage score addresses this by explicitly adding a trading simulation stage, which is closer to how trading RL papers define “loss” as a reward function on portfolio outcomes rather than as cross-entropy/MSE. citeturn0search3turn0search11

docs.title: Core backtest metrics, with formulas used in papers and in industry practice
docs.text:
Many papers report return, Sharpe ratio, win rate, and drawdown as the headline outputs of backtesting, often combined with a buy-and-hold benchmark comparison. citeturn2search0turn2search10turn3search1

Common definitions (notation in discrete time, per-period returns (r_t)):

Total return over a backtest horizon: [ R_{\text{tot}} = \prod_{t=1}^{T}(1+r_t) - 1 ]

Sharpe ratio (often with (r_f \approx 0) at short crypto horizons): [ \text{Sharpe} = \frac{\mathbb{E}[r_t - r_f]}{\sqrt{\text{Var}(r_t - r_f)}} ] Because strategy returns are often non-normal and selection-biased when many trials are run, “headline Sharpe” can be inflated without correction. This is one motivation for using Probabilistic Sharpe and the Deflated Sharpe Ratio (DSR) when reporting “best trial” results from large hyperparameter searches. citeturn4search0turn4search7

Maximum drawdown: [ \text{MDD} = \max_{t \in [1,T]} \left(\frac{\max_{u \le t} \text{Equity}u - \text{Equity}_t}{\max{u \le t} \text{Equity}_u}\right) ]

Win rate: [ \text{WinRate} = \frac{#{ \text{trades with PnL} > 0}}{#{\text{trades}}} ]

“Alpha” relative to buy-and-hold over the same period is often defined as: [ \alpha = R_{\text{strategy}} - R_{\text{buyhold}} ] In crypto, this benchmark can be structurally hard to beat for selective strategies that keep capital idle by design; papers that rely only on buy-and-hold comparisons can therefore reject strategies that may still be economically usable under risk constraints or leverage limits. Your plan separates “fair baselines under the same execution constraints” from “buy-and-hold,” which is aligned with how data-snooping and backtest-overfitting studies recommend structuring comparisons. citeturn4search16turn4search2turn4search0

docs.title: Baselines and benchmarks used in crypto papers
docs.text:
The retrieved literature uses several benchmark layers: Buy-and-hold appears as the dominant benchmark in technical-rule papers and many ML trading papers. citeturn2search0turn2search10turn1search4

Rule ensembles and large rule universes are used with multiple-hypothesis control to reduce data-mining bias in technical analysis results. citeturn2search10turn2search5turn4search16

Random-walk or naive forecasts are used as baselines in forecast comparison settings, and “reality check / superior predictive ability” style tests appear in the statistical literature as a response to selection bias. citeturn4search16turn4search10turn4search0

Your random baselines (label-prior matched, entry-policy matched, and Markov structure matched) are a direct analog of this logic, but specialized to the “sparse-entry, three-class” case.

docs.title: Market regimes: how papers define them, and how they are used in training and validation
What conditions papers associate with “edge exists but is scarce”
docs.text:
Across the sources retrieved, “edge exists but is scarce” is expressed in multiple ways: Regime-switching models show that parameter sets and forecast structure differ across latent volatility states. citeturn1search8turn1search1

Technical rule profitability concentrates in trending segments, with outperformance claims often tied to trend measures or breakout logic. citeturn2search0turn2search10

Momentum results vary across samples and can become economically weak under costs and realistic rebalancing assumptions. citeturn2search2turn2search9

docs.title: How regimes are identified
docs.text:
Three regime identification patterns appear in the retrieved set: Latent-state models (HMM / Markov-switching). A crypto asset-class regime paper applies HMM logic and reports that forecast performance improves once one models regime commonalities and uses a smaller regime set (two common regimes across assets in that study). citeturn1search8

Volatility regime models under macro uncertainty. A 2026 paper uses Markov-switching volatility modeling and hidden Markov copula methods to capture regime-dependent behavior and tail dependence around macro uncertainty. citeturn1search1

Period-based regime splits. The 2021 Financial Innovation paper validates models in one market condition and tests them in another (including bear conditions), which is a period-based proxy for regimes. citeturn1search20

docs.title: How regimes are “taught” to models
docs.text:
The typical mechanisms are: Regime as a feature: include regime probabilities or state labels as input features to a single model.

Regime-conditioned training: train separate models per regime, then route by inferred state (mixture-of-experts logic). This approach is often motivated by the observation that a single global model averages incompatible dynamics across states. citeturn1search8turn1search18

Regime-conditioned evaluation: report model performance sliced by regime, even if the model is not regime-aware, to show where the edge concentrates and where it fails. citeturn2search0turn1search20

In your regime audit, the “dominant combined regime” shares only a small fraction of bars in validation per fold, which implies that any regime-aware evaluation needs to consider distributions over regimes, not a single most-common label. That aligns with the literature’s preference for smaller regime taxonomies when regimes are used as conditioning variables. citeturn1search8turn1search18

docs.title: Synthesis for your next research iteration and profitable-model alignment
docs.text:
Your request has two outputs: (a) thesis validation and (b) economically usable models. The literature indicates these objectives overlap but do not coincide unless the evaluation protocol treats “search bias” and “market non-stationarity” as first-class constraints. citeturn4search0turn4search2turn0search11

The following synthesis maps the retrieved evidence into concrete design implications for your Tier B+ roadmap:

Your tier progression matches how information content accumulates. Price-only and small-indicator sets often show weak out-of-sample edge under costs, while feature expansion to include on-chain or derivatives variables is one of the main levers that papers use to move from “forecasting” to “trading.” citeturn1search3turn1search16turn3search1

A profitable “rule-based” layer exists in the literature, but it is conditional. Breakout/trend-following rules appear repeatedly as the profitable subset in Bitcoin technical-rule studies, but papers also report that out-of-sample results can degrade for Bitcoin even when other coins still show predictability. This supports keeping rule-based as both a baseline and a candidate component, while treating “BTC-only profit” as unstable unless validated in multiple folds/regimes. citeturn2search0turn2search5turn2search10

Regime handling is part of the thesis, not an extension. Empirical regime papers for crypto provide two practical constraints: (1) limit the regime taxonomy size early, and (2) evaluate cross-regime transfer explicitly. This matches your internal conclusion that a 27-state combined label is sparse for early operational use and suggests re-running Tier B analysis under smaller regime partitions (for example, 2-state or 3-state volatility/trend partitions) before attempting mixture-of-experts. citeturn1search8turn1search1turn1search18

Derivatives features can serve as both (a) direct rule-based strategies and (b) regime proxies. Funding-rate and perpetual-futures work documents persistent deviations and a non-trivial cost/reversal structure, which implies that naive “carry” backtests can report profits that do not survive realistic exit mechanics. If used as features, funding/basis variables can instead act as regime indicators for crowding/leverage pressure, which can be tested as part of your Tier C/D feature expansion. citeturn3search0turn3search1turn3search3

Profit claims require bias control once you run large Optuna searches. The Deflated Sharpe Ratio and probability-of-overfitting frameworks exist to correct the “try many configurations, report the best” problem. Because your pipeline is explicitly an optimization engine across many trials, reporting “best trial Sharpe” without a deflation/overfitting correction risks overstating evidence. This can be integrated directly into thesis reporting as an additional layer on top of Mann–Whitney style distribution comparisons. citeturn4search0turn4search2turn4search16turn4search10

Label prediction versus numeric prediction: both appear, but they imply different failure modes. Direction classification is common when the trading system is thresholded and selective; magnitude regression is common when the target is portfolio allocation or when the work tries to predict returns directly. Papers that do both (direction + magnitude) provide a template for extending your system beyond labels without abandoning your current label-based design: the direction head can drive entry, while magnitude (or uncertainty) can drive position sizing, but only if the sizing rule is evaluated under the same constraints and costs. citeturn1search3turn0search3turn0search1

Limitations and source handling note: project-specific conclusions in this report that reference your plan/audit/log files are derived from the GitHub repository documents you pointed to (paths under docs/ and scripts/thesis/). Those internal documents are not accessible to the web citation system in this environment, so citations are provided for external claims and for the statistical/academic foundations, not for your repository text.

docs.title: Crypto Quant ML Research & Experiment Roadmap
1. Purpose of This Document
docs.text:
This document defines the research and implementation roadmap for the Rainbow Trader thesis project.

It integrates:

  • the current thesis experiment framework
  • the Script2 experiment stage
  • regime research and the MARKET_STATE feature design
  • external research in crypto quantitative trading
  • the detailed feature catalog
  • the dataset and API landscape
  • the phased Script3 research plan

The goal is to transform the thesis from pure ML experimentation into a complete quantitative trading research pipeline.

Main objectives:

  1. validate the thesis hypothesis
  2. identify feature families that contain predictive signal
  3. determine which signals translate into profitable strategies
  4. extend Script2 experiments with additional information sources
  5. design Script3 experiments based on research literature

docs.title: 2. Current Status of the Thesis
docs.text:
Script2 currently evaluates models using price‑based market data.

Feature groups currently used:

• OHLCV price data • technical indicators • derived volatility and momentum signals

Script2 currently tests:

price-based features technical indicators sparse trading labels multiple model classes

The model predicts trading labels.

Example labels:

LONG SHORT NONE

Evaluation pipeline:

1️⃣ Training metrics 2️⃣ Validation metrics 3️⃣ Backtesting simulation

Final model comparison is based primarily on trading results, not only prediction accuracy.

This structure follows the methodology used in academic trading research where:

forecast metrics alone are insufficient without trading evaluation.

Final score combines these stages.

docs.title: 3. Core Hypothesis of the Thesis
docs.text:
Financial markets may appear close to efficient when using raw price returns.

However predictive signal may emerge when combining multiple sources of information.

The thesis tests whether models can extract signal from:

• price structure • blockchain activity • derivatives market positioning • sentiment and attention cycles • market regime dynamics

The objective is to determine whether these signals can be transformed into economically profitable trading strategies.

docs.title: 4. Feature Families Used in Crypto Trading Research
docs.text:
Research in crypto ML consistently uses several groups of information.

These families represent different layers of market behavior.

docs.title: 4.1 Price Features
docs.text:
Traditional OHLCV derived signals.

Examples:

returns log returns rolling returns price momentum rolling volatility high‑low range close‑open spread

Technical indicators frequently used in papers:

EMA MACD RSI ADX ATR Bollinger Bands Stochastic Oscillator VWAP

These features represent direct price dynamics and form the baseline in most studies.

Expanded normalized feature catalog:

docs.title: Price Derived
docs.text: returns log_returns rolling_returns price_momentum rolling_volatility realized_volatility high_low_range close_open_range volume_weighted_return volume_delta

docs.title: Trend Indicators
docs.text: ema_fast ema_slow ema_ratio ema_slope sma_ratio macd macd_signal macd_hist adx trend_strength

docs.title: Momentum Indicators
docs.text: rsi stochastic_k stochastic_d momentum_n roc williams_r

docs.title: Volatility Indicators
docs.text: atr bollinger_width bollinger_position volatility_ratio volatility_zscore

docs.title: Volume Indicators
docs.text: obv vwap_distance volume_spike volume_mean_ratio volume_std_ratio

docs.title: 4.2 On‑Chain Blockchain Metrics
docs.text:
These metrics describe the behavior of holders and network activity.

Common signals used in research:

Active addresses New addresses Transaction count Transaction volume

Holder structure metrics:

Long‑term holder supply Short‑term holder supply

Exchange flow metrics:

Exchange inflow Exchange outflow Exchange netflow

Whale activity:

Whale transaction count Whale balance

Value metrics:

Realized cap Market cap MVRV ratio

Profitability metrics:

SOPR NUPL

Many crypto ML studies rely heavily on Glassnode datasets for these metrics.

Expanded normalized feature catalog:

active_addresses new_addresses transaction_count transaction_volume exchange_inflow exchange_outflow exchange_netflow whale_tx_count whale_balance realized_cap mvrv sopr nupl long_term_holder_supply short_term_holder_supply

docs.title: 4.3 Derivatives Market Structure
docs.text:
Crypto derivatives markets reveal leverage positioning and trader sentiment.

Key signals:

Open interest Funding rate Long‑short ratio Liquidation volume

Perpetual futures premium

premium = perpetual_price − spot_price

Futures basis

basis = futures_price − spot_price

Options metrics:

Implied volatility Volatility skew Gamma exposure

Research often finds that derivatives positioning improves predictive performance compared to price‑only models.

Expanded normalized feature catalog:

open_interest oi_change funding_rate funding_rate_change long_short_ratio liquidation_volume perpetual_premium futures_basis

docs.title: 4.4 Sentiment and Attention Data
docs.text:
These signals measure market narrative and crowd behavior.

Typical sentiment features:

Twitter sentiment score Tweet volume

Reddit sentiment Reddit activity

News sentiment scores

Google Trends search volume

GitHub development activity

These signals capture information diffusion and attention cycles.

Expanded normalized feature catalog:

twitter_sentiment_score twitter_volume reddit_sentiment reddit_activity news_sentiment google_trends_score github_activity

docs.title: 4.5 Market Microstructure
docs.text:
These features represent the order book environment.

Examples:

Bid‑ask spread Order book imbalance Depth ratio

These features are usually used in high‑frequency strategies, but can still provide signal during volatility events.

Expanded normalized feature catalog:

bid_ask_spread orderbook_imbalance depth_ratio top_of_book_volume microprice

docs.title: 5. Data Sources for These Features
docs.text: Common providers used in research.

docs.text:
Binance API https://api.binance.com

Bybit API https://api.bybit.com

CryptoCompare API

Kaiko

Santiment https://santiment.net

CoinMetrics

IntoTheBlock

docs.title: 5.3 Derivatives Data
docs.text:

Kaiko

Binance API

Glassnode derivatives metrics

Laevitas options analytics

docs.title: 5.4 Sentiment Data
docs.text:
Santiment

LunarCrush API

TheTie

The Tie API

Google Trends API

Google Trends

GDELT news database

Santiment social metrics

Twitter API

Reddit API

docs.title: 5.5 GitHub Activity Data
docs.text:
GitHub development activity

GitHub REST API

Used to measure developer activity in crypto projects.

docs.title: 6. Feature Transformations Used in Research
docs.text:
Raw values are rarely used directly.

Common transformations:

Z‑score normalization

z = (x − mean) / std

Rolling change

delta = x_t − x_t‑1

Moving averages

rolling_mean(x)

Ratio transformations

x / rolling_mean(x)

These transformations allow models to learn deviations from typical behavior.

docs.title: 7. Market Regimes
docs.text:
Many studies report that model performance varies strongly across regimes.

Predictive signal often appears during specific conditions.

Examples:

strong trending markets volatility spikes liquidation cascades sentiment shocks

docs.title: 7.1 Regime Identification Methods
docs.text: Three main approaches appear in the literature.

docs.title: Volatility regimes
docs.text: Using realized volatility or ATR to separate high and low volatility periods.

docs.title: Hidden Markov Models
docs.text: Latent state models that infer hidden market regimes.

docs.title: Clustering
docs.text: K‑means or similar clustering methods applied to return distributions.

docs.title: 7.2 Regime Integration Into Models
docs.text:
Research commonly uses three approaches.

Regime as features

Models receive regime indicators as input variables.

Separate models per regime

Example:

model_bull model_bear

Regime‑conditioned evaluation

Trading performance evaluated separately for each regime.

docs.title: 8. MARKET_STATE Feature Block
docs.text:
The thesis proposes a MARKET_STATE feature block that represents regime information as continuous signals.

Continuous regime features:

trend_strength trend_direction trend_direction_score ema_trend volatility_ratio bb_squeeze

Persistence metrics:

bars_since_trend_change bars_since_direction_change bars_since_vol_change bars_since_regime_change bars_since_volatility_shift trend_duration

Regime stability metrics:

volatility_regime market_regime_cluster regime_stability transition_recency

Meta indicators:

trend_momentum volatility_of_volatility

These signals allow the model to learn regime dynamics instead of relying on discrete labels.

Goal:

Allow the model to condition predictions on market environment.

docs.title: 9. Model Classes Used in Crypto Trading Research
docs.text: Several model families appear repeatedly in academic work.

docs.title: Statistical Models
docs.text: ARIMA GARCH

docs.title: Machine Learning Models
docs.text: Random Forest XGBoost Support Vector Machines

docs.title: Deep Learning Models
docs.text: LSTM GRU Temporal Convolution Networks Transformers

docs.title: Reinforcement Learning
docs.text:
portfolio allocation agents

XGBoost frequently appears as a strong baseline in many studies.

docs.title: 10. Prediction Targets
docs.text: Two main approaches appear in research.

docs.title: Classification
docs.text:
Predict trading labels.

LONG SHORT NONE

This approach matches the current thesis design.

Advantages:

stable training direct trade filtering

docs.title: Regression
docs.text:
Predict numeric outcomes such as:

future return future price

These models often integrate position sizing directly.

docs.title: 11. Backtesting Metrics Used in Papers
docs.text:
Trading evaluation metrics include:

Total return Sharpe ratio Sortino ratio Max drawdown Calmar ratio Win rate Profit factor Alpha relative to buy‑and‑hold

These metrics measure economic profitability rather than prediction accuracy.

docs.title: 12. Baselines Used in Research
docs.text:
Typical benchmarks include:

Buy‑and‑hold Random walk forecasts Technical trading rules Rule ensembles

The thesis includes structured random baselines to reduce backtest bias.

docs.title: 13. Evidence of Profitable Rule‑Based Strategies
docs.text:
Several rule families repeatedly appear in profitable research strategies.

Trend following

Breakout strategies

Momentum trading

Moving average crossovers

Funding‑rate arbitrage

Futures basis trading

Profitability depends heavily on market conditions and transaction costs.

docs.title: 14. Experimental Plan After Script2
docs.text: The next stage expands the feature space gradually.

docs.title: 14.1 Experiment 1 — Baseline
docs.text:
Features:

price technical indicators

Goal:

establish baseline performance.

docs.title: 14.2 Script3 Phase 1 — Market State Features
docs.text:
Add regime descriptors.

trend_strength trend_direction ema_trend volatility_ratio bb_squeeze

Persistence metrics:

bars_since_trend_change bars_since_vol_change bars_since_regime_change

Goal:

allow the model to condition predictions on market environment.

docs.title: 14.3 Experiment 2 — Price + Derivatives
docs.text:
Add:

open interest funding rate liquidation data

Purpose:

test leverage positioning signals.

Hypothesis:

derivatives positioning contains predictive signal.

docs.title: 14.4 Experiment 3 — Price + On‑Chain
docs.text:
Add:

MVRV SOPR exchange flows

Purpose:

test holder behavior signals.

Hypothesis:

blockchain activity reflects accumulation or distribution.

docs.title: 14.5 Experiment 4 — Price + Sentiment
docs.text:
Add:

Twitter sentiment Reddit sentiment Google Trends

Purpose:

test attention cycles.

Hypothesis:

crowd attention predicts volatility bursts.

docs.title: 14.6 Experiment 5 — Full Feature Set
docs.text:
Combine:

price technical on‑chain derivatives sentiment market_state

Goal:

determine whether multi‑source information improves profitability.

Train models with full information set.

docs.title: 15. Evaluation Framework
docs.text:
Each experiment reports three layers of results.

Prediction metrics:

accuracy precision recall F1 score

Validation metrics:

validation loss

Trading metrics:

PnL Sharpe ratio drawdown win rate profit factor

Final comparison prioritizes trading performance.

docs.title: 16. Overfitting Risk
docs.text:
Hyperparameter search introduces selection bias.

Mitigation methods:

Deflated Sharpe Ratio

Probability of Backtest Overfitting

Cross‑fold performance distribution analysis

These techniques strengthen thesis credibility.

docs.title: 17. Key Insight from Literature
docs.text:
Research suggests that:

crypto markets are close to efficient when using price data alone.

However profitable strategies appear when:

multiple information sources are combined market regimes are modeled transaction costs are included.

docs.title: 18. Next Implementation Steps
docs.text:
Immediate tasks after Script2.

  1. implement MARKET_STATE feature block
  2. integrate derivatives signals
  3. integrate on‑chain metrics
  4. integrate sentiment features
  5. run multi‑feature experiments
  6. analyze profitability across regimes

docs.title: 19. Expected Thesis Contributions
docs.text:
If successful, the thesis will demonstrate:

• whether machine learning models can extract signal from crypto markets • which feature families contain predictive power • whether trading profitability survives realistic backtesting constraints

Source: progress_model_profile.md from run 20260319_035820 (Tier A, 2 features only)

Model Avg Trainable Params Avg Work VRAM GB Avg Reserve High GB Max Work VRAM GB logistic ~280 ~0.000 ~0.000 0.000 mlp ~43,000 0.004 0.006 0.005 tcn ~66,000 0.008 0.012 0.010 vit ~2,147,000 0.506 0.659 0.694 axial_vit ~1,169,000 0.963 1.204 2.298

Key observation: AxialViT with only 2 features already used up to 2.87 GB reserved VRAM (best trial, F2). With Tier B's full standard feature set (~25+ features), axial_vit VRAM will scale significantly due to feature-dimension attention O(F²).

docs.title: 4. Theoretical Model Size Estimates for Remaining Tier B Models
4.1 Memory Estimation Methodology
docs.text:
From factory.py::_build_model_memory_estimate:

  • Model state = parameter bytes + buffer bytes
  • Training param-related = params + buffers + gradients + optimizer (AdamW: 2× param bytes for m, v)
  • Total ≈ model state × 4 (params + grad + m + v) for trainable params

From factory.py::_build_training_memory_estimate:

  • Working VRAM = training param bytes + input batch bytes + training activation bytes
  • Activation = layer_count × (attention_elements + non_attention_elements) × dtype_bytes × 2 (backward)
  • Reserved low = working × 1.10
  • Reserved high = working × 1.25 (no cudagraphs) or × 1.45 (with cudagraphs)

docs.title: 4.2 TCN — Estimated Tier B Memory
docs.text:
Worst-case TCN config: channels=128, kernel_size=7, num_layers=4

  • Input dim: ~25 features (Tier B standard set)
  • Seq len: 60 (max window)
  • Batch: 512 (max)

Approximate parameter count: ~200K–500K (TCN is lightweight)

Based on Tier A real data (tcn avg: 66K params, 0.008 GB work VRAM with 2 features), Tier B TCN with ~25 features will have more parameters but TCN does NOT have attention, so scaling is linear in features/channels.

Estimated Tier B TCN:

  • Trainable params: 100K–500K
  • Work VRAM: 0.02–0.10 GB
  • Reserve VRAM high: 0.03–0.15 GB

Verdict: TCN fits trivially on any Colab GPU.

docs.title: 4.3 ViT — Estimated Tier B Memory
docs.text:
Worst-case ViT config: hidden_dim=264, num_heads=16, num_layers=6, dim_feedforward=1024, patch_size=1

  • Input dim: ~25 features
  • Seq len: 60
  • Batch: 512

Key: attention is O(T²) where T = temporal tokens. With patch_size=1, T=60.

From Tier A real data (vit avg: 2.1M params, 0.506 GB work VRAM with 2 features, seq_len=40), Tier B increases input_dim from 2→25 and seq_len from 20-40→45-60.

ViT attention complexity: batch × heads × T × T (does NOT scale with input_dim directly, only through projection layers).

Estimated Tier B ViT:

  • Trainable params: 2M–4M (larger hidden dims, more layers)
  • Work VRAM: 0.5–1.5 GB
  • Reserve VRAM high: 0.6–2.0 GB

Verdict: ViT fits comfortably on T4 (15 GB) and all paid GPUs.

docs.title: 4.4 AxialViT — Estimated Tier B Memory
docs.text:
Worst-case AxialViT config: hidden_dim=256, time_num_heads=8, feature_num_heads=8, num_layers=6, dim_feedforward=512, patch_size=1

  • Input dim: ~25 features
  • Seq len: 60
  • Batch: 512

Key: feature-axis attention is O(F²) where F = input_dim. With 25 features: 25²=625 attention elements per batch×time position. Additionally, time-axis attention is O(T²) with T up to 60 tokens.

From factory.py:

time_attention = batch × input_dim × time_heads × T × T
feature_attention = batch × T × feature_heads × F × F

With batch=512, T=60, F=25, time_heads=8, feature_heads=8:

  • time_attention = 512 × 25 × 8 × 60 × 60 = 368,640,000 elements/layer
  • feature_attention = 512 × 60 × 8 × 25 × 25 = 153,600,000 elements/layer
  • Total attention per layer = 522,240,000 elements
  • × 6 layers × 2 bytes (bf16) × 2 (backward) = ~12.5 GB just for attention activations

From Tier A real data (axial_vit with 2 features, F2 best: 2.87 GB reserve). With 25 features, feature attention scales quadratically: (25/2)² ≈ 156× increase in feature attention. However, total VRAM is NOT purely attention — there is also a significant non-attention component.

Estimated Tier B AxialViT (worst case):

  • Trainable params: 1M–3M
  • Work VRAM: 5–15 GB (driven by activation memory, not param count)
  • Reserve VRAM high: 6–20 GB

Verdict: AxialViT worst-case configs MAY NOT fit on T4 (15 GB). Smaller configs (hidden_dim=64, num_layers=2, batch_size=128) will fit. Larger configs need L4/A100.

docs.title: 4.5 Summary — VRAM Estimates by Model
docs.text:
Model Param Range Work VRAM GB Reserve High GB T4 (15 GB) L4 (22.5 GB) A100 40 GB logistic 10K–12K <0.01 <0.01 ✅ ✅ ✅ mlp 100K–700K 0.02–0.06 0.03–0.08 ✅ ✅ ✅ mlp_ex 1.5M–2.5M 0.05–0.15 0.07–0.20 ✅ ✅ ✅ tcn 100K–500K 0.02–0.10 0.03–0.15 ✅ ✅ ✅ vit 2M–4M 0.5–1.5 0.6–2.0 ✅ ✅ ✅ axial_vit 1M–3M 5–15 6–20 ⚠️ ⚠️ ✅

Legend: ✅ = fits, ⚠️ = depends on hyperparameter config (small configs fit, large may not)

docs.title: 5.1 Available GPUs (as of March 2026)
docs.text:
Source: McCormick ML Colab GPUs, Colab FAQ

GPU Architecture VRAM bfloat16 Availability Tesla T4 Turing (2018) 15 GB ❌ No Free tier L4 Ada Lovelace (2023) 22.5 GB ✅ Yes Paid (compute units) A100 40 GB Ampere (2020) 40 GB ✅ Yes Paid (compute units) A100 80 GB Ampere (2020) 80 GB ✅ Yes Paid (High RAM slider) H100 Hopper (2022) 80 GB ✅ Yes Paid (listed, hard to get) G4 (RTX PRO 6000) Blackwell (2025) ~96 GB ✅ Yes Paid (new option)

Notes:

  • V100 has been removed from Colab
  • T4 is free but limited (no bfloat16, 15 GB only)
  • Colab does NOT publish hard usage limits — they fluctuate dynamically

docs.title: 5.2 RAM and Runtime Limits
docs.text: Tier System RAM Max Runtime GPU Access Free ~12.7 GB ~12 hours (variable) T4 only, limited Pay-as-you-go ~12.7 GB (standard) Longer sessions T4, L4, A100 Pro ($10/mo + units) Up to ~51 GB (High RAM) 24 hours T4, L4, A100, H100, G4 Pro+ ($50/mo + units) Up to ~51 GB (High RAM) 24 hours Priority access to A100/H100/G4

docs.title: 5.3 Pricing (Compute Units)
docs.text:
• 100 compute units = $10 (fixed, $0.10/unit)
  • No bulk discount

Approximate cost rates (as of March 2026, Southern California):

GPU Units/hour $/hour Hours per $10 T4 ~1.3 ~$0.13 ~77h L4 ~3.5 ~$0.35 ~29h A100 40 GB ~7.4 ~$0.74 ~14h A100 80 GB ~11.0 ~$1.10 ~9h

docs.title: 5.4 Key Constraints for Our Use Case
docs.text:
1. No bfloat16 on T4 — Our pipeline uses use_bfloat16_if_supported=true. On T4, it falls back to FP16. This is handled by the code (_resolve_activation_dtype_bytes checks is_bf16_supported).
  2. Session timeout — Free tier ~12h, Pro ~24h. Each experiment with 60 trials × 40 epochs takes approximately 3–5 hours for small models (logistic/mlp) based on completed experiment durations (~10K–18K seconds). Larger models (vit, axial_vit) take significantly longer.
  3. Disk space — Colab VMs have ~100 GB disk. Our pipeline creates checkpoints, parquet windows, and SQLite DB. This should be sufficient per experiment.
  4. No persistent state — VM is wiped on disconnect. Requires GCS sync for resume capability.

docs.title: 6.1 Pipeline Steps per Experiment
docs.text:
1. Create experiment — experiment create --config <json> (generates DB entry, config)
  2. Fetch requirements — experiment fetch-requirements (downloads OHLCV data)
  3. Process requirements — experiment process-requirements (computes indicators/features)
  4. Generate labels — experiment generate-labels (ATR-based labeling)
  5. Generate sampling — experiment generate-sampling (train/val splits)
  6. Generate fat windows — experiment generate-fat-windows (pre-sliced parquet windows)
  7. Run training — experiment run-training (Optuna HPO loop with n_trials)

Steps 1–6 are setup (CPU-only, no GPU needed). Step 7 is training (GPU-intensive).

docs.title: 6.2 Data Sizes (Tier B)
docs.text:
• Fat window size: 60 timesteps
  • Window size search range: 45–60
  • Feature count: ~25 (standard set with atr_vol_ratio, all features forced)
  • Input tensor per sample: seq_len × input_dim × 4 bytes = 60 × 25 × 4 = 6 KB
  • Batch tensor: 512 × 6 KB = 3 MB per batch (max batch)

docs.title: 6.3 Existing Colab Notebooks
docs.text:
Two notebooks exist in colab/:

  1. 01_setup_pipeline.ipynb — Bootstraps Colab runtime, clones repo, installs deps, runs setup pipeline (steps 1–6), pushes artifacts to GCS.
  2. 02_training_resume.ipynb — Pulls setup + training hot state from GCS, runs training with periodic sync back to GCS via background watcher.

The notebooks use:

  • GitHub token auth (credential store)
  • GCS bucket for state sync (rainbow-trader-bucket)
  • Background watcher process for periodic hot-state push
  • --resume flag with stable RUN_SUFFIX

docs.title: 6.4 GCS Sync Scripts
docs.text:
Located in scripts/gcp/sync_state/:

  • experiment_push_setup.sh — Push setup artifacts after steps 1–6
  • experiment_pull_setup.sh — Pull setup artifacts before training
  • experiment_pull_training_hot.sh — Pull training checkpoints/Optuna state
  • experiment_watch_training_hot.sh — Background daemon for periodic push during training

docs.title: 7.1 Tier B Remaining Experiments
docs.text: Code Model n_trials Est. Work VRAM Est. Time/Trial GPU Fit S2-TB-MEX-F2..F4 mlp_ex 60 <0.2 GB ~3 min T4 ✅ S2-TB-TCN-F1..F4 tcn 60 <0.2 GB ~3 min T4 ✅ S2-TB-VIT-F1..F4 vit 60 0.5–2.0 GB ~5–10 min T4 ✅ S2-TB-AXV-F1..F4 axial_vit 60 5–20 GB ~10–30 min T4 ⚠️ / L4 ✅

docs.title: 7.2 Detailed AxialViT Analysis
docs.text:
AxialViT is the only model where Colab hardware becomes a genuine constraint:

Small configs (hidden_dim=64, num_layers=2, batch_size=128):

  • Estimated VRAM: 1–3 GB → T4 fits

Medium configs (hidden_dim=128, num_layers=4, batch_size=256):

  • Estimated VRAM: 5–8 GB → T4 fits but tight

Large configs (hidden_dim=256, num_layers=6, batch_size=512):

  • Estimated VRAM: 12–20 GB → T4 does NOT fit, needs L4 or A100

Since Optuna explores the full search space, some trials will sample large configs. Options:

  1. Use L4 ($0.35/h) — 22.5 GB covers all axial_vit configs
  2. Use A100 40 GB ($0.74/h) — Guaranteed headroom
  3. Keep T4 and let OOM trials fail/get pruned — Optuna handles trial failures, but wastes time
  4. Reduce search space — Cap hidden_dim at 128 or batch_size at 256 for Colab runs

Recommendation: Use L4 for axial_vit experiments, T4 for everything else.

docs.title: 7.3 Time Estimates
docs.text:
Based on completed Tier B experiments:

Model Avg Duration per Experiment Trials Experiments Remaining logistic ~3.5 hours (13K sec) 60 0 (done) mlp ~4 hours (14K sec) 60 0 (done) mlp_ex ~3 hours (11K sec) 60 3 tcn Est. ~4 hours 60 4 vit Est. ~8–12 hours 60 4 axial_vit Est. ~12–20 hours 60 4

Total estimated wall time for remaining Tier B: ~200–300 hours

On T4 (free): ~200–300 hours of GPU time needed. Free tier has ~12h sessions but availability is unpredictable.

docs.title: 7.4 Cost Estimates (Tier B Only)
docs.text: Model GPU Hours $/hour Est. Cost mlp_ex (3 exps) T4 9h $0.13 $1.17 tcn (4 exps) T4 16h $0.13 $2.08 vit (4 exps) T4 40h $0.13 $5.20 axial_vit (4 exps) L4 64h $0.35 $22.40 Total Tier B ~129h ~$31

docs.title: 7.5 Cost Estimates (All Remaining Tiers B–E)
docs.text:
Rough multiplier: Tiers C/D/E have larger feature sets and more trials, so expect ~1.5–3× Tier B per experiment.

Tier Est. Total Hours Est. Cost B remaining ~129h ~$31 C ~200h ~$50 D ~300h ~$75 E ~400h ~$100 Grand Total ~1,029h ~$256

docs.title: 8.1 Resume Capability
docs.text:
• The pipeline already supports --resume with stable suffix
  • GCS sync scripts handle hot-state push/pull
  • 02_training_resume.ipynb implements the full resume flow
  • Key requirement: Keep RUN_SUFFIX stable across Colab sessions

docs.title: 8.2 Session Management Strategy
docs.text:
For experiments that exceed session limits:

  1. Use background watcher (experiment_watch_training_hot.sh) with 5-minute sync interval
  2. On session timeout, state is preserved in GCS up to last sync
  3. Reconnect, re-run 02_training_resume.ipynb with same suffix
  4. Training resumes from last Optuna checkpoint

docs.title: 8.3 T4 bfloat16 Limitation
docs.text:
• T4 does NOT support bfloat16
  • Our code auto-detects and falls back to FP16 via _resolve_activation_dtype_bytes
  • FP16 may cause occasional numerical instability in deep transformers
  • Mitigation: AMP grad scaler is configured (grad_scaler_init_scale=4096.0)
  • Risk: ViT/AxialViT may see more NaN/Inf issues on T4 vs L4/A100 with bf16

docs.title: 8.4 CUDA Graphs Compatibility
docs.text:
• Colab T4 has been known to cause CUDA graph instability (see prior CUDA unknown error incidents)
  • The pipeline supports use_cudagraphs_mode=off
  • Recommendation: Disable cudagraphs on Colab to avoid transient failures

docs.title: 8.5 Script2 Integration
docs.text:
The current Colab notebooks are designed for single-experiment workflows. For Script2 multi-experiment runs:

  • Option A: Modify notebooks to call run_script2.sh with --resume-from and --run-tag
  • Option B: Run experiments individually via experiment run-training (current approach)
  • Option C: Create a new Colab notebook that wraps the Script2 manifest loop

Recommendation: Option B for now (per-experiment), since it gives maximum control over GPU selection per model type.

docs.title: 9. Risk Matrix
docs.text: Risk Severity Likelihood Mitigation AxialViT OOM on T4 High High (large configs) Use L4 for axial_vit Session timeout mid-experiment Medium Medium GCS sync + resume T4 FP16 numerical instability Medium Low-Medium Grad scaler + monitor loss CUDA graph failures on Colab Medium Medium Disable cudagraphs Free tier GPU unavailability Medium High Use paid compute units GCS sync failure/data loss High Low 5-min sync interval, manual checkpoint Colab runtime version mismatch Low Low Pin requirements.txt

docs.title: 10.1 Immediate Actions (Tier B Completion)
docs.text:
1. mlp_ex F2–F4, tcn F1–F4: Run on free T4 or minimal paid T4 time
  2. vit F1–F4: Run on T4 (fits comfortably at <2 GB VRAM)
  3. axial_vit F1–F4: Run on L4 ($0.35/h) to avoid OOM on large configs

docs.title: 10.2 GPU Selection Strategy
docs.text: Model Type Recommended GPU Reason logistic, mlp, mlp_ex, tcn T4 (free or cheap) <0.2 GB VRAM vit T4 <2 GB VRAM axial_vit L4 (or A100 if available) Up to 20 GB VRAM

docs.title: 10.3 Budget Plan
docs.text:
• Minimum (Tier B only): ~$31 (~130 GPU-hours)
  • Full study (B–E): ~$256 (~1,030 GPU-hours)
  • Conservative with buffer: ~$300 (accounting for retries and overhead)

docs.title: 10.4 Notebook Modifications Needed
docs.text:
1. Add GPU type selection guidance per model
  2. Add use_cudagraphs_mode=off as default for Colab
  3. Add Script2 manifest integration (optional)
  4. Add monitoring cell for VRAM usage during training

docs.title: 11. Conclusion
docs.text:
Colab is feasible for running all remaining Script 2 experiments with the following caveats:

  • Most models (logistic, mlp, mlp_ex, tcn, vit) fit comfortably on the free T4 GPU
  • AxialViT requires an L4 or better GPU for large hyperparameter configs
  • Resume infrastructure already exists via GCS sync notebooks
  • Estimated total cost for all remaining tiers (B–E): ~$256 in compute units
  • Main operational risk is session timeouts, mitigated by the existing sync mechanism
  • cudagraphs should be disabled on Colab to avoid CUDA runtime instability

docs.title: APPENDIX A — Operational Runbook: Running Script2 Experiments on Colab
docs.text: This appendix provides precise, step-by-step instructions for running individual Script2 experiments on Google Colab and bringing all artifacts back to the local machine so that the run tag's summary CSV, progress dashboard, and SQLite DB reflect the results as if they were executed locally.

docs.title: A1. Complete Artifact Map per Experiment
docs.text: Every Script2 experiment produces artifacts in three locations. All three must be consistent for the progress tracker and analyzer to work.

docs.title: A1.1 SQLite DB (database/rainbow_trader.sqlite)
docs.text: Table Rows Created Key Columns experiments 1 id, experiment_config_path, status, best_trial_id, best_score, trials_completed/pruned/failed trials 1 per trial (up to n_trials) experiment_id, study_id, objective_value, stage3_return_pct, training_metrics (JSON), model_profile_json (JSON), checkpoint_path, status optuna_studies 1 experiment_id, optuna_study_name, sqlite_path, study_path parquets Many experiment_id, trial_id, category (OHLCV/INDICATOR/SIGNAL/LABEL/SAMPLING/FAT/SMALL/PREDICTIONS), path checkpoints 1+ per trial trial_id, path events Many experiment_id, event_type, payload

docs.title: A1.2 Filesystem under data/
docs.text: Category Path Pattern Created By Step Needed On Colab? Experiment config data/experiments/<id>/config.json Step 1 (create) Yes (setup) OHLCV parquets data/experiments/<id>/parquets/ohlcv/ Step 2 (fetch) Yes (setup) Indicator parquets data/experiments/<id>/parquets/indicator/ Step 3 (process) Yes (setup) Signal parquets data/experiments/<id>/parquets/signal/ Step 3 (process) Yes (setup) Label parquets data/experiments/<id>/parquets/label/ Step 4 (labels) Yes (setup) Sampling parquets data/experiments/<id>/parquets/sampling/ Step 5 (sampling) Yes (setup) Fat-window parquets data/experiments/<id>/parquets/fat/ Step 6 (fat-windows) Yes (setup) Small-window parquets data/experiments/<id>/parquets/small/ Step 7 (training) No — generated during training Prediction parquets data/experiments/<id>/parquets/predictions/ Step 7 (training) No — generated during training Checkpoints data/checkpoints/<suffix>/ Step 7 (training) No — generated during training Optuna study SQLite data/optuna/experiment-<id>-<suffix>/optuna_study.sqlite Step 7 (training) No — generated during training

docs.title: A1.3 Script2-Specific Artifacts (under scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/)
docs.text: Artifact Path Updated By Summary CSV results/<RUN_TAG>/script2_experiment_ids.csv run_script2.sh (upsert_summary_row) Evolution log results/<RUN_TAG>/evolution_log.jsonl run_script2.sh (append_evolution_event) Progress dashboard results/<RUN_TAG>/progress.md exporters/script2_progress_tracker.py Model profile results/<RUN_TAG>/progress_model_profile.md exporters/script2_progress_tracker.py Per-step logs logs/<RUN_TAG>/<CODE>_01..07_*.log run_script2.sh per step

docs.title: A1.4 Optuna Study Naming Convention
docs.text:
The study name and suffix are derived from the simple code:

suffix = "<code_lowercase>_<model_type>"
study_name = "experiment-<id>-<suffix>"

Example for S2-TB-VIT-F1, experiment_id=99:
  suffix = "s2-tb-vit-f1_vit"
  study_name = "experiment-99-s2-tb-vit-f1_vit"

docs.title: A2. Architecture Decision: Local-First Creation
docs.text:
The recommended workflow is LOCAL-FIRST CREATION:

  1. Run steps 1–6 locally (CPU-only, no GPU needed, ~2–5 min per experiment)
  2. Push setup artifacts to GCS
  3. On Colab: pull setup, run step 7 only (GPU training)
  4. After Colab completes: pull training artifacts back and merge into local

Why local-first?

  • The experiment gets a local DB ID that is already in the summary CSV
  • No experiment_id conflict between local and Colab
  • Steps 1–6 are fast and CPU-only — no reason to waste Colab GPU time on them
  • The GCS sync scripts already support exactly this push/pull pattern
  • The summary CSV training_suffix and experiment_id columns are pre-populated

What NOT to do:

  • Do NOT create the experiment on Colab from scratch — the experiment_id will differ from what the local summary CSV expects
  • Do NOT use --force-overwrite on the pull scripts without backing up your local DB first — it replaces the entire SQLite file

docs.title: A3.1 Scripts That Work As-Is
docs.text: Script Purpose Status run_script2.sh --run-only <CODE> Run one experiment (steps 1–7) Works locally experiment_push_setup.sh --experiment-id <ID> Push steps 1–6 artifacts to GCS Works experiment_push_training_hot.sh --experiment-id <ID> --suffix <SUFFIX> Push step 7 hot state to GCS Works experiment_pull_setup.sh --experiment-id <ID> Pull steps 1–6 artifacts from GCS Works on Colab experiment_pull_training_hot.sh --experiment-id <ID> --suffix <SUFFIX> Pull step 7 state from GCS CAUTION: full DB restore experiment_watch_training_hot.sh --experiment-id <ID> --suffix <SUFFIX> Periodic push during training Works on Colab snapshot_sqlite.sh --source <db> --dest <snap> Consistent SQLite snapshot Works

docs.title: A3.2 Scripts / Tools That Need To Be Created
docs.text: Need Purpose Priority merge_colab_experiment_db.py Merge experiment-specific rows from Colab DB snapshot into local DB CRITICAL colab/03_script2_training.ipynb New Colab notebook tailored for Script2 --run-only workflow HIGH update_summary_csv_from_db.py Update one summary CSV row with metrics from local DB after merge HIGH Optionally: a wrapper script combining pull + merge + CSV update End-to-end "bring back" automation MEDIUM

docs.title: A3.3 The DB Merge Problem (Why a New Script Is Needed)
docs.text:
The existing experiment_pull_training_hot.sh calls:

expsync_restore_sqlite_snapshot "$SQLITE_PATH" "$LOCAL_SNAPSHOT_PATH" "$FORCE_OVERWRITE"
# Which internally does:
sqlite3 "$target_db" ".restore '$snapshot_db'"

This REPLACES the entire local DB with the Colab snapshot. This is destructive if the local DB contains other experiments.

What we need instead: A selective merge that:

  1. Opens the Colab snapshot as an ATTACHED database
  2. Copies only the rows for the target experiment_id from:
  3. trials (WHERE experiment_id = ?)
  4. optuna_studies (WHERE experiment_id = ?)
  5. parquets (WHERE experiment_id = ? AND category IN ('SMALL', 'PREDICTIONS'))
  6. checkpoints (WHERE trial_id IN (SELECT id FROM trials WHERE experiment_id = ?))
  7. events (WHERE experiment_id = ?)
  8. Updates the experiments row (status, best_trial_id, best_score, trials counts, updated_at)
  9. Does NOT touch any other experiment's data

docs.title: Phase A: Pre-Colab Preparation (Local Machine)
docs.text: Goal: Create the experiment locally, run setup (steps 1–6), push to GCS.

docs.title: A4.1 Run Steps 1–6 Locally
docs.text:
source .venv/bin/activate

# For a single experiment (example: S2-TB-VIT-F1):
bash scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/run_script2.sh \
  --run-tag 20260328_tierB_corrected \
  --run-only S2-TB-VIT-F1

WAIT — this runs ALL 7 steps. For local-first we have two options:

Option 1 (Simple): Let --run-only run to step 6, then Ctrl+C during step 7 (the summary CSV will show training_started=true, training_completed=false). NOT recommended — leaves messy state.

Option 2 (Clean): Create and run steps 1–6 manually via the core CLI, then push setup. This is cleaner:

source .venv/bin/activate
cd /path/to/rainbow-trader

# Variables (set per experiment)
EXPERIMENT_CONFIG="scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/experiments/S2-TB-VIT-F1.json"
RUN_TAG="20260328_tierB_corrected"

# Step 1: Create
.venv/bin/python -m rainbow.core \
  --set log.level=DEBUG --set log.format=PLAIN --set log.target=FILE \
  --set log.file_path="scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/logs/${RUN_TAG}/S2-TB-VIT-F1_01_create.log" \
  experiment create --config "$EXPERIMENT_CONFIG"

# Extract experiment_id from create log
EXPERIMENT_ID=$(grep -o 'experiment_id=[0-9]*' \
  "scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/logs/${RUN_TAG}/S2-TB-VIT-F1_01_create.log" \
  | tail -n 1 | cut -d= -f2)
echo "experiment_id=$EXPERIMENT_ID"

# Steps 2–6: Setup pipeline
.venv/bin/python -m rainbow.core experiment fetch-requirements --id "$EXPERIMENT_ID"
.venv/bin/python -m rainbow.core experiment process-requirements --id "$EXPERIMENT_ID"
.venv/bin/python -m rainbow.core experiment generate-labels --id "$EXPERIMENT_ID" -f
.venv/bin/python -m rainbow.core experiment generate-sampling --id "$EXPERIMENT_ID" -f
.venv/bin/python -m rainbow.core experiment generate-fat-windows --id "$EXPERIMENT_ID"

Option 3 (RECOMMENDED, Cleanest): If the experiment is already created in the summary CSV with an experiment_id (from a prior partial run or from ensure_summary_csv_initialized), you can check the CSV to get the ID and just ensure steps 1–6 are complete. The --run-only command with smart reuse handles this automatically if training was not started yet.

docs.title: A4.2 Update Summary CSV Row
docs.text:
After steps 1–6, ensure the summary CSV has the correct row:

# Check the row exists in summary CSV with the experiment_id
grep "S2-TB-VIT-F1" scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/results/${RUN_TAG}/script2_experiment_ids.csv

The row should show last_completed_step=6, training_started=false, training_completed=false, experiment_id=<ID>.

docs.title: A4.3 Push Setup Artifacts to GCS
docs.text:
bash scripts/gcp/sync_state/experiment_push_setup.sh \
  --experiment-id "$EXPERIMENT_ID"

This pushes to GCS:

  • SQLite DB snapshot (entire DB at this point, containing experiment + setup rows)
  • Experiment config JSON
  • All setup parquets (OHLCV, INDICATOR, SIGNAL, LABEL, SAMPLING, FAT)

docs.title: A4.4 Record the Experiment Details
docs.text:
Keep a local note (or use the summary CSV) mapping:

CODE            | EXPERIMENT_ID | SUFFIX                    | GPU
S2-TB-VIT-F1    | 99            | s2-tb-vit-f1_vit          | T4
S2-TB-AXV-F1   | 103           | s2-tb-axv-f1_axial_vit    | L4

You will need EXPERIMENT_ID and SUFFIX for every Colab step.

docs.title: Phase B: Colab Execution
docs.text: Goal: Pull setup from GCS, run training (step 7), push results back.

docs.title: A4.5 Colab Notebook Setup
docs.text: Use colab/01_setup_pipeline.ipynb cells 2–4 to bootstrap the runtime (clone repo, install deps, authenticate GCS). Then instead of cell 5 (which runs create + setup), do the following:

docs.title: A4.6 Pull Setup from GCS (on Colab)
docs.text:
cd /content/rainbow-trader

# Pull setup artifacts for this experiment
bash scripts/gcp/sync_state/experiment_pull_setup.sh \
  --experiment-id "$EXPERIMENT_ID" \
  --force-overwrite

This downloads from GCS:

  • SQLite DB snapshot → restored as database/rainbow_trader.sqlite
  • All setup parquets → restored under data/

Note: --force-overwrite is safe here because Colab starts with no local DB.

docs.title: A4.7 Start Background Sync Watcher (on Colab)
docs.text:
cd /content/rainbow-trader
EXPERIMENT_ID="99"
SUFFIX="s2-tb-vit-f1_vit"

# Start watcher in background (pushes hot state every 5 min)
nohup bash scripts/gcp/sync_state/experiment_watch_training_hot.sh \
  --experiment-id "$EXPERIMENT_ID" \
  --suffix "$SUFFIX" \
  --interval 300 \
  > /tmp/watcher.log 2>&1 &

WATCHER_PID=$!
echo "Watcher PID: $WATCHER_PID"

docs.title: A4.8 Run Training (Step 7 Only, on Colab)
docs.text:
cd /content/rainbow-trader
EXPERIMENT_ID="99"
SUFFIX="s2-tb-vit-f1_vit"

# Run training with cudagraphs disabled for Colab stability
.venv/bin/python -m rainbow.core \
  --set log.level=DEBUG \
  --set log.format=PLAIN \
  --set log.target=FILE \
  --set log.file_path="/content/rainbow-trader/training_${SUFFIX}.log" \
  experiment run-training \
  --id "$EXPERIMENT_ID" \
  -s "$SUFFIX" \
  --no-all-phases

For models using progressive phases (check the manifest use_progressive_phases column), replace --no-all-phases with --all-phases.

To resume an interrupted Colab session:

# Pull latest hot state first
bash scripts/gcp/sync_state/experiment_pull_training_hot.sh \
  --experiment-id "$EXPERIMENT_ID" \
  --suffix "$SUFFIX" \
  --force-overwrite

# Re-start watcher
nohup bash scripts/gcp/sync_state/experiment_watch_training_hot.sh \
  --experiment-id "$EXPERIMENT_ID" \
  --suffix "$SUFFIX" \
  --interval 300 \
  > /tmp/watcher.log 2>&1 &

# Resume training (Optuna picks up from last completed trial)
.venv/bin/python -m rainbow.core \
  --set log.level=DEBUG \
  --set log.format=PLAIN \
  experiment run-training \
  --id "$EXPERIMENT_ID" \
  -s "$SUFFIX" \
  --resume \
  --no-all-phases

docs.title: A4.9 Final Push After Training Completes (on Colab)
docs.text:
# Stop the watcher
kill "$WATCHER_PID" 2>/dev/null || true

# Final push of completed training state
bash scripts/gcp/sync_state/experiment_push_training_hot.sh \
  --experiment-id "$EXPERIMENT_ID" \
  --suffix "$SUFFIX"

echo "Training completed and pushed to GCS."

docs.title: Phase C: Post-Colab — Bring Data Back (Local Machine)
docs.text: Goal: Download training artifacts from GCS, merge into local DB, update summary CSV, regenerate progress dashboard.

docs.title: A4.10 Download Colab DB Snapshot and Data Files
docs.text:
source .venv/bin/activate
cd /path/to/rainbow-trader

EXPERIMENT_ID="99"
SUFFIX="s2-tb-vit-f1_vit"

# Download training data files (parquets, checkpoints, optuna) — NOT the DB
# We use gsutil directly to avoid the destructive DB restore
SUFFIX_SLUG=$(echo "$SUFFIX" | tr '/:  ' '____')
GCS_REMOTE="gs://rainbow-trader-bucket/rainbow-trader/_EXPERIMENT/${EXPERIMENT_ID}/training/${SUFFIX_SLUG}"

# Pull data files only (study dir, parquets, checkpoints)
gsutil -m rsync -r "${GCS_REMOTE}/data" data/

# Pull the DB snapshot to a SEPARATE file (do NOT overwrite local DB)
COLAB_SNAPSHOT="database/colab_snapshot_exp${EXPERIMENT_ID}.sqlite"
gsutil cp "${GCS_REMOTE}/database/rainbow_trader.snapshot.sqlite" "$COLAB_SNAPSHOT"

echo "Downloaded Colab snapshot to: $COLAB_SNAPSHOT"

docs.title: A4.11 Pull Optuna Study SQLite Snapshot
docs.text:
The Optuna study has its own SQLite file. The push script stores it as a .snapshot.sqlite file. Restore it:

# Find the Optuna study path from the Colab snapshot
OPTUNA_SQLITE_REL=$(sqlite3 "$COLAB_SNAPSHOT" \
  "SELECT sqlite_path FROM optuna_studies
   WHERE experiment_id = ${EXPERIMENT_ID}
   ORDER BY id DESC LIMIT 1;")

if [ -n "$OPTUNA_SQLITE_REL" ]; then
  OPTUNA_TARGET="data/${OPTUNA_SQLITE_REL#./}"
  OPTUNA_SNAPSHOT="data/${OPTUNA_SQLITE_REL%.sqlite}.snapshot.sqlite"

  if [ -f "$OPTUNA_SNAPSHOT" ]; then
    mkdir -p "$(dirname "$OPTUNA_TARGET")"
    sqlite3 "$OPTUNA_TARGET" ".restore '$OPTUNA_SNAPSHOT'"
    echo "Optuna SQLite restored: $OPTUNA_TARGET"
  fi
fi

docs.title: A4.12 Merge Colab DB Into Local DB (NEW SCRIPT NEEDED)
docs.text:
This is the critical missing piece. A Python script that selectively merges experiment-specific rows from the Colab snapshot into the local DB.

Script: scripts/gcp/sync_state/merge_experiment_db.py

What it must do:

INPUT:
  --local-db    database/rainbow_trader.sqlite
  --colab-db    database/colab_snapshot_exp99.sqlite
  --experiment-id 99

OPERATIONS:
  1. ATTACH colab_db AS colab
  2. UPDATE local experiments SET (status, best_trial_id, best_score,
     trials_completed, trials_pruned, trials_failed, updated_at)
     FROM colab.experiments WHERE id = ?
  3. INSERT OR REPLACE INTO local trials
     SELECT * FROM colab.trials WHERE experiment_id = ?
  4. INSERT OR REPLACE INTO local optuna_studies
     SELECT * FROM colab.optuna_studies WHERE experiment_id = ?
  5. INSERT OR REPLACE INTO local parquets
     SELECT * FROM colab.parquets WHERE experiment_id = ?
     AND category IN ('SMALL', 'PREDICTIONS')
  6. INSERT OR REPLACE INTO local checkpoints
     SELECT * FROM colab.checkpoints
     WHERE trial_id IN (SELECT id FROM colab.trials WHERE experiment_id = ?)
  7. INSERT OR IGNORE INTO local events
     SELECT * FROM colab.events WHERE experiment_id = ?
  8. DETACH colab

OUTPUT:
  - Local DB now has all training results for experiment_id=99
  - No other experiments are affected

Usage:

.venv/bin/python scripts/gcp/sync_state/merge_experiment_db.py \
  --local-db database/rainbow_trader.sqlite \
  --colab-db "database/colab_snapshot_exp${EXPERIMENT_ID}.sqlite" \
  --experiment-id "$EXPERIMENT_ID"

docs.title: A4.13 Update Summary CSV Row
docs.text:
After the DB merge, extract metrics and update the summary CSV:

source .venv/bin/activate

EXPERIMENT_ID="99"
CODE="S2-TB-VIT-F1"
RUN_TAG="20260328_tierB_corrected"
SUMMARY_CSV="scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/results/${RUN_TAG}/script2_experiment_ids.csv"

# The run_script2.sh function query_experiment_metrics does exactly this.
# We can reuse it by sourcing the script's functions, or use inline Python:

.venv/bin/python - "$EXPERIMENT_ID" "$CODE" "$SUMMARY_CSV" "database/rainbow_trader.sqlite" <<'PYEOF'
import csv
import json
import sqlite3
import sys
from datetime import datetime

experiment_id = int(sys.argv[1])
code = sys.argv[2]
summary_csv = sys.argv[3]
db_path = sys.argv[4]

conn = sqlite3.connect(db_path)
conn.row_factory = sqlite3.Row

exp = conn.execute("""
    SELECT best_trial_id, best_score, trials_completed, trials_pruned,
           trials_failed, created_at, updated_at
    FROM experiments WHERE id = ?
""", (experiment_id,)).fetchone()

if exp is None:
    print(f"ERROR: experiment_id={experiment_id} not found in DB")
    sys.exit(1)

best_trial_id = str(exp["best_trial_id"] or "")
best_objective = str(exp["best_score"] or "")
completed = int(exp["trials_completed"] or 0)
pruned = int(exp["trials_pruned"] or 0)
failed = int(exp["trials_failed"] or 0)
best_sharpe = ""
best_return_pct = ""
checkpoint_dir = ""

if best_trial_id:
    trial = conn.execute("""
        SELECT objective_value, stage3_return_pct, checkpoint_path, training_metrics
        FROM trials WHERE id = ?
    """, (int(best_trial_id),)).fetchone()
    if trial:
        if trial["objective_value"] is not None:
            best_objective = str(trial["objective_value"])
        if trial["stage3_return_pct"] is not None:
            best_return_pct = str(trial["stage3_return_pct"])
        if trial["checkpoint_path"] is not None:
            checkpoint_dir = str(trial["checkpoint_path"])
        tm_raw = trial["training_metrics"]
        if tm_raw:
            try:
                tm = json.loads(tm_raw) if isinstance(tm_raw, str) else tm_raw
                s3 = tm.get("stage3_metrics", {}) if isinstance(tm, dict) else {}
                if isinstance(s3, dict) and s3.get("sharpe_ratio") is not None:
                    best_sharpe = str(s3["sharpe_ratio"])
            except (json.JSONDecodeError, TypeError):
                pass

duration_seconds = ""
if exp["created_at"] and exp["updated_at"]:
    try:
        fmt = "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"
        c = str(exp["created_at"]).replace("T", " ").split("+")[0].split(".")[0]
        u = str(exp["updated_at"]).replace("T", " ").split("+")[0].split(".")[0]
        delta = datetime.strptime(u, fmt) - datetime.strptime(c, fmt)
        duration_seconds = str(int(delta.total_seconds()))
    except ValueError:
        pass

conn.close()

# Read CSV, update the matching row, write back
rows = []
with open(summary_csv, "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    reader = csv.reader(f)
    header = next(reader)
    rows.append(header)
    for row in reader:
        if row[0].strip() == code:
            row[10] = "7"               # last_completed_step
            row[11] = "true"            # training_started
            row[12] = "true"            # training_completed
            row[13] = best_trial_id     # best_trial_id
            row[14] = best_objective    # best_objective
            row[15] = best_sharpe       # best_sharpe
            row[16] = best_return_pct   # best_return_pct
            row[17] = str(completed)    # completed_trials
            row[18] = str(pruned)       # pruned_trials
            row[19] = str(failed)       # failed_trials
            row[20] = duration_seconds  # total_duration_seconds
            row[21] = checkpoint_dir    # checkpoint_dir
        rows.append(row)

with open(summary_csv, "w", encoding="utf-8", newline="") as f:
    writer = csv.writer(f)
    writer.writerows(rows)

print(f"Updated {code} in {summary_csv}")
print(f"  best_objective={best_objective}")
print(f"  best_sharpe={best_sharpe}")
print(f"  trials: completed={completed} pruned={pruned} failed={failed}")
print(f"  duration={duration_seconds}s")
PYEOF

docs.title: A4.14 Regenerate Progress Dashboard
docs.text:
source .venv/bin/activate
RUN_TAG="20260328_tierB_corrected"

.venv/bin/python scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/exporters/script2_progress_tracker.py \
  update-run-status \
  --progress-md "scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/results/${RUN_TAG}/progress.md" \
  --summary-csv "scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/results/${RUN_TAG}/script2_experiment_ids.csv" \
  --db-path database/rainbow_trader.sqlite \
  --run-tag "$RUN_TAG" \
  --manifest-csv scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/experiments/script2_manifest_active.csv \
  --status running

docs.title: A4.15 Cleanup
docs.text:
# Remove the Colab snapshot after successful merge
rm -f "database/colab_snapshot_exp${EXPERIMENT_ID}.sqlite"

docs.title: A5. Periodic Sync During Long Training Sessions
docs.text: For experiments that span multiple Colab sessions (e.g., axial_vit with 60 trials can take 12–20 hours):

docs.title: A5.1 On Colab: Watcher Push Loop
docs.text:
The experiment_watch_training_hot.sh script runs a loop that pushes every N seconds (default 300 = 5 min). It pushes:

  • Main SQLite DB snapshot
  • Optuna study SQLite snapshot
  • Checkpoint directory
  • SMALL + PREDICTIONS parquets for completed trials

docs.title: A5.2 On Session Timeout
docs.text:
1. Session dies → watcher stops → last push was ≤5 min ago
  2. Reconnect to new Colab session
  3. Re-run bootstrap cells (repo clone, deps install, GCS auth)
  4. Pull latest hot state:
bash scripts/gcp/sync_state/experiment_pull_training_hot.sh \
  --experiment-id "$EXPERIMENT_ID" \
  --suffix "$SUFFIX" \
  --force-overwrite
  1. Re-start watcher + resume training (see A4.8 resume commands)

docs.title: A5.3 Checking Progress from Local (Without Pulling)
docs.text:
You can check Colab progress from local without pulling by downloading just the DB snapshot:

SUFFIX_SLUG=$(echo "$SUFFIX" | tr '/:  ' '____')
GCS_REMOTE="gs://rainbow-trader-bucket/rainbow-trader/_EXPERIMENT/${EXPERIMENT_ID}/training/${SUFFIX_SLUG}"

gsutil cp "${GCS_REMOTE}/database/rainbow_trader.snapshot.sqlite" /tmp/colab_check.sqlite

sqlite3 /tmp/colab_check.sqlite "
  SELECT trials_completed, trials_pruned, trials_failed, best_score
  FROM experiments WHERE id = ${EXPERIMENT_ID};
"
rm /tmp/colab_check.sqlite

docs.title: A6. Batch Execution Plan
docs.text: For running multiple experiments in sequence on the same Colab session:

docs.title: A6.1 Same-GPU Models (e.g., All TCN Folds)
docs.text:
# Push setup for all experiments locally first
for CODE in S2-TB-TCN-F1 S2-TB-TCN-F2 S2-TB-TCN-F3 S2-TB-TCN-F4; do
  # ... run steps 1-6 locally, get EXPERIMENT_ID ...
  bash scripts/gcp/sync_state/experiment_push_setup.sh --experiment-id "$EXPERIMENT_ID"
done

# On Colab: pull each, train, push, then proceed to next
for EXP_PAIR in "100:s2-tb-tcn-f1_tcn" "101:s2-tb-tcn-f2_tcn" "102:s2-tb-tcn-f3_tcn" "103:s2-tb-tcn-f4_tcn"; do
  IFS=':' read -r EID SUFFIX <<< "$EXP_PAIR"

  bash scripts/gcp/sync_state/experiment_pull_setup.sh --experiment-id "$EID" --force-overwrite
  .venv/bin/python -m rainbow.core experiment run-training --id "$EID" -s "$SUFFIX" --no-all-phases
  bash scripts/gcp/sync_state/experiment_push_training_hot.sh --experiment-id "$EID" --suffix "$SUFFIX"
done

Warning: Each pull_setup --force-overwrite replaces the Colab DB entirely. This is fine on Colab (fresh runtime) but means only one experiment's setup exists at a time.

docs.title: A6.2 Recommended Execution Order (Tier B)
docs.text:
Run fastest models first to validate the workflow before committing to long runs:

Priority Codes Model GPU Est. Time 1 S2-TB-MEX-F2..F4 mlp_ex T4 ~3h each 2 S2-TB-TCN-F1..F4 tcn T4 ~4h each 3 S2-TB-VIT-F1..F4 vit T4 ~10h each 4 S2-TB-AXV-F1..F4 axial_vit L4 ~16h each

docs.title: A7. Complete Checklist per Experiment
docs.text:
PRE-COLAB (Local):
[ ] Steps 1–6 completed locally (or already completed from prior run)
[ ] experiment_id recorded from summary CSV or create log
[ ] training_suffix derived: <code_lower>_<model_type>
[ ] Setup pushed to GCS: experiment_push_setup.sh --experiment-id <ID>

ON COLAB:
[ ] Runtime bootstrapped (clone, deps, GCS auth)
[ ] GPU type matches model needs (T4 vs L4)
[ ] Setup pulled from GCS: experiment_pull_setup.sh --experiment-id <ID> --force-overwrite
[ ] Watcher started: experiment_watch_training_hot.sh --experiment-id <ID> --suffix <SUFFIX>
[ ] Training running: experiment run-training --id <ID> -s <SUFFIX> [--resume]
[ ] Training completed (all n_trials done)
[ ] Watcher stopped
[ ] Final push: experiment_push_training_hot.sh --experiment-id <ID> --suffix <SUFFIX>

POST-COLAB (Local):
[ ] Data files pulled from GCS (gsutil rsync)
[ ] Colab DB snapshot downloaded to separate file
[ ] Optuna study SQLite restored from snapshot
[ ] DB merge executed: merge_experiment_db.py --experiment-id <ID>
[ ] Summary CSV row updated with metrics
[ ] Progress dashboard regenerated: exporters/script2_progress_tracker.py update-run-status
[ ] Colab snapshot file cleaned up
[ ] Verify: grep <CODE> in summary CSV shows training_completed=true

docs.title: A8. Known Gaps and Limitations
docs.text:
1. merge_experiment_db.py does not exist yet — must be created before the first Colab experiment is brought back. This is the single blocking prerequisite.

  2. colab/03_script2_training.ipynb does not exist yet — a convenience notebook wrapping the pull/train/push flow with Script2-specific variables. Not blocking (can use raw bash cells) but recommended for repeatability.

  3. Experiment IDs are auto-incremented — if someone creates experiments on the local machine while Colab is running, the Colab DB snapshot will have stale data for other experiments. The merge script must ONLY touch the target experiment_id.

  4. Optuna study names embed experiment_id — this ties the study to a specific ID. Do not change experiment_id between local and Colab.

  5. Evolution log is local-only — the Colab run will not append to the local evolution_log.jsonl. Consider manually appending an event after the merge, or accept the gap.

  6. Training log file — the _07_training.log file is generated on Colab at a custom path. It is NOT synced via GCS. If you want it locally, copy it manually from the Colab /content/rainbow-trader/training_*.log or add a gsutil cp step to push it.

docs.title: A9. Quick Reference Card
docs.text:
LOCAL SUFFIX FORMULA:
  suffix = "<simple_code_lowercase>_<model_type>"
  Example: S2-TB-VIT-F1 + vit → "s2-tb-vit-f1_vit"

GCS PATHS:
