  Setup:    gs://<bucket>/<prefix>/<experiment_id>/setup/
  Training: gs://<bucket>/<prefix>/<experiment_id>/training/<suffix_slug>/

KEY SCRIPTS:
  Push setup:     bash scripts/gcp/sync_state/experiment_push_setup.sh --experiment-id <ID>
  Pull setup:     bash scripts/gcp/sync_state/experiment_pull_setup.sh --experiment-id <ID> --force-overwrite
  Push training:  bash scripts/gcp/sync_state/experiment_push_training_hot.sh --experiment-id <ID> --suffix <SUFFIX>
  Pull training:  gsutil -m rsync -r <GCS_TRAINING>/data data/
  Watch:          bash scripts/gcp/sync_state/experiment_watch_training_hot.sh --experiment-id <ID> --suffix <SUFFIX>

TRAINING COMMAND:
  .venv/bin/python -m rainbow.core experiment run-training --id <ID> -s <SUFFIX> [--resume] [--all-phases|--no-all-phases]

PROGRESS UPDATE:
  .venv/bin/python scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/exporters/exporters/script2_progress_tracker.py update-run-status \
    --progress-md <path> --summary-csv <path> --db-path <path> --run-tag <tag> --manifest-csv <path> --status running

docs.title: Trade Selectivity Analysis — Levers for Reducing Trade Count in Tier B Rescue
docs.text:
• Date: 2026-03-31
  • Scope: Identify all available mechanisms to make models more selective (fewer, higher-quality trades) within the Script 2 thesis framework
  • Context: The Tier B Rescue Audit (Section 11.2, "Friction Paradox") showed that high trade count destroys PnL even when signal quality (S2) is strong. Only 14/1185 trials achieved positive net PnL. Profitable trials average ≤200 trades vs 300+ for unprofitable ones.
  • Status: Analysis complete. One actionable lever identified. Others documented for future use.

docs.title: 1. The Problem — Why Trade Reduction Matters
docs.text:
From the Tier B Rescue Audit, Section 11.2 (The Friction Paradox):

  • Best S2 trials are the worst PnL performers. The top-15 S2 ranking has ZERO positive-PnL trials.
  • Friction kills edge: The gap between best gross edge (+$1,681) and best net PnL (+$186) is ~$1,500 destroyed by transaction costs.
  • Profitable trials are selective: 12 of 14 profitable trials have ≤200 trades. T2530 (TCN-F3, best overall) made only 17 trades.
  • F2 and F4 produce zero profitable trials out of 612 combined — a regime + friction effect.

Conclusion: The model needs to be more selective about WHEN to trade. The question is: what levers exist to achieve this within the thesis experimental framework?

docs.title: 2. The Combined Confidence Formula
docs.text:
The bot's entry decision is governed by:

# File: modules/model-trainer/src/rainbow/model_trainer/helpers/combined_confidence.py
c = probability ** probability_weight
sq = (strength ** strength_weight) * (quality ** quality_weight)
combined = c + alpha * (1 - c) * sq

Parameters:

Parameter Type Optuna Range Current Script 2 Value Effect alpha Optuna hyperparameter [0.0, 1.0] FIXED at 0.0 Blend factor: how much strength/quality lift combined_confidence above probability alone probability_weight Optuna hyperparameter [0.1, 1.0] FIXED at 1.0 Exponent on classification probability strength_weight Optuna hyperparameter [0.1, 1.0] FIXED at 1.0 Exponent on predicted strength (irrelevant when alpha=0) quality_weight Optuna hyperparameter [0.1, 1.0] FIXED at 1.0 Exponent on predicted quality (irrelevant when alpha=0)

With Script 2 fixed values (alpha=0.0, probability_weight=1.0):

combined_confidence = probability^1.0 = probability

Strength and quality have ZERO contribution to the entry signal. Combined confidence equals raw classification probability.

Source of ranges: config/experiments/optuna.yaml, lines 212-230 (combined_confidence_weights_config).

Script 2 fixation mechanism: Each tier config JSON sets min == max to collapse the range:

"alpha": { "type": "float", "min": 0.0, "max": 0.0 },
"probability_weight": { "type": "float", "min": 1.0, "max": 1.0 }

Confirmed in all 4 tier configs: script2_tiers_config_ORIG.json, *_v2_tierA_rescue.json, *_v3_tierB_mlp_ex.json, *_v4_tierB_corrected.json.

docs.title: 3. The Bot Entry Gate Sequence
docs.text:
When the model makes a prediction, the bot applies these gates in order before entering a trade:

┌─ Gate 1: SIGNAL DIRECTION CHECK
│  └─ Reject if signal NOT in {LONG, SHORT} (model predicts NONE → no trade)
│
├─ Gate 2: COMBINED CONFIDENCE THRESHOLD
│  └─ Reject if combined_confidence < entry_threshold (0.55)
│  └─ With alpha=0: this is just "probability < 0.55"
│
├─ Gate 3: MINIMUM QUALITY THRESHOLD (INDEPENDENT from combined_confidence)
│  └─ Reject if quality < min_quality_threshold (0.30)
│  └─ Quality is checked SEPARATELY even though alpha=0 removes it from combined_confidence
│
└─ Gate 4: POSITION SCALING RULES
   └─ Cooldown, duplicate entry checks, confidence-beating rules

Source: modules/bot-logic/src/rainbow/bot_logic/bot_logic.py, lines 483-496.

Critical detail: The quality gate (Gate 3) operates independently — quality still filters trades even with alpha=0. However, min_quality_threshold=0.30 is a very permissive filter.

docs.title: 4.1 Lever: combined_confidence_weights (alpha, probability_weight, etc.)
docs.text:
Status: LOCKED — DO NOT CHANGE FOR THESIS

Why it was fixed (Decision #18, EXPERIMENT_PLAN_THESIS_VALIDATION.md Section 8.6.19):

  1. For non-trainable models (rule_based, random, always_none): These produce fixed strength=0.5 and quality=0.5 constants. With fixed constants, varying alpha is mathematically equivalent to varying entry_threshold — a confounded variable that defeats the thesis purpose. Mathematical proof:

    sq = 0.5^str_w * 0.5^qual_w = CONSTANT combined = c + alpha * (1 - c) * CONSTANT

    The ranking of predictions by combined_confidence is preserved (monotonic in c) for any alpha ≥ 0. Only the absolute values shift, which is equivalent to sliding the entry threshold.

  2. For trainable models: Even with learned strength/quality that vary per input, optimizing these weights confounds "signal quality" (what the thesis measures) with "filter tuning" (an optimization trick). A model with mediocre classification but a well-tuned entry filter could beat a model with superior classification but an untuned filter. The thesis must isolate model signal quality as the single variable.

  3. Multi-task regularization is preserved: Even with alpha=0, the strength and quality heads still provide gradient signal during training via their respective loss weights. The shared representation benefits from multi-task learning — the outputs are simply not used at inference/entry time.

Reference: docs/plans/EXPERIMENT_PLAN_THESIS_VALIDATION.md, Section 8.6.19 ("Optuna Parameter Control Matrix by Model Category"), Decision Log entries #17-#18.

For future use (post-thesis): Unlocking alpha to [0.0, 0.5] and probability_weight to [0.5, 1.0] would allow the entry signal to incorporate strength and quality, potentially reducing trade count by requiring "confident AND strong AND clean" signals. This is the highest-impact lever for a production system but is incompatible with the thesis comparison framework.

docs.title: 4.2 Lever: entry_threshold (bot.yaml)
docs.text:
Status: NOT RECOMMENDED FOR TIER B RESCUE — COMPARABILITY CONCERN

Current value: 0.55 (from config/experiments/bot.yaml)

What it controls:

  • Training: The entry gate loss uses it as reference: BCE((combined_confidence - entry_threshold) × 10, trade_target). The model learns to separate trade/no-trade predictions around this threshold.
  • Backtest: The bot rejects entries where combined_confidence < entry_threshold.
  • Live: Same gate as backtest.

Full data flow (verified):

config/experiments/bot.yaml (entry_threshold: 0.55)
  → BotConfig.strategy_config.entry_threshold
    → training.py line 213: float(experiment_config.bot_config.strategy_config.entry_threshold)
      → MultiOutputCriterion.__init__(entry_threshold=...) line 799
        → _entry_gate_loss(): entry_logits = (combined_confidence - 0.55) * 10.0
          → BCE_with_logits(entry_logits, trade_targets)

Why it would help: Raising to 0.65 would:

  • Shift the training target to require higher confidence for trade signals
  • Filter out the 0.55-0.65 "marginal confidence" trades
  • Estimated 20-40% trade reduction based on confidence distributions

Why we are NOT changing it: Changing entry_threshold in bot.yaml is a global config change that affects BOTH training AND backtesting fundamentally. Models trained with entry_threshold=0.65 learn a different objective than models trained with entry_threshold=0.55. This means:

  • Rescue results would not be comparable to previous Tier A, Tier B, and Tier B corrected runs
  • Seeds extracted from prior runs were trained with 0.55 — retraining neighbors at 0.65 changes the loss landscape, potentially invalidating the seed transfer hypothesis
  • The thesis comparison framework requires identical bot configuration across all experiments to isolate the model as the only variable

For future use (post-thesis): If the thesis establishes which models have genuine signal quality, a follow-up study could test entry_threshold ∈ {0.55, 0.60, 0.65, 0.70} as a controlled experiment to quantify the selectivity/profitability tradeoff. This is a one-line config change with full training + backtest propagation.

docs.title: 4.3 Lever: min_quality_threshold (bot.yaml)
docs.text:
Status: NOT RECOMMENDED FOR TIER B RESCUE — COMPARABILITY CONCERN

Current value: 0.30 (from config/experiments/bot.yaml)

What it controls: A post-combined-confidence gate in bot_logic.py that rejects trades where predicted quality (path cleanliness) is below the threshold. This is INDEPENDENT from combined_confidence — it operates as a separate filter.

Why we are NOT changing it: Same comparability argument as entry_threshold. Although min_quality_threshold does NOT affect training (it's purely a backtest/live filter), changing it would mean rescue backtest results use a different entry gate pipeline than all prior runs. The thesis must compare model quality under identical conditions.

Note: With alpha=0 and min_quality_threshold=0.30, this gate is extremely permissive. Quality would need to be below 0.30 (very choppy path prediction) to be filtered. In practice, most models produce quality > 0.30 for most inputs, making this gate nearly inactive.

For future use: Raising to 0.45-0.50 would filter "medium quality" entries without affecting training. This is a pure selectivity improvement for production use.

docs.title: 4.4 Lever: entry_gate Loss Weight (Optuna [0.1, 2.0]) — THE VIABLE LEVER
docs.text:
Status: ALREADY TUNABLE — THIS IS THE MAIN LEVER WE HAVE

Current range: [0.1, 2.0] (Optuna-tuned per trial)

What it controls: The entry_gate weight in the multi-output loss determines how much the training gradient prioritizes the entry gate BCE loss relative to classification, strength, and quality losses:

total_loss = cls_weight × classification_loss
           + str_weight × strength_loss
           + qual_weight × quality_loss
           + gate_weight × entry_gate_loss     ← THIS ONE

How it affects selectivity:

  • Low gate_weight (0.1): Model barely trains the entry gate head. The entry gate loss contributes tiny gradients compared to classification. Result: the model's combined_confidence distribution is diffuse — many predictions cluster around the 0.55 threshold, producing high trade counts with ambiguous confidence.
  • High gate_weight (1.5-2.0): Model strongly trains the entry gate head. The BCE loss (combined_confidence - 0.55) × 10 receives strong gradients. Result: the model learns to push predictions decisively above or below 0.55, producing a bimodal confidence distribution — fewer trades but with higher conviction.

Evidence from audit data (Section 7, Optuna Parameter Importance):

Experiment entry_gate Importance Rank Logistic-F4 (45 features) 31.3% #1 most important parameter Logistic-F2 (45 features) 11% Top 3 MLP studies Lower (~2-5%) Moderate

The entry_gate weight being the #1 most important parameter for Logistic-F4 is remarkable — it means the trading-signal calibration boundary matters MORE than any feature or training hyperparameter for that model/fold combination.

Why it is thesis-compatible:

  1. It is already part of the Optuna search space — all prior runs tuned it
  2. It does not change the bot configuration — all models see the same entry_threshold (0.55)
  3. It does not change what combined_confidence means — it only changes how strongly the model optimizes for the entry gate during training
  4. Optuna can discover the best value per model/fold combination automatically

Recommendation for Tier B Rescue: No range change needed. The [0.1, 2.0] range already allows Optuna to explore high selectivity (gate_weight → 2.0). The rescue run's 3-block structure (seeds → neighbors → TPE) should naturally discover whether high gate_weight helps or hurts for each model/fold.

Optional enhancement (if desired): The rescue could bias the neighbor generation to explore higher gate_weight values for seed trials that had low gate_weight. For example, if a seed trial had gate_weight=0.19 (like the best VIT trial T2798), a neighbor variant with gate_weight=1.5 would test whether that same model configuration becomes more selective with stronger gate training. This is within the existing search space and does not require any code or config changes.

docs.title: 4.5 Lever: classification, strength, quality Loss Weights (Optuna [0.1, 2.0])
docs.text:
Status: ALREADY TUNABLE — INDIRECT EFFECT

These are tuned by Optuna within [0.1, 2.0]. Their effect on selectivity is indirect through multi-task learning:

  • High classification weight: Model focuses on getting direction right → stronger probability predictions → more decisive signals
  • High quality weight: Model focuses on predicting path cleanliness → better internal representations → indirectly helps classification through shared features
  • High strength weight: Model focuses on predicting move magnitude → same mechanism

These do NOT directly control trade count but influence the confidence distribution through training dynamics. They are already optimized and do not require changes.

docs.title: 4.6 Lever: Model Architecture Hyperparameters (Optuna)
docs.text:
Status: ALREADY TUNABLE — STRUCTURAL EFFECT

Regularization parameters affect selectivity indirectly:

  • Dropout: Higher dropout → more conservative predictions → fewer extreme confidences → potentially fewer trades but also fewer correct entries
  • Weight decay: Higher weight decay → simpler learned function → potentially cleaner decision boundaries

From audit data (Section 3):

  • Best trial T2530 (TCN-F3, 17 trades): dropout=0.3, weight_decay=1.13e-06
  • Best VIT T2798 (194 trades): dropout=0.1, weight_decay=5.21e-03

The relationship between regularization and trade count is not monotonic — Optuna should search the space rather than us prescribing values.

docs.title: 5. Summary — What We Can and Cannot Touch
Cannot Touch (Thesis Integrity)
docs.text: Parameter Value Reason alpha 0.0 Confounds signal quality with filter tuning. combined_confidence = probability ensures all models are evaluated on classification quality alone. probability_weight 1.0 Same. No distortion of probability. strength_weight 1.0 Irrelevant (alpha=0). Fixed for cleanliness. quality_weight 1.0 Irrelevant (alpha=0). Fixed for cleanliness. entry_threshold 0.55 Changes training loss target AND backtest gate. Rescue results would be non-comparable to prior runs. min_quality_threshold 0.30 Changes backtest gate. Rescue results would be non-comparable to prior runs.

docs.title: Can Touch (Already Tunable, Thesis-Compatible)
docs.text: Parameter Range Mechanism entry_gate loss weight [0.1, 2.0] Primary lever. Controls how strongly the model trains its entry gate boundary. High values → bimodal confidence → fewer but more decisive trades. Was #1 most important param for Logistic-F4 (31.3%). classification loss weight [0.1, 2.0] Indirect. Stronger classification gradients → more decisive probabilities. strength loss weight [0.1, 2.0] Indirect. Multi-task regularization benefit. quality loss weight [0.1, 2.0] Indirect. Multi-task regularization benefit. Architecture hyperparams per model Indirect. Regularization (dropout, weight_decay) affects confidence distribution.

docs.title: 6. Actionable Recommendation for Tier B Rescue
docs.text:
1. No config changes required. The existing Optuna search space already contains the viable selectivity lever (entry_gate weight).
  2. Neighbor generation strategy: When extracting seeds and generating neighbors, consider including entry_gate weight as a dimension to perturb — especially for seeds with low gate weight values. If a seed had gate_weight=0.19, create neighbors at gate_weight ∈ {0.5, 1.0, 1.5} to test whether stronger gate training improves that configuration's selectivity.
  3. Analysis after rescue run: Track the correlation between entry_gate weight and trade count across all rescue trials. If high gate_weight consistently reduces trades while maintaining S2/S3, this validates the mechanism and informs future production config (where alpha, entry_threshold, and min_quality_threshold CAN be unlocked).

docs.title: 7. Future Work (Post-Thesis)
docs.text:
Once the thesis experiments are complete and the comparison framework is no longer a constraint, the following levers become available for a production optimization run:

Lever Priority Expected Impact Change Required Unlock alpha to [0.0, 0.5] HIGH Allows strength+quality to boost/attenuate entry signal. Most powerful selectivity mechanism. Tier config JSON: change alpha.max from 0.0 to 0.5 Raise entry_threshold to 0.60-0.65 HIGH Changes training target + backtest gate. Estimated 20-40% trade reduction. config/experiments/bot.yaml: 1 line change Raise min_quality_threshold to 0.45 MEDIUM Filters choppy-path entries. Post-training only. config/experiments/bot.yaml: 1 line change Unlock probability_weight to [0.5, 1.0] LOW Minor selectivity effect. Exponent on probability. Tier config JSON: change probability_weight.min from 1.0 to 0.5

These are documented here for reference. They should NOT be applied to any Script 2 thesis experiment.

docs.title: 8. Code References
docs.text: Component File Lines Combined confidence formula (scalar) modules/model-trainer/src/rainbow/model_trainer/helpers/combined_confidence.py 15-46 Combined confidence formula (tensor) modules/model-trainer/src/rainbow/model_trainer/helpers/combined_confidence.py 48-75 Entry gate loss computation modules/model-trainer/src/rainbow/model_trainer/losses/multi_output_criterion.py 68-90 MultiOutputCriterion init modules/model-trainer/src/rainbow/model_trainer/losses/multi_output_criterion.py 48-56 entry_threshold extraction in training modules/model-trainer/src/rainbow/model_trainer/helpers/model/training.py 213 entry_threshold passed to criterion modules/model-trainer/src/rainbow/model_trainer/helpers/model/training.py 799 Bot entry gate sequence modules/bot-logic/src/rainbow/bot_logic/bot_logic.py 483-496 Optuna combined_confidence_weights config config/experiments/optuna.yaml 212-230 Bot strategy thresholds config/experiments/bot.yaml strategy section Script 2 alpha=0 fixation (Tier B corrected) scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/generate_experiments/script2_tiers_config_v4_tierB_corrected.json 39-40 Parameter Control Matrix decision docs/plans/EXPERIMENT_PLAN_THESIS_VALIDATION.md Section 8.6.19 Decision Log entries #17-#18 docs/plans/EXPERIMENT_PLAN_THESIS_VALIDATION.md ~2210-2220

docs.title: 9. Audit Trail
docs.text: Date Action Author 2026-03-16 alpha=0.0, probability_weight=1.0 fixed for Script 2 (Decision #18) Manu + Agent 2026-03-31 Full selectivity analysis performed. Confirmed entry_gate loss weight as sole viable lever. Agent 2026-03-31 Decision: do NOT change entry_threshold or min_quality_threshold for thesis comparability. Manu 2026-03-31 Decision: do NOT unlock alpha for thesis fairness. Manu

docs.title: Script 2 Ensemble and Regime Status
docs.text:
• Date: 2026-04-01
  • Last Updated: 2026-04-03
  • Scope: Current status record for the Script 2 ensemble workflow and the still-pending regime work
  • Context: This document records what was actually built, which strategic decisions were taken, how the workflow currently operates, and what remains pending.
  • Status: Ensemble first pass implemented and usable. Regime bucket work still pending.

docs.title: 1. Purpose and Scope
docs.text:
This note exists to keep one practical record of the current Script 2 thesis-side work around:

  1. Ensemble backtesting across trained trials
  2. Trade analysis by regime buckets

The two topics remain connected, but they are at different maturity levels:

  • the ensemble workflow now exists end-to-end
  • the regime workflow still needs design finalization and implementation

This document focuses on:

  • what is already implemented
  • the strategic decisions that now define the ensemble workflow
  • the current operating model
  • the real pending work that still matters

docs.title: 2. Why This Work Exists
docs.text:
The current Script 2 evidence still suggests the same core thesis problem:

  • models appear to learn something
  • that signal rarely survives as robust net profitability
  • excessive trade count and friction remain major pain points

The current working intuition is:

  • different trained trials may capture different partial edges
  • a single trial may be too fragile to represent the best available signal for a fold
  • useful selectivity may come from stricter agreement rules
  • if edge is conditional on market structure, regime analysis may later explain where ensembles should or should not trade

That leads to a two-part roadmap:

  1. build a serious ensemble execution workflow first
  2. use regime buckets later to study whether edge depends on market context

docs.title: 3. Ensemble Work Completed
docs.text: The following ensemble components now exist and are usable.

docs.title: 3.1 Selection and Planning
docs.text:
A thesis-oriented planner now exists under ensemble_backtests/ and selects source trials through:

  • one or more --summary-csv inputs
  • one required --fold
  • one required --top-per-model
  • optional --tier
  • optional model filtering
  • configurable ranking modes

Primary planner inputs are:

  • one or more --summary-csv
  • one required --fold
  • one required --top-per-model

Its current selection model is:

  • same fold
  • cross-model
  • cross-run
  • top N per model
  • strict compatibility bucket validation after candidate gathering

This matches the intended thesis workflow and keeps selection aligned with the artifacts already used in the Script 2 reporting flow.

docs.title: 3.2 Compatibility Validation
docs.text:
Selected source trials are now validated through a strict execution-compatibility check.

At minimum, source members must share:

  • base_symbol
  • base_timeframe
  • effective stage-3 execution range
  • bot_config
  • simulation_config
  • combined_confidence_weights

The ensemble is therefore allowed to mix:

  • different model families
  • different runs
  • different experiments
  • optionally different tiers

but only when their execution context is truly compatible.

docs.title: 3.3 Synthetic Experiment, Study, and Trials
docs.text:
The ensemble workflow now creates:

  1. one synthetic experiment
  2. one synthetic study
  3. one synthetic trial per merge configuration

This keeps the workflow aligned with the current database and reporting model, which remains trial-centric.

The synthetic study folder now:

  • uses a proper checkpoint-style name
  • is marked as synthetic
  • includes a README.md
  • exists for traceability, not for real training checkpoints

docs.title: 3.4 Prediction Preparation
docs.text:
The ensemble workflow now has an explicit prediction preparation layer:

  1. look for a reusable predictions parquet
  2. validate strict ensemble schema
  3. validate coverage for the exact range
  4. reuse if valid
  5. regenerate if invalid or missing

The strict ensemble-ready persisted prediction payload now includes:

  • timestamp
  • signal
  • confidence
  • prob_none
  • prob_long
  • prob_short
  • strength
  • quality
  • combined_confidence

This is now the contract used by the ensemble merge logic.

docs.title: 3.5 Standalone Prediction Generation Improvements
docs.text:
The standalone prediction generation path used for regeneration was improved to make it practical:

  • it now calibrates batch sizes similarly to the backtesting engine
  • it persists once per outer batch
  • it keeps parquet fragmentation under control
  • it has better progress visibility during prediction generation

docs.title: 3.6 Backtesting From Precomputed Predictions
docs.text:
The backtesting engine now supports running from precomputed prediction frames for the synthetic ensemble trials.

This keeps ensemble execution focused on merged predictions rather than normal per-model inference inside the main backtest loop.

Conceptually, the workflow is now:

  1. select compatible trained trials
  2. prepare each source predictions artifact
  3. merge predictions into one synthetic prediction stream
  4. backtest directly from that merged prediction stream

docs.title: 3.7 Reporting and Artifacts
docs.text:
The ensemble workflow now produces a structured artifact bundle similar in spirit to extra_backtests:

  • plan.json
  • plan.md
  • results.json
  • results.csv
  • comparisons.csv
  • report.md
  • ensemble_backtests.log

The report now includes:

  • selection context
  • prediction preparation
  • selected source trials
  • ensemble executions
  • ranking across all runs
  • pairwise ensemble-vs-source comparisons
  • friction-aware metrics
  • optional selection-diversity diagnostics based on prediction correlation

docs.title: 4. Strategic Decisions
docs.text: These are the decisions that currently define the ensemble workflow.

docs.title: 4.1 Selection Strategy
docs.text:
The primary selection strategy is now:

  • same fold
  • cross-model
  • cross-run
  • top N per model

The planner is centered on choosing a compatible fold-wide source pool.

The fold is the main comparability anchor because it fixes the validation window and makes source trials more directly comparable.

docs.title: 4.2 Input UX
docs.text:
The main planner input is one or more script2_experiment_ids.csv files passed through --summary-csv.

This keeps planner usage aligned with the real thesis workflow, where experiment summaries are already the main selection artifact.

docs.title: 4.3 Baseline Policy
docs.text:
The ensemble runner does not rerun source baselines by default.

Instead, the default source baseline mode is:

  • use the original stage-3 backtest sessions from the selected trials

This keeps the workflow lighter and preserves meaningful comparisons.

An explicit source rerun remains available when needed.

docs.title: 4.4 Prediction Payload Policy
docs.text:
The main prediction payload is strict and explicit.

Important explicit decisions:

  • trade_confidence is not persisted in the ensemble prediction schema
  • full class probabilities are preserved
  • trade_probability may exist locally as a derived value, but not as a persisted redundant field

docs.title: 4.5 Synthetic Trial Policy
docs.text:
The workflow keeps synthetic experiment/study/trial records as the main execution-traceability model.

This was chosen because:

  • the current DB model remains trial-centric
  • the first-pass ensemble should fit the existing infrastructure
  • traceability matters more than domain purity at this stage

docs.title: 4.6 Merge Rule Policy
docs.text:
The merge system now supports the following family of rules:

  • average
  • weighted_average
  • all_must_agree
  • majority_vote[:threshold]
  • weighted_majority_vote[:threshold]

Important semantic decision:

For vote-based rules, the winner is resolved first, and the threshold is applied after winner resolution.

This means:

  • majority_vote
  • majority_vote:0
  • majority_vote:0.0

are equivalent by the new unified flow, not through legacy compatibility code.

Weighted rules use planner-derived selection_score values normalized into positive merge weights.

docs.title: 4.7 Prediction Diversity Policy
docs.text:
Correlation-aware selection is now treated as an important extension of the ensemble workflow.

The current design decision is:

  • prediction diversity should be measured on real predictions, not on proxy trial metrics
  • similarity should be evaluated on the real merge-relevant probabilities: prob_none, prob_long, and prob_short
  • the planner keeps its existing top_per_model structure
  • when correlation-aware selection is enabled, each new candidate is compared against the full selected pool
  • overly redundant candidates are skipped and replaced by the next ranked candidate from that same model family

This keeps the current planner shape intact while reducing the risk of building an ensemble from near-duplicate members.

docs.title: 4.8 Friction Lens
docs.text:
The ensemble reporting layer now includes a friction-aware lens aligned with the extra-backtests workflow.

Current ensemble reporting now tracks:

  • estimated friction
  • PnL + friction
  • gross-per-trade proxy

This is important because net PnL improvements alone are not sufficient if they come only from friction or trade-count artifacts.

docs.title: 5. Current Workflow
docs.text:
The current ensemble workflow is:

  1. choose one or more --summary-csv files from real Script 2 runs
  2. choose one --fold
  3. optionally restrict by --tier and/or --models
  4. choose --top-per-model
  5. rank candidates by one configured selection metric
  6. split candidates into strict compatibility buckets
  7. keep the best bucket
  8. select the top N members per model inside that bucket
  9. optionally prepare predictions during planning when prediction-correlation diversity is enabled
  10. persist the full plan into plan.json
  11. prepare reusable or regenerated prediction artifacts
  12. create synthetic experiment/study/trials
  13. run one ensemble backtest per configured merge rule
  14. compare the merged runs against the original stage-3 source baselines
  15. render a final report

docs.title: 5.1 Current Ranking Modes
docs.text:
The planner currently supports:

  • promise
  • friction
  • objective
  • pnl
  • stage2

Current default:

  • promise

The friction mode intentionally follows the same scoring family used by extra_backtests.

docs.title: 5.2 Current Correlation-Aware Selection
docs.text:
The planner can now operate in two selection styles:

  • score-only shortlist selection
  • score-first shortlist selection with prediction-correlation filtering

When the correlation-aware mode is enabled:

  • the planner may load reusable predictions during planning
  • missing predictions may be generated during planning
  • Pearson correlation is evaluated separately on prob_none, prob_long, and prob_short
  • the largest positive probability correlation is used as the redundancy check
  • redundancy is checked against the full selected pool

This is intentionally heavier than pure score-only planning, but it gives the ensemble workflow a real way to prefer behavioral diversity instead of only architectural diversity.

docs.title: 5.3 Current Default Merge Rule Set
docs.text:
The default exploratory merge-rule set currently includes:

  • average
  • majority_vote
  • all_must_agree
  • majority_vote:0.75
  • majority_vote:0.85
  • weighted_average
  • weighted_majority_vote:0.75
  • weighted_majority_vote:0.85

This is not the final thesis conclusion set.

It is the current working battery for broader experimentation.

docs.title: 5.4 Current Output Style
docs.text:
The workflow is now designed so that:

  • plan.md explains how the source pool was selected
  • report.md explains how the ensemble executions behaved
  • results.json/csv and comparisons.csv provide machine-readable outputs

The report is meant to be human-readable first while still preserving enough structure for downstream comparisons.

docs.title: 6. Non-Final Exploratory Status
docs.text:
Initial exploratory runs have already been executed to validate the workflow end-to-end.

In addition, the first systematic discovery batch has now been executed:

  • 20260403_021500_phase1_block_a
  • 72 completed executions
  • 576 ensemble rule runs

That batch should still be treated as preliminary rather than final thesis evidence.

However, it is already strong enough to support two practical conclusions:

  • the ensemble direction is economically promising and worth continuing
  • the current workflow can now produce meaningful campaign-level evidence, not just infrastructure validation

These runs are useful for:

  • checking that the planner works
  • checking that synthetic context creation works
  • checking that prediction preparation works
  • checking that the report and comparison outputs are coherent
  • checking that merge rules can be explored safely

These runs are not treated as thesis findings.

Current ensemble execution results should be considered:

  • exploratory
  • infrastructure validation
  • useful for workflow iteration

Formal interpretation should still come only after broader, multi-block ensemble runs are executed under a deliberate experiment plan.

The first systematic batch already exists, but it should be treated as:

  • preliminary evidence
  • selectivity-oriented validation
  • support for continuing to later blocks, not as a final merge-rule verdict

One especially important caution from that batch is:

  • merge-rule rankings are still weak when the selected pools are very small
  • with only 2 or 3 members, several merge rules can collapse to very similar behavior
  • stronger merge-rule conclusions should wait for larger and broader multi-member ensembles

docs.title: 7. Pending Work
docs.text: The following work remains pending and important.

docs.title: 7.1 Regime Buckets
docs.text:
The regime side is still pending implementation.

Work still required:

  • the exact 27 -> 9 -> 3 bucket mapping
  • the operational meaning of each grouped bucket
  • the trade-tagging strategy
  • the report format for regime summaries

docs.title: 7.2 Regime Mining
docs.text:
The next regime-oriented layer goes beyond bucket counting.

It should eventually answer questions like:

  • which regimes concentrate profitable trades
  • which regimes concentrate losses
  • whether some model families have stable regime preferences
  • whether ensemble gating should later be made regime-aware

docs.title: 7.3 Large-Scale Ensemble Batch
docs.text:
The current workflow still needs a larger, deliberate ensemble run campaign.

That future batch should be used to:

  • stress the current merge rules across multiple folds and candidate pools
  • compare ranking modes more systematically
  • test stricter vote thresholds more broadly
  • test whether weighted rules outperform unweighted rules anywhere
  • test whether prediction-correlation-aware selection produces better source pools than score-only selection
  • inspect whether gains come from signal quality, selectivity, or both

docs.title: 7.4 Merge Rule Exploration
docs.text:
The merge framework is flexible enough to support broader experimentation, but the exploration itself is still pending.

Important open execution questions:

  • which thresholds deserve full study
  • whether weighted rules help in practice
  • whether cross-model pools beat narrower pools
  • whether some merge rules mainly improve by reducing trades

docs.title: 7.5 Workflow Cleanup
docs.text:
Some practical workflow cleanup is still worth doing as larger batches are executed:

  • log noise reduction
  • report polish
  • better run naming conventions where useful
  • batch execution ergonomics

These are not the thesis question, but they matter because they affect the speed and reliability of iteration.

docs.title: 8. Next Steps
docs.text:
The next recommended sequence is:

  1. use the current ensemble workflow to run a broader backtest battery
  2. compare merge modes, thresholds, weighting, and ranking strategies at larger scale
  3. only then treat any ensemble result as a real finding
  4. after that, implement regime buckets and regime mining
  5. use the regime work to guide a later regime-aware ensemble phase if the evidence supports it

In short:

  • the ensemble infrastructure is ready for real experimentation
  • the regime work is the main pending research extension
  • the immediate next milestone is a larger and more deliberate ensemble campaign

docs.title: Research Notes: Ensemble Methods, Stacking, and Numerai-Style Aggregation
Purpose
docs.text:
This document consolidates the main findings and practical ideas discussed about ensemble modeling for trading systems, with focus on:

  • ensemble methods in machine learning and quantitative finance
  • why ensembles can outperform a single model
  • common aggregation rules
  • stacking and meta-model design
  • Numerai-style rank aggregation
  • correlation penalties and model diversity
  • numeric examples
  • practical implications for a trading system such as Rainbow Trader

docs.title: 1. Ensemble Methods in Finance and Machine Learning
1.1 The Elements of Statistical Learning — Hastie, Tibshirani, Friedman
docs.text:
This is not specific to trading, but it is one of the main classical references for ensemble methods.

It introduces and develops:

  • bagging
  • boosting
  • stacking

Core conclusion:

  • ensembles can reduce variance
  • ensembles can reduce overfitting
  • ensembles often improve generalization compared with a single model

This is one of the conceptual foundations behind many practical ensemble systems, including quantitative models.

docs.title: 1.2 Advances in Financial Machine Learning — Marcos López de Prado
docs.text:
This is a major reference in quantitative finance.

It discusses ideas highly relevant to ensemble-based trading systems, including:

  • ensemble of classifiers
  • meta-labeling
  • bagging with financial features

A central practical idea is:

  • model 1 predicts a signal
  • model 2 decides whether that signal is worth acting on

That is already a hierarchical ensemble.

This is especially important in trading because the question is not only whether price may move, but whether that prediction is actionable under risk, noise, and execution constraints.

docs.title: 1.3 Super Learner — van der Laan et al. (2007)
docs.text:
This is one of the important references on stacking.

Main idea:

  • combine multiple models through optimized weights
  • do not rely only on plain average
  • learn how to combine models from validation behavior

Instead of saying all models count equally, the system learns which models deserve more influence.

This is conceptually close to what many practical meta-model systems do.

docs.title: 1.4 Deep learning comparisons for financial time series
docs.text:
A broad family of papers in time-series forecasting and financial ML compare model families such as:

  • LSTM
  • CNN
  • Transformer
  • XGBoost
  • LightGBM

A common finding is:

  • an ensemble often performs better or more robustly than a single model

This usually happens not because each model is excellent alone, but because each one captures different structures and fails in different ways.

docs.title: 1.5 Numerai-style research direction
docs.text:
Numerai is one of the clearest modern examples of large-scale ensemble thinking.

Conceptually, Numerai uses:

  • many independent models
  • aggregation of submitted predictions
  • rank-based normalization
  • preference for diversified signals
  • attention to correlation between models

Its logic is not just simple mean of raw probabilities.

The core ideas are closer to:

  • rank averaging
  • diversity reward
  • correlation control

docs.title: 2. Main aggregation methods
docs.text: There are several standard ways to consolidate predictions from multiple models.

docs.title: 2.1 Average (mean)
docs.text:
The simplest method.

If models produce probabilities:

P_final = (P1 + P2 + P3 + ... + Pn) / n

Advantages:

  • simple
  • stable
  • easy to implement
  • useful as a baseline

Weakness:

  • weak models affect the result
  • it assumes all models are equally useful
  • it does not learn context

docs.title: 2.2 Weighted average
docs.text:
Very common in trading.

P_final = w1 * P1 + w2 * P2 + ... + wn * Pn

Weights can be assigned using:

  • validation score
  • Sharpe ratio
  • accuracy
  • F1
  • precision on tradeable classes
  • stability across time splits

Example:

Model A weight = 0.5
Model B weight = 0.3
Model C weight = 0.2

This is often better than plain average because stronger models influence the result more.

docs.title: 2.3 Majority vote
docs.text:
Used for classification.

Example:

Model 1 = LONG
Model 2 = LONG
Model 3 = SHORT

Final = LONG

This is common in classification ensembles such as random forests.

Weakness:

  • ignores confidence
  • treats weak and strong signals the same

docs.title: 2.4 Probability averaging plus threshold
docs.text:
Very common in trading systems.

Example:

P_long = average(probabilities)

Trade only if:

P_long > 0.60

This is practical because the ensemble can provide one continuous score, and the trading engine decides whether the confidence is sufficient.

docs.title: 2.5 Stacking (meta-model)
docs.text:
One of the strongest methods.

Process:

  • model 1 prediction
  • model 2 prediction
  • model 3 prediction

These predictions become features for a second-level model:

X_meta = [pred_model1, pred_model2, pred_model3]

The meta-model can be:

  • logistic regression
  • XGBoost
  • neural network
  • LightGBM

This second-level model learns when to trust each model.

This is stronger than hand-written averaging because the combination is learned from data.

docs.title: 2.6 Rank averaging (Numerai style)
docs.text:
Numerai-style aggregation avoids depending too much on calibration scale.

Each model produces:

predictions -> ranks

Then aggregation becomes:

final = mean(ranks)

This allows models with different score scales to still combine well.

The ensemble depends on ordering, not absolute probability level.

docs.title: 2.7 Correlation-penalized ensemble
docs.text:
A strong practical idea in ensemble design is that two models that predict nearly the same thing do not add much combined value.

A conceptual scoring view is:

score = performance - lambda * correlation

This encourages:

  • predictive models
  • low redundancy
  • diverse errors

This is important in trading because many models can look different architecturally but still produce almost identical signals.

docs.title: 3. Practical rules often used by quants
docs.text:
In trading, useful ensemble design usually emphasizes:

  • model diversity
  • low correlation between predictions
  • low correlation between errors
  • weighted blending or stacking instead of blind averaging

A typical practical pipeline is:

train many models
evaluate on validation
remove correlated models
build weighted or stacked ensemble

The logic is that diversity can matter more than standalone score.

A model with slightly worse individual performance can still improve the portfolio of predictions if it fails differently.

docs.title: 4. Why ensembles often outperform a single model
docs.text:
Many machine learning and quantitative finance works suggest that an ensemble can be more robust than a single model.

Main reasons:

  • reduction of variance
  • reduction of dependence on one model's mistakes
  • combination of different pattern detectors
  • better robustness across changing market conditions
  • improvement when model errors are not perfectly correlated

In trading, this is often more important than getting the best isolated validation score with one model.

A model with slightly lower standalone performance may still be very useful if its errors are different from the others.

docs.title: 5.1 Core idea
docs.text:
Suppose we have:

  • Model A
  • Model B
  • Model C

Each model predicts the probability that the next move is positive.

Instead of combining them by hand, we train a new model on top of their predictions.

The meta-model input becomes:

X_meta = [predA, predB, predC]

The target is still the real label.

So the meta-model can learn rules such as:

  • trust Model A when it is very confident
  • trust Model B more in certain situations
  • increase confidence when B and C agree
  • ignore a model when it becomes noisy in a specific region

docs.title: 5.2 Numeric example
docs.text:
Suppose the original task is binary classification:

  • 1 = LONG
  • 0 = NOT LONG

The base models produce:

Sample Real Model A Model B Model C 1 1 0.70 0.40 0.60 2 0 0.60 0.30 0.40 3 1 0.80 0.70 0.50 4 0 0.40 0.20 0.30 5 1 0.65 0.55 0.60

The meta-dataset becomes:

Sample predA predB predC target 1 0.70 0.40 0.60 1 2 0.60 0.30 0.40 0 3 0.80 0.70 0.50 1 4 0.40 0.20 0.30 0 5 0.65 0.55 0.60 1

Now train a meta-model, for example logistic regression.

Suppose it learns a rule similar to:

final_score = 0.6 * predA + 0.2 * predB + 0.3 * predC

This is only an illustration. A real logistic regression also applies bias and sigmoid.

Now suppose a new sample produces:

predA = 0.75
predB = 0.40
predC = 0.65

Then:

final_score = 0.6 * 0.75 + 0.2 * 0.40 + 0.3 * 0.65
final_score = 0.45 + 0.08 + 0.195
final_score = 0.725

If the trading threshold is 0.60, this becomes a valid LONG signal.

docs.title: 5.3 Why stacking can be stronger than averaging
docs.text:
A simple average would do:

(0.75 + 0.40 + 0.65) / 3 = 0.60

This gives 0.60.

The stacked model gave 0.725 because it learned that:

  • Model A is more reliable
  • Model B contributes less
  • Model C still adds useful information

So stacking can express learned trust, not just equal blending.

docs.title: 5.4 Important warning: data leakage
docs.text:
A stacking pipeline must be built carefully.

If the meta-model is trained on predictions generated from the same data used to train the base models, the result is contaminated.

The correct approach is to use out-of-fold predictions.

Basic idea:

  1. split the training data into folds
  2. for each fold:
  3. train base models on the other folds
  4. predict on the held-out fold
  5. collect all held-out predictions
  6. use those predictions to train the meta-model

This gives honest inputs for the second layer.

docs.title: 6. Numerai-style aggregation
docs.text:
Numerai is known for combining many model predictions, but not by trusting raw score scales directly.

One key idea is rank-based aggregation.

docs.title: 6.1 Why ranks
docs.text:
Different models may use different score scales.

Example:

Sample Model A Model B 1 0.90 0.10 2 0.70 0.20 3 0.40 0.80 4 0.20 0.60 5 0.10 0.40

If raw values are averaged directly, scale differences may distort the combination.

Instead, each model's predictions are converted into ranks.

docs.title: 6.2 Numeric rank example
docs.text:
For Model A:

Value Rank 0.10 0.00 0.20 0.25 0.40 0.50 0.70 0.75 0.90 1.00

For Model B:

Value Rank 0.10 0.00 0.20 0.25 0.40 0.50 0.60 0.75 0.80 1.00

Now the ranked predictions are:

Sample rankA rankB 1 1.00 0.00 2 0.75 0.25 3 0.50 1.00 4 0.25 0.75 5 0.00 0.50

The Numerai-style ensemble can then be:

final_rank = (rankA + rankB) / 2

Result:

Sample final_rank 1 0.50 2 0.50 3 0.75 4 0.50 5 0.25

This avoids depending on absolute calibration.

The system combines relative ordering, not raw probabilities.

docs.title: 7. Correlation and model diversity
docs.text:
A central idea in ensemble design is that different models are useful when they are both:

  • individually informative
  • not too correlated with each other

If two models make almost the same predictions, the second adds little value.

If two models have similar standalone performance but make different mistakes, the ensemble can improve a lot.

docs.title: 7.1 Small intuition example
docs.text:
Suppose two models each have accuracy 0.75, but their mistakes happen on different samples.

Then a simple vote or average may correct those mistakes.

Example:

Sample Model A Model B Target 1 1 1 1 2 1 0 1 3 0 1 1 4 0 0 0

Model A accuracy:

  • correct on 1, 2, 4
  • 0.75

Model B accuracy:

  • correct on 1, 3, 4
  • 0.75

A sensible ensemble can recover the correct answer on all four samples.

This is why diversity matters.

docs.title: 8. What Numerai conceptually encourages
docs.text:
At a high level, a Numerai-style system favors:

  • models with predictive signal
  • models with differentiated behavior
  • rank-based aggregation
  • resistance to calibration mismatch
  • reduced dependence on one single model

The exact internal details may vary by era and competition design, but the general pattern is:

  • predictions are normalized by rank
  • many models are blended
  • diversity is valuable
  • correlation between models matters

docs.title: 9. What this means for a trading system
docs.text: For a trading system, there are several practical ensemble designs.

docs.title: 9.1 Simple weighted probability ensemble
docs.text:
Each model produces:

  • P(LONG)
  • P(SHORT)
  • P(NONE)

Then:

P_final = w1 * predA + w2 * predB + w3 * predC

Trade only if confidence exceeds a threshold.

This is a strong baseline.

docs.title: 9.2 Stacked trading ensemble
docs.text:
Base models can be different model families:

  • Transformer
  • XGBoost
  • LSTM
  • CNN
  • LightGBM
  • logistic regression on handcrafted features

The meta-model can receive:

  • model probabilities
  • confidence margin
  • disagreement measures
  • regime features
  • volatility features
  • spread or execution context

This allows the second layer to learn when a model is trustworthy.

docs.title: 9.3 Regime-aware ensemble
docs.text:
One strong extension is to combine models differently depending on market regime.

Example regimes:

  • trending
  • mean-reverting
  • low volatility
  • high volatility
  • risk-on
  • risk-off

Then the combination rule can change by regime.

This can be implemented by:

  • explicit regime classifier
  • regime feature as input to the meta-model
  • separate ensemble weights per regime

This is one of the practical extensions that many trading systems can benefit from.

docs.title: 9.4 Timeframe ensemble
docs.text:
Another useful design is to combine models trained on different time horizons.

Example:

  • one model on 5m
  • one model on 1h
  • one model on 4h context

Then the meta-model can learn things such as:

  • a short-term signal is strong only when higher timeframe context supports it
  • a lower timeframe signal should be ignored in unstable conditions

docs.title: 10. What I would likely do for Rainbow Trader
docs.text:
A strong setup would be something like:

  • Model A: Transformer
  • Model B: XGBoost
  • Model C: LSTM
  • Model D: CNN

Each one produces:

  • P(LONG)
  • P(SHORT)
  • P(NONE)

Then combine with either:

P_final = weighted_average

or:

meta_model(predA, predB, predC, predD, regime_features, volatility_features)

A key point:

In trading, diversity is often more important than the best standalone score.

An ensemble works best when models have:

  • useful signal
  • low correlation in errors

not when all models are effectively the same predictor in different wrappers.

docs.title: 11. Practical implementation path
docs.text: A practical order of implementation is:

docs.title: Step 1
docs.text: Build a simple weighted average ensemble.

docs.title: Step 2
docs.text:
Measure:

  • standalone performance
  • correlation between model predictions
  • correlation between model errors

docs.title: Step 3
docs.text: Keep only models that add differentiated information.

docs.title: Step 4
docs.text: Build proper stacking with out-of-fold predictions.

docs.title: Step 5
docs.text:
Test regime-aware or timeframe-aware stacking.

This progression is usually better than jumping straight into a complex meta-architecture.

docs.title: 12. Key conclusions
docs.text:
1. Ensembles often outperform single models because they reduce variance and combine different strengths.

  2. Stacking is more powerful than plain averaging because it learns when to trust each model.

  3. Proper stacking requires out-of-fold predictions to avoid leakage.

  4. Numerai-style aggregation emphasizes rank-based blending rather than raw score averaging.

  5. Diversity matters more than simply having many models.

  6. In trading, disagreement, regime dependence, and execution context can matter as much as standalone validation metrics.

  7. A useful path for a trading system is:

  8. weighted ensemble first
  9. then stacking
  10. then regime-aware and multi-timeframe extensions

docs.title: 13. Future research directions
docs.text:
Possible next research directions:

  • compare weighted average vs stacking on the same walk-forward splits
  • measure prediction correlation and error correlation between model families
  • test rank averaging against probability averaging
  • study regime-specific ensemble weights
  • test whether a meta-model should include execution-related inputs such as spread, volatility, or confidence dispersion

docs.title: Script 2 Ensemble Discovery Campaign
docs.text:
• Date: 2026-04-03
  • Scope: Phase 1 systematic ensemble campaign for Script 2
  • Goal: find which model families and cross-model combinations have the best chance of producing net profit
  • Status: ready for execution

docs.title: 1. Purpose
docs.text:
This document defines the first large ensemble campaign to answer one practical thesis-side question:

Which models, and which combinations of models, have the best chance of making money under real friction?

This is not a generic grid search.

The campaign is intentionally centered on:

  • the folds where positive net PnL already appeared
  • the runs that currently define the strongest Tier B frontier
  • merge rules that explicitly test selectivity versus permissiveness
  • diversity-aware selection as a first-class axis, not as a side experiment

docs.title: 2. Evidence Base
docs.text: The campaign is driven by the following evidence.

docs.title: 2.1 Folds
docs.text:
From 20260401_SCRIPT2_PRELIMINARY_ANALYSIS.md:

  • only 14 / 1185 trainable trials have positive PnL
  • all positive trials are in F1 or F3
  • F2 and F4 produced zero positive trials

Decision: Phase 1 targets F1 and F3 only.

docs.title: 2.2 Core problem
docs.text:
From 20260331_SCRIPT2_SECTION11_REGEN.md:

  • Trades vs PnL = -0.81
  • S2 vs PnL = -0.01
  • friction drag dominates monetization

From 20260331_TRADE_SELECTIVITY_ANALYSIS.md:

  • the current system already shows that selectivity is the main lever
  • stricter gating is a plausible path to monetization

Decision: merge-rule coverage must include permissive, selective, and highly selective rules.

docs.title: 2.3 Runs to use
docs.text:
From 20260331_SCRIPT2_SECTION2_REGEN.md:

  • 20260325_tierB_mlp_ex contributes heavily to the current best frontier
  • 20260328_tierB_corrected is the corrected forced-feature Tier B run and must be part of the search frontier

From 20260328_SCRIPT2_V4_TIER_B_CORRECTED_AUDIT.md:

  • 20260328_tierB_corrected is structurally different from 20260325_tierB_mlp_ex
  • its role is not redundant; it is the forced-feature contrast

Decision: Phase 1 uses the combined source pool:

  • 20260325_tierB_mlp_ex
  • 20260328_tierB_corrected

This maximizes the candidate frontier while staying inside the strongest currently audited Tier B sources.

docs.title: 2.4 Model families
docs.text:
From 20260331_SCRIPT2_TIER_B_RESCUE_AUDIT.md:

  • TCN is the strongest overall money-oriented family in Tier B
  • VIT is consistently strong and appears repeatedly in top rankings
  • MLP remains a credible money candidate
  • logistic is useful as a baseline/simple family but not a core money leader
  • mlp_ex is powerful but overfitting-prone
  • axial_vit is exploratory

Decision: Phase 1 centers on:

  • mlp
  • tcn
  • vit

and includes one broader cross-model pool with logistic to test whether a simple family helps stabilize voting.

docs.title: 2.5 Diversity-aware selection
docs.text:
From the current ensemble work in 20260401_SCRIPT2_ENSEMBLE_AND_REGIME_IDEAS.md:

  • prediction-diversity-aware planning is now implemented
  • it can constrain:
  • probability correlation on prob_none, prob_long, prob_short
  • signal agreement on final LONG/SHORT/NONE decisions

Decision: diversity is a primary axis of Phase 1, not an optional afterthought.

docs.title: 3. Phase 1 Design
docs.text:
Phase 1 is a discovery campaign.

Its job is:

  • identify which families can monetize as single-family ensembles
  • identify which cross-model combinations look stronger than single-family pools
  • test whether diversity-aware selection helps or hurts monetization

Phase 1 does not try to answer every possible question.

It does not include:

  • Tier A
  • 20260401_tierB_rescue_v5
  • axial_vit
  • mlp_ex as a core Phase 1 family
  • F2 or F4

Those can come in Phase 2 after we see what looks alive.

docs.title: 4.1 Fixed source pool
docs.text:
All Phase 1 plans use these two summary CSVs together:

  • scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/results/20260325_tierB_mlp_ex/script2_experiment_ids.csv
  • scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/results/20260328_tierB_corrected/script2_experiment_ids.csv

docs.title: 4.2 Folds
docs.text:
• F1
  • F3

docs.title: 4.3 Ranking modes
docs.text:
• promise
  • friction

docs.title: 4.4 Diversity modes
docs.text:
Three planner modes are used:

  1. off No diversity filter.

  2. mild

  3. --max-prob-corr 0.85

  4. --max-signal-agreement 0.60

  5. tight

  6. --max-prob-corr 0.60
  7. --max-signal-agreement 0.45

These are not claimed as optimal values. They are simply the first coarse ladder for answering:

  • does diversity-aware selection help at all?
  • and does a tighter diversity constraint help more than a softer one?

docs.title: 4.5 Merge rules
docs.text:
Every plan uses the current full 8-rule set:

  • average
  • majority_vote
  • all_must_agree
  • majority_vote:0.75
  • majority_vote:0.85
  • weighted_average
  • weighted_majority_vote:0.75
  • weighted_majority_vote:0.85

This is important because Phase 1 is not only about source selection. It is also about the merge rule itself.

docs.title: 5. Block Structure
docs.text: Phase 1 contains two blocks.

docs.title: 5.1 Block A — Single-Family Monetization
docs.text:
Purpose:

  • test whether multiple trials from the same family can stabilize and monetize that family's edge

Families:

  • mlp
  • tcn
  • vit

Top-per-model:

  • 2
  • 3

Count:

  • 3 families
  • 2 folds
  • 2 rankings
  • 2 top-per-model values
  • 3 diversity modes

Total:

  • 3 * 2 * 2 * 2 * 3 = 72 plans

docs.title: 5.2 Block B — Cross-Model Monetization
docs.text:
Purpose:

  • test whether certain family combinations are stronger than any one family alone

Model pools:

  1. tcn,vit
  2. mlp,tcn
  3. mlp,vit
  4. mlp,tcn,vit
  5. logistic,mlp,tcn,vit

Top-per-model:

  • 1

Rationale:

  • tcn,vit tests the two strongest money/frontier families together
  • mlp,tcn tests whether mlp complements tcn
  • mlp,vit tests whether mlp complements vit
  • mlp,tcn,vit is the main three-family money pool
  • logistic,mlp,tcn,vit tests whether the simple linear family adds stabilizing value rather than just noise

Count:

  • 5 model pools
  • 2 folds
  • 2 rankings
  • 1 top-per-model value
  • 3 diversity modes

Total:

  • 5 * 2 * 2 * 1 * 3 = 60 plans

docs.title: 5.3 Total Phase 1 Size
docs.text:
• Block A: 72 plans
  • Block B: 60 plans

Phase 1 total:

  • 132 plans

With 8 merge rules each:

  • 132 * 8 = 1056 ensemble backtests

docs.title: 6. Command Template
docs.text:
All Phase 1 commands use the same shell helpers.

source .venv/bin/activate

PLANNER="scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/ensemble_backtests/generate_ensemble_backtest_plan.py"
RUNNER="scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/ensemble_backtests/run_ensemble_backtests.py"
ROOT="scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/ensemble_backtests/results/20260403_phase1_discovery"

SRC_A="scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/results/20260325_tierB_mlp_ex/script2_experiment_ids.csv"
SRC_B="scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/results/20260328_tierB_corrected/script2_experiment_ids.csv"

MERGE8="average,majority_vote,all_must_agree,majority_vote:0.75,majority_vote:0.85,weighted_average,weighted_majority_vote:0.75,weighted_majority_vote:0.85"

gen_plan() {
  local name="$1"
  local fold="$2"
  local rank_by="$3"
  local top_per_model="$4"
  local models="$5"
  local diversity="$6"

  shift 6

  local extra=()
  if [ "$diversity" = "mild" ]; then
    extra=(--max-prob-corr 0.85 --max-signal-agreement 0.60)
  elif [ "$diversity" = "tight" ]; then
    extra=(--max-prob-corr 0.60 --max-signal-agreement 0.45)
  fi

  .venv/bin/python "$PLANNER" \
    --summary-csv "$SRC_A" \
    --summary-csv "$SRC_B" \
    --fold "$fold" \
    --tier B \
    --models "$models" \
    --top-per-model "$top_per_model" \
    --rank-by "$rank_by" \
    --merge-rules "$MERGE8" \
    "${extra[@]}" \
    --output-dir "$ROOT/$name"
}

run_plan() {
  local name="$1"
  .venv/bin/python "$RUNNER" --plan-json "$ROOT/$name/plan.json"
}

mkdir -p "$ROOT"

docs.title: 7. Block A Commands — Single-Family Monetization
docs.text:
for fold in 1 3; do
  for rank_by in promise friction; do
    for top_per_model in 2 3; do
      for model in mlp tcn vit; do
        for diversity in off mild tight; do
          name="A_${model}_f${fold}_${rank_by}_top${top_per_model}_${diversity}"
          gen_plan "$name" "$fold" "$rank_by" "$top_per_model" "$model" "$diversity"
          run_plan "$name"
        done
      done
    done
  done
done

This block produces 72 plans.

What it answers:

  • can one family monetize better when we ensemble several of its own best trials?
  • does that answer differ between F1 and F3?
  • does friction ranking select better members than promise ranking?
  • does diversity-aware selection help even inside one family?

docs.title: 8. Block B Commands — Cross-Model Monetization
docs.text:
for fold in 1 3; do
  for rank_by in promise friction; do
    for diversity in off mild tight; do
      name="B_tcn_vit_f${fold}_${rank_by}_top1_${diversity}"
      gen_plan "$name" "$fold" "$rank_by" 1 "tcn,vit" "$diversity"
      run_plan "$name"

      name="B_mlp_tcn_f${fold}_${rank_by}_top1_${diversity}"
      gen_plan "$name" "$fold" "$rank_by" 1 "mlp,tcn" "$diversity"
      run_plan "$name"

      name="B_mlp_vit_f${fold}_${rank_by}_top1_${diversity}"
      gen_plan "$name" "$fold" "$rank_by" 1 "mlp,vit" "$diversity"
      run_plan "$name"

      name="B_mlp_tcn_vit_f${fold}_${rank_by}_top1_${diversity}"
      gen_plan "$name" "$fold" "$rank_by" 1 "mlp,tcn,vit" "$diversity"
      run_plan "$name"

      name="B_log_mlp_tcn_vit_f${fold}_${rank_by}_top1_${diversity}"
      gen_plan "$name" "$fold" "$rank_by" 1 "logistic,mlp,tcn,vit" "$diversity"
      run_plan "$name"
    done
  done
done

This block produces 60 plans.

What it answers:

  • which pair or trio of money-relevant families combine best?
  • does logistic add stabilizing signal or just dilute the pool?
  • does diversity-aware selection improve the cross-model pools more than it improves single-family pools?

docs.title: 9. Recommended Execution Order
docs.text:
Do not run the 132 plans in arbitrary order.

Use this order:

  1. Block B first, rank_by=promise, diversity=off
  2. Block B, rank_by=promise, diversity=mild
  3. Block B, rank_by=friction, diversity=off
  4. Block B, rank_by=friction, diversity=mild
  5. Block A, rank_by=promise, diversity=off
  6. Block A, rank_by=promise, diversity=mild
  7. Add all tight runs only after the earlier groups finish cleanly

Why:

  • the cross-model pools are the main thesis question
  • promise is the main ranking hypothesis
  • friction is the main challenger ranking
  • tight diversity is important, but it should not block the first read of the study

docs.title: 10. What To Audit After Phase 1
docs.text:
For every completed run family, compare:

  • best ensemble net PnL
  • best ensemble PnL+Fric
  • best ensemble gross/trade
  • whether the winner came from:
  • single-family
  • cross-model
  • diversity off
  • diversity mild
  • diversity tight
  • promise
  • friction

The most important questions after Phase 1 are:

  1. Which family or family-combination appears most often among the least-bad or profitable runs?
  2. Does diversity-aware selection improve monetization, or only reduce trade count without real edge preservation?
  3. Which merge rules win most often for the strongest source pools?
  4. Does friction ranking beat promise ranking when judged by net PnL and gross proxy?

docs.title: 11. Explicitly Deferred To Phase 2
docs.text:
Phase 2 should come only after Phase 1 has been audited.

Deferred items:

  • F2 and F4 control folds
  • source provenance ablation:
  • 20260325 only
  • 20260328 only
  • combined
  • mlp_ex expansion
  • axial_vit exploration
  • tighter diversity ladders beyond off / mild / tight
  • ranking sensitivity beyond promise / friction
  • any use of 20260401_tierB_rescue_v5

docs.title: 12. Bottom Line
docs.text:
This Phase 1 campaign is intentionally money-oriented.

It is not trying to prove every abstract ensemble idea.

It is trying to answer:

  • whether mlp, tcn, vit, and their combinations can be turned into better net trading outcomes
  • whether diversity-aware selection helps those pools
  • and which merge rules are most compatible with the surviving pools

If Phase 1 produces signal, Phase 2 can then expand and validate.

docs.title: Script 2 Ensemble Backtests
docs.text:
This folder contains the thesis workflow for testing whether a group of compatible Script 2 models can produce a better trading signal than the individual source trials.

The workflow is intentionally split into three steps:

  1. generate a reproducible plan from one or more real Script 2 runs
  2. execute that plan and persist the raw artifacts
  3. regenerate the markdown report when the report logic evolves

The planner is built around one main question:

  • given one or more real Script 2 runs,
  • for one comparable validation fold,
  • which models should enter the ensemble pool?

The runner is built around a second question:

  • once the pool is fixed,
  • which merge rule produces the best execution?

docs.title: Files
docs.text:
• generate_ensemble_backtest_plan.py
  • run_ensemble_backtests.py
  • run_ensemble_backtest_batch.py
  • audit_ensemble_batch_results.py
  • render_ensemble_backtest_report.py

docs.title: Core Idea
docs.text:
This workflow starts from the same run sidecars already used across the Script 2 thesis exporters and merged views:

  • scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/results/<RUN_TAG>/script2_experiment_ids.csv

Then you choose one fold. That fold defines the comparable validation window.

Inside that fold, the planner:

  1. loads only kind=real experiment entries
  2. loads completed trials with checkpoints from the DB
  3. applies optional metric filters
  4. ranks candidates inside each model family
  5. keeps the top N per model
  6. enforces strict execution compatibility
  7. writes a full plan.json

If you enable --max-prob-corr and/or --max-signal-agreement, the planner keeps the same structure but changes the take top N per model step:

  • it still walks candidates inside each model_type in ranking order
  • it compares each new candidate against the full pool already selected
  • if the candidate is too correlated, it is skipped
  • the planner then tries the next ranked candidate from that same model family
  • to decide this, the planner may reuse or regenerate predictions during planning

The runner then uses that plan to:

  1. reuse or regenerate predictions for each selected source trial
  2. merge them with one or more ensemble rules
  3. run one backtest per merge rule
  4. compare the merged runs against the original stage-3 baselines
  5. write report artifacts

docs.title: Strict Compatibility
docs.text:
The planner and runner only mix trials that match exactly on:

  • experiment_type
  • base_symbol
  • base_timeframe
  • stage-3 validation range
  • full bot_config
  • full simulation_config
  • combined_confidence_weights

This is intentionally conservative. It avoids mixing models that would trade under different execution assumptions.

docs.title: 1. Generate the Plan
docs.text:
Use one or more real run summary CSVs:

  • scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/results/<RUN_TAG>/script2_experiment_ids.csv

Recommended mental model:

  • the planner chooses the pool
  • the plan also defines which ensemble merge rules will be executed later

docs.title: 2. Execute the Plan
docs.text:
The runner consumes plan.json.

That plan already contains:

  • selected source trials
  • merge rules
  • the comparable stage-3 execution range
  • ranking rationale

docs.title: 3. Read or Regenerate the Report
docs.text: The runner writes all raw artifacts. The renderer can rebuild report.md later without rerunning backtests.

docs.title: What It Selects
docs.text:
The planner selects trials for:

  • one required fold
  • optional tier
  • optional model subset
  • one or more source runs

It keeps:

  • completed trials only
  • checkpointed trials only
  • candidates that survive the chosen ranking-mode eligibility
  • only one strict compatibility bucket in the final plan

Inside the chosen bucket it keeps the top N trials per model family.

With --max-prob-corr and/or --max-signal-agreement, it still targets top-per-model, but only accepts a candidate when it is not too redundant with the pool selected so far.

docs.title: Planner Parameters
docs.text:
generate_ensemble_backtest_plan.py

  • --summary-csv Repeatable. Path to one source script2_experiment_ids.csv.

  • --db-path Path to database/rainbow_trader.sqlite. Default: database/rainbow_trader.sqlite

  • --fold Required. Validation fold to compare.

  • --top-per-model Required. Number of selected trials to keep for each model family.

  • --tier Optional. Restrict to one tier like A or B. If omitted, the planner may mix tiers as long as strict compatibility still holds.

  • --models Optional comma-separated model filter. Example: logistic,mlp,vit,tcn

  • --rank-by Optional. Default: promise

Supported values: - promise - friction - objective - pnl - stage2

  • --min-stage2 Optional lower bound on stage2_result.

  • --min-stage3 Optional lower bound on stage3_result.

  • --min-sharpe Optional lower bound on Sharpe.

  • --min-trades Optional lower bound on stage-3 trades.

  • --max-trades Optional upper bound on stage-3 trades.

  • --max-prob-corr Optional maximum allowed positive Pearson correlation between a candidate and each already selected member.

The planner computes three correlations on the aligned prediction streams: - corr(prob_none_left, prob_none_right) - corr(prob_long_left, prob_long_right) - corr(prob_short_left, prob_short_right)

Then it uses the largest of those three values as the redundancy check.

When this is enabled: - the planner may load reusable prediction parquets during planning - if predictions are missing, it may generate them during planning - candidates are still tried in the normal ranking order inside each model family - redundant candidates are skipped and replaced by the next ranked candidate

  • --max-signal-agreement Optional maximum allowed signal agreement between a candidate and each already selected member.

Signal agreement is the share of aligned timestamps where both models emit the same final signal: - LONG - SHORT - NONE

This is a discrete complement to --max-prob-corr.

When this is enabled: - the planner still keeps the same bucket/ranking/top-per-model structure - candidates are skipped if they agree too often with the already selected pool - it can be used alone or together with --max-prob-corr

  • --merge-rules Optional. Defaults to the current recommended 8-rule exploration set:
  • average
  • majority_vote
  • all_must_agree
  • majority_vote:0.75
  • majority_vote:0.85
  • weighted_average
  • weighted_majority_vote:0.75
  • weighted_majority_vote:0.85

Use comma-separated syntax. Thresholded vote rules are written as: - majority_vote:0.75 - weighted_majority_vote:0.85

  • --range-start

  • --range-end Optional exact range override to persist in plan.json. If omitted, the planner uses the common selected stage-3 validation range.

  • --output-root Parent folder for timestamped planner outputs.

  • --output-dir Exact planner output directory.

docs.title: promise
docs.text:
Default ranking mode. This is the general ensemble-oriented composite.

Weights:

  • objective: 1.5
  • stage2: 2.5
  • stage3: 1.0
  • sharpe: 1.25
  • win_rate: 1.25
  • trades_inverse: 0.75
  • total_pnl: 0.75

Use this when you want a balanced shortlist of individually decent models.

docs.title: friction
docs.text:
This reuses the rescue lens from:

  • scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/extra_backtests/generate_extra_backtest_plan.py

Additional eligibility:

  • PnL < 0
  • PnL + Est.Friction > 0

Use this when you suspect the model family may contain friction victims and want to see whether an ensemble can recover latent gross edge.

docs.title: Raw Metric Modes
docs.text:
• objective
  • pnl
  • stage2

Use these when you want a more controlled experiment based on one single selection signal.

docs.title: Correlation-Aware Selection
docs.text:
This is the main prediction-diversity extension in the current planner.

Without --max-prob-corr and without --max-signal-agreement, selection is pure ranking:

  • bucket first
  • then ranked top-per-model

With --max-prob-corr and/or --max-signal-agreement, selection becomes:

  • same bucket
  • same ranking order
  • same top-per-model
  • plus one extra rule: a candidate must not be too correlated with the already selected pool

Important:

  • this is not a proxy metric
  • it uses real prediction streams
  • it compares the real merge-relevant probabilities:
  • prob_none
  • prob_long
  • prob_short
  • it computes Pearson correlation separately for each of those three series
  • the effective redundancy score is the largest of the three correlations
  • signal agreement can also be constrained as a separate discrete threshold
  • the threshold is applied against the whole selected pool, not only against the same model family

Practical example:

  • top-per-model=2
  • max-prob-corr=0.85
  • max-signal-agreement=0.60

Then the planner behaves like this for one model family:

  1. try the best ranked candidate
  2. compare it against everything already selected
  3. if it passes, keep it
  4. if it fails, skip it
  5. try the next ranked candidate from that same model family

This preserves the existing planner shape while avoiding obviously redundant members.

docs.title: What It Does
docs.text:
run_ensemble_backtests.py

  1. loads the selected source trials from the plan
  2. prepares predictions for each source trial
  3. reuses existing prediction parquets when the schema is complete
  4. regenerates predictions when the parquet is missing or legacy
  5. uses original stage-3 baselines by default
  6. runs one merged backtest per merge rule
  7. writes raw artifacts and regenerates report.md

docs.title: What It Does
docs.text:
run_ensemble_backtest_batch.py

This is the orchestration layer for thesis campaigns.

Use it when:

  • you already defined a campaign block in JSON
  • you want one command to launch many planner + runner executions
  • you want one root folder that contains every execution subfolder
  • you want resumable batch behavior for long campaigns

For each execution inside the JSON batch spec, the batch runner:

  1. creates one dedicated subfolder
  2. runs generate_ensemble_backtest_plan.py
  3. optionally runs run_ensemble_backtests.py
  4. records the execution status in batch_results.json

docs.title: Batch JSON Structure
docs.text:
The batch spec has two main sections:

  1. defaults Shared values that apply to every execution unless overridden.
  2. executions The list of concrete planner/runner combinations to launch.

Real example shape:

{
  "batch_name": "20260403_phase1_block_a",
  "output_dir": "scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/ensemble_backtests/results/20260403_phase1_block_a",
  "defaults": {
    "summary_csvs": [
      "scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/results/20260325_tierB_mlp_ex/script2_experiment_ids.csv",
      "scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/results/20260328_tierB_corrected/script2_experiment_ids.csv"
    ],
    "tier": "B",
    "merge_rules": [
      "average",
      "majority_vote",
      "all_must_agree",
      "majority_vote:0.75",
      "majority_vote:0.85",
      "weighted_average",
      "weighted_majority_vote:0.75",
      "weighted_majority_vote:0.85"
    ]
  },
  "executions": [
    {
      "name": "block_a_tcn_f1_promise_top2_mild",
      "fold": 1,
      "models": ["tcn"],
      "top_per_model": 2,
      "rank_by": "promise",
      "max_prob_corr": 0.85,
      "max_signal_agreement": 0.60
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: Batch Parameters
docs.text:
run_ensemble_backtest_batch.py

  • --batch-json Required path to the JSON batch specification.

  • --output-dir Optional batch root override.

Resolution order: 1. CLI --output-dir 2. JSON output_dir 3. fallback: scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/ensemble_backtests/results/<batch_name>

Important: - the batch runner requires run folders to include a full timestamp - if the resolved folder name does not already start with YYYYMMDD_HHMMSS, the batch runner automatically inserts HHMMSS into the folder name - when the requested folder already starts with YYYYMMDD_, the runner keeps that date and transforms it into: - YYYYMMDD_HHMMSS_<rest> - otherwise it creates: - YYYYMMDD_HHMMSS_<original_name>

Example: - requested root: 20260403_phase1_block_a - effective root: 20260403_021530_phase1_block_a

  • --only-execution Optional repeatable execution-name filter.

Use this when: - you want a smoke test before launching the full block - one execution failed and you want to rerun only that one - you want to compare off vs mild vs tight for a single family first

  • --rerun-existing Force planner and runner re-execution even when the execution folder already contains results.json.

Without this flag: - completed executions are skipped - this makes long batches resumable

  • --plan-only Generate planner artifacts only.

This means the batch will run: - generate_ensemble_backtest_plan.py

and it will not run: - run_ensemble_backtests.py

Each execution folder will contain: - ensemble_plan.log - plan.md - plan.json

Each execution folder will not contain: - ensemble_backtests.log - results.json - results.csv - comparisons.csv - report.md

Use this when: - you want to inspect selected source pools before paying the backtest cost - you want to audit diversity-aware selection behavior first - you want to split a large campaign into plan first, run later

  • --fail-fast Stop the whole batch on the first planner or runner failure.

Without this flag: - the failure is recorded in batch_results.json - the batch continues with the remaining executions

docs.title: Batch Examples
docs.text:
Run the full Phase 1 Block A batch:

.venv/bin/python scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/ensemble_backtests/run_ensemble_backtest_batch.py \
  --batch-json scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/ensemble_backtests/batches/20260403_phase1_block_a.json
