Generate all plans first, but do not run any backtests yet:

.venv/bin/python scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/ensemble_backtests/run_ensemble_backtest_batch.py \
  --batch-json scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/ensemble_backtests/batches/20260403_phase1_block_a.json \
  --plan-only

Run only one named execution:

.venv/bin/python scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/ensemble_backtests/run_ensemble_backtest_batch.py \
  --batch-json scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/ensemble_backtests/batches/20260403_phase1_block_a.json \
  --only-execution block_a_tcn_f1_promise_top2_mild

Force a rerun of one named execution even if it already has results.json:

.venv/bin/python scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/ensemble_backtests/run_ensemble_backtest_batch.py \
  --batch-json scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/ensemble_backtests/batches/20260403_phase1_block_a.json \
  --only-execution block_a_tcn_f1_promise_top2_mild \
  --rerun-existing

docs.title: Batch Artifacts
docs.text:
At the batch root:

  • ensemble_batch.log High-level batch orchestration log.

  • batch_spec.json Snapshot copy of the JSON spec used for the batch.

  • batch_results.json Status summary of every execution:

  • completed
  • planned
  • skipped_existing
  • planner_failed
  • runner_failed

Inside each execution folder:

  • normal planner artifacts
  • and, unless --plan-only was used, normal runner artifacts too

docs.title: What It Does
docs.text:
audit_ensemble_batch_results.py

This is the campaign-level reader for one completed batch root.

Use it when:

  • the batch already finished
  • you want one summary across all execution folders
  • you want campaign-level CSVs for spreadsheet analysis
  • you want one compact markdown report instead of opening 72 individual reports

It reads:

  • batch_results.json
  • every completed execution's plan.json
  • every completed execution's results.json

It writes, by default, into the same batch root you pass in --batch-dir.

docs.title: Batch Audit Parameters
docs.text:
audit_ensemble_batch_results.py

  • --batch-dir Required path to the completed batch root.

  • --output-dir Optional override for the audit package destination.

If omitted: - the audit files are written directly into the same folder passed to --batch-dir

  • --top-n Optional leaderboard size for campaign_report.md. Default: 10

docs.title: Batch Audit Outputs
docs.text:
Written into the audit output folder:

  • campaign_executions.csv One row per execution.

Best for questions like: - which execution had the strongest best ensemble? - which execution beat its best source baseline? - how did off vs mild vs tight compare?

  • campaign_rules.csv One row per execution × merge rule.

Best for questions like: - which merge rule wins most often? - which merge rule has the best average PnL? - which rule improves PnL+Fric most often?

  • campaign_group_summary.csv Aggregated campaign views by:
  • model family
  • fold
  • ranking mode
  • top-per-model
  • diversity mode

  • selected pair combinations like models_label__diversity_mode

  • campaign_correlations.csv Pearson correlations over execution-level numeric columns.

This is a secondary appendix, not the primary reading artifact.

  • campaign_report.md Human-readable campaign summary:
  • quick takeaways
  • top executions by net PnL
  • top executions by PnL+Fric
  • merge rule leaderboard
  • family summary
  • diversity summary
  • fold summary

  • numeric correlation appendix

  • campaign_audit_manifest.json Small manifest describing what was written.

docs.title: Batch Audit Examples
docs.text:
Audit a finished batch in place:

.venv/bin/python scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/ensemble_backtests/audit_ensemble_batch_results.py \
  --batch-dir scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/ensemble_backtests/results/20260403_181718_phase1_block_a

Write the campaign audit package into a dedicated subfolder:

.venv/bin/python scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/ensemble_backtests/audit_ensemble_batch_results.py \
  --batch-dir scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/ensemble_backtests/results/20260403_181718_phase1_block_a \
  --output-dir scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/ensemble_backtests/results/20260403_181718_phase1_block_a/audit_views

Render shorter leaderboards:

.venv/bin/python scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/ensemble_backtests/audit_ensemble_batch_results.py \
  --batch-dir scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/ensemble_backtests/results/20260403_181718_phase1_block_a \
  --top-n 5

docs.title: Runner Parameters
docs.text:
• --plan-json Recommended mode. Load the full recipe from the planner output.

  • --source-trial-id Technical/manual mode. Repeatable. Use only when you want to bypass the planner.

  • --merge-rules Optional override. If omitted, the runner uses the merge rules already stored in plan.json.

Important: - no hidden fallback at execution time - if neither CLI nor plan.json provides merge rules, the runner fails

  • --range-start

  • --range-end Optional execution override.

  • --initial-equity Initial backtest equity. Default: 10000

  • --include-source-baselines Also rerun the original-trial baselines on the exact execution range. If omitted, the runner compares against the original stage-3 backtests stored in the DB.

  • --output-dir Exact runner output directory. If omitted and --plan-json is provided, the runner writes into the same folder that contains plan.json. If omitted and no plan is used, the runner creates a new timestamped folder.

docs.title: Merge Rules
docs.text:
This is the most important practical section.

The merge rule is what transforms multiple model predictions into one merged signal at each timestamp.

docs.title: 1. average
docs.text:
What it does:

  • averages prob_none, prob_long, prob_short
  • averages strength and quality
  • derives the final signal from the averaged probabilities

Practical example:

  • model A says LONG with high confidence
  • model B also says LONG
  • model C is less convinced and leans NONE

The ensemble still becomes LONG, but with moderated confidence.

Use this when:

  • you want a smooth consensus signal
  • you do not want one model to dominate

Risk:

  • it can still trade a lot
  • weak models can pull the merged probabilities toward noisy trades

docs.title: 2. weighted_average
docs.text:
What it does:

  • same as average
  • but each model contributes proportionally to its plan selection score

How weights are built:

  • the runner takes each selected trial's selection_score
  • shifts the scores to positive values if necessary
  • normalizes them to sum to 1.0

Practical example:

  • a top-ranked TCN can influence the merged probabilities more than a weaker VIT
  • but every selected model still contributes

Use this when:

  • you trust the planner ranking
  • you want a softer ensemble than hard voting

Risk:

  • if the selection score is a poor proxy for ensemble usefulness, this can over-trust the wrong models

docs.title: 3. all_must_agree
docs.text:
What it does:

  • only emits LONG if every source model says LONG
  • only emits SHORT if every source model says SHORT
  • otherwise emits NONE

Practical example:

  • LONG, LONG, LONG, LONG -> LONG
  • LONG, LONG, SHORT, LONG -> NONE

Use this when:

  • you want maximum selectivity
  • you want to test whether the ensemble improves by trading much less

Risk:

  • it can become so strict that it barely trades

docs.title: 4. majority_vote
docs.text:
What it does:

  • each model gives one vote: LONG, SHORT, or NONE
  • the signal with the most votes wins
  • ties are broken by summed probabilities
  • no minimum vote share is required

Practical example:

  • 5 models
  • votes: LONG=2, SHORT=2, NONE=1
  • tie between LONG and SHORT
  • summed probabilities decide the winner

Use this when:

  • you want a simple voting baseline
  • you want to compare against thresholded voting

docs.title: 5. majority_vote:<threshold>
docs.text:
What it does:

  1. chooses the same winner as majority_vote
  2. computes that winner's share of all votes
  3. keeps it only if the share is at least the threshold
  4. otherwise emits NONE

Important:

  • majority_vote:0 is equivalent to the current unthresholded majority_vote
  • majority_vote:0.75 means the winning signal must control at least 75% of votes

Practical example:

  • 8 models
  • vote winner: LONG
  • LONG share = 6 / 8 = 0.75

Then:

  • majority_vote:0.50 -> LONG
  • majority_vote:0.75 -> LONG
  • majority_vote:0.85 -> NONE

Use this when:

  • you want something between majority_vote and all_must_agree

Recommended thresholds:

  • 0.75 for a strong but still practical consensus
  • 0.85 for a very conservative version

docs.title: 6. weighted_majority_vote:<threshold>
docs.text:
What it does:

  1. each model votes LONG, SHORT, or NONE
  2. votes are weighted by the normalized selection_score
  3. the winner is the signal with the highest total vote weight
  4. ties are broken by weighted summed probabilities
  5. the weighted winner is kept only if its total vote-weight share is at least the threshold
  6. otherwise emits NONE

Practical example:

Weights:

  • model A: 0.60
  • model B: 0.15
  • model C: 0.15
  • model D: 0.10

Votes:

  • A -> LONG
  • B -> SHORT
  • C -> SHORT
  • D -> SHORT

Count-based vote:

  • SHORT wins 3-to-1

Weighted vote:

  • LONG weight = 0.60
  • SHORT weight = 0.40

Then:

  • weighted_majority_vote:0.50 -> LONG
  • weighted_majority_vote:0.75 -> NONE

Use this when:

  • you want vote-style behavior
  • but you also want the stronger selected models to matter more

docs.title: Practical Recommendations
docs.text:
If you are exploring a new fold for the first time, a good default set is:

  • average
  • majority_vote
  • all_must_agree
  • majority_vote:0.75
  • majority_vote:0.85
  • weighted_average
  • weighted_majority_vote:0.75
  • weighted_majority_vote:0.85

If your current ensembles are trading too much:

  • focus on majority_vote:0.75
  • majority_vote:0.85
  • weighted_majority_vote:0.75
  • weighted_majority_vote:0.85
  • all_must_agree

If your current ensembles look too inactive:

  • compare average
  • weighted_average
  • majority_vote

If the planner used rank_by=friction, the weighted modes become especially interesting because the score already encodes a friction-aware preference.

docs.title: Example 1: Default 8-rule exploration across several runs
docs.text:
.venv/bin/python scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/ensemble_backtests/generate_ensemble_backtest_plan.py \
  --summary-csv scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/results/20260319_035820/script2_experiment_ids.csv \
  --summary-csv scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/results/20260323_tierA_rescue_v2/script2_experiment_ids.csv \
  --summary-csv scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/results/20260325_tierB_mlp_ex/script2_experiment_ids.csv \
  --summary-csv scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/results/20260328_tierB_corrected/script2_experiment_ids.csv \
  --fold 2 \
  --top-per-model 2 \
  --output-dir scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/ensemble_backtests/results/20260402_default8_f2

docs.title: Example 2: Friction-oriented selection with conservative voting
docs.text:
.venv/bin/python scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/ensemble_backtests/generate_ensemble_backtest_plan.py \
  --summary-csv scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/results/20260319_035820/script2_experiment_ids.csv \
  --summary-csv scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/results/20260325_tierB_mlp_ex/script2_experiment_ids.csv \
  --summary-csv scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/results/20260328_tierB_corrected/script2_experiment_ids.csv \
  --fold 2 \
  --tier B \
  --top-per-model 3 \
  --rank-by friction \
  --merge-rules majority_vote:0.75,majority_vote:0.85,weighted_majority_vote:0.75,weighted_majority_vote:0.85 \
  --output-dir scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/ensemble_backtests/results/20260402_friction_f2_tierb

docs.title: Example 3: Correlation-aware planning
docs.text:
.venv/bin/python scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/ensemble_backtests/generate_ensemble_backtest_plan.py \
  --summary-csv scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/results/20260319_035820/script2_experiment_ids.csv \
  --summary-csv scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/results/20260325_tierB_mlp_ex/script2_experiment_ids.csv \
  --summary-csv scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/results/20260328_tierB_corrected/script2_experiment_ids.csv \
  --fold 1 \
  --tier B \
  --top-per-model 1 \
  --rank-by promise \
  --max-prob-corr 0.85 \
  --output-dir scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/ensemble_backtests/results/20260402_corraware_f1_tierb

Use this when you want:

  • ranking by score first
  • but automatic rejection of highly redundant prediction streams

docs.title: Example 4: Execute exactly what the plan defines
docs.text:
.venv/bin/python scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/ensemble_backtests/run_ensemble_backtests.py \
  --plan-json scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/ensemble_backtests/results/20260402_fold2_mix/plan.json

docs.title: Example 5: Override the merge rules at execution time
docs.text:
.venv/bin/python scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/ensemble_backtests/run_ensemble_backtests.py \
  --plan-json scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/ensemble_backtests/results/20260402_fold2_mix/plan.json \
  --merge-rules average,weighted_average,majority_vote:0.75,weighted_majority_vote:0.75

docs.title: Example 6: Regenerate only the report

.venv/bin/python scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/ensemble_backtests/render_ensemble_backtest_report.py \

  --results-json scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/ensemble_backtests/results/20260402_fold2_mix/results.json \
  --output-md scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/ensemble_backtests/results/20260402_fold2_mix/report.md

docs.title: Example 7: Execute one full campaign block from a JSON batch spec
docs.text:
.venv/bin/python scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/ensemble_backtests/run_ensemble_backtest_batch.py \
  --batch-json scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/ensemble_backtests/batches/20260403_phase1_block_a.json

This creates:

  • one batch root folder
  • one ensemble_batch.log
  • one batch_results.json
  • one subfolder per execution, each with its own planner and runner artifacts

docs.title: Example 8: Run only one named execution from the batch

  --batch-json scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/ensemble_backtests/batches/20260403_phase1_block_a.json \
  --only-execution block_a_tcn_f1_promise_top2_mild

docs.title: Artifacts
docs.text:
Planner output:

  • ensemble_plan.log
  • plan.md
  • plan.json

Runner output:

  • ensemble_backtests.log
  • results.json
  • results.csv
  • comparisons.csv
  • report.md

Batch output:

  • ensemble_batch.log
  • batch_spec.json
  • batch_results.json
  • one subfolder per execution with normal planner + runner artifacts

docs.title: Output Reading Guide
docs.text:
• plan.md Human-readable description of how the shortlist was built and why each trial entered. When correlation-aware selection is enabled, it also includes pairwise diversity metrics for the final pool.

  • ensemble_plan.log Planner-time log file. This is where you inspect planning-time prediction reuse or regeneration progress, especially when --max-prob-corr forces the planner to prepare predictions during selection.

  • plan.json Machine-readable recipe used by the runner.

  • results.csv One row per executed run, including source baselines and ensemble runs.

  • comparisons.csv One row per ensemble rule × source trial comparison.

  • results.json Rich metadata including selection policy, prediction preparation state, and all result rows.

  • report.md Self-contained thesis report with selection context, prediction preparation, source tables, ensemble tables, ranking, pairwise comparisons, selection diversity, and appendix notes.

docs.title: Script 2 Extra Backtests
docs.text:
This folder contains a small, thesis-oriented workflow for running additional manual backtests on already-trained Script 2 trials with stricter execution thresholds.

The design goal is simple:

  • keep the original training experiment and trial intact
  • choose promising friction-victim candidates automatically from the DB
  • propose a 3-step entry_threshold ladder (mild, recommended, strict) from prediction parquet analysis
  • rerun only the manual backtest layer with explicit, traceable overrides

docs.title: Artifacts
docs.text:
Each execution uses one timestamped folder under:

scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/extra_backtests/results/<run_tag>/

Expected files:

  • backtesting.log: shared structured log for the rerun step
  • plan.md: human-readable shortlist and threshold proposals
  • plan.json: structured plan consumed by the rerun script
  • results.csv: flat table with one row per executed rerun
  • results.json: structured rerun payload
  • report.md: human-readable final summary, rendered from the CSV artifacts

docs.title: generate_extra_backtest_plan.py
docs.text:
Reads the SQLite DB, computes trial metrics and estimated friction, selects friction-victim candidates, inspects their original prediction parquets, and writes plan.md plus plan.json.

Example:

.venv/bin/python scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/extra_backtests/generate_extra_backtest_plan.py \
  --db-path database/rainbow_trader.sqlite \
  --per-fold-k 5 \
  --min-trades 100

docs.title: run_extra_backtests.py
docs.text:
Consumes plan.json, applies the official backtest strategy override helper, and reruns the selected thresholds as manual backtests. After writing results.csv and results.json, it regenerates report.md through the shared standalone renderer so the normal flow and the report-only flow stay identical.

Example:

.venv/bin/python scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/extra_backtests/run_extra_backtests.py \
  --plan-json scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/extra_backtests/results/20260331_132116/plan.json \
  --selection-mode all

docs.title: render_extra_backtest_report.py
docs.text:
Rebuilds report.md from an existing results.csv without rerunning any backtests. This is the right tool when the reporting logic changes after a run has already finished.

Example:

.venv/bin/python scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/extra_backtests/render_extra_backtest_report.py \
  --results-csv scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/extra_backtests/results/20260331_132841/results.csv

docs.title: run_extra_backtests.sh
docs.text:
Convenience wrapper for:

  1. generating the plan
  2. optionally stopping there
  3. running the reruns into the same folder
  4. writing backtesting.log automatically into that folder during the rerun step
  5. optionally rebuilding report.md only from an existing results.csv

Examples:

# Plan only
bash scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/extra_backtests/run_extra_backtests.sh --plan-only

# Plan only, but keep 7 candidates per fold
bash scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/extra_backtests/run_extra_backtests.sh --plan-only --per-fold-k 7

# Full flow
bash scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/extra_backtests/run_extra_backtests.sh

# Reuse an existing plan and run all three threshold variants
bash scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/extra_backtests/run_extra_backtests.sh \
  --plan-json scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/extra_backtests/results/20260331_170000/plan.json \
  --selection-mode all

# Regenerate report.md only from an existing results.csv
bash scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/extra_backtests/run_extra_backtests.sh \
  --report-only \
  --results-csv scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/extra_backtests/results/20260331_132841/results.csv

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Candidate selection is automatic and DB-driven. The audit markdown is not an input.
  • By default the planner keeps 5 candidates per fold, aiming for 20 total reruns when every fold has enough eligible trials.
  • The selection score is now rescue-oriented: it still values S2/F1/Objective, but also gives more weight to gross edge per trade and moderate turnover instead of simply favoring the highest-trade friction victims.
  • Threshold proposals are based on prediction parquets and are meant to guide reruns, not to guarantee a target trade count.
  • The default rerun mode is all, which means 20 candidates × 3 thresholds = 60 manual reruns when every fold has enough eligible trials.
  • The rerun period stays aligned to the original experiment fold because the extra-backtest runner reuses the stored experiment range and only overrides strategy thresholds.
  • The final markdown report is now produced through the shared standalone renderer, so run_extra_backtests.py and render_extra_backtest_report.py produce the same report.md structure.
  • The actual execution override path is implemented in: modules/core/src/rainbow/core/helpers/backtest_strategy_overrides.py

docs.title: What This File Is
docs.text:
What this section is: an automatically generated shortlist of friction-victim Script 2 trials selected from the database, plus proposed higher entry-threshold reruns.

  • Database: database/rainbow_trader.sqlite
  • Trials scanned: 1837
  • Candidates selected: 20
  • Target per fold: 5
  • Minimum trades filter: 100
  • Output folder: scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/extra_backtests/results/20260331_132841

docs.title: Selection Rules
docs.text:
• Completed Script 2 trainable trials only.
  • Negative realized PnL required.
  • Positive PnL + Est.Fric required.
  • Minimum trade count required.
  • Candidates are ranked inside each fold separately, then the top per-fold-k are kept from each fold.
  • Final score emphasizes Objective, S2, Entry F1, F1 Macro, low JS, gross-edge proxy, and friction drag.
  • Threshold proposals come from the original prediction parquets and are meant for rerun selection, not for guaranteed trade-count targeting.

docs.title: Column Legend
docs.text:
• Thr Orig: original entry_threshold stored in the experiment strategy config for the baseline trial.
  • Thr Rec: recommended stricter entry_threshold inferred from the trial's prediction parquet.
  • Thr Alt: alternate stricter threshold, usually more aggressive than Thr Rec.
  • Keep Rec: share of rows that pass the current confidence gate and would still pass after switching to Thr Rec.
  • Keep Alt: same retention concept, but for Thr Alt.
  • BT Prog%: completion ratio of the original stage-3 backtest, computed as processed_count / prediction_parquet_rows.

docs.title: What This File Is
docs.text:
What this section is: a regenerated thesis-grade report built from an existing extra-backtests CSV artifact.

  • Results CSV: scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/extra_backtests/results/20260331_132841/results.csv
  • Output markdown: scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/extra_backtests/results/20260331_132841/report.md
  • Plan: scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/extra_backtests/results/20260331_132841/plan.json
  • Selection mode: alternate
  • Log file: scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/extra_backtests/results/20260331_132841/backtesting.log
  • Runs requested: 20
  • Runs completed: 20
  • Runs failed: 0
  • Net PnL improved: 18/20
  • Gross proxy improved: 1/20
  • PnL/trade improved: 4/20
  • Gross/trade improved: 5/20
  • Better-than-proportional net improvement: 4/20
  • Corr(Trade Red%, Delta PnL): +0.70

docs.title: Proportionality Lens
docs.text: This section asks whether lower net losses come only from trading less, or whether the rerun also looks more selective on a per-trade basis.

docs.title: Interpretation Table Legend
docs.text:
What this section is: plain-language definitions for the columns in the interpretation table below.

  • Rank: Candidate priority carried from the original plan.
  • Trial: Original Script 2 trial ID.
  • Variant: Threshold ladder choice used in the rerun (mild, recommended, or strict).
  • Trade Red%: Percentage reduction in trades versus the original stage-3 backtest. Higher means the rerun traded less.
  • PnL Imp%: Net PnL improvement relative to the magnitude of the original net PnL. Positive is better. Example: +50% means the rerun recovered half of the original net loss magnitude.
  • Gross Chg%: Change in PnL+Fric relative to the magnitude of the original PnL+Fric. Positive is better. This is the closest column to asking whether gross edge survived after removing friction effects.
  • Orig PnL/trade: Original net PnL divided by original trade count.
  • New PnL/trade: Rerun net PnL divided by rerun trade count.
  • Orig Gross/trade: Original PnL+Fric divided by original trade count.
  • New Gross/trade: Rerun PnL+Fric divided by rerun trade count.
  • Interpretation: Automatic label comparing PnL Imp% against Trade Red%.

docs.title: What To Look At
docs.text:
What this section is: a fast reading guide for deciding whether a rerun looks genuinely promising.

  • First check Interpretation: Better than proportional is the strongest signal. It means net PnL improved more than a simple reduction in turnover would suggest.
  • Then check Gross Chg%: positive values are especially important because they suggest the rerun kept or improved gross edge, not just reduced friction.
  • Then compare Orig Gross/trade versus New Gross/trade: if the new value is higher, the bot appears to be selecting better opportunities per trade.
  • PnL Imp% is useful, but by itself it can be inflated by merely trading less. It matters most when it is paired with positive or less-negative Gross Chg%.
  • A very high Trade Red% with poor Gross Chg% usually means the threshold became too strict: the bot traded much less, but also discarded too much gross edge.

docs.text:
• Completed Script 2 trainable trials only.

docs.title: Column Legend
docs.text:
• Thr Orig: original entry_threshold stored in the experiment strategy config for the baseline trial.

  • Thr Rec: middle threshold, used as the main rerun candidate.
  • Thr Strict: strongest threshold in the ladder, kept as a stress-test variant.
  • Keep Mild: share of rows that pass the current confidence gate and would still pass after switching to Thr Mild.
  • Keep Rec: same retention concept, but for Thr Rec.
  • Keep Strict: same retention concept, but for Thr Strict.
  • BT Prog%: completion ratio of the original stage-3 backtest, computed as processed_count / prediction_parquet_rows.

docs.title: Proportionality Lens
docs.text: This section asks whether lower net losses come only from trading less, or whether the rerun also looks more selective on a per-trade basis.

docs.title: Interpretation Table Legend
docs.text:
What this section is: plain-language definitions for the columns in the interpretation table below.

  • Rank: Candidate priority carried from the original plan.
  • Trial: Original Script 2 trial ID.
  • Variant: Threshold ladder choice used in the rerun (mild, recommended, or strict).
  • Trade Red%: Percentage reduction in trades versus the original stage-3 backtest. Higher means the rerun traded less.
  • PnL Imp%: Net PnL improvement relative to the magnitude of the original net PnL. Positive is better. Example: +50% means the rerun recovered half of the original net loss magnitude.
  • Gross Chg%: Change in PnL+Fric relative to the magnitude of the original PnL+Fric. Positive is better. This is the closest column to asking whether gross edge survived after removing friction effects.
  • Orig PnL/trade: Original net PnL divided by original trade count.
  • New PnL/trade: Rerun net PnL divided by rerun trade count.
  • Orig Gross/trade: Original PnL+Fric divided by original trade count.
  • New Gross/trade: Rerun PnL+Fric divided by rerun trade count.
  • Interpretation: Automatic label comparing PnL Imp% against Trade Red%.

docs.title: What To Look At
docs.text:
What this section is: a fast reading guide for deciding whether a rerun looks genuinely promising.

  • First check Interpretation: Better than proportional is the strongest signal. It means net PnL improved more than a simple reduction in turnover would suggest.
  • Then check Gross Chg%: positive values are especially important because they suggest the rerun kept or improved gross edge, not just reduced friction.
  • Then compare Orig Gross/trade versus New Gross/trade: if the new value is higher, the bot appears to be selecting better opportunities per trade.
  • PnL Imp% is useful, but by itself it can be inflated by merely trading less. It matters most when it is paired with positive or less-negative Gross Chg%.
  • A very high Trade Red% with poor Gross Chg% usually means the threshold became too strict: the bot traded much less, but also discarded too much gross edge.

docs.title: Best Delta
docs.text:
The strongest gross-proxy delta came from T3710 (TCN-F2, recommended) at +$134.31 versus baseline.

Interpretation guide: Better than proportional means net PnL improved by more than the trade-count reduction alone would suggest. Roughly proportional means the gain is consistent with simply trading less. Worse than proportional means turnover fell, but net PnL did not improve enough to support a strong selectivity claim.

docs.title: Script 2 Hyper Extractions
docs.text:
This folder contains a small, self-contained workflow to generate ordered hyperparameter JSON artifacts for the Script 2 rescue process.

The goal is simple:

  • Read completed Script 2 trials from the application SQLite DB.
  • Use script2_experiment_ids.csv files from results/ as the source of truth for simple_code, tier, fold, and model_type.
  • Select top trials by metric x source_fold x model with systematic same-category backfill.
  • Emit two families of JSON files:
  • exact: fully fixed hyperparameter payloads
  • neighbor: Optuna-ready ranged hyperparameter payloads
  • Compile one final rescue artifact for generate_script2_experiments.py.

The output is written per model in a clean folder layout.

docs.title: Files
docs.text:
• script2_hyper_extraction_input.json
  • Main input configuration.
  • Defines source runs, top-N selection, and per-model neighbor policies.
  • generate_script2_hyper_extractions.py
  • Generator script.
  • Reads the input config, queries SQLite, ranks trials, and writes output JSONs.

docs.title: Why CSV Instead of DB Metadata
docs.text:
The SQLite snapshot does not reliably expose simple_code as a stable top-level column for the Script 2 rescue workflow. The result CSV files already contain the operational metadata we care about:

  • simple_code
  • tier
  • fold
  • model_type
  • experiment_id

So this tool uses those CSVs to map experiments cleanly, and the DB only for the actual trial metrics and resolved hyperparameters.

docs.title: Input Config Shape
docs.text:
The input JSON is organized in three blocks:

  1. Global paths
  2. Selection rules
  3. Per-model neighbor policies

Example:

{
  "db_path": "database/rainbow_trader.sqlite",
  "source_run_csv_paths": [
    "scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/results/20260319_035820/script2_experiment_ids.csv",
    "scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/results/20260325_tierB_mlp_ex/script2_experiment_ids.csv",
    "scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/results/20260328_tierB_corrected/script2_experiment_ids.csv"
  ],
  "output_dir": "scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/generate_experiments/hyper_extractions/generated",
  "selection": {
    "metrics": ["s3", "s2", "pnl", "sharpe", "entry_f1"],
    "folds": [1, 2, 3, 4],
    "top_n_exact_per_metric_source_fold": 2,
    "top_n_neighbor_per_metric_source_fold": 1
  },
  "models": {
    "tcn": {
      "source_tiers": ["A", "B"],
      "window_size_policy": { "kind": "int_clamp", "radius": 4, "min": 45, "max": 60 },
      "training_policies": {
        "learning_rate": { "kind": "float_pct", "pct": 0.2, "min": 1e-05, "max": 0.0003, "log_scale": true }
      }
    }
  }
}

docs.title: Selection Rules
docs.text:
The current workflow implements:

  • Metrics:
  • s3
  • s2
  • pnl
  • sharpe
  • entry_f1
  • Folds:
  • 1
  • 2
  • 3
  • 4
  • Exact selection:
  • top 2 per metric x source_fold
  • Neighbor selection:
  • top 1 per metric x source_fold

Selection/backfill means:

  1. Iterate categories in configured order.
  2. For one destination fold and category, the base exact quota is:
  3. len(folds) * top_n_exact_per_metric_source_fold
  4. with the current config: 4 * 2 = 8
  5. For neighbors, the base quota is:
  6. len(folds) * top_n_neighbor_per_metric_source_fold
  7. with the current config: 4 * 1 = 4
  8. Walk rank depth i = 1, 2, 3, ... inside the same category.
  9. At each i, fetch the #i candidate from every source fold.
  10. Offer that candidate to every destination fold except its own source fold.
  11. Deduplicate per destination fold by exact hyperparameter signature.
  12. Stay in the same category until either:
  13. all destination-fold quotas are full, or
  14. there are no more ranked candidates in any source fold.
  15. Any deficit carries to the next category.

That gives the current operational split per destination fold:

  • 40 exact enqueue trials:
  • 5 categories x 4 source folds x top-2
  • 20 neighbor anchors:
  • 5 categories x 4 source folds x top-1
  • 80 total rescue trials once Tier B rescue adds:
  • 20 warmup random trials inside the merged neighbor space
  • 20 TPE-guided trials after warmup

docs.title: Metrics Source
docs.text:
The generator extracts:

  • s2 from trials.stage2_result
  • s3 from trials.stage3_result
  • pnl from trials.stage3_total_pnl
  • sharpe from training_metrics.stage3_metrics.sharpe_ratio
  • entry_f1 from training_metrics.stage2_metrics.prediction_health.binary_trade_detection.overall.f1

Only completed trials with non-null required fields are considered.

docs.title: Output Layout
docs.text:
The generator writes:

generated/
  logistic/
    exact/
      01_f1_s3_t1234.json
      ...
    neighbor/
      01_f1_s3_t1234.json
      ...
    manifest.json
  mlp/
    ...
  script2_tierb_rescue_compiled.json

Each output JSON has:

  • selection metadata
  • final hyperparameters_config
  • requested_fold and source_fold separated for same-fold exclusion

Example exact payload:

{
  "kind": "exact",
  "selection": {
    "source_trial_id": 2530,
    "experiment_id": 78,
    "simple_code": "S2-TB-TCN-F3",
    "tier": "B",
    "requested_fold": 3,
    "source_fold": 3,
    "model_type": "tcn",
    "metric": "sharpe",
    "rank_within_metric_fold": 1,
    "metric_value": 3.58
  },
  "hyperparameters_config": {
    "window_size": { "type": "fixed", "value": 52 },
    "training_hyperparameters_config": {
      "learning_rate": { "type": "fixed", "value": 0.00024 }
    }
  }
}

Example neighbor payload:

{
  "kind": "neighbor",
  "selection": {
    "source_trial_id": 2530,
    "experiment_id": 78,
    "simple_code": "S2-TB-TCN-F3",
    "tier": "B",
    "requested_fold": 3,
    "source_fold": 3,
    "model_type": "tcn",
    "metric": "sharpe",
    "rank_within_metric_fold": 1,
    "metric_value": 3.58
  },
  "hyperparameters_config": {
    "window_size": { "type": "int", "min": 48, "max": 56 },
    "training_hyperparameters_config": {
      "weight_decay": {
        "type": "float_ranges",
        "ranges": [
          { "min": 1e-06, "max": 3e-06, "log_scale": true },
          { "min": 3e-06, "max": 9e-06, "log_scale": true }
        ]
      }
    }
  }
}

The compiled rescue JSON contains, per model:

  • enqueue_trials_by_fold
  • exact trials already filtered and ordered for each destination fold
  • hyperparameters_config
  • one merged Optuna space built from neighbors and exact anchors

docs.title: Policy Kinds
docs.text:
The current script supports these policy kinds for neighbor generation:

  • fixed
  • float_pct
  • float_ranges_split
  • int_clamp
  • categorical_adjacent
  • categorical_clamped

These are enough to express:

  • exact anchors
  • local search around anchors
  • split search ranges where the rescue logic wants disjoint numeric bands

docs.title: How To Run
docs.text:
From repo root:

source .venv/bin/activate
python scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/generate_experiments/hyper_extractions/generate_script2_hyper_extractions.py

Or with an explicit config path:

source .venv/bin/activate
python scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/generate_experiments/hyper_extractions/generate_script2_hyper_extractions.py \
  --config-path scripts/thesis/script_2_thesis/generate_experiments/hyper_extractions/script2_hyper_extraction_input.json

docs.title: When To Edit The Input JSON
docs.text:
Edit script2_hyper_extraction_input.json when you want to change:

  • source runs
  • top-N selection
  • output folder
  • tiers allowed per model
  • neighbor search shape per model
  • regularization floors or split ranges

docs.title: Expected Workflow
docs.text:
1. Adjust script2_hyper_extraction_input.json.
  2. Run the generator.
  3. Inspect generated/<model>/manifest.json.
  4. Review exact/ and neighbor/.
  5. Copy or merge the final hyperparameters_config payloads into the downstream rescue assembly workflow.

docs.title: Script 2 — Top-3 Trial Candidates per Model per Fold
docs.text:
Generated: 2026-03-31 05:32 UTC

Tiers present: A + B + C + D + E (No tier filter — all tiers included)

Source runs: - 20260319_035820 - 20260323_tierA_rescue_v2 - 20260325_tierB_mlp_ex - 20260328_tierB_corrected

Ranked by PNL (stage3_total_pnl) descending, top 3 per (model × fold). Trials without PNL data are ranked last. Tier A and Tier B are merged — no tier separation.

docs.title: rule_based
docs.text:
• Same feature and same rules in all four folds:
  • rule_raw_btc_5m_rsi_14{-1} > 70 -> SHORT
  • rule_raw_btc_5m_rsi_14{-1} < 30 -> LONG
  • The observed difference is not caused by a configuration mismatch.
Fold Backtest Status Sharpe Return % Trades Entry Rate Win Rate JSD Status Reason F1 COMPLETED -0.6450 -0.0189 149 0.0615 0.3020 0.0092 F2 PRUNED -6.7285 -0.0448 100 0.0747 0.2400 0.0116 min_sharpe_ratio at 54.1% progress F3 PRUNED -5.0322 -0.0487 104 0.0913 0.2596 0.0141 min_sharpe_ratio at 56.0% progress F4 PRUNED -7.2522 -0.0497 100 0.0776 0.2100 0.0052 min_sharpe_ratio at 72.2% progress
  • F1 is not good; it is only less bad than F2-F4.
  • JSD is low in all four folds, so the main gap is not "broken prediction distribution".
  • F2-F4 trade more often and still lose faster, so Stage 3 kills them on economics, not on configuration mismatch.

docs.title: same_hypers_across_folds
docs.text:
• Each experiment creates its own Optuna study.
  • All studies start with the same sampler seed: 2026.
  • While different folds have the same trial history, the same optuna_trial_number uses the same hyperparameters.
  • Verified first divergence point by model:
Model Same Hyperparameters Across Folds Until First Divergence mlp Trial 7 Trial 8 tcn Trial 10 Trial 11 vit Trial 7 Trial 8 axial_vit Trial 7 Trial 8 logistic Trial 9 Trial 10
  • This means early fold-to-fold differences are not explained by "Optuna tried different settings".
  • They are explained by the same settings behaving differently on different fold data, then TPE diverging later because the observed outcomes are no longer the same.
Model Same Trial Number Same Hyperparameters Fold Outcome A Fold Outcome B logistic 9 Yes F2: COMPLETED, objective=0.2419 F1: PRUNED by distribution_drift vit 6 Yes F2: COMPLETED, objective=0.2334 F1: PRUNED, later ends in degenerate_solution

docs.title: pruned_models
docs.text:
Experiment degenerate_solution distribution_drift optuna_pruner Other MLP-F4 12 17 11 0 TCN-F1 37 1 2 0 TCN-F2 25 10 4 1 AXV-F1 36 0 4 0
  • MLP-F4 is not a pure degenerate_solution case; it mixes hard collapse with many near-NONE distribution_drift prunes.
  • TCN-F1 is the clearest hard-collapse case.
  • TCN-F2 mixes both failure modes, but many distribution_drift events are still almost all NONE.
  • AXV-F1 is dominated by hard collapse in its final state.
Experiment Trial Result Predicted Shares MLP-F4 1 PRUNED by distribution_drift NONE=0.9844, LONG=0.0044, SHORT=0.0112 MLP-F4 3 PRUNED by distribution_drift NONE=0.9724, LONG=0.0067, SHORT=0.0209 TCN-F1 6 PRUNED by distribution_drift NONE=1.0000, LONG=0.0000, SHORT=0.0000 TCN-F2 18 PRUNED by distribution_drift NONE=0.9990, LONG=0.0000, SHORT=0.0010 TCN-F2 21 PRUNED by distribution_drift NONE=0.9906, LONG=0.0000, SHORT=0.0094 Model / Fold Trial Status Hyperparameters Predicted Shares / Note MLP-F2 1 PRUNED hidden_dim=128, num_hidden_layers=2, dropout=0.0, activation=silu, gamma=1.5 NONE=0.9977, LONG=0.0000, SHORT=0.0023 MLP-F2 11 COMPLETED hidden_dim=256, num_hidden_layers=2, dropout=0.2, activation=gelu, gamma=1.0 NONE=0.8658, LONG=0.0893, SHORT=0.0449
  • MLP-F2 shows that hyperparameters can materially change the prediction distribution inside the same fold.
  • This supports continuing the investigation as "which parts of the search space avoid collapse", not as "fold data alone explains everything".
Model / Fold Trial Status Hyperparameters Stage 2 Distribution Snapshot Stage 3 Snapshot TCN-F4 28 COMPLETED channels=32, kernel_size=3, num_layers=3, dropout=0.3, activation=silu, gamma=2.5 NONE=1.0000, LONG=0.0000, SHORT=0.0000 Backtest predicts LONG=7029, SHORT=57, NONE=0; jsd=0.5834; sharpe=-4.4170 TCN-F1 28 PRUNED channels=64, kernel_size=7, num_layers=4, dropout=0.0, activation=gelu, gamma=1.0 NONE=1.0000, LONG=0.0000, SHORT=0.0000 No surviving Stage 3 result TCN-F2 28 PRUNED channels=128, kernel_size=5, num_layers=2, dropout=0.0, activation=relu, label_smoothing=0.1 NONE=1.0000, LONG=0.0000, SHORT=0.0000 No surviving Stage 3 result
  • TCN has a second problem beyond early collapse: at least one completed trial survives pruning while still producing a very distorted final prediction mix in backtest.

docs.title: transferable_hyperparameter_clues
docs.text:
Target Fold Completed Trials Same-Model Folds With Completed Trials Exact Replay Of Best Peer Survivors In Target Fold MLP-F4 0 F1, F2, F3 No TCN-F1 0 F3, F4 No TCN-F2 0 F3, F4 No AXV-F1 0 F2 No
  • For these three zero-complete folds, the strongest same-model survivors from other folds were not tried exactly in the failing fold.
  • This means the current evidence does not support the statement "the best neighboring settings were already tested here and failed".
Model Survivor Evidence From Other Folds Repeated Pattern In Survivors MLP 8 completed trials across F1-F3 hidden_dim=256, batch=256/512, low-to-moderate dropout, cross_entropy more common than focal TCN 4 completed trials across F3-F4 channels=32, activation=silu, kernel_size=3/5, num_layers=2/3, low lr, very low weight_decay, dropout=0.1-0.3 Target Fold Peer Survivor Peer Objective Replayed Exactly In Target Fold MLP-F4 MLP-F3 trial 37 0.4764 No MLP-F4 MLP-F1 trial 29 0.2464 No MLP-F4 MLP-F2 trial 33 0.2095 No TCN-F1 TCN-F4 trial 36 0.3451 No TCN-F1 TCN-F4 trial 28 0.3179 No TCN-F2 TCN-F4 trial 36 0.3451 No TCN-F2 TCN-F3 trial 19 0.1277 No
  • MLP-F4 still has an untested transfer hypothesis from its sibling folds.
  • TCN-F1 and TCN-F2 still have untested transfer hypotheses from TCN-F3/F4.
  • AXV-F1 now has an untested transfer hypothesis from AXV-F2.
  • The most defensible next use of extra trials in these folds is not random expansion, but targeted replay or neighborhood search around the survivor patterns above.

docs.title: axial_vit
docs.text:
Fold Training Status Completed Pruned Best Trial Best Objective Best Sharpe Best Return % Note F1 COMPLETED 0 40 all trials pruned F2 COMPLETED 2 38 1355 0.2451 -3.9148 -0.0645 first viable AXV fold F3 PENDING experiment row exists, not started F4 NOT_CREATED no experiment id in summary CSV Fold Trial ID Optuna Trial Objective Sharpe Return % Window Loss LR Batch Weight Decay F2 1351 30 0.2374 -3.2350 -0.0311 38 cross_entropy 3.9318e-05 512 7.9152e-05 F2 1355 34 0.2451 -3.9148 -0.0645 39 cross_entropy 2.4718e-05 512 1.5527e-04
  • Shared pattern in the two completed AXV-F2 survivors:
  • hidden_dim=96
  • time_num_heads=2
  • feature_num_heads=8
  • num_layers=6
  • dim_feedforward=512
  • activation=relu
  • norm_first=false
  • dropout=0.2
  • time_attention_dropout=0.1
  • patch_size=4
  • patch_stride=2
  • pooling_type_2d=attention
  • attn_pool_hidden_dim=128
  • attn_pool_dropout=0.2
  • time_patch_proj_type=none
  • loss_type=cross_entropy
  • label_smoothing=0.05
  • batch_size=512
  • learning_rate in the low 2.47e-05 to 3.93e-05 band

  • weight_decay in the low 7.9e-05 to 1.55e-04 band

  • Main difference between the two completed survivors:

  • trial 1351: time_pos_encoding_type=hybrid, feature_attention_dropout=0.0

  • trial 1355: time_pos_encoding_type=relative_bias, feature_attention_dropout=0.3

  • AXV-F2 disproves the statement "axial vit always collapses in Tier A".

  • The strongest rescue transfer for AXV-F1/F3/F4 should start from these two exact survivors.

docs.title: Scope
docs.text: This handoff summarizes: 1. What was validated with full vs lazy feature loading. 2. Why predictions metadata JSON shows stale part_count=1. 3. Test gaps and concrete test improvements for another agent to implement.

docs.title: 1) Full vs Lazy loading is equivalent for predictions
docs.text:
• The replay script generated predictions through four scenarios:
  • full + last checkpoint
  • full + best checkpoint
  • lazy + last checkpoint
  • lazy + best checkpoint
  • Full-range run result (117,946 timestamps):
  • lazy_last vs full_last: exact match (all columns).
  • lazy_best vs full_best: exact match (all columns).
  • Conclusion: lazy feature loading is not the source of prediction drift in this case.

docs.title: 2) Historical reference predictions match LAST checkpoint behavior, not BEST
docs.text:
• full_last vs historical reference:
  • Same row count, same timestamps, same signals.
  • Only tiny floating diffs (~1e-6 max abs) in numeric columns.
  • full_best vs historical reference:
  • Large differences in signals and scores.
  • Conclusion: the historical run that produced trades was effectively using LAST-epoch behavior.

docs.title: 3) BEST checkpoint output is much weaker for trade entry in this setup
docs.text:
• Bot logic opens only when combined_confidence >= entry_threshold.
  • In this experiment:
  • entry_threshold = 0.55.
  • full_best has combined_confidence max ~= 0.0457 (no rows can pass 0.55).
  • full_last has many rows above 0.55.
  • Conclusion: switching from last to best can legitimately collapse trades to near-zero in this trial.

docs.title: 4) metadata.json becomes stale after multipart appends
docs.text:
• DB row (parquets table) gets updated correctly on append:
  • part_count, range_end, parquet_rows are correct.
  • Disk metadata JSON is written at initial creation only and is not refreshed after append.
  • This is why folders show:
  • metadata.json part_count=1
  • multiple part_*.parquet files on disk
  • DB with correct part_count > 1

docs.title: Code Locations (for fix)
docs.text:
• Initial metadata JSON write at create:
  • modules/core/src/rainbow/core/services/parquet_service.py:875
  • DB metadata update on append:
  • modules/core/src/rainbow/core/services/parquet_service.py:1112
  • Append returns without rewriting metadata.json:
  • modules/core/src/rainbow/core/services/parquet_service.py:1137
  • Prediction writer that triggers append path:
  • modules/backtesting-engine/src/rainbow/backtesting_engine/helpers/prediction_parquet_writer.py:255

docs.title: Minimal Fix Proposal
docs.text:
In append_parquet_file(): 1. Keep DB update as-is. 2. After updated is returned by repository update, call: - self.create_parquet_metadata_json(updated, parquet_path, config_app) 3. Then return updated.

This keeps JSON metadata aligned with DB and on-disk part files after each append.

docs.title: Optional Follow-Up Fixes
docs.text:
1. Logging clarity:
  2. Add a debug log on append with updated part_count, range_end, and parquet_rows.
  3. Checkpoint selection clarity:
  4. If stage evaluation intends strict best behavior, avoid silent fallback semantics and log actual loaded file/mode.

docs.title: A) Metadata synchronization tests (critical)
docs.text:
• Add tests around multipart append to verify all three are synchronized after each append:
  • disk part count
  • DB part_count / range_end / parquet_rows
  • metadata.json part_count / range_end

Suggested tests: 1. test_append_updates_metadata_json_part_count 2. test_append_updates_metadata_json_range_end 3. test_prediction_writer_multipart_metadata_consistency

docs.title: B) End-to-end lazy vs full prediction equivalence tests
docs.text:
• Current equivalence tests focus on window generation logic.
  • Add an integration test that runs prediction generation both ways for the same trial/checkpoint and compares:
  • row count
  • timestamp equality
  • signal equality
  • numeric diffs with strict tolerance

Suggested tests: 1. test_backtest_predictions_lazy_equals_full_last_checkpoint 2. test_backtest_predictions_lazy_equals_full_best_checkpoint

docs.title: C) Checkpoint provenance tests (best vs last)
docs.text:
• Add tests that guarantee expected checkpoint mode is used by evaluation/backtesting flows.
  • Use fixtures where best and last checkpoints are intentionally different and easy to detect.

Suggested tests: 1. test_stage_eval_loads_best_checkpoint_when_available 2. test_backtest_loader_checkpoint_mode_is_explicit 3. test_checkpoint_fallback_is_logged_and_detectable

docs.title: D) Threshold-aware behavioral regression tests
docs.text:
• Add smoke tests that validate prediction distribution against bot entry logic:
  • % rows with combined_confidence >= entry_threshold
  • expected non-zero opportunity count for selected known fixture

This catches “no trades” regressions even when window equivalence still passes.

docs.title: E) Real-data alignment stress tests
docs.text:
• Existing lazy equivalence tests use mostly synthetic contiguous data.
  • Add cases with real gaps and multi-timeframe boundary edges:
  • irregular missing candles
  • mixed 5m/15m/1h/4h with edge-range truncation
  • exact timestamp-set equivalence checks across lazy/full paths

docs.title: VPN Scripts - Guía Rápida
docs.text: Scripts para usar ExpressVPN con Rainbow Trader desde regiones bloqueadas (Indonesia).

docs.title: 1. Crear archivo de credenciales
docs.text:
• Ve a tu cuenta de ExpressVPN
  • expressvpn.com/setup#manual
  • Copia Usuario y Contraseña

Copia scripts/vpn/config/.env.example a scripts/vpn/config/.env y completa tus credenciales de ExpressVPN:

cp scripts/vpn/config/.env.example scripts/vpn/config/.env
USER=tu_usuario_expressvpn
PASSWORD=tu_contraseña_expressvpn

docs.title: 2. Descargar configuración OpenVPN
docs.text:
• Ve a tu cuenta de ExpressVPN
  • expressvpn.com/setup#manual
  • Descarga el archivo .ovpn para el servidor que quieras usar
  • Guárdalo en scripts/vpn/config/
  • Actualiza la variable CONFIG_FILE en start_vpn.sh si el nombre es diferente

docs.title: Probar conexión (sin VPN)
docs.text:
./scripts/vpn/test_connection.sh

Esto prueba: 1. ✅ Google (verifica que tienes internet) 2. ❌ Binance (debería fallar desde Indonesia)

docs.title: Iniciar VPN
docs.text:
./scripts/vpn/start_vpn.sh

Esto: - Lee credenciales desde .env - Inicia OpenVPN en modo daemon - Crea un proxy SOCKS5 local en 127.0.0.1:1080

docs.title: Probar conexión (con VPN)
docs.text:
./scripts/vpn/test_connection.sh

Ahora ambos deberían funcionar: 1. ✅ Google 2. ✅ Binance

docs.title: Usar data-fetcher con VPN
docs.text:
python -m rainbow.data_fetcher fetch-symbol \
  --symbol BTCUSDT \
  --timeframe 1d \
  --start 2024-01-01 \
  --end 2024-04-01 \
  --proxy socks5://127.0.0.1:1080

docs.title: Detener VPN
docs.text: ./scripts/vpn/stop_vpn.sh

docs.title: 📁 Estructura de Archivos
docs.text:
scripts/vpn/
├── README.md                    # Este archivo
├── config/
│   ├── .env                     # Credenciales (NO commitear)
│   └── *.ovpn                   # Configuración OpenVPN
├── install_vpn.sh               # Instala OpenVPN (solo una vez)
├── start_vpn.sh                 # Inicia el VPN
├── stop_vpn.sh                  # Detiene el VPN
├── test_connection.sh           # Wrapper bash para test
└── test_binance.py              # Script Python de prueba

docs.title: Error: "Archivo .env no encontrado"
docs.text: Copia scripts/vpn/config/.env.example a scripts/vpn/config/.env y completa tus credenciales.

docs.title: Error: "USER y PASSWORD deben estar definidos"
docs.text:
Verifica que el archivo .env tenga el formato correcto (sin espacios alrededor del =):

USER=mi_usuario
PASSWORD=mi_contraseña

docs.title: VPN no se conecta
docs.text:
1. Verifica que OpenVPN esté instalado: openvpn --version
  2. Verifica que el archivo .ovpn exista en scripts/vpn/config/
  3. Verifica que las credenciales sean correctas
  4. Revisa los logs: sudo journalctl -u openvpn -f

docs.title: Binance sigue fallando con VPN
docs.text:
1. Verifica que el VPN esté corriendo: pgrep openvpn
  2. Detén y reinicia el VPN: bash ./scripts/vpn/stop_vpn.sh ./scripts/vpn/start_vpn.sh
  3. Espera 10-20 segundos y prueba de nuevo

docs.title: 💡 Notas
docs.text:
• El VPN solo afecta al tráfico que uses con --proxy, no a toda tu PC
  • Las credenciales en .env están en .gitignore y no se subirán a Git
  • El proxy SOCKS5 corre en el puerto 1080 por defecto
  • Para cambiar el puerto, edita la variable SOCKS_PORT en start_vpn.sh
